topics.title: Classification of Solid Wastes (Source and Type Based)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET831 - WASTE MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Classification of Solid Wastes (Source and Type Based)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Solid Waste** is defined under the *Solid Waste Management Rules (SWM), 2016* (Government of India) as any discarded solid or semi-solid material arising from human and animal activities, including municipal, industrial, biomedical, and construction & demolition waste. From an engineering perspective, solid waste is heterogeneous refuse produced by residential, commercial, institutional, agricultural, and industrial operations which requires systematic handling, storage, collection, transportation, treatment, and disposal.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Source-based & Type-based Classification)**
> Classification of solid waste is the **systematic grouping of refuse materials** based on two fundamental criteria: **(i) Source of generation** (where the waste originates) and **(ii) Type/Composition** (what the waste physically and chemically consists of). This classification is the foundational step in planning any **Integrated Solid Waste Management (ISWM)** system.

## 1.2 Why Classification is the First Step in Waste Management

Before any treatment, recovery, or disposal can be planned, an engineer must answer two questions:
1. **Where is the waste coming from?** (Source-based)
2. **What is it made of?** (Type-based)

Without classification, the *4R principle* (Reduce, Reuse, Recycle, Recover) cannot be applied effectively.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Household Recycling Trolley"**
> Imagine a domestic kitchen. When you throw leftovers (vegetable peels, food scraps) into a green bin, plastic bottles into a blue bin, and broken glass into a red bin — you are essentially doing **type-based classification**. Now consider a larger picture: a city's waste management department receives waste from **homes (domestic)**, **offices (commercial)**, **hospitals (biomedical)**, and **factories (industrial)**. Separating and handling each stream differently is **source-based classification**. Both are necessary to prevent hazardous contamination (e.g., hospital waste mixing with food waste) and to maximize resource recovery.

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (As per KTU & CPCB Norms)

| Parameter | Standard Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Per capita solid waste generation (India) | **0.21 – 0.62** | kg/person/day |
| Bulk density of mixed municipal solid waste (MSW) | **300 – 500** | kg/m³ |
| Density of compacted MSW | **600 – 800** | kg/m³ |
| Moisture content of fresh MSW | **40 – 60** | % |
| Calorific value of MSW (Indian conditions) | **800 – 1500** | kcal/kg |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Source-Based Classification of Solid Wastes

The waste is grouped by its **point of generation**. The principal sources recognized in KTU 2024 syllabus are:

### A. Residential / Domestic Waste
- **Origin:** Single-family homes, apartments, hostels.
- **Composition:** Food waste, paper, plastics, garden trimmings, glass, textiles.
- **Quantity Driver:** Number of residents × per-capita generation rate.

### B. Commercial Waste
- **Origin:** Shops, offices, restaurants, hotels, markets, entertainment venues.
- **Composition:** High paper, cardboard, packaging, food waste (from hotels).
- **Characteristic:** **High volume, low hazard**, generated during business hours.

### C. Institutional Waste
- **Origin:** Schools, colleges, government offices, prisons, military bases.
- **Composition:** Mixed paper, cafeteria waste, e-waste (from computer labs).
- **Note:** Universities like KTU campuses fall under this category.

### D. Municipal Waste
- **Origin:** Street sweeping, parks, beaches, construction debris from civic works, sewage treatment plant residues.
- **Characteristic:** Includes **Municipal Solid Waste (MSW)** — a major focus area in KTU.

### E. Industrial Waste
- **Origin:** Manufacturing plants, refineries, mines, power stations.
- **Sub-types:** Process waste, slag, fly ash, sludge, scrap metal.
- **Hazard:** Can be **hazardous or non-hazardous** depending on industry.

### F. Construction and Demolition (C&D) Waste
- **Origin:** New construction, renovation, demolition of buildings and roads.
- **Composition:** Concrete, bricks, tiles, wood, metals, dust.
- **Characteristic:** **Bulky, inert, but high in volume** — typically **30%** of total urban waste in India.

### G. Agricultural Waste
- **Origin:** Farms, plantations, dairies, poultry, fisheries.
- **Composition:** Crop residues, animal dung, husk, straw, packaging.
- **Use:** Often reused as fodder, mulch, or compost — a circular economy example.

### H. Biomedical / Hospital Waste
- **Origin:** Hospitals, clinics, pathology labs, blood banks, veterinary hospitals.
- **Composition:** Human anatomical waste, sharps, medicines, chemicals, infectious materials.
- **Governed by:** *Biomedical Waste Management Rules, 2016 (amended 2018)*.
- **Hazard Category:** **Hazardous** — handled separately.

### I. Electronic Waste (E-Waste)
- **Origin:** Discarded computers, mobiles, TVs, batteries, circuit boards.
- **Governed by:** *E-Waste Management Rules, 2016 (amended 2018)*.

### J. Hazardous Waste
- **Origin:** Industries, laboratories, households (paints, pesticides, batteries).
- **Characteristic:** Toxic, flammable, reactive, corrosive — requires **specialized treatment**.

---

## 2.2 Type-Based Classification of Solid Wastes

Waste is classified by its **physical, chemical, and biological nature**:

### 1. Organic / Bio-degradable Waste
- **Examples:** Food waste, yard waste, paper (lower grade), manure, sewage.
- **Property:** Decomposes naturally by microbial action.
- **Treatment:** Composting, vermicomposting, anaerobic digestion, biogas generation.

### 2. Recyclable Waste
- **Examples:** Paper, plastics, metals (ferrous & non-ferrous), glass, rubber, textiles.
- **Property:** Can be reprocessed into new products.
- **KTU High-Yield Point:** Source segregation at the household level is critical.

### 3. Inert Waste
- **Examples:** Construction debris, sand, stones, broken tiles.
- **Property:** Does not undergo significant physical, chemical, or biological changes.
- **Disposal:** Used for landfilling or road sub-base construction.

### 4. Hazardous Waste
- **Examples:** Industrial chemicals, batteries, pesticides, paints, hospital waste, e-waste.
- **Property:** Poses substantial threats to public health or environment.
- **Treatment:** Secure landfilling, incineration, stabilization.

### 5. Composite / Mixed Waste
- **Examples:** Tetra packs, multilayer plastics, laminated paper, mixed fabrics.
- **Property:** Difficult to recycle; contains bonded layers of different materials.
- **Disposal:** Usually incinerated or landfilled.

### 6. Biomedical Waste
- **Examples:** Human tissue, blood, body fluids, used syringes, expired drugs.
- **Treated separately** as per *Biomedical Waste Management Rules, 2016*.

---

## 2.3 The 'Why' and 'How' Behind Each Classification

- **Why Source-based?** — Determines the *collection logistics* (vehicle type, frequency), *regulatory body*, and *point-of-generation segregation strategy*.
- **Why Type-based?** — Determines the *treatment technology* (composting vs. recycling vs. incineration) and the *end-of-pipe disposal method*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** A well-designed MSW management system needs **both** classifications simultaneously. For example, source-based data tells a city engineer *how much waste* is expected from a residential zone, while type-based data tells the plant designer *what kind of processing equipment* (shredder, magnetic separator, composting unit) is required.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $q = \dfrac{W}{P \cdot t}$ | Per-capita waste generation rate ($q$) | kg/person/day |
| 2 | $W = q \cdot P \cdot t$ | Total waste generated from a population | kg or tonnes |
| 3 | $\rho_b = \dfrac{m}{V}$ | Bulk density of waste | kg/m³ |
| 4 | $V = \dfrac{W}{\rho_b}$ | Volume occupied by waste (for bin/landfill design) | m³ |
| 5 | $MC(\%) = \dfrac{W_w - W_d}{W_w} \times 100$ | Moisture content of waste | % |
| 6 | $C_v = \dfrac{HHV_{waste}}{HHV_{fuel}}$ | Calorific value ratio (energy recovery) | dimensionless |
| 7 | $P_{organic}(\%) = \dfrac{M_{organic}}{M_{total}} \times 100$ | Organic fraction of waste | % |
| 8 | $n = \dfrac{V_{total}}{V_{container}}$ | Number of bins/containers required | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Variable Notation**
> $W$ = Total mass of waste, $P$ = Population, $t$ = Time period, $m$ = Mass, $V$ = Volume, $W_w$ = Wet weight, $W_d$ = Dry weight, $HHV$ = Higher Heating Value.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Municipal Engineers** use source-based data to design **collection routes** and **vehicle fleet size**.
- **Process Engineers** use type-based data to design **Material Recovery Facilities (MRF)**, **composting plants**, and **incinerators**.
- **Environmental Engineers** use both classifications to perform **Life Cycle Assessment (LCA)** of waste management systems.
- **Civil Engineers** use bulk density and volume calculations to design **sanitary landfills** with proper compaction and leachate management.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Per-Capita Waste Generation (q)

The **per-capita waste generation rate** is the most fundamental metric in solid waste engineering. It is derived as follows.

### Step 1: Define the Total Waste Generated Over a Period

Let a city generate $W$ kilograms of solid waste over a period of $t$ days. This $W$ is measured at the weighbridge of the disposal site or processing plant.

### Step 2: Define the Contributing Population

Let $P$ be the total population contributing to that waste. For a residential zone, $P$ is the number of residents; for an institutional zone, $P$ is the number of staff + students + visitors.

### Step 3: Set Up the Proportional Relationship

If every person contributes equally, the average per-capita rate $q$ is:

$$q = \dfrac{\text{Total waste generated}}{\text{Population} \times \text{Time period}}$$

### Step 4: Express in Standard Engineering Units

In SI units (commonly used in KTU problems):

$$q = \dfrac{W \left[ \text{kg} \right]}{P \left[ \text{persons} \right] \times t \left[ \text{days} \right]}$$

This gives $q$ in **kg/person/day**.

### Step 5: Rearranged Forms (For Exam Use)

Rearranging for any unknown:

$$W = q \cdot P \cdot t$$

$$P = \dfrac{W}{q \cdot t}$$

$$t = \dfrac{W}{q \cdot P}$$

---

## 3.2 Worked-Out Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A town has a population of **50,000**. The per-capita waste generation rate is **0.45 kg/person/day**. The bulk density of waste is **350 kg/m³**. The municipal authority wants to store 3 days' worth of waste in a temporary transfer station. Calculate:
- (i) Total waste generated in 3 days.
- (ii) Volume of storage space required.

### Solution (Step-by-Step)

**Given:**
- $P = 50{,}000$ persons
- $q = 0.45$ kg/person/day
- $\rho_b = 350$ kg/m³
- $t = 3$ days

**Step (i): Total waste in 3 days**

Apply the formula:
$$W = q \cdot P \cdot t$$

Substitute the values:
$$W = 0.45 \times 50{,}000 \times 3$$

$$W = 67{,}500 \text{ kg} = 67.5 \text{ tonnes}$$

**Step (ii): Volume required**

Apply the bulk density formula:
$$V = \dfrac{W}{\rho_b}$$

$$V = \dfrac{67{,}500}{350}$$

$$V \approx 192.86 \text{ m}^3$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Answer:** The town generates **67.5 tonnes** of waste in 3 days, requiring a storage volume of approximately **192.86 m³**. This volume is critical for sizing the transfer station's primary collection hopper.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Source vs. Type

The matrix below shows how different **sources** produce different **types** of waste. This cross-mapping is a frequently asked concept in KTU module questions.

| Source $\downarrow$ \ Type $\rightarrow$ | Organic | Recyclable | Inert | Hazardous | Composite |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Residential** | High (Food) | High (Paper, Plastic) | Low | Low (Batteries) | Medium |
| **Commercial** | Medium (Hotel waste) | High (Cardboard) | Low | Low | High (Packaging) |
| **Institutional** | Low | High (Paper) | Low | Medium (E-waste) | Low |
| **Municipal** | Medium (Sweeping) | Low | High (Road waste) | Low | Low |
| **Industrial** | Low | High (Scrap metal) | Medium (Slag) | **Very High** (Chemicals) | High |
| **C&D** | Negligible | Medium (Metal) | **Very High** | Low (Paint tins) | Medium |
| **Agricultural** | **Very High** (Crops) | Low | Low | Medium (Pesticides) | Low |
| **Biomedical** | Medium (Anatomical) | Low (Glass vials) | Low | **Very High** (Sharps) | Medium |

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Number of Storage Containers (Bins) Required

A frequently asked derivation in KTU exams is sizing the number of bins for a waste collection zone.

### Step 1: Total Waste Volume Over a Period

Using $V_{total} = \dfrac{W}{\rho_b}$ from the formula sheet.

### Step 2: Volume of a Single Container

Let $V_{container}$ be the geometric volume of one bin (typically 1.1 m³ for standard 120 L HDPE bins in India, or 660 L for wheeled bins).

### Step 3: Number of Bins

$$n = \dfrac{V_{total}}{V_{container}}$$

### Step 4: Practical Adjustment Factor

In practice, engineers multiply by a **fill factor** $f$ (usually 0.75–0.85) and a **spare factor** $s$ (1.15) to account for overfilling and breakdown:

$$n_{design} = \left\lceil \dfrac{V_{total}}{V_{container} \cdot f} \right\rceil \cdot s$$

The $\lceil \cdot \rceil$ denotes the **ceiling function** (round up to next integer).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why a ceiling function?** Because you cannot deploy a "fraction" of a bin. If the calculation gives $n = 12.3$ bins, the engineer must provide **13 bins** to ensure adequate storage.

---

## 3.5 Classification Decision Tree (Logic Flow)

The decision flow used by KTU examiners when a question asks *"Classify the following wastes"* is:

1. **Is the source known?**
   - Yes → Apply **source-based** category (e.g., residential, industrial).
   - No → Move to step 2.
2. **Is the material biodegradable?**
   - Yes → **Organic** (if decomposes within weeks-months).
   - No → Move to step 3.
3. **Is it toxic, reactive, flammable, or corrosive?**
   - Yes → **Hazardous**.
   - No → Move to step 4.
4. **Does it have recyclable value?**
   - Yes → **Recyclable**.
   - No → Move to step 5.
5. **Does it undergo significant transformation?**
   - No → **Inert**.
   - Yes (mixed layers) → **Composite**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hierarchical Classification — Source-Based (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[ROOT: SOLID WASTE] --> B[Source-Based Classification]
    A --> C[Type-Based Classification]
    B --> D[Residential / Domestic]
    B --> E[Commercial]
    B --> F[Institutional]
    B --> G[Municipal]
    B --> H[Industrial]
    B --> I[Construction and Demolition]
    B --> J[Agricultural]
    B --> K[Biomedical / Hospital]
    B --> L[Electronic Waste]
    B --> M[Hazardous Waste]
    D --> D1[Food, Paper, Plastic, Garden waste]
    E --> E1[Cardboard, Packaging, Office waste]
    F --> F1[Paper, Cafeteria, E-waste]
    G --> G1[Street sweeping, Sewage residue, Parks]
    H --> H1[Slag, Sludge, Fly ash, Process scrap]
    I --> I1[Concrete, Bricks, Tiles, Wood, Metal]
    J --> J1[Crop residue, Manure, Husk]
    K --> K1[Anatomical, Sharps, Pathological]
    L --> L1[PCBs, Batteries, Cables, Mobiles]
    M --> M1[Chemicals, Pesticides, Paints, Oils]
```

## 4.2 Hierarchical Classification — Type-Based (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[ROOT: SOLID WASTE] --> B[Type-Based Classification]
    B --> C[Organic / Biodegradable]
    B --> D[Recyclable]
    B --> E[Inert]
    B --> F[Hazardous]
    B --> G[Composite]
    B --> H[Biomedical]
    C --> C1[Food waste, Yard waste, Paper]
    C --> C2[Treatment: Composting, Anaerobic Digestion]
    D --> D1[Paper, Plastic, Glass, Metal, Rubber]
    D --> D2[Treatment: Material Recovery Facility MRF]
    E --> E1[Construction debris, Sand, Stones]
    E --> E2[Disposal: Landfill, Road sub-base]
    F --> F1[Chemicals, Batteries, Pesticides, E-waste]
    F --> F2[Treatment: Secure Landfill, Incineration]
    G --> G1[Multilayer plastics, Tetra packs, Laminates]
    G --> G2[Disposal: Incineration, Co-processing]
    H --> H1[Human tissue, Blood, Sharps, Drugs]
    H --> H2[Treatment: Autoclave, Incineration, Deep burial]
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Source to Treatment Mapping

This diagram shows how a **source-based stream** is mapped to its appropriate **type-based treatment** in an Integrated Solid Waste Management (ISWM) framework.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Source: Residential Zone] --> B[Segregation at Household: Wet and Dry]
    B --> C[Collection: LCV / Auto tipper]
    C --> D[Transfer Station]
    D --> E[Material Recovery Facility MRF]
    D --> F[Composting / Biogas Plant]
    D --> G[Sanitary Landfill]
    E --> E1[Recyclables to Industry]
    F --> F1[Compost to Farms]
    G --> G1[Inert Residue Disposed]
    A2[Source: Hospital] --> B2[Color-coded Bins as per BMW Rules]
    B2 --> C2[Dedicated Biomedical Vehicle]
    C2 --> D2[Common Biomedical Waste Treatment Facility CBMWTF]
    D2 --> E2[Autoclave for Infectious]
    D2 --> F2[Incinerator for Pathological]
    D2 --> G2[Sharps Pit for Sharps]
    A3[Source: Industry] --> B3[Hazardous Waste Storage]
    B3 --> C3[Common Hazardous Waste TSDF]
    C3 --> D3[Secure Landfill]
    C3 --> E3[Stabilization / Solidification]
    C3 --> F3[Incineration]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Each "Source" block (left) routes waste through a *segregation → collection → transfer → treatment* cascade. Notice how **biomedical waste** and **industrial hazardous waste** take completely separate, dedicated routes — they never mix with municipal solid waste. This is the cornerstone principle of modern waste engineering.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define the term "Solid Waste" as per the Solid Waste Management Rules, 2016. List any four source-based categories of solid waste.**

**Model Answer:**
Solid Waste is any solid or semi-solid discarded material arising from human and animal activities, including municipal, industrial, biomedical, and construction & demolition waste, as defined under SWM Rules 2016.

Four source-based categories:
1. Residential / Domestic waste
2. Commercial waste
3. Industrial waste
4. Biomedical / Hospital waste

*(Any four from the list of 10 categories in SECTION 2.1 are accepted.)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between organic and inorganic solid waste. Give two examples of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Organic Waste | Inorganic Waste |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Waste of biological origin that decomposes naturally | Waste of non-biological origin that does not decompose easily |
| **Decomposition** | Weeks to months | Years to centuries |
| **Examples** | Food scraps, garden trimmings, paper | Plastic bottles, glass, metal scrap, bricks |
| **Treatment** | Composting, biogas | Recycling, landfilling, incineration |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** and offers internal choice. The structure below is *Question A* OR *Question B*. Sub-parts (a) and (b) carry **7 marks each**.

---

### QUESTION A `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Classify solid wastes in detail based on **source of generation**. List at least **six categories** with example materials and one characteristic of each.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A residential locality has a population of **25,000** with a per-capita waste generation rate of **0.50 kg/person/day**. The bulk density of the collected waste is **400 kg/m³**. Calculate:
- (i) Total mass of waste generated per day.
- (ii) Total volume of waste generated per day.
- (iii) Number of standard **1.1 m³** bins required to store this daily waste, assuming a fill factor of 0.80 and a spare factor of 1.10. Use ceiling function for final value.

**Model Solution for (a):**

Refer to **Section 2.1** of these notes. The six most important categories to write:

1. **Residential / Domestic** — Food waste, paper, garden trimmings; *Characteristic: Mixed biodegradable and recyclable; handled by local municipality.*
2. **Commercial** — Cardboard, packaging, food waste; *Characteristic: High in volume during business hours.*
3. **Institutional** — Paper, cafeteria waste; *Characteristic: Predictable generation pattern.*
4. **Industrial** — Slag, sludge, scrap metal; *Characteristic: May be hazardous; regulated by State Pollution Control Board.*
5. **Construction & Demolition (C&D)** — Concrete, bricks; *Characteristic: Bulky, inert, ~30% of urban waste.*
6. **Biomedical / Hospital** — Sharps, anatomical waste; *Characteristic: Highly infectious; governed by BMW Rules 2016.*

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Given:**
- $P = 25{,}000$ persons
- $q = 0.50$ kg/person/day
- $\rho_b = 400$ kg/m³
- $V_{container} = 1.1$ m³
- $f = 0.80$, $s = 1.10$

**(i) Total mass per day:**
$$W = q \cdot P \cdot t = 0.50 \times 25{,}000 \times 1 = 12{,}500 \text{ kg/day}$$

**(ii) Total volume per day:**
$$V = \dfrac{W}{\rho_b} = \dfrac{12{,}500}{400} = 31.25 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}$$

**(iii) Number of bins:**

Apply the design formula:
$$n = \left\lceil \dfrac{V}{V_{container} \cdot f} \right\rceil \cdot s$$

Substitute:
$$n = \left\lceil \dfrac{31.25}{1.1 \times 0.80} \right\rceil \times 1.10$$

$$n = \left\lceil \dfrac{31.25}{0.88} \right\rceil \times 1.10$$

$$n = \left\lceil 35.51 \right\rceil \times 1.10$$

$$n = 36 \times 1.10 = 39.6 \approx \mathbf{40 \text{ bins}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Answer:** (i) 12,500 kg/day (ii) 31.25 m³/day (iii) **40 bins** required.

---

### QUESTION B `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Classify solid wastes based on **type/composition**. Explain any **five type-based categories** with examples and suitable treatment methods.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A university campus with **8,000 students and staff** generates institutional solid waste at a rate of **0.20 kg/person/day**. The waste composition is: 50% organic, 30% recyclable, 15% inert, and 5% composite. Calculate:
- (i) Total daily waste in kg.
- (ii) Mass of organic waste that can be composted, in kg/day.
- (iii) If 1 tonne of composted organic waste produces **200 kg of compost**, how much compost is produced daily?

**Model Solution for (a):**

Refer to **Section 2.2**. Five type-based categories to detail:

1. **Organic / Biodegradable** — Food waste, yard trimmings → Composting, anaerobic digestion, vermicomposting.
2. **Recyclable** — Paper, plastic, glass, metal → Material Recovery Facility (MRF) for segregation and baling.
3. **Inert** — Construction debris, sand, stones → Sanitary landfilling or used as road sub-base.
4. **Hazardous** — Industrial chemicals, pesticides, batteries → Secure landfilling, incineration, stabilization.
5. **Composite / Mixed** — Tetra packs, multilayer plastics → Co-processing in cement kilns, incineration with energy recovery.

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Given:**
- $P = 8{,}000$ persons
- $q = 0.20$ kg/person/day
- Organic fraction = 50%, Recyclable = 30%, Inert = 15%, Composite = 5%
- Compost yield = 200 kg per 1,000 kg (1 tonne) of organic waste

**(i) Total daily waste:**
$$W = q \cdot P \cdot t = 0.20 \times 8{,}000 \times 1 = 1{,}600 \text{ kg/day}$$

**(ii) Mass of organic waste:**
$$M_{organic} = W \times 50\% = 1{,}600 \times 0.50 = 800 \text{ kg/day}$$

**(iii) Compost produced daily:**

Given 1 tonne (1,000 kg) of organic waste → 200 kg compost
Therefore, yield ratio = 200 / 1,000 = 0.20

$$\text{Compost} = M_{organic} \times 0.20 = 800 \times 0.20 = 160 \text{ kg/day}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Answer:** (i) 1,600 kg/day (ii) 800 kg/day (iii) **160 kg/day of compost**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Unit Mismatch:** Writing $W$ in tonnes but $q$ in kg — always convert to consistent units. *KTU examiners deduct 1 full mark for unit errors.*
> 2. **Confusing source-based with type-based:** "Food waste" is a *type* (organic), not a *source*. The source is "residential kitchen". Mixing these definitions costs 2–3 marks.
> 3. **Forgetting the ceiling function $\lceil \cdot \rceil$:** Students often write $n = 35.51$ bins. Examiners mark this as wrong — you must round **up** to 36.
> 4. **Skipping the fill factor and spare factor:** In real design, $f$ and $s$ must be applied. Marks are reserved for the *design equation* in part (b).
> 5. **Not writing units in the final answer:** Every numerical answer in KTU must carry units. "40" without "bins" loses 0.5 mark.
> 6. **Vague type-based examples:** Writing "plastic" alone is not enough — specify "PET bottles" or "HDPE containers" for full credit.
> 7. **Missing governing rules:** Always mention the relevant Indian rule (SWM 2016, BMW 2016, EWaste 2016) for biomedical, hazardous, and e-waste classifications.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024**

- Solid waste is any **discarded solid/semi-solid material** from human/animal activity.
- **Two main classifications** in KTU Module 1: **Source-based** and **Type-based**.
- **Source-based (10 main categories):** Residential, Commercial, Institutional, Municipal, Industrial, C&D, Agricultural, Biomedical, E-Waste, Hazardous.
- **Type-based (6 main categories):** Organic, Recyclable, Inert, Hazardous, Composite, Biomedical.
- **Biomedical waste** is governed by **BMW Rules 2016 (amended 2018)**.
- **E-waste** is governed by **E-Waste Management Rules 2016 (amended 2018)**.
- **Hazardous waste** is governed by **Hazardous and Other Wastes (Management & Transboundary Movement) Rules, 2016**.
- **Key formulas:** $q = W / (P \cdot t)$, $\rho_b = m/V$, $V = W/\rho_b$, $n = \lceil V/(V_{container} \cdot f) \rceil \cdot s$.
- **Per-capita waste generation (India):** **0.21 – 0.62 kg/person/day**.
- **Bulk density of MSW:** **300 – 500 kg/m³** (uncompacted).
- **Moisture content of fresh MSW:** **40 – 60%**.
- **C&D waste** constitutes about **30%** of total urban solid waste in India.
- **Decision flow for type-based classification:** Source known? → Biodegradable? → Hazardous? → Recyclable? → Inert? → Composite.
- **Always use ceiling function** for the number of bins/containers.
- **Always include units** in the final numerical answer.
- **Cross-mapping matrix** (source vs. type) is a high-frequency KTU exam question.
- **Mermaid-friendly terms to memorize:** ISWM, MRF, CBMWTF, TSDF, 4R Principle.
- **Most-tested combination:** A definition question (Part A) + a numerical problem based on per-capita generation and bin sizing (Part B).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Solid Waste Management (SWM)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET831 - WASTE MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Solid Waste Management (SWM)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Solid Waste Management (SWM) — Module 1

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Solid Waste Management (SWM)** is defined as the systematic, scientific, and regulatory discipline associated with the **generation, segregation at source, storage, collection, transfer, transport, processing, treatment, and final disposal of solid wastes** in a manner that is environmentally sustainable, publicly hygienic, economically viable, and socially acceptable, as prescribed under Indian Standard IS 14543:1998 and the Municipal Solid Waste (Management and Handling) Rules, 2000 (and subsequent amendments including SWM Rules 2016).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Definition (verbatim from syllabus):** Solid waste refers to any discarded material arising from domestic, commercial, industrial, agricultural, mining, construction, and institutional activities that is not a liquid or a contained gas. SWM is the **complete functional chain** that handles this waste from "cradle to grave."

### Components of SWM (The 6R + Functional Chain)

| Hierarchy Level | Functional Stage | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Level 0** | **Refuse** / Rethink | Avoid generation at source |
| **Level 1** | **Reduce** | Minimize volume at source |
| **Level 2** | **Reuse** | Use the item again for same/different function |
| **Level 3** | **Recycle** | Reprocess into new raw materials |
| **Level 4** | **Recover** | Energy recovery (e.g., RDF, biogas) |
| **Level 5** | **Residual Disposal** | Engineered landfilling |

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Analogy:** Think of SWM like the **"circulatory system of a city."** Just as the human body generates metabolic waste (CO₂, urea) that must be **collected (veins) → transported (bloodstream) → treated (kidneys/liver) → safely discharged**, a city generates Municipal Solid Waste (MSW) that must follow an identical life-support pipeline. If the "kidneys" (treatment plants) fail, the "blood" (environment) gets poisoned. SWM is the *kidney of urban infrastructure*.

### Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (KTU Board Favourite)

- **Bulk Density of MSW (Indian context):** $\rho_b = \mathbf{300\text{ to } 500 \text{ kg/m}^3}$ (uncompacted), $\mathbf{600\text{ to } 900 \text{ kg/m}^3}$ (compacted by compactor).
- **Standard Waste Generation Rate (Indian cities):** $\mathbf{0.5 \text{ kg/capita/day}$ (lower-tier cities) to $0.8 \text{ kg/capita/day}$ (metropolitan cities like Mumbai, Delhi).}$
- **Moisture Content of Fresh MSW:** $\mathbf{40\% \text{ to } 60\%}$ by weight.
- **Calorific Value of MSW (Indian context):** $\mathbf{1000\text{ to } 1500 \text{ kcal/kg}$ (low) — pre-drying incinerable, India often uses $\le 1500$ kcal/kg.}$
- **Per capita MSW projection in India (CPHEEO):** $\mathbf{0.6 \text{ kg/capita/day}$ by 2025.}$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Sources of Solid Waste (KTU Module 1 Syllabus Directive)

Solid waste is classified based on **origin**. The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly requires students to enumerate and explain the following 8 source categories:

1. **Residential / Domestic** — Kitchen waste, paper, plastics, garden trimmings.
2. **Commercial** — Office buildings, shops, restaurants, hotels (food waste dominant).
3. **Institutional** — Schools, colleges, hospitals (biomedical crossover).
4. **Construction & Demolition (C&D)** — Concrete, bricks, wood, steel.
5. **Industrial** — Process-specific hazardous and non-hazardous waste.
6. **Municipal Services** — Street sweeping, parks, water treatment sludge.
7. **Agricultural** — Crop residues, animal manure, pesticides containers.
8. **Biomedical & E-Waste** — Specialized regulated streams.

## 2.2 Classification by Type (Engineering Material Perspective)

- **Organic (Bio-degradable):** Food waste, yard waste, paper — $\mathbf{40\text{–}60\%}$ of Indian MSW.
- **Recyclable (Non-biodegradable, inert):** Plastics, glass, metals — $\mathbf{20\text{–}30\%}$.
- **Inert / Construction:** Construction rubble, sand — $\mathbf{10\text{–}15\%}$.
- **Domestic Hazardous / E-Waste:** Batteries, paints, CFLs — $\mathbf{1\text{–}5\%}$.

## 2.3 Physical & Chemical Properties of MSW (High-Yield Board Topic)

| Property | Symbol | Formula / Description | Typical Indian MSW Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Density (Bulk)** | $\rho_b$ | $\rho_b = \dfrac{M}{V_{bulk}}$ | $300\text{–}500 \text{ kg/m}^3$ |
| **Moisture Content** | $M_c$ | $M_c = \dfrac{W_w - W_d}{W_w} \times 100$ | $40\text{–}60\%$ |
| **Volatile Solids** | $VS$ | $VS = \dfrac{W_{dry} - W_{ash}}{W_{dry}} \times 100$ | $40\text{–}60\%$ |
| **Calorific Value** | $CV$ | Energy released per kg (Bomb Calorimeter) | $1000\text{–}1500 \text{ kcal/kg}$ |
| **pH** | $pH$ | Log of H⁺ ion concentration | $5\text{–}7$ (acidic to neutral) |
| **C/N Ratio** | $C/N$ | $\dfrac{\% \text{ Carbon}}{\% \text{ Nitrogen}}$ | $25:1 \text{ to } 35:1$ |
| **Particle Size** | $d_p$ | Sieve analysis | Highly heterogeneous |

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" Behind C/N Ratio:** For efficient composting, the C/N ratio should ideally be **30:1**. If MSW has high C/N (carbon-rich), microbial decomposition slows because nitrogen becomes the limiting nutrient. This is why KTU examiners frequently ask: *"Why is fresh MSW difficult to compost directly?"* — Answer: Because its high C/N ratio (often 50:1 or more) is suboptimal.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **DO NOT use the `\|` symbol** inside the table. Use `\vert` for absolute values.

| # | Formula | Description | KTU Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q = P \times q \times 10^{-3}$ | Total MSW per day (tonnes/day) | Master Equation |
| 2 | $V = \dfrac{Q}{\rho_b}$ | Daily volume of waste (m³/day) | Collection vehicle sizing |
| 3 | $M_c = \dfrac{W_w - W_d}{W_w} \times 100$ | Moisture content (%) | Composting/incineration design |
| 4 | $VS = \dfrac{W_d - W_a}{W_d} \times 100$ | Volatile solids (%) | Energy recovery |
| 5 | $N_v = \dfrac{Q}{C \times n \times f}$ | Number of vehicles required | Fleet planning |
| 6 | $\rho_c = \rho_b \times R_c$ | Compacted density | Landfill volume estimation |
| 7 | $L_v = \dfrac{V_{total}}{A_{landfill}}$ | Landfill volume required | Site sizing |
| 8 | $N_c = \dfrac{Q}{T_d \times C_t}$ | Number of trips per vehicle | Routing optimization |

Where:
- $Q$ = Quantity of waste (tonnes/day)
- $P$ = Population (number of people)
- $q$ = Per capita waste generation (kg/capita/day)
- $\rho_b$ = Bulk density (kg/m³)
- $R_c$ = Compaction ratio (typically $3:1$ to $4:1$)
- $C$ = Capacity of vehicle (tonnes)
- $n$ = Trips per day
- $f$ = Frequency of collection
- $T_d$ = Time per trip
- $C_t$ = Capacity per trip
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Master Equation Derivation: Total MSW Generation

The fundamental equation for estimating **total municipal solid waste generated per day** in a city is derived from first principles.

**Given:**
- $P$ = Population of the city (number of inhabitants)
- $q$ = Per capita waste generation rate (kg/capita/day)
- $Q$ = Total waste generated per day (kg/day)

**Step 1: Define per capita generation.**

Per capita generation is the average mass of waste produced by **one person in one day**.

$$q = \frac{\text{Total waste per day}}{\text{Total population}} = \frac{Q_{kg/day}}{P}$$

**Step 2: Invert the relationship to obtain Q.**

$$Q_{kg/day} = P \times q$$

**Step 3: Convert from kg/day to tonnes/day (since landfill and vehicle capacities are in tonnes).**

Since $1 \text{ tonne} = 1000 \text{ kg}$:

$$Q_{tonnes/day} = \frac{P \times q}{1000}$$

**Final Master Equation:**

$$\boxed{Q = P \times q \times 10^{-3} \;\; [\text{tonnes/day}]}$$

---

## 3.2 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Board Pattern)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is a high-frequency KTU ESE question (Dec 2022, June 2023 pattern).** Memorize the methodology.

**Problem:** A Kerala municipality has a population of $\mathbf{2,50,000}$. The per capita waste generation rate is $\mathbf{0.55 \text{ kg/capita/day}}$. The bulk density of uncompacted waste is $\mathbf{400 \text{ kg/m}^3}$. Calculate:
1. Total waste generated per day (in tonnes).
2. Volume of waste generated per day.
3. Number of compactor trucks of $5$-tonne capacity required if each makes $3$ trips/day.

### Solution:

**Part (a): Total waste per day**

$$P = 2{,}50{,}000, \quad q = 0.55 \text{ kg/capita/day}$$

$$Q = P \times q \times 10^{-3}$$

$$Q = 2{,}50{,}000 \times 0.55 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$Q = \frac{2{,}50{,}000 \times 0.55}{1000}$$

$$Q = \frac{1{,}37{,}500}{1000}$$

$$\boxed{Q = 137.5 \text{ tonnes/day}}$$

**Part (b): Daily volume of waste**

Given: $\rho_b = 400 \text{ kg/m}^3$

$$V = \frac{Q \times 1000}{\rho_b} \quad [\text{since } Q \text{ is in tonnes, convert to kg first}]$$

$$V = \frac{137.5 \times 1000}{400}$$

$$V = \frac{1{,}37{,}500}{400}$$

$$\boxed{V = 343.75 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}}$$

**Part (c): Number of compactor trucks**

Given: Capacity $C = 5$ tonnes, Trips per day $n = 3$.

$$N_v = \frac{Q}{C \times n}$$

$$N_v = \frac{137.5}{5 \times 3}$$

$$N_v = \frac{137.5}{15}$$

$$\boxed{N_v = 9.17 \approx 10 \text{ trucks}}$$

**KTU Board Insight:** Always round **up** for fleet sizing, never round down.

---

## 3.3 Compaction Ratio Derivation

When a compactor passes over loose MSW, the **compaction ratio ($R_c$)** is defined as:

$$R_c = \frac{V_{loose}}{V_{compacted}} = \frac{\rho_{compacted}}{\rho_{loose}}$$

**Derivation logic:**
- Mass is conserved during compaction: $M = \rho_{loose} \cdot V_{loose} = \rho_{compacted} \cdot V_{compacted}$
- Therefore: $\dfrac{V_{loose}}{V_{compacted}} = \dfrac{\rho_{compacted}}{\rho_{loose}}$

**Typical values:**

$$R_c = \begin{cases} 3:1 \text{ to } 4:1 & \text{Stationary compactor} \\ 2:1 \text{ to } 3:1 & \text{Movable compactor} \end{cases}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Compaction reduces transport trips, saves fuel, and extends landfill life. This is why **compactor trucks are the standard** in modern SWM systems.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Hierarchy of SWM (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Waste Generation at Source] --> B[Segregation at Source]
    B --> C1[Wet Waste - Green Bin]
    B --> C2[Dry Waste - Blue Bin]
    B --> C3[Hazardous - Red Bin]
    C1 --> D[Primary Collection]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    D --> E[Transfer Station]
    E --> F[Processing and Treatment]
    F --> F1[Composting]
    F --> F2[Anaerobic Digestion]
    F --> F3[Incineration and RDF]
    F --> F4[Recycling]
    F1 --> G[Residue]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    F4 --> G
    G --> H[Engineered Sanitary Landfill]
    H --> I[Leachate Management]
    H --> J[Landfill Gas Capture]
```

## 4.2 SWM Functional Flow — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SRC[Source Stage]
        S1[Domestic Waste]
        S2[Commercial Waste]
        S3[Institutional Waste]
    end
    
    subgraph STG[Storage Stage]
        R1[Community Bin]
        R2[Segregation Point]
    end
    
    subgraph COL[Collection Stage]
        K1[Primary Collection Vehicle]
        K2[Secondary Collection Vehicle]
    end
    
    subgraph TRN[Transfer and Transport]
        T1[Transfer Station]
        T2[Long Haul Trucks]
    end
    
    subgraph PRC[Processing]
        P1[Composting Plant]
        P2[Material Recovery Facility MRF]
        P3[Waste to Energy Plant]
    end
    
    subgraph DSF[Disposal]
        D1[Engineered Landfill]
    end
    
    S1 --> R1
    S2 --> R1
    S3 --> R1
    R1 --> R2
    R2 --> K1
    K1 --> T1
    T1 --> K2
    K2 --> T2
    T2 --> P1
    T2 --> P2
    T2 --> P3
    P1 --> D1
    P2 --> D1
    P3 --> D1
```

## 4.3 Solid Waste Classification Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    MSW[Municipal Solid Waste MSW] --> ORG[Organic]
    MSW --> INORG[Inorganic]
    MSW --> HAZ[Hazardous]
    
    ORG --> O1[Food Waste]
    ORG --> O2[Yard Waste]
    ORG --> O3[Paper]
    ORG --> O4[Wood]
    
    INORG --> I1[Plastic]
    INORG --> I2[Glass]
    INORG --> I3[Metal Ferrous]
    INORG --> I4[Metal Non Ferrous]
    INORG --> I5[Rubber and Leather]
    
    HAZ --> H1[Biomedical]
    HAZ --> H2[EWaste]
    HAZ --> H3[Battery]
    HAZ --> H4[Paint and Chemical]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Examiners often draw a simple 2-class (Organic vs Inorganic) or 4-bin (Wet / Dry / Hazardous / Sanitary) diagram and ask students to extend. Memorize the **3-bin color code**: Green (wet), Blue (dry), Red (hazardous) — this is mandatory under SWM Rules 2016.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, June 2024 Model]**
**Define Municipal Solid Waste. List any four sources of solid waste.**

**Model Answer (verbatim, board-pattern):**

**Definition:** Municipal Solid Waste (MSW) refers to the composite of solid discarded materials generated from residential, commercial, institutional, and municipal service activities within an urban or semi-urban jurisdiction, excluding industrial hazardous waste and biomedical waste.

> Note: This definition is adapted from IS 14543 and CPHEEO Manual on MSW.

**Four Sources of Solid Waste:**

1. **Residential/Domestic Sources:** Kitchen waste, garden waste, paper, packaging, plastic containers from households.
2. **Commercial Sources:** Waste from offices, shops, hotels, and restaurants — predominantly food waste and paper.
3. **Construction and Demolition (C&D) Sources:** Concrete, bricks, timber, steel, tiles from construction activities.
4. **Municipal Service Sources:** Street sweeping waste, drain cleaning silt, park waste, and waste from water treatment plants.

> [!NOTE]
> **Board Tip:** Examiners allot **1 mark for the definition** and **2 marks for the sources list with brief explanation**. Always use bullet points, not paragraphs.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model 2024]**
**Differentiate between Municipal Solid Waste and Biomedical Waste.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Municipal Solid Waste (MSW) | Biomedical Waste (BMW) |
|---|---|---|
| **Source** | Households, offices, streets | Hospitals, clinics, labs |
| **Composition** | Food, paper, plastic, glass | Anatomical, soiled dressings, sharps |
| **Regulatory Body** | SWM Rules 2016 (MoEFCC) | BMW Rules 2016 (MoEFCC) |
| **Color Code** | Green, Blue, Red bins | Yellow, Red, Blue, White, Black (5 bins) |
| **Treatment** | Composting, landfilling | Autoclaving, incineration, deep burial |
| **Hazard Level** | Generally low | Potentially infectious / hazardous |
| **Generation Rate (India)** | 0.5–0.8 kg/capita/day | 0.5–2.0 kg/bed/day |

---

## Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Pattern]**

**(a)** Define Municipal Solid Waste Management. Explain in detail the **functional elements of a typical SWM system** with a neat block diagram. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A city has a population of **5,00,000** with per capita waste generation of **0.6 kg/capita/day**. The bulk density is **450 kg/m³**. Determine:
   (i) Total waste generated per day in tonnes. 
   (ii) Volume of waste per day. 
   (iii) Number of compactor trucks of **8-tonne capacity** required if each truck makes **4 trips/day**. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

**Definition (2 marks):** Municipal Solid Waste Management (MSWM) is the complete administrative and engineering process associated with **generation, segregation, storage, collection, transfer, transport, processing, treatment, and disposal** of municipal solid waste in a manner compliant with environmental and public health regulations.

**Functional Elements (4 marks):**

1. **Waste Generation** — The stage at which materials become valueless to the owner and are discarded. KTU board insists on: $Q = P \times q \times 10^{-3}$ tonnes/day.
2. **Segregation at Source** — Separation into **wet, dry, and hazardous** streams to enable downstream processing. Mandated under SWM Rules 2016.
3. **Storage** — Temporary on-site storage in community bins or household containers. Must be covered to prevent vector breeding and leachate.
4. **Collection** — Movement of waste from storage points to transfer stations or processing facilities. Can be **house-to-house, community bin, or curb-side**.
5. **Transfer and Transport** — When transfer stations are used, long-haul vehicles carry waste in bulk to processing/disposal sites.
6. **Processing and Treatment** — Composting, anaerobic digestion, incineration, refuse-derived fuel (RDF), and material recovery.
7. **Final Disposal** — **Engineered Sanitary Landfill** is the only scientifically accepted terminal disposal method, with **leachate collection** and **landfill gas (LFG) management** systems.

**Block Diagram (1 mark):** Refer to Section 4.2 of these notes for the KTU-pattern functional block diagram.

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

**Given:** $P = 5{,}00{,}000$, $q = 0.6 \text{ kg/capita/day}$, $\rho_b = 450 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $C = 8 \text{ tonnes}$, $n = 4 \text{ trips/day}$.

**(i) Total waste per day:**

$$Q = P \times q \times 10^{-3}$$

$$Q = 5{,}00{,}000 \times 0.6 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$Q = 300 \text{ tonnes/day} \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}$$

**(ii) Volume of waste per day:**

$$V = \frac{Q \times 1000}{\rho_b}$$

$$V = \frac{300 \times 1000}{450}$$

$$V = 666.67 \text{ m}^3/\text{day} \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}$$

**(iii) Number of trucks:**

$$N_v = \frac{Q}{C \times n} = \frac{300}{8 \times 4} = \frac{300}{32}$$

$$N_v = 9.375 \approx 10 \text{ trucks} \quad \textbf{(3 marks)}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Converting tonnes to kg incorrectly. Remember: **1 tonne = 1000 kg**, and the Master Equation already incorporates $10^{-3}$ for the kg-to-tonnes conversion. Do not divide twice.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Rounding $9.375$ down to $9$. Always **round up** for fleet sizing — a city cannot operate with fractional trucks.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Forgetting to multiply $\rho_b$ by $1000$ when $Q$ is in tonnes. **Always use SI-consistent units**: convert tonnes to kg before dividing by $\rho_b$ (kg/m³).
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Writing the answer as $9.38$ trucks. Trucks are discrete — round up to the next whole number.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks): [Alternative Choice – KTU Model Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the **physical and chemical characteristics of Municipal Solid Waste** with relevant formulas. How do these characteristics influence the **selection of treatment and disposal methods**? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is **segregation at source**? Describe the **two-bin, three-bin, and four-bin** color-coded systems prescribed by SWM Rules 2016. Discuss the challenges of source segregation in Indian urban contexts. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

**Physical Characteristics (3.5 marks):**

| Property | Significance for Treatment/Disposal |
|---|---|
| **Bulk Density ($\rho_b$)** | Affects storage volume, transport vehicle sizing, and landfill compaction efficiency. Low $\rho_b$ (300 kg/m³) means high volume, requiring frequent collection. |
| **Moisture Content ($M_c$)** | High moisture ($>$50%) discourages incineration (calorific value drops) and favors composting or anaerobic digestion. |
| **Particle Size** | Affects sorting efficiency at MRF. Heterogeneous size in Indian MSW reduces recyclability. |

**Chemical Characteristics (3.5 marks):**

| Property | Significance |
|---|---|
| **Calorific Value (CV)** | Determines feasibility of incineration/WtE. Indian MSW (1000–1500 kcal/kg) is borderline; co-firing with coal is often required. |
| **Volatile Solids (VS)** | High VS indicates high organic content, suitable for composting. |
| **C/N Ratio** | Optimal for composting is 30:1. Indian MSW often exceeds this, slowing microbial action. |
| **pH** | Influences microbial activity during composting; optimal pH is 6.5–7.5. |

**Formulas (must be written explicitly):**

$$M_c = \frac{W_w - W_d}{W_w} \times 100$$

$$VS = \frac{W_d - W_a}{W_d} \times 100$$

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

**Definition of Segregation (1 mark):** Source segregation is the practice of separating waste **at the point of generation** (household, commercial unit) into distinct streams to facilitate downstream processing, recycling, and safe disposal.

**Color-Coded Systems (3 marks):**

| System | Bins | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **2-Bin System** | Green (wet), Blue (dry) | Basic urban localities |
| **3-Bin System** | Green (wet), Blue (dry), Red (domestic hazardous) | Most Indian municipalities (SWM Rules 2016) |
| **4-Bin System** | Green, Blue, Red (hazardous), Black (sanitary/inert) | Advanced municipalities; Bengaluru, Indore |

**Challenges in Indian Urban Context (3 marks):**

1. **Behavioral inertia** — Lack of awareness and willingness among citizens.
2. **Mixed collection infrastructure** — Waste pickers and informal sector disrupt segregation during handling.
3. **Apartment complex logistics** — High-rise buildings lack space for multiple bins per flat.
4. **Inadequate monitoring** — Local bodies do not enforce segregation compliance strictly.
5. **Cultural taboos** — Organic waste, especially kitchen waste, is often mixed with sanitary waste.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Question B):**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Listing color codes but failing to mention the **SWM Rules 2016** reference. Always cite the regulation.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Confusing the 4-bin system with biomedical waste color codes. MSW and BMW have **entirely different** color systems.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Writing characteristics as a flat list without **linking to treatment selection**. The KTU board awards marks for the "Why/How" linkage.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Board Checklist — Solid Waste Management (SWM):**

- [x] **MSW Definition:** Solid waste from residential + commercial + institutional + municipal services, excluding industrial hazardous and BMW.
- [x] **6R Hierarchy:** Refuse, Reduce, Reuse, Recycle, Recover, Residual Disposal.
- [x] **8 Sources:** Domestic, Commercial, Institutional, Industrial, C&D, Municipal, Agricultural, Biomedical/E-Waste.
- [x] **Master Equation:** $Q = P \times q \times 10^{-3}$ tonnes/day — **memorize this and its three derived forms**.
- [x] **Volume Equation:** $V = Q \times 1000 / \rho_b$ (with unit consistency).
- [x] **Fleet Equation:** $N_v = Q / (C \times n)$ — round **up** always.
- [x] **Moisture Content:** $M_c = [(W_w - W_d)/W_w] \times 100$.
- [x] **Volatile Solids:** $VS = [(W_d - W_a)/W_d] \times 100$.
- [x] **C/N Ratio optimal:** $\mathbf{30:1}$ for composting; Indian MSW often higher.
- [x] **Calorific Value:** India $= 1000\text{–}1500$ kcal/kg (low — pre-drying needed for incineration).
- [x] **SWM Rules 2016:** Mandates source segregation, 3-bin system (Green/Blue/Red), and engineered sanitary landfill as terminal disposal.
- [x] **BMW Rules 2016:** 5-bin system (Yellow/Red/Blue/White/Black) — **do not confuse with MSW**.
- [x] **Functional Elements of SWM:** Generation → Segregation → Storage → Collection → Transfer → Transport → Processing/Treatment → Disposal.
- [x] **CPHEEO Norm:** $0.6$ kg/capita/day by 2025; bulk density $= 300\text{–}500$ kg/m³.
- [x] **Compactor Ratio:** $R_c = 3:1$ to $4:1$ (stationary); saves transport trips and landfill space.
- [x] **Sanitary Landfill:** Has **liner (HDPE), leachate collection, and LFG capture** — different from open dumps.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Elements of SWM
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET831 - WASTE MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Elements of SWM

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Solid Waste Management (SWM)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Solid Waste (KTU Definition):** Any discarded material arising from human and animal activities that is no longer valued by the generator and has no direct or perceived utility to the generator. Per the **Municipal Solid Waste (Management and Handling) Rules, 2000** and superseded by the **Solid Waste Management Rules, 2016** of the Government of India, solid waste includes solid, semi-solid, liquid, or contained gaseous material discarded from various sources.

**Solid Waste Management (SWM)** is a comprehensive, scientific discipline that governs the systematic **generation, segregation, storage, collection, transportation, processing, recovery, and final disposal** of solid waste in a manner that is environmentally sustainable, economically viable, and socially acceptable. The modern framework is referred to as **Integrated Solid Waste Management (ISWM)**, which combines waste streams, treatment options, and public participation into a unified hierarchy dominated by the **3R Principle: Reduce → Reuse → Recycle**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Elements"?** In Module 1, "Elements of SWM" refers to the **fundamental building blocks** — the *functional elements* of any SWM system (Generation, Storage, Collection, Transport, Processing, Disposal) along with the *source/classification framework* used to characterize the waste itself.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "City as a Human Body" View

Think of a city as a living organism:
- **Waste Generation** is the natural metabolic output of the body (food digestion produces waste).
- **Storage at Source** is the *temporary holding cell* (like a household dustbin).
- **Collection** is the *plumbing network* that removes waste from each house.
- **Transportation** is the *bloodstream / logistics truck* that moves waste to a central processing facility.
- **Processing & Recovery** is the *liver/kidney* — it detoxifies, extracts useful nutrients, and recycles.
- **Disposal** is the *final metabolism* (landfill / sanitary burial).

If any "organ" fails, the system collapses — this is precisely why KTU lays strong emphasis on the **functional elements** as a continuous chain.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in SWM

- **Per Capita Waste Generation (PCWG)**: **0.21 – 0.62 kg/capita/day** (Indian urban average ≈ **0.45 kg/capita/day**).
- **Bulk Density of MSW (India)**: **300 – 500 kg/m³** (loose); **600 – 900 kg/m³** (compacted).
- **Moisture Content (Indian MSW)**: **40 – 60%** (wet basis).
- **Calorific Value**: **800 – 1500 kcal/kg** for Indian MSW (significantly lower than the EU/US average of **2000 – 3000 kcal/kg**).
- **Standard reference unit**: **1 tonne = 1000 kg**; **1 hectare = 10,000 m²**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 OECET831):** Module 1 explicitly asks students to understand the *classification, sources, characteristics, and functional elements* of Municipal Solid Waste (MSW). Mastery of these definitions is compulsory for Part A and Part B questions.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Solid Waste

Solid waste is classified along three principal axes:

### A. Classification by Source (Origin-Based)
| Category | Examples | % of MSW (India, typical) |
|---|---|---|
| **Residential** | Kitchen waste, paper, plastics, garden trimmings | 40 – 60% |
| **Commercial** | Office paper, packaging, food waste from restaurants | 15 – 25% |
| **Institutional** | School/collab waste, hospital non-medical waste | 5 – 10% |
| **Street Sweeping** | Road dust, leaves, silt | 5 – 15% |
| **Construction & Demolition (C&D)** | Concrete, wood, metals, tiles | 10 – 30% |
| **Industrial (non-hazardous)** | Fly ash, slag, scrap metal | Varies |
| **Biomedical & E-Waste** | Syringes, batteries, computers | Special handling |

### B. Classification by Type (Physical/Nature-Based)
- **Organic / Biodegradable** (food, yard, paper) — decomposes by microbes.
- **Recyclable / Inorganic Non-Biodegradable** (plastics, glass, metals).
- **Hazardous / Toxic** (batteries, paints, pesticides, medical waste).
- **Inert** (C&D debris, construction rubble).

### C. Classification by State
- **Solid / Semi-Solid** (bulk MSW)
- **Liquid / Sludge** (sewage sludge, industrial effluents)
- **Contained Gaseous** (compressed gas cylinders)

## 2.2 The Six Functional Elements of SWM (Tchobanoglous Framework — KTU-Mandated)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This **6-stage chain** is the most heavily tested KTU topic. Always draw a *block diagram* in Part B answers.

1. **Waste Generation** — the *where* and *how much* of waste.
2. **On-Site Storage, Handling & Segregation** — source-level bins (green/blue/black colour-coding under SWM 2016 Rules).
3. **Collection** — house-to-house, community bin, or curb-side pickup.
4. **Transfer & Transport** — primary (small vehicles), secondary (larger trucks), and transfer stations.
5. **Processing & Recovery** — material recovery facilities (MRF), composting, anaerobic digestion, refuse-derived fuel (RDF), incineration with energy recovery (WTE).
6. **Disposal** — **Sanitary Landfill** is the only environmentally accepted terminal disposal for residual waste.

## 2.3 Physical & Chemical Properties (KTU High-Yield)

### Physical Properties
- **Density** (ρ = M / V)
- **Moisture Content** (MC%)
- **Particle Size Distribution**
- **Permeability / Porosity**
- **Field Capacity**

### Chemical Properties
- **Proximate Analysis** (Moisture, Volatile Matter, Fixed Carbon, Ash)
- **Ultimate Analysis** (C, H, O, N, S, Cl — % by mass)
- **Calorific Value / Heating Value** (kcal/kg or kJ/kg)

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** All absolute-value or "divided by" expressions use `\vert` or `\mid` to prevent markdown table breakage.

| # | Formula | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $q = \dfrac{Q_{total}}{P \times t}$ | Per capita waste generation (kg/capita/day) |
| 2 | $MC = \dfrac{W_{wet} - W_{dry}}{W_{wet}} \times 100$ | Moisture content (% wet basis) |
| 3 | $\rho_{bulk} = \dfrac{M}{V}$ | Bulk density of MSW (kg/m³) |
| 4 | $W_g = \dfrac{P \times q \times n}{1000}$ | Waste storage container capacity (kg) |
| 5 | $V = \dfrac{W_g}{\rho_{bulk}}$ | Container volume (m³) |
| 6 | $CV = 337C + 1418(H - \dfrac{O}{8}) + 92S$ | Dulong's Calorific Value formula (kcal/kg) |
| 7 | $V_c = \dfrac{P \times g \times n}{1000 \times \rho}$ | Combined storage volume for n days |
| 8 | $R_c = \dfrac{L_c}{V_c}$ | Collection route loading ratio |
| 9 | $E_w = \dfrac{W_g \times CV}{3600}$ | Energy content of waste (MJ) |
| 10 | $\eta = \dfrac{Useful\ output}{Input} \times 100$ | Efficiency of recovery process (%) |

**Where:**
- $q$ = per capita generation; $Q_{total}$ = total mass; $P$ = population; $t$ = days
- $W_{wet}, W_{dry}$ = wet and oven-dry mass of sample
- $W_g$ = waste mass to be stored; $n$ = days between collections
- $C, H, O, S$ = mass percentages of carbon, hydrogen, oxygen, sulphur
- $g$ = generation rate (kg/capita/day); $\rho$ = bulk density (kg/m³)

## 2.5 Real-World Utility of These Equations

- **PCWG ($q$)** is the primary input to *container sizing, vehicle fleet planning, and landfill volume estimation*.
- **MC%** dictates **composting viability** (must be 50 – 60% for aerobic composting).
- **Calorific Value ($CV$)** determines whether **incineration/WTE** is feasible (Threshold: **$CV \geq 1000$ kcal/kg** for Indian plants).
- **Bulk density** governs **transport economics** — higher density ⇒ more mass per trip ⇒ lower cost/tonne.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Per Capita Waste Generation ($q$)

The fundamental mass balance for any municipal jurisdiction:

$$
\text{Total waste generated in time } t = Q_{total}\ (\text{kg})
$$

For a population $P$ served uniformly over time $t$ (days), each person contributes an average quantity $q$ per day.

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{total}\ (\text{kg}) &= P\ (\text{capita}) \times q\ (\text{kg/capita/day}) \times t\ (\text{days}) \\
\therefore q &= \dfrac{Q_{total}}{P \times t} \quad \big[\text{kg/capita/day}\big]
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** The units are cancelled as $kg \div (capita \times days)$, yielding kg/capita/day. This is the **inverse of population-weighted mass conservation.**

### 3.1.1 Solved Numerical — KTU Style
**Problem:** A city of **3 lakh population** generates **150 tonnes** of waste in **30 days**. Find the per capita generation.

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= 3\ \text{lakh} = 3 \times 10^{5}\ \text{capita} \\
Q_{total} &= 150\ \text{tonnes} = 150 \times 1000 = 1.5 \times 10^{5}\ \text{kg} \\
t &= 30\ \text{days} \\
q &= \dfrac{Q_{total}}{P \times t} = \dfrac{1.5 \times 10^{5}}{3 \times 10^{5} \times 30} = \dfrac{1.5}{9.0} \\
q &= 0.1667\ \text{kg/capita/day} \\
\therefore q &\approx 0.167\ \text{kg/capita/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Examiner's Comment:** Always show unit conversion (tonnes → kg) explicitly. KTU penalizes missing unit conversions by **½ to 1 mark**.

## 3.2 Derivation: Moisture Content ($MC\%$)

Wet sample (mass $W_{wet}$) contains both dry solids and water. Oven-drying at **103 – 105 °C** for 24 hours removes the free water, leaving dry solids ($W_{dry}$).

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{water} &= W_{wet} - W_{dry} \\
MC (\%) &= \dfrac{W_{water}}{W_{wet}} \times 100 \\
\therefore MC &= \dfrac{W_{wet} - W_{dry}}{W_{wet}} \times 100
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2.1 Solved Numerical
**Problem:** A wet MSW sample weighs **2.45 kg**. After oven-drying, mass is **1.32 kg**. Calculate the moisture content.

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{wet} &= 2.45\ \text{kg} \\
W_{dry} &= 1.32\ \text{kg} \\
W_{water} &= 2.45 - 1.32 = 1.13\ \text{kg} \\
MC &= \dfrac{1.13}{2.45} \times 100 = 46.12\ \%
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation: Waste Storage Container Volume ($V$)

For a community with population $P$, per-capita generation $g$ (kg/capita/day), and waste to be stored for $n$ days between collections:

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_g\ (\text{kg}) &= P \times g \times n \\
V\ (\text{m}^3) &= \dfrac{W_g}{\rho_{bulk}} = \dfrac{P \times g \times n}{\rho_{bulk} \times 1000}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Note:** The factor $1000$ converts kg to tonnes *if* $\rho$ is in kg/m³; for $\rho$ in tonnes/m³, drop the 1000.

### 3.3.1 Solved Numerical
**Problem:** A housing society has **500 families × 5 members each** = 2500 persons. Per-capita generation = **0.50 kg/day**. Collection is once every **2 days**. If bulk density = **400 kg/m³**, find required container volume.

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= 2500\ \text{capita} \\
g &= 0.50\ \text{kg/capita/day} \\
n &= 2\ \text{days} \\
\rho_{bulk} &= 400\ \text{kg/m}^3 \\
W_g &= 2500 \times 0.50 \times 2 = 2500\ \text{kg} \\
V &= \dfrac{2500}{400} = 6.25\ \text{m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.4 Derivation: Calorific Value via Modified Dulong's Formula

The heating value of waste governs its suitability for incineration/WTE. For ultimate-analysis data of carbon (C%), hydrogen (H%), oxygen (O%), and sulphur (S%):

$$
\begin{aligned}
HHV\ (\text{kcal/kg}) &= 337C + 1418\left(H - \dfrac{O}{8}\right) + 92S \\
\text{Convert to MJ/kg: } CV_{MJ} &= \dfrac{CV_{kcal}}{239}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Physical Meaning:** 
> - **337C** — energy released by combusting carbon to CO₂.
> - **1418 H** — energy from hydrogen combustion; H₂O is produced.
> - **−(O/8)** correction — oxygen already bound to hydrogen reduces net H₂O formation (i.e., less latent heat loss).
> - **92S** — exothermic combustion of sulphur to SO₂.

### 3.4.1 Solved Numerical
**Problem:** Ultimate analysis of MSW: C = 25%, H = 4%, O = 20%, S = 0.5%. Calculate HHV.

$$
\begin{aligned}
HHV &= 337(25) + 1418\left(4 - \dfrac{20}{8}\right) + 92(0.5) \\
&= 8425 + 1418(4 - 2.5) + 46 \\
&= 8425 + 1418(1.5) + 46 \\
&= 8425 + 2127 + 46 \\
&= 10598\ \text{kcal/kg} \\
\therefore HHV &\approx 10598\ \text{kcal/kg} = 44.34\ \text{MJ/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Engineering Insight:** Typical Indian MSW HHV is **800 – 1500 kcal/kg**; values above **1500 kcal/kg** are necessary for self-sustaining incineration without auxiliary fuel.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: When to Use Which Property

| Decision / Process | Controlling Property | Threshold (KTU Reference) |
|---|---|---|
| Composting suitability | C/N ratio, MC% | C/N = 25–35, MC = 50–60% |
| Incineration / WTE | Calorific value, MC% | CV ≥ 1000 kcal/kg, MC ≤ 50% |
| Landfill (sanitary) | Density, permeability | ρ ≥ 500 kg/m³, low permeability of liner |
| Vermicomposting | MC%, pH | MC = 60–80%, pH = 6.5–7.5 |
| Anaerobic digestion | VS (volatile solids), pH, temp | VS ≥ 60%, mesophilic 35°C |
| Recycling (MRF) | Density, source separation | Manual / magnetic / eddy current |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Elements of SWM (Master Flowchart)

The diagram below traces the **6-stage functional chain** as mandated by the Tchobanoglous framework, adopted in all KTU SWM pedagogy.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    n1[Stage 1: Waste Generation]:::primaryNode
    n2[Stage 2: On-Site Storage and Segregation]:::primaryNode
    n3[Stage 3: Collection]:::primaryNode
    n4[Stage 4: Transfer and Transport]:::primaryNode
    n5[Stage 5: Processing and Recovery]:::primaryNode
    n6[Stage 6: Disposal]:::primaryNode
    n7[Recycling MRF]:::secNode
    n8[Composting / Biomethanation]:::secNode
    n9[Refuse Derived Fuel RDF]:::secNode
    n10[Incineration WTE]:::secNode
    n11[Sanitary Landfill]:::secNode

    n1 --> n2
    n2 --> n3
    n3 --> n4
    n4 --> n5
    n5 --> n6
    n5 -.-> n7
    n5 -.-> n8
    n5 -.-> n9
    n5 -.-> n10
    n6 -.-> n11

    classDef primaryNode fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#0b2c4a,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef secNode fill:#a9d18e,stroke:#3a6a35,color:#000000,stroke-width:1px
```

## 4.2 Sources of Municipal Solid Waste (Radial Map)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    hub[Total Municipal Solid Waste]:::hubNode
    src1[Residential]:::srcNode
    src2[Commercial]:::srcNode
    src3[Institutional]:::srcNode
    src4[Street Sweeping]:::srcNode
    src5[Construction and Demolition]:::srcNode
    src6[Municipal Services]:::srcNode
    src7[Agricultural Residue]:::srcNode

    src1 --> hub
    src2 --> hub
    src3 --> hub
    src4 --> hub
    src5 --> hub
    src6 --> hub
    src7 --> hub

    classDef hubNode fill:#c00000,stroke:#7f0000,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:3px
    classDef srcNode fill:#fbe5d6,stroke:#a35620,color:#000000,stroke-width:1px
```

## 4.3 Hierarchical Classification of Solid Waste

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    root[Solid Waste]:::rootNode
    org[Organic Biodegradable]:::leafNode
    inorg[Inorganic Non Biodegradable]:::leafNode
    haz[Hazardous Toxic]:::leafNode
    inert[Inert C and D]:::leafNode

    food[Food and Kitchen Waste]:::subNode
    yard[Yard and Garden Waste]:::subNode
    paper[Paper Cardboard]:::subNode

    plast[Plastics]:::subNode
    glass[Glass]:::subNode
    metal[Metals]:::subNode
    rubber[Rubber Leather]:::subNode

    biom[Biomedical Waste]:::subNode
    ewaste[E Waste]:::subNode
    chem[Industrial Chemicals]:::subNode

    concrete[Concrete and Tiles]:::subNode
    rubble[Rubble and Soil]:::subNode

    root --> org
    root --> inorg
    root --> haz
    root --> inert

    org --> food
    org --> yard
    org --> paper

    inorg --> plast
    inorg --> glass
    inorg --> metal
    inorg --> rubber

    haz --> biom
    haz --> ewaste
    haz --> chem

    inert --> concrete
    inert --> rubble

    classDef rootNode fill:#002060,stroke:#001040,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:3px
    classDef leafNode fill:#ffd966,stroke:#806000,color:#000000,stroke-width:2px
    classDef subNode fill:#d9e1f2,stroke:#44546a,color:#000000,stroke-width:1px
```

## 4.4 Decision Tree: Process Selection by Waste Property

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[Incoming MSW]:::startNode
    q1{Bulk Density and Composition}:::questionNode
    q2{Calorific Value MC%}:::questionNode
    q3{Waste is Wet and Organic}:::questionNode

    p1a[Composting]:::processNode
    p1b[Landfill Disposal]:::processNode
    p2a[Incineration WTE]:::processNode
    p2b[Refuse Derived Fuel]:::processNode
    p3a[Anaerobic Digestion]:::processNode
    p3b[Vermicomposting]:::processNode

    start --> q1
    q1 -- High density Low MC --> q2
    q1 -- Low density High MC --> q3
    q2 -- CV over 1000 kcal/kg and MC under 50 percent --> p2a
    q2 -- CV below 1000 --> p2b
    q3 -- Mesophilic Digestion --> p3a
    q3 -- Earthworm Action --> p3b
    q1 -- Mixed Residual --> p1a
    q1 -- Final Residue --> p1b

    classDef startNode fill:#c55a11,stroke:#7f3a0a,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef questionNode fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#bf9000,color:#000000,stroke-width:1px
    classDef processNode fill:#a9d08e,stroke:#548235,color:#000000,stroke-width:1px
```
