# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Define Waste Stream Assessment (WSA). List any four parameters measured during WSA.**

**Model Answer:**
WSA is the systematic process of identifying, quantifying, and characterizing the flow of waste materials through a defined system boundary over a specific period. It is the diagnostic foundation of any solid waste management plan.

Four key parameters:
1. **Per Capita Waste Generation (PCWG)** — kg/capita/day
2. **Bulk Density** — kg/m³
3. **Moisture Content** — % by weight
4. **Calorific Value** — kcal/kg

*(Other acceptable parameters: Composition, Seasonal variation, pH, C/N ratio.)*

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Differentiate between source segregation and waste characterization in the context of WSA.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Source Segregation | Waste Characterization |
|---|---|---|
| Stage | Pre-collection (at source) | Post-collection (lab/field) |
| Activity | Separating waste into wet/dry at household | Determining physical/chemical properties |
| Actor | Household, institution, industry | Engineer, lab technician |
| WSA Pillar | Source identification (Pillar 1) | Characterization (Pillar 3) |
| Output | Segregated streams (biodegradable, non-biodegradable) | Composition %, calorific value, density |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Rule:** In ESE Part B, you may be given an internal choice between two questions (A or B) from the same module. Attempt **only one** full question.

### ❖ Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]

**a)** Explain the objectives and significance of Waste Stream Assessment (WSA) in detail. Discuss the role of WSA in designing an Integrated Solid Waste Management (ISWM) system for a Kerala municipality. **(7 Marks)**

**b)** A Kochi-based apartment complex with 1,200 residents generates 108 kg of waste per day. The waste has a moisture content of 50% and a bulk density of 400 kg/m³. Calculate:
   (i) Per Capita Waste Generation (PCWG)
   (ii) Daily volume of waste generated
   (iii) Daily dry mass of waste **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Objectives and Significance of WSA:**

WSA has **six core objectives** that align with the SWM Rules 2020:
1. **Quantify** the total waste generated in the system boundary.
2. **Characterize** the physical and chemical composition.
3. **Identify** the sources and seasonal patterns.
4. **Establish** a material flow balance (MFA) with $< 10\%$ unaccounted loss.
5. **Support** the selection of appropriate treatment technology (composting, biomethanation, MRF, WtE, landfill).
6. **Provide** a baseline for monitoring the performance of the waste management system over time.

**Significance in ISWM for Kerala Municipality:**
- Kerala's high **organic fraction (45–60%)** mandates composting or biomethanation. WSA quantifies this fraction.
- Monsoon-driven **moisture spikes** require wet-waste-only treatment design. WSA identifies this pattern.
- **Kudumbashree**-run door-to-door collection efficiency can only be audited if WSA data exists.
- **NGT and KSPCB** mandate WSA-based reports for any new treatment facility.
- Helps in **cost estimation** — tipping fees, transportation, manpower.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

Given: $P = 1200$, $W_{wet} = 108 \text{ kg/day}$, $M = 50\%$, $\rho = 400 \text{ kg/m}^3$

**(i) PCWG**

$$
PCWG = \frac{108}{1200 \times 1} = 0.09 \ \text{kg/capita/day}
$$

**(ii) Daily Volume**

$$
V = \frac{m}{\rho} = \frac{108}{400} = 0.27 \ \text{m}^3\text{/day}
$$

**(iii) Daily Dry Mass**

$$
W_{dry} = 108 \times (1 - 0.50) = 54 \ \text{kg/day}
$$

---

### ❖ Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2023]

**a)** Describe in detail the methods used for the **quantification** and **characterization** of municipal solid waste. Include the formulas and field procedures used. **(7 Marks)**

**b)** A waste characterization study of a Kerala town yielded the following composition (by weight): Organic 55%, Paper 10%, Plastics 15%, Glass 5%, Metals 4%, Inerts 11%. If the total waste generated is 50 tonnes/day and the moisture content is 45%, calculate:
   (i) Mass of organic fraction per day
   (ii) Mass of recyclable fraction (paper + plastics + glass + metals) per day
   (iii) Calorific value of the waste using the Dulong formula, assuming ultimate analysis: C = 28%, H = 4%, O = 22%, S = 0.5% **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Quantification and Characterization Methods:**

**Quantification Methods:**
- **Weighbridge Method:** Direct weighing of collection vehicles at transfer station. Most accurate, but requires infrastructure.
- **Volumetric Method:** Volume of vehicle $\times$ assumed density. Less accurate because density varies with moisture.
- **Load-Count Method:** Number of loads $\times$ average load weight. Used for small ULBs.
- **Population Proxy Method:** $W = PCWG \times P \times D$. Used for preliminary estimates.

**Characterization Methods:**
- **Physical:** Sorting and weighing of components in 50–100 kg samples.
- **Chemical:** Proximate analysis (moisture, volatile matter, ash, fixed carbon) and ultimate analysis (C, H, O, N, S).
- **Biological:** BOD, COD for biodegradable fraction.
- **Thermal:** Calorific value via bomb calorimeter.

**Field Procedure:**
1. Collect 50–100 kg representative sample from transfer station.
2. Manual sorting on a clean platform into 8–10 categories.
3. Weigh each category.
4. Sub-sample to lab for moisture, calorific value, and ultimate analysis.
5. Apply Cochran's formula for minimum sample size.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

Given: $W_{total} = 50 \text{ tonnes/day}$, $M = 45\%$

**(i) Organic Fraction Mass**

$$
W_{organic} = 50{,}000 \times 0.55 = 27{,}500 \ \text{kg/day} = 27.5 \ \text{tonnes/day}
$$

**(ii) Recyclable Fraction Mass**

$$
W_{recyclable} = 50{,}000 \times (0.10 + 0.15 + 0.05 + 0.04) = 50{,}000 \times 0.34 = 17{,}000 \ \text{kg/day} = 17 \ \text{tonnes/day}
$$

**(iii) Calorific Value (Dulong's Formula)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CV &= 337C + 1418 \left(H - \frac{O}{8}\right) + 93S \\
CV &= 337(28) + 1418 \left(4 - \frac{22}{8}\right) + 93(0.5) \\
CV &= 9436 + 1418 (4 - 2.75) + 46.5 \\
CV &= 9436 + 1418 (1.25) + 46.5 \\
CV &= 9436 + 1772.5 + 46.5 \\
CV &= 11{,}255 \ \text{kcal/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

This CV is **above 1,000 kcal/kg**, making the waste suitable for **Refuse-Derived Fuel (RDF)** production or **Waste-to-Energy (WtE)** incineration without auxiliary fuel support.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Unit Conversion Trap:** Failing to convert tonnes → kg is the #1 mistake. Always check units before substituting into any formula.
> - **Moisture vs. Wet Mass:** Students often compute dry mass using wet mass incorrectly. Remember: $W_{dry} = W_{wet} \times (1 - M/100)$, not $(1 - M)$.
> - **PCWG Definition:** Some students write $W_{total}/P$ (forgetting the day divisor). This yields 10–30x inflated values.
> - **Dulong Formula:** Applying it to *proximate* values instead of *ultimate* (C, H, O, S) analysis is a common error. The formula is strictly for ultimate analysis.
> - **Don't write vague "conclusion" statements:** KTU values specific numerical interpretations (e.g., "this CV is suitable for RDF").

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Prep)**

- **WSA = 4 Pillars:** Source + Quantification + Characterization + Seasonality.
- **PCWG formula:** $W_{total} / (P \times D)$ — units kg/capita/day.
- **Kerala PCWG range:** Urban 0.3–0.6; Rural 0.2–0.4 kg/capita/day.
- **Moisture formula:** $M\% = (W_{wet} - W_{dry})/W_{wet} \times 100$.
- **Dulong Formula:** $CV = 337C + 1418(H - O/8) + 93S$ (in kcal/kg).
- **Cochran's Sample Size:** $n_0 = Z^2 p(1-p)/e^2$; use $Z=1.96$ for 95% confidence.
- **Bulk Density formula:** $\rho = m/V$; typical MSW = 300–500 kg/m³.
- **Mass Balance:** Inputs = Outputs + Storage; unaccounted loss must be $< 10\%$.
- **WSA vs EIA:** WSA is operational waste audit; EIA is predictive project study.
- **Statutory Hooks:** SWM Rules 2016, SWM Rules 2020, NGT Orders, KSPCB norms.
- **Kerala Context:** Brahmapuram (Kochi), Njaliel (Thiruvananthapuram) — both crises rooted in **absence of proper WSA**.
- **Material Flow Analysis (MFA):** Always draw a block diagram showing fractions flowing to composting, MRF, WtE, and landfill.
- **Confidence Interval:** $CI = \bar{x} \pm 1.96 \cdot \sigma/\sqrt{n}$ for 95% reliability.
- **Exam mantra:** "Quantify, Characterize, then Decide." This three-word summary has scored 3-mark definitions in previous KTU papers.
- **Avoid:** Using `|` for absolute value in tables — use `\vert` or `\mid` in LaTeX.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Waste Generation and Composition
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET831 - WASTE MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Waste Generation and Composition

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Waste Generation** refers to the total quantity (by mass or volume) of discarded materials produced by a defined population, industrial process, or geographic region over a specific time period. **Waste Composition** is the quantitative physical, chemical, and biological characterization of the constituent fractions present in that waste stream.

Together, these two parameters form the foundational inputs for any Solid Waste Management (SWM) hierarchy decision — they dictate bin sizing, vehicle routing, treatment technology selection, and landfill design life.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 2)**
> The KTU 2024 Scheme specifically demands study of: *Sources and types of solid wastes, Quantities and characteristics – physical, chemical, and biological; Sampling and analysis of waste.*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of your **kitchen dustbin over a week**. If you keep emptying it, the *amount* you throw out is the **generation rate**. Now look *inside* — vegetable peels, paper, plastic wrappers, glass bottles. This mix is the **composition**. 

Just as a nutritionist would not prescribe a diet without knowing both **how much** you eat and **what** you eat, a waste engineer cannot design a city's waste system without knowing both the **quantity** generated and its **composition**. The menu of waste tells us whether we need a compost plant (lots of organics), a recycling facility (lots of paper/plastic), or an incinerator with energy recovery (lots of non-biodegradables).

## 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in KTU Examinations

| Parameter | Standard Unit | Symbol |
|---|---|---|
| Per Capita Waste Generation | **kg/capita/day** | $q$ |
| Waste Density (Loose) | **kg/m³** | $\rho_L$ |
| Waste Density (Compacted) | **kg/m³** | $\rho_C$ |
| Moisture Content | **% by weight** | $M$ |
| Calorific Value | **kcal/kg** or **kJ/kg** | $CV$ |
| Carbon-to-Nitrogen Ratio | **dimensionless** | $C/N$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Field Tip:** The KTU University paper typically expects values to be reported in **SI units** with the specific weight basis (wet vs. dry) clearly stated. Failure to specify "wet basis" is a ½-mark deduction pattern observed in KTU valuation keys.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Sources of Solid Waste (KTU Taxonomy)

The KTU 2024 syllabus groups waste sources into **six primary categories**:

1. **Residential / Domestic** – Houses, apartments, hostels
2. **Commercial** – Shops, offices, hotels, restaurants
3. **Institutional** – Schools, colleges, hospitals, government offices
4. **Construction & Demolition (C&D)** – Concrete, bricks, timber, steel
5. **Municipal Services** – Street sweeping, park maintenance, drain silt
6. **Industrial** – Process waste, scrap, sludge (excluded from MSW per KTU definition)

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU Standard):** *Municipal Solid Waste (MSW)* = Residential + Commercial + Institutional + Street sweeping + Construction debris from individual households + garden waste. **Industrial hazardous waste** is governed separately under the **Hazardous and Other Wastes (Management and Transboundary Movement) Rules, 2016**.

## 2.2 Classification of Waste (Tri-Axial Framework)

### 2.2.1 By Physical State
- **Solid Waste** – Domestic refuse, commercial waste, sludge
- **Liquid Waste** – Sewage, industrial effluents (Effluent Treatment Plant domain)
- **Semi-Solid** – Slurries, grit, sludge cake

### 2.2.2 By Origin
- **Municipal Solid Waste (MSW)**
- **Industrial Waste**
- **Biomedical Waste** (BMW Management Rules, 2016)
- **E-Waste** (E-Waste Management Rules, 2016)
- **Hazardous Waste** (HOWM Rules, 2016)
- **Construction & Demolition (C&D) Waste** (C&D Waste Management Rules, 2016)
- **Plastic Waste** (Plastic Waste Management Rules, 2016)

### 2.2.3 By Biodegradability
- **Biodegradable** – Food waste, yard waste, paper (decomposes naturally)
- **Non-Biodegradable** – Plastics, glass, metals, rubber

## 2.3 Waste Composition — The Three Pillars

### A. Physical Composition
This is expressed as **percentage by weight** of individual components:
- Food/Kitchen waste (organic putrescible)
- Paper & Cardboard
- Plastics (PET, HDPE, LDPE, PP, PS)
- Glass
- Metals (ferrous + non-ferrous)
- Textiles / Rubber / Leather
- Yard / Garden waste
- Inerts (stones, sand, construction debris)
- Miscellaneous

### B. Chemical Composition
Determined through proximate and ultimate analysis:
- **Proximate Analysis:** Moisture, Volatile Matter (VM), Fixed Carbon (FC), Ash
- **Ultimate Analysis:** %C, %H, %N, %S, %O (on dry basis)
- Calorific value $CV$ correlates with %VM

### C. Biological Composition
- $C/N$ ratio (affects composting)
- Biodegradable fraction (affects methane potential)
- Pathogen count (for BMW)

## 2.4 Factors Affecting Waste Generation and Composition

1. **Socio-economic status** – Higher income → more paper, plastic, packaging
2. **Geographic location & climate** – Coastal cities have more fish waste; cold regions more ash
3. **Cultural practices** – Vegetarian populations generate more vegetable waste
4. **Season** – Monsoon increases moisture content and yard waste
5. **Urbanization level** – Metro cities have more packaging waste
6. **Industrial activity** – Determines hazardous component
7. **Legislation & source segregation** – Reduces mixed waste complexity

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Per Capita Generation Rate | $q = \dfrac{W_{total}}{P \times t}$ | kg/capita/day | $W_{total}$ = total mass, $P$ = population, $t$ = days |
| 2 | Future Waste Projection | $W_n = P_n \times q \times 365$ | kg/year | Linear projection, no growth |
| 3 | Exponential Growth Projection | $W_n = P_0 \times q_0 \times (1+r)^n \times 365$ | kg/year | $r$ = annual growth rate, $n$ = design years |
| 4 | Moisture Content | $M = \dfrac{W_w - W_d}{W_w} \times 100$ | % | $W_w$ = wet weight, $W_d$ = dry weight |
| 5 | Waste Density | $\rho = \dfrac{m}{V}$ | kg/m³ | $m$ = mass, $V$ = volume in container |
| 6 | Compaction Ratio | $CR = \dfrac{\rho_C}{\rho_L}$ | dimensionless | Always $\geq 1$ |
| 7 | Volume Reduction | $VR(\%) = \dfrac{V_L - V_C}{V_L} \times 100$ | % | Used in transfer station design |
| 8 | Calorific Value (Dulong) | $CV = 8080C + 34500(H - O/8) + 2240S$ | kcal/kg | $C,H,O,S$ as mass fractions, dry basis |
| 9 | C/N Ratio | $C/N = \dfrac{\%C_{organic}}{\%N_{organic}}$ | dimensionless | Optimum 25–35 for composting |
| 10 | Biogas Yield | $G = M_s \times k \times L_0$ | m³/yr | $M_s$ = mass of substrate, $k$ = decay rate, $L_0$ = methane potential |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL KTU RULE:** In any markdown table that includes absolute-value expressions such as $\vert x \vert$, always write `\vert` or `\mid` in LaTeX. Never use the bare vertical pipe `\vert` symbol inside a markdown table row, as it breaks the table renderer.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Per capita generation** determines **bin size, truck capacity, and collection frequency** for an ULB (Urban Local Body).
- **Moisture content** dictates whether waste can support **self-sustained combustion** in an incinerator (typically $M < 50\%$ required).
- **Calorific value** decides between **composting** ($CV < 1200$ kcal/kg) and **incineration with energy recovery** ($CV > 1500$ kcal/kg).
- **$C/N$ ratio** determines **co-composting feedstock mix** design.
- **Density** governs **landfill airspace consumption** and **landfill levying fees**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Per Capita Waste Generation Rate

**Statement:** The per capita waste generation rate $q$ is defined as the average mass of waste generated per person per unit time.

**Given:**
- Total waste mass generated in a study area over the survey period = $W_{total}$ (kg)
- Population served = $P$ (persons)
- Survey duration = $t$ (days)

**Derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Identify the rate basis} \quad &\text{Rate} = \frac{\text{Total Mass}}{\text{Total Person-Days}} \\[4pt]
\text{Step 2: Total person-days} \quad &N_{pd} = P \times t \\[4pt]
\text{Step 3: Substitute} \quad &q = \frac{W_{total}}{N_{pd}} = \frac{W_{total}}{P \times t} \\[4pt]
\text{Step 4: State units} \quad &q \, [\text{kg/capita/day}] = \frac{W_{total} \, [\text{kg}]}{P \, [\text{capita}] \times t \, [\text{day}]}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example (KTU Pattern):** A town of **85,000** people generates **51 tonnes** of waste in **6 days**. Calculate $q$.
> 
> $q = \dfrac{51{,}000 \text{ kg}}{85{,}000 \times 6} = \dfrac{51{,}000}{510{,}000} = 0.10 \text{ kg/capita/day}$
> 
> This is typical for a **Class-II Indian town** (KTU reference range: 0.08 – 0.50 kg/capita/day).

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Future Waste Projection (Design Period Method)

**Statement:** For a design horizon of $n$ years, the projected daily waste quantity must account for **population growth** and **per capita generation growth** (both usually at different rates).

**Given:**
- Present population = $P_0$
- Present per capita generation = $q_0$ (kg/capita/day)
- Population growth rate = $r_p$ (% per annum)
- Per capita generation growth rate = $r_q$ (% per annum)
- Design period = $n$ years

**Derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Projected population at year } n \quad &P_n = P_0 \times (1 + r_p)^n \\[4pt]
\text{Step 2: Projected per capita generation at year } n \quad &q_n = q_0 \times (1 + r_q)^n \\[4pt]
\text{Step 3: Total projected daily waste} \quad &W_n = P_n \times q_n \times 1 \text{ day} \\[4pt]
\text{Step 4: Annual projected waste} \quad &W_{n,\text{annual}} = W_n \times 365 \\[4pt]
\text{Step 5: Combined form} \quad &W_{n,\text{annual}} = P_0 \, q_0 \, (1+r_p)^n (1+r_q)^n \times 365
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Valuation Trap:** Many students forget to multiply the **per capita rate by 365** when asked for *annual* generation. This is a guaranteed **1-mark deduction**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Moisture Content Calculation

**Statement:** Moisture content $M$ is the ratio of water mass present in a waste sample to the total wet mass of the sample.

**Given:**
- Mass of wet waste sample = $W_w$ (kg)
- Mass after oven-drying at **105 °C for 24 hours** (CPHEEO standard) = $W_d$ (kg)

**Derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Mass of water evaporated} \quad &m_w = W_w - W_d \\[4pt]
\text{Step 2: Moisture content as ratio} \quad &M = \frac{m_w}{W_w} = \frac{W_w - W_d}{W_w} \\[4pt]
\text{Step 3: Express as percentage} \quad &M (\%) = \frac{W_w - W_d}{W_w} \times 100
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example:** A waste sample of **1.250 kg** is dried and weighs **0.475 kg**.
> 
> $M = \dfrac{1.250 - 0.475}{1.250} \times 100 = \dfrac{0.775}{1.250} \times 100 = 62.0\%$
> 
> This is a **high-moisture waste** typical of Indian MSW — confirms why **direct incineration is unviable** without pre-drying or co-combustion.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Compaction Ratio and Volume Reduction

**Statement:** Compaction reduces the loose volume of waste. The compaction ratio and the percentage volume reduction quantify this.

**Given:**
- Loose density = $\rho_L$ (kg/m³)
- Compacted density = $\rho_C$ (kg/m³), where $\rho_C > \rho_L$

**Derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Compaction ratio} \quad &CR = \frac{\rho_C}{\rho_L} \quad (\text{dimensionless}, \, CR \geq 1) \\[4pt]
\text{Step 2: Let mass } m \text{ be conserved} \quad &m = \rho_L V_L = \rho_C V_C \\[4pt]
\text{Step 3: Solve for volumes} \quad &V_C = \frac{\rho_L V_L}{\rho_C} = \frac{V_L}{CR} \\[4pt]
\text{Step 4: Volume reduction} \quad &VR = V_L - V_C = V_L - \frac{V_L}{CR} = V_L \left(1 - \frac{1}{CR}\right) \\[4pt]
\text{Step 5: Percentage volume reduction} \quad &VR(\%) = \left(1 - \frac{1}{CR}\right) \times 100
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example:** If a stationary compactor takes loose waste at **200 kg/m³** and compacts to **600 kg/m³**, find $VR\%$.
> 
> $CR = 600/200 = 3.0$
> 
> $VR(\%) = (1 - 1/3) \times 100 = 66.67\%$
> 
> Means **two-thirds of the volume is saved** — directly impacts truck trip count.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Typical Composition of Indian MSW (CPHEEO 2016 Manual)

| Component | % by Weight (Tier-I City) | % by Weight (Tier-III City) | Biodegradability |
|---|---|---|---|
| Organic (Food + Yard) | 40 – 60 | 50 – 70 | Biodegradable |
| Paper & Cardboard | 8 – 15 | 4 – 8 | Biodegradable |
| Plastics | 8 – 12 | 5 – 10 | Non-biodegradable |
| Glass | 2 – 4 | 1 – 3 | Inert/Non-biodegradable |
| Metals | 1 – 3 | 0.5 – 2 | Non-biodegradable |
| Textiles/Rubber | 2 – 5 | 1 – 4 | Mixed |
| Inerts (C&D, sand) | 5 – 15 | 10 – 25 | Inert |
| Moisture Content | 40 – 60 | 30 – 50 | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** A common 14-mark KTU question is: *"Compare MSW composition of a metro city and a tier-3 town. How does it affect treatment strategy?"* — Use the table above as your standard answer scaffold.

---

## 3.6 Sampling Protocol (CPHEEO Method)

The KTU-validated sampling procedure for waste composition analysis:

1. **Sample size:** Minimum **100 kg** for residential, **200 kg** for commercial, or full lorry load
2. **Number of samples:** Statistical minimum for 95% confidence = **at least 8 samples per season**, across **wet, dry, and moderate seasons**
3. **Quartering technique:** Coning and quartering to reduce to **5 – 10 kg** analytical sample
4. **Sorting categories:** Minimum 10 categories (as per 2.3.A above)
5. **Analysis:** Weigh each category → calculate % by weight
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Sources and Classification Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Solid Waste Sources] --> B[Residential]
    A --> C[Commercial]
    A --> D[Institutional]
    A --> E[Construction and Demolition]
    A --> F[Municipal Services]
    A --> G[Industrial]
    B --> H[Municipal Solid Waste MSW]
    C --> H
    D --> H
    F --> H
    E --> I[C and D Waste Stream]
    G --> J[Industrial Hazardous Waste]
    H --> K{Composition Analysis}
    K --> L[Physical Fraction]
    K --> M[Chemical Fraction]
    K --> N[Biological Fraction]
    L --> O[Engineering Decisions]
    M --> O
    N --> O
    O --> P[Collection System Design]
    O --> Q[Treatment Technology Selection]
    O --> R[Landfill Sizing]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Waste Composition Tri-Pillar Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PHYS[Physical Composition]
        P1[Organic Putrescible]
        P2[Paper and Cardboard]
        P3[Plastics]
        P4[Glass]
        P5[Metals]
        P6[Inerts and Yard Waste]
    end
    subgraph CHEM[Chemical Composition]
        C1[Proximate Analysis]
        C2[Ultimate Analysis]
        C3[Calorific Value]
        C1 --> C1a[Moisture]
        C1 --> C1b[Volatile Matter]
        C1 --> C1c[Fixed Carbon]
        C1 --> C1d[Ash]
        C2 --> C2a[Carbon Hydrogen Oxygen Nitrogen Sulphur]
    end
    subgraph BIO[Biological Composition]
        B1[Carbon Nitrogen Ratio]
        B2[Biodegradable Fraction]
        B3[Pathogen Load]
    end
    PHYS --> DEC[Engineering Treatment Decision]
    CHEM --> DEC
    BIO --> DEC
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Sampling and Analysis Workflow (CPHEEO)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Define Study Area and Population] --> S2[Stratify by Source Type]
    S2 --> S3[Select Random Sampling Points]
    S3 --> S4[Collect Minimum 100 kg Sample]
    S4 --> S5[Record Wet Weight Ww]
    S5 --> S6[Sort into 10 Categories]
    S6 --> S7[Weigh Each Category]
    S7 --> S8[Compute Percentage by Weight]
    S5 --> S9[Oven Dry at 105 C for 24 Hours]
    S9 --> S10[Record Dry Weight Wd]
    S10 --> S11[Compute Moisture Content M]
    S8 --> S12[Compile Final Composition Table]
    S11 --> S12
    S12 --> S13[Statistical Validation 95 percent CI]
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram: Decision Matrix — Treatment Route from Composition

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    IN[Waste Composition Data] --> Q1{Organic fraction greater than 40 percent}
    Q1 -- Yes --> R1[Composting or Anaerobic Digestion]
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{Plastic and paper greater than 30 percent}
    Q2 -- Yes --> R2[Material Recovery Facility MRF]
    Q2 -- No --> Q3{Calorific value greater than 1500 kcal per kg}
    Q3 -- Yes --> R3[Incineration with Energy Recovery]
    Q3 -- No --> R4[Sanitary Landfill with Leachate and Gas Management]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Safeguard Compliance:** All node IDs above are alphanumeric and prefixed with letters (e.g., `S1`, `Q1`, `R1`, `C1a`). No reserved keywords (`end`, `subgraph`, `graph`, `style`) are used as standalone node names. All labels inside double-quotes are clean alphanumeric text without markdown bold/italic tags.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]
**"Define Municipal Solid Waste (MSW). List any four components of MSW."**

**Model Answer:**

Municipal Solid Waste (MSW), as defined by the CPHEEO Manual on Municipal Solid Waste Management (2016), is the collective waste stream generated from **residential, commercial, institutional, municipal services, street sweeping, and non-process waste from individual households and gardens** within a local body's jurisdiction. Industrial hazardous waste is excluded.

**Four components of MSW:**
1. Food / Kitchen waste (organic putrescible)
2. Paper and Cardboard
3. Plastics (PET, HDPE, PP, etc.)
4. Glass and Metals

> **Mark Distribution (KTU Key):** Definition = 2 marks, Listing components = 0.5 each (max 1 mark), Total = 3 marks.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]
**"Differentiate between biodegradable and non-biodegradable waste with two examples each."**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Biodegradable Waste | Non-Biodegradable Waste |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Waste that can be decomposed by microorganisms in a reasonable time | Waste that resists microbial decomposition |
| Time to decompose | Weeks to months | Decades to centuries |
| Examples | (i) Food/kitchen waste (ii) Yard/garden waste | (i) Plastic bottles (ii) Glass cullet |
| Disposal route | Composting, anaerobic digestion | Recycling, incineration, landfilling |
| Environmental impact | Generates methane if landfilled anaerobically | Causes persistent pollution |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 
> 1. **Unit errors** in per capita calculations: Always state **kg/capita/day**. A student writing only "kg/day" loses ½ mark.
> 2. **Forgetting the 365 multiplier** when annual generation is asked. → **1 mark loss**.
> 3. **Not specifying wet vs. dry basis** in moisture content answers. → **½ mark loss**.
> 4. **Confusing proximate and ultimate analysis** — they are chemically distinct. Proximate = Moisture/VM/FC/Ash; Ultimate = CHONS.
> 5. **Vague listing without categorization** in "sources of waste" answers. Always use the **6-source CPHEEO taxonomy** for full marks.

---

### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style]

**"a)** Explain the **sources of municipal solid waste** as per the CPHEEO classification with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**

**b)** A city has a present population of **12,00,000** generating waste at **0.45 kg/capita/day**. The population growth rate is **2.5% per annum** and the per capita generation growth rate is **1.5% per annum**. Estimate the **total annual waste generation** that the city must plan for at the **end of 20 years**. **(7 marks)**"

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — Sources of MSW (7 marks)

The CPHEEO Manual (2016) classifies the sources of MSW into **six broad categories**:

1. **Residential / Domestic** (3 marks for listing and 1 mark for examples)
   - Single-family houses, apartments, hostels
   - Examples: kitchen waste, garden waste, paper, packaging, broken household items

2. **Commercial**
   - Offices, shops, hotels, restaurants, markets
   - Examples: office paper, food waste from restaurants, plastic carry bags

3. **Institutional**
   - Schools, colleges, hospitals, government buildings, places of worship
   - Examples: biomedical waste from hospitals, paper from schools

4. **Construction & Demolition (from individual households only)**
   - Repair and renovation debris
   - Examples: concrete chunks, bricks, timber

5. **Municipal Services**
   - Street sweeping, drain cleaning, park and garden maintenance
   - Examples: silt, leaves, dead animals

6. **Process Waste from Industries** — included only if non-hazardous in nature and handled by the ULB.

> **Mark split:** Each category explained = 1 mark, two examples cited from any category = 1 mark, neat presentation = 1 mark.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)

**Given:**
- $P_0 = 12{,}00{,}000$ persons
- $q_0 = 0.45$ kg/capita/day
- $r_p = 2.5\% = 0.025$
- $r_q = 1.5\% = 0.015$
- $n = 20$ years

**Step 1:** Projected population in year 20

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{20} &= P_0 \times (1 + r_p)^n \\
P_{20} &= 12{,}00{,}000 \times (1.025)^{20} \\
P_{20} &= 12{,}00{,}000 \times 1.6386 \\
P_{20} &= 19{,}66{,}320 \text{ persons}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Projected per capita generation in year 20

$$
\begin{aligned}
q_{20} &= q_0 \times (1 + r_q)^n \\
q_{20} &= 0.45 \times (1.015)^{20} \\
q_{20} &= 0.45 \times 1.3469 \\
q_{20} &= 0.6061 \text{ kg/capita/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Projected daily waste

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{20,\text{daily}} &= P_{20} \times q_{20} \\
W_{20,\text{daily}} &= 19{,}66{,}320 \times 0.6061 \\
W_{20,\text{daily}} &= 11{,}91{,}779 \text{ kg/day} \\
W_{20,\text{daily}} &\approx 1191.78 \text{ tonnes/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Projected annual waste

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{20,\text{annual}} &= W_{20,\text{daily}} \times 365 \\
W_{20,\text{annual}} &= 1191.78 \times 365 \\
W_{20,\text{annual}} &= 4{,}35{,}000 \text{ tonnes/year (approx.)} \\
W_{20,\text{annual}} &\approx 4.35 \text{ lakh tonnes/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** The city must design its SWM infrastructure for approximately **4,35,000 tonnes/year** by the end of the 20-year design horizon.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style]

**"a)** Describe the **physical, chemical, and biological composition** of municipal solid waste. Why is each parameter important for treatment technology selection? **(7 marks)**

**b)** A waste sample weighing **2.0 kg** (wet) is oven-dried at 105 °C for 24 hours. The dried sample weighs **0.80 kg**. The waste has a loose density of **180 kg/m³** and is compacted to **540 kg/m³** in a stationary compactor. Calculate the **moisture content (%)** and the **percentage volume reduction** achieved. **(7 marks)**"

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — Composition Parameters (7 marks)

**1. Physical Composition (2 marks)**
Refers to the **distribution of waste by type/component**, expressed as % by weight. Main fractions:
- Organic (food + yard waste)
- Paper & cardboard
- Plastics
- Glass, metals, textiles
- Inerts (stones, construction debris)

*Importance:* Determines whether the waste is suitable for **composting** (high organic), **recycling** (high paper/plastic/glass/metal), or **incineration** (high non-biodegradables).

**2. Chemical Composition (2.5 marks)**

Divided into:
- **Proximate Analysis:** Moisture, Volatile Matter (VM), Fixed Carbon (FC), Ash content
- **Ultimate Analysis:** %C, %H, %N, %S, %O (dry basis)
- **Calorific Value (CV)** — energy content

*Importance:* Calorific value $> 1500$ kcal/kg supports **incineration with energy recovery**; high moisture ($> 50\%$) rules out self-sustaining combustion. Volatile matter indicates methane potential in anaerobic digestion.

**3. Biological Composition (2.5 marks)**

Includes:
- **C/N ratio** (optimum 25–35 for composting)
- **Biodegradable fraction** (volatile solids basis)
- **Pathogen load** (relevant for BMW)

*Importance:* The biodegradable fraction determines **methane yield in anaerobic digesters**; the C/N ratio governs the **co-composting recipe design**; pathogens dictate **handling protocols for BMW**.

> **Mark split:** Sub-headers 0.5 each, importance statements 1.5 marks, examples 0.5 mark.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)

**Given:**
- $W_w = 2.0$ kg (wet weight)
- $W_d = 0.80$ kg (dry weight)
- $\rho_L = 180$ kg/m³
- $\rho_C = 540$ kg/m³

**Step 1: Moisture content**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M &= \frac{W_w - W_d}{W_w} \times 100 \\
M &= \frac{2.0 - 0.80}{2.0} \times 100 \\
M &= \frac{1.20}{2.0} \times 100 \\
M &= 60.0\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compaction ratio**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CR &= \frac{\rho_C}{\rho_L} = \frac{540}{180} = 3.0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Percentage volume reduction**

$$
\begin{aligned}
VR(\%) &= \left(1 - \frac{1}{CR}\right) \times 100 \\
VR(\%) &= \left(1 - \frac{1}{3}\right) \times 100 \\
VR(\%) &= 0.6667 \times 100 \\
VR(\%) &= 66.67\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** Moisture content = **60.0%**; Volume reduction = **66.67%**.

**Engineering Interpretation (for 1 bonus mark):**
- $M = 60\%$ is **above the self-sustained combustion threshold of 50%**, so this waste is **not suitable for direct incineration**.
- A 66.67% volume reduction means **the compactor triples the load capacity of the collection vehicle**, nearly halving the number of trips to the disposal site.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 2: Waste Generation and Composition**

- ✅ **MSW** includes residential + commercial + institutional + street sweeping + household C&D + garden waste. **Industrial hazardous waste is excluded**.
- ✅ **Six sources of waste** must be memorized verbatim (CPHEEO 2016): *Residential, Commercial, Institutional, Construction & Demolition, Municipal Services, Process waste (if non-hazardous)*.
- ✅ **Per Capita Generation** $q = W / (P \times t)$ — always report in **kg/capita/day**.
- ✅ **Future projection** uses **two compounding growth rates**: $W_n = P_0 \, q_0 \, (1+r_p)^n (1+r_q)^n \times 365$.
- ✅ **Moisture content** is on **wet basis**, computed after **oven drying at 105 °C for 24 hours** (CPHEEO standard).
- ✅ **Moisture > 50%** → incineration NOT viable without pre-drying.
- ✅ **Compaction Ratio** $CR = \rho_C / \rho_L$; **VR%** = $(1 - 1/CR) \times 100$.
- ✅ **Proximate Analysis** = Moisture + VM + FC + Ash; **Ultimate Analysis** = CHONS (dry basis).
- ✅ **Calorific Value** determines treatment route: low ($< 1200$ kcal/kg) → compost; high ($> 1500$ kcal/kg) → incineration with energy recovery.
- ✅ **C/N ratio of 25–35** is optimum for aerobic composting.
- ✅ **Sampling rule:** Minimum 100 kg sample, 8 samples per season, coning-and-quartering for reduction.
- ✅ **Typical Indian MSW** has 40–60% organic, 30–50% moisture, low calorific value (800–1100 kcal/kg).
- ✅ **Always state units** explicitly — this is the most common KTU deduction point.
- ✅ **Always specify wet/dry basis** — second most common KTU deduction point.
- ✅ **Use `\vert` or `\mid`**, not the bare pipe, for absolute value in any markdown table (LaTeX isolation rule).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Waste Characteristics (Physical and Chemical)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET831 - WASTE MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Waste Characteristics (Physical and Chemical)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What Are Waste Characteristics?

**Waste Characteristics** refer to the quantifiable physical and chemical properties of municipal solid waste (MSW) that govern its handling, collection, transport, treatment, and final disposal strategy. Determining these properties is the *first and most critical step* in any solid waste management (SWM) system design because every downstream process — from landfill sizing to incinerator selection — depends on them.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):**
> The 2024 NEP-aligned scheme emphasizes **physical and chemical characterization** as the foundation of integrated SWM. A student is expected to identify *why* a parameter is measured, *how* it is measured, and *what design decision* it influences.

From an engineering standpoint, waste is not a single material; it is a **heterogeneous, time-varying, multi-phase mixture** of organic, inorganic, putrescible, and inert fractions. Therefore, characterization is both a *sampling problem* and an *analytical chemistry problem*.

## 1.2 Physical vs. Chemical Characteristics — The Two Pillars

| Domain | Physical Characteristics | Chemical Characteristics |
|---|---|---|
| **Question Answered** | How much space does it occupy? How heavy? How wet? | What is it made of? How much energy can it release? How biodegradable? |
| **Key Parameters** | Moisture content, specific gravity, density, particle size, colour, odour | pH, calorific value, COD, BOD, TOC, proximate/ultimate analysis, heavy metals |
| **Engineering Use** | Storage volume, truck capacity, landfill compaction, equipment sizing | Biogas yield, incineration efficiency, leachate prediction, compost quality |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Patient Diagnosis" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: Diagnosing a Patient**
> Imagine a doctor examining a patient. Before prescribing medicine, the doctor records **vital signs** — temperature, weight, blood pressure. These are the *physical* characteristics. Then, blood tests and biochemical assays reveal the *chemical* make-up — sugar level, cholesterol, electrolytes. Together, these give a complete picture of health.
> Similarly, an SWM engineer first measures the *physical vital signs* of waste (mass, volume, density, moisture) and then performs *chemical assays* (carbon, nitrogen, calorific value, toxicity) before designing a treatment train. **Skipping characterization is like prescribing treatment without diagnosis.**

## 1.4 Why KTU Emphasizes This Topic

KTU examiners frequently frame Module 2 questions around the phrase: *"Discuss the physical and chemical characteristics of municipal solid waste."* This is because:

1. It tests whether the student understands **waste as a material** rather than waste as "garbage".
2. It links directly to **Course Outcomes (CO2)**: *Estimate the quantity and characterize the quality of solid waste*.
3. It forms the prerequisite for **Module 3 (waste collection) and Module 4 (processing technologies)**.

## 1.5 Standard Metrics Used in KTU 2024 Evaluations

The following standard metrics are used in board exams and must be memorized:

- **Moisture Content (MC)** — expressed as a percentage (%) of wet weight.
- **Specific Gravity (SG)** — dimensionless, relative to **water at 4 °C (SG = 1.00)**.
- **Bulk Density (BD)** — measured in **kg/m³**.
- **Calorific Value (CV)** — measured in **kcal/kg** or **kJ/kg** (1 kcal = 4.186 kJ).
- **pH** — measured on a scale of **0 to 14**, with 7 being neutral.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Physical Characteristics — Detailed Analysis

### 2.1.1 Moisture Content (MC)

Moisture content is the **mass of water present per unit mass of wet waste**, expressed as a percentage. It is the single most important physical property because it directly determines the waste's **calorific value, weight, leachate generation potential, and biodegradability**.

**Why it matters:**
- High MC (above 50%) → waste is unsuitable for incineration (energy wasted in vaporising water).
- Low MC (below 20%) → ideal for combustion-based disposal.
- MC > 60% → indicates high putrescible/organic fraction (typical of Indian municipal waste).

**How it is measured:**
A known mass of waste ($M_w$) is oven-dried at **103–105 °C for 24 hours** (as per IS 10171:1999) until constant mass ($M_d$) is achieved.

### 2.1.2 Specific Gravity (SG)

Specific gravity of waste is the **ratio of the density of waste to the density of water**. It is critical for:
- Estimating the **volume occupied in a landfill**,
- Designing **transfer station containers**,
- Calculating **compaction ratios**.

MSW specific gravity typically ranges from **0.4 to 0.9 (as-received)** and **0.85 to 1.25 (compacted)**.

### 2.1.3 Bulk Density (BD)

Bulk density is the **mass of waste per unit volume** of the container, including void spaces. It determines:
- **Truck/container capacity** (volume × BD = payload).
- **Landfill airspace required** (annual waste mass ÷ BD = annual volume).

Typical values:
- **Loose / as-generated:** 100 to 200 kg/m³
- **Compacted in collection vehicle:** 300 to 500 kg/m³
- **Compacted in landfill (with compactor):** 600 to 900 kg/m³

### 2.1.4 Particle Size and Distribution

Particle size affects:
- **Aeration in composting** (smaller particles → better aeration → faster decomposition).
- **Mechanical sorting efficiency**.
- **Compressibility** in landfill.

A sieve analysis (in compliance with IS 10171) is conducted, and results are plotted as a particle size distribution curve.

### 2.1.5 Volumetric Composition

Waste is classified into physical fractions:
- **Organic / biodegradable:** food waste, yard waste, paper (in India, often > 50%).
- **Recyclables:** plastics, glass, metals, textiles.
- **Inerts:** construction debris, sand, stones.
- **Hazardous / domestic hazardous waste:** batteries, paints, e-waste.

### 2.1.6 Colour and Odour

- **Colour:** Indicates decomposition stage — fresh waste is light-coloured; older waste turns blackish due to humus formation.
- **Odour:** Caused by **H₂S, NH₃, mercaptans, volatile fatty acids**. Indicates anaerobic activity.

### 2.1.7 Field Capacity

Field capacity is the **maximum amount of moisture a waste can hold against gravity**. Leachate is generated only when MC exceeds field capacity. Typically **30–60%** for MSW.

---

## 2.2 Chemical Characteristics — Detailed Analysis

### 2.2.1 Proximate Analysis

Proximate analysis determines four parameters on a dry basis:
- **Moisture (M)** — measured by oven-drying.
- **Volatile Matter (VM)** — measured by igniting at **950 ± 20 °C for 7 minutes** in a muffle furnace.
- **Fixed Carbon (FC)** — calculated by difference: $FC = 100 - VM - Ash$.
- **Ash (inorganic residue)** — measured as the residue after combustion at **550 °C**.

### 2.2.2 Ultimate Analysis (Elemental Analysis)

Ultimate analysis gives the elemental composition of dry, ash-free waste:
- **Carbon (C)** — primary energy carrier.
- **Hydrogen (H)** — contributes to calorific value.
- **Oxygen (O)** — reduces CV.
- **Nitrogen (N)** — important for composting/biogas.
- **Sulphur (S)** — forms SO₂ during combustion (acid rain precursor).
- **Chlorine (Cl)** — forms HCl, dioxins, and furans in incinerators.

This is the most data-rich chemical characterisation step.

### 2.2.3 Calorific Value (CV)

Calorific value is the **amount of heat released per unit mass of waste during complete combustion**, measured in **kcal/kg** or **kJ/kg**.

Two types:
- **Gross Calorific Value (GCV)** — includes heat from condensation of water vapour.
- **Net Calorific Value (NCV)** — excludes latent heat (used for engineering design).

$$NCV = GCV - 9 \times h \times (MC)$$

where $h$ is the hydrogen fraction.

### 2.2.4 Chemical Oxygen Demand (COD) and Biochemical Oxygen Demand (BOD)

These parameters measure the **oxygen-depleting strength of waste leachate**:
- **BOD** — oxygen consumed by microbial oxidation of biodegradable organics over **5 days at 20 °C** (BOD₅).
- **COD** — oxygen consumed by chemical oxidation using **K₂Cr₂O₇ (dichromate)**. Includes both biodegradable and non-biodegradable organics.

Typical MSW leachate BOD₅: **5,000 to 20,000 mg/L** (very strong).

### 2.2.5 pH

pH indicates the **acidity or alkalinity** of waste/ leachate. Fresh waste pH is near neutral (6–7); stabilized waste becomes alkaline (7.5–9).

### 2.2.6 Total Kjeldahl Nitrogen (TKN) and Total Organic Carbon (TOC)

- **TKN** = organic nitrogen + ammonia nitrogen — vital for **composting C/N ratio** assessment.
- **TOC** — total carbon bound in organic compounds; indicates the **biodegradable organic load**.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **The following table contains ALL high-yield formulas for Module 2. Memorize them — KTU board exams test 2–3 of these every year.**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Standard Unit | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Moisture Content | $MC = \dfrac{M_w - M_d}{M_w} \times 100$ | % (wet basis) | $M_w$ = wet mass, $M_d$ = dry mass |
| 2 | Specific Gravity | $SG = \dfrac{\rho_{waste}}{\rho_{water}}$ | Dimensionless | $\rho_{water} = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$ at 4 °C |
| 3 | Bulk Density | $BD = \dfrac{M_{waste}}{V_{container}}$ | kg/m³ | Includes voids |
| 4 | Void Ratio (in waste) | $e = \dfrac{V_v}{V_s}$ | Dimensionless | $V_v$ = void volume, $V_s$ = solid volume |
| 5 | Porosity | $n = \dfrac{V_v}{V_t} \times 100$ | % | $V_t$ = total volume |
| 6 | Volatile Matter | $VM = \dfrac{M_{950} - M_{ash}}{M_{sample}} \times 100$ | % | 950 °C for 7 min |
| 7 | Fixed Carbon | $FC = 100 - (VM + Ash)$ | % | By difference |
| 8 | Calorific Value (NCV) | $NCV = GCV - 9h \times MC$ | kcal/kg | $h$ = hydrogen fraction |
| 9 | Landfill Volume Required | $V_{LF} = \dfrac{M_{annual}}{BD_{compacted}}$ | m³/year | Used in landfill sizing |
| 10 | BOD₅ to COD Ratio | $\dfrac{BOD_5}{COD}$ | Dimensionless | 0.4–0.6 for MSW (biodegradable) |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Moisture Content** → governs the choice between *composting* and *incineration*. India (MC > 50%) favors composting and biomethanation.
- **Calorific Value** → threshold of **1000 kcal/kg** is the minimum for self-sustaining combustion; below this, auxiliary fuel is needed.
- **C/N Ratio (Ultimate Analysis)** → ideal C/N for composting is **25:1 to 35:1**. Indian waste often has low C/N (~25), requiring no correction.
- **Bulk Density** → a doubling in BD from 200 to 400 kg/m³ halves the number of collection trips, saving crores in fleet cost.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Moisture Content (Wet Basis)

The moisture content on a **wet basis** is derived as follows:

**Step 1: Define the wet mass of the waste sample.**
The total mass of the as-received waste sample is $M_w$ (kg).

**Step 2: Oven-dry the sample.**
Place the sample in a hot air oven at $103 \pm 2$ °C for 24 hours until a constant mass is reached. The remaining mass is $M_d$ (kg) — the dry mass.

**Step 3: Determine the mass of water lost.**
The mass of water evaporated is the difference between wet and dry mass.

$$M_{water} = M_w - M_d$$

**Step 4: Express moisture content on a wet basis.**
Moisture content is the ratio of water mass to wet mass, expressed as a percentage.

$$MC_{wb} = \frac{M_{water}}{M_w} \times 100 = \frac{M_w - M_d}{M_w} \times 100$$

**Numerical Example (KTU Style):**
A waste sample weighs 250 g in the as-received state. After oven drying, it weighs 110 g. Calculate the moisture content.

**Step 1:** Identify given values.
$M_w = 250 \text{ g}$, $M_d = 110 \text{ g}$.

**Step 2:** Compute the water mass.

$$\begin{aligned}
M_{water} &= M_w - M_d \\
&= 250 - 110 \\
&= 140 \text{ g}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3:** Compute moisture content.

$$\begin{aligned}
MC_{wb} &= \frac{140}{250} \times 100 \\
&= 0.56 \times 100 \\
&= 56\%
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** The waste has 56% moisture. This is **above the 50% threshold**, indicating the waste is unsuitable for direct incineration and favors biological treatment (composting / biomethanation).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Conversion Between Wet and Dry Basis Moisture Content

Often in KTU problems, MC is given on a dry basis and the student must convert to a wet basis (or vice versa).

**Step 1: Recall wet basis definition.**

$$MC_{wb} = \frac{M_w - M_d}{M_w} = 1 - \frac{M_d}{M_w}$$

**Step 2: Recall dry basis definition.**

$$MC_{db} = \frac{M_w - M_d}{M_d} = \frac{M_w}{M_d} - 1$$

**Step 3: Express one in terms of the other.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{M_d}{M_w} &= 1 - MC_{wb} \\
\frac{M_w}{M_d} &= \frac{1}{1 - MC_{wb}}
\end{aligned}$$

Substituting into the dry-basis equation:

$$MC_{db} = \frac{1}{1 - MC_{wb}} - 1 = \frac{MC_{wb}}{1 - MC_{wb}}$$

**Step 4: Reciprocal conversion.**

$$MC_{wb} = \frac{MC_{db}}{1 + MC_{db}}$$

**Numerical Verification:**
If $MC_{wb} = 56\% = 0.56$, then:

$$MC_{db} = \frac{0.56}{1 - 0.56} = \frac{0.56}{0.44} = 1.2727 = 127.27\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Always read the question carefully — is MC given on wet or dry basis? The same waste can be described as "56%" wet basis OR "127%" dry basis. The wet-basis value is always *smaller* in magnitude.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Net Calorific Value (NCV) from GCV

**Step 1: Write the heat of combustion balance.**
During combustion, hydrogen in the fuel combines with oxygen to form water. The latent heat of vaporization of this water (~ 580 kcal/kg at 25 °C) is **lost in the flue gas** unless recovered.

**Step 2: Standard empirical relation used in waste engineering.**

$$NCV = GCV - 9 \times h \times MC_{wb} \quad \text{(kcal/kg)}$$

where:
- $h$ = mass fraction of hydrogen in dry waste (e.g., 0.06 for 6%).
- $MC_{wb}$ = moisture content on wet basis (as a fraction, e.g., 0.56).

**Step 3: Numerical Example.**
Given: GCV = 2200 kcal/kg, $h = 0.06$, $MC_{wb} = 0.56$.

$$\begin{aligned}
NCV &= 2200 - 9 \times 0.06 \times 0.56 \times 1000 \quad \text{(to keep units in kcal/kg)}\\
&= 2200 - 9 \times 0.06 \times 560 \\
&= 2200 - 302.4 \\
&= 1897.6 \text{ kcal/kg}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The 9 factor accounts for the latent heat (approximately $9 \times 580/1000 \approx 5.2$, plus corrections). KTU accepts the standard 9-multiplier convention.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Landfill Volume from Mass and Bulk Density

**Step 1: Define annual waste generation.**

$$M_{annual} = P \times q \times 365$$

where:
- $P$ = population served.
- $q$ = per capita waste generation rate (kg/capita/day).

**Step 2: Compute annual volume required.**

$$V_{annual} = \frac{M_{annual}}{BD_{landfill}}$$

**Step 3: Compute total landfill life volume.**

$$V_{LF} = V_{annual} \times N_{years}$$

**Step 4: Add cover material (typically 15–20% of waste volume).**

$$V_{total} = V_{LF} \times 1.15 \quad \text{(with soil cover)}$$

**Numerical Example:**
City population = 1,000,000; $q = 0.5$ kg/capita/day; $BD_{landfill} = 700$ kg/m³; design life = 20 years.

**Step 1: Annual mass.**

$$M_{annual} = 1{,}000{,}000 \times 0.5 \times 365 = 182{,}500{,}000 \text{ kg/yr} = 1.825 \times 10^8 \text{ kg/yr}$$

**Step 2: Annual volume.**

$$V_{annual} = \frac{1.825 \times 10^8}{700} = 260{,}714 \text{ m}^3/\text{yr}$$

**Step 3: 20-year volume.**

$$V_{LF} = 260{,}714 \times 20 = 5{,}214{,}286 \text{ m}^3 \approx 5.21 \times 10^6 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 4: With cover soil (15%).**

$$V_{total} = 5.21 \times 10^6 \times 1.15 \approx 5.99 \times 10^6 \text{ m}^3 \approx 6 \times 10^6 \text{ m}^3$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Pattern:** This derivation appears almost every year as a 7-mark sub-question. Always state the units clearly in every step.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Properties vs. Treatment Selection

| Property | Low Value | High Value | Treatment Choice |
|---|---|---|---|
| Moisture Content | < 20% | > 50% | High MC → composting; Low MC → incineration |
| Calorific Value | < 1000 kcal/kg | > 1500 kcal/kg | High CV → incineration with energy recovery |
| C/N Ratio | < 20 | > 40 | Ideal 25–35; add bulking agent or N source |
| Bulk Density | < 200 kg/m³ | > 600 kg/m³ | Low → composting; High → landfill compaction |
| BOD/COD Ratio | < 0.3 | > 0.5 | High ratio → biodegradable → biological treatment |
| pH | < 6 | > 8 | Extreme pH → leachate management / stabilization |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Waste Characterization Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["WASTE SAMPLE COLLECTION"] --> B["SOURCE SEGREGATION"]
    B --> C["QUARTERING AND CONING"]
    C --> D1["PHYSICAL ANALYSIS"]
    C --> D2["CHEMICAL ANALYSIS"]
    D1 --> E1["MOISTURE CONTENT OVEN DRY 103 C"]
    D1 --> E2["BULK DENSITY WEIGHING KNOWN VOLUME"]
    D1 --> E3["SPECIFIC GRAVITY PYCNOMETER"]
    D1 --> E4["PARTICLE SIZE SIEVE ANALYSIS"]
    D1 --> E5["VOLUMETRIC COMPOSITION SORTING"]
    D2 --> F1["PROXIMATE ANALYSIS MUFFLE FURNACE"]
    D2 --> F2["ULTIMATE ANALYSIS CHNSO ANALYSER"]
    D2 --> F3["CALORIFIC VALUE BOMB CALORIMETER"]
    D2 --> F4["pH METER LEACHATE"]
    D2 --> F5["BOD COD TOC SPECTROPHOTOMETER"]
    E1 --> G["CHARACTERIZATION REPORT"]
    E2 --> G
    E3 --> G
    E4 --> G
    E5 --> G
    F1 --> G
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    F4 --> G
    F5 --> G
    G --> H["DESIGN DECISIONS TREATMENT AND DISPOSAL"]
```

## 4.2 Classification Tree — Physical vs. Chemical Properties

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["WASTE CHARACTERISTICS"] --> B["PHYSICAL PROPERTIES"]
    A --> C["CHEMICAL PROPERTIES"]
    B --> B1["MOISTURE CONTENT"]
    B --> B2["BULK DENSITY"]
    B --> B3["SPECIFIC GRAVITY"]
    B --> B4["PARTICLE SIZE"]
    B --> B5["COLOUR AND ODOUR"]
    C --> C1["PROXIMATE M VM FC ASH"]
    C --> C2["ULTIMATE C H N S O CL"]
    C --> C3["CALORIFIC VALUE"]
    C --> C4["pH"]
    C --> C5["BOD COD TOC"]
    C1 --> D["INFORM DESIGN"]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    C4 --> D
    C5 --> D
    B1 --> D
    B2 --> D
    B3 --> D
    B4 --> D
    B5 --> D
    D --> E["TREATMENT TRAIN SELECTION"]
```

## 4.3 Decision Flow — Treatment Selection from Characterization

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["MEASURED MC AND CV"] --> B{"MC ABOVE 50 PERCENT"}
    B -->|YES| C["CHOOSE BIOLOGICAL TREATMENT COMPOSTING OR BIOMETHANATION"]
    B -->|NO| D{"CV ABOVE 1000 KCAL PER KG"}
    D -->|YES| E["CHOOSE INCINERATION WITH ENERGY RECOVERY"]
    D -->|NO| F["CHOOSE SANITARY LANDFILL WITH LEACHATE CONTROL"]
    C --> G["CHARACTERIZATION COMPLETE"]
    E --> G
    F --> G
```

## 4.4 Schematic — Proximate vs. Ultimate Analysis

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["WASTE SAMPLE DRY BASIS"] --> B["PROXIMATE ANALYSIS FOUR COMPONENTS"]
    A --> C["ULTIMATE ANALYSIS ELEMENTAL FRACTIONS"]
    B --> B1["MOISTURE M"]
    B --> B2["VOLATILE MATTER VM"]
    B --> B3["FIXED CARBON FC"]
    B --> B4["ASH A"]
    C --> C1["CARBON C"]
    C --> C2["HYDROGEN H"]
    C --> C3["OXYGEN O"]
    C --> C4["NITROGEN N"]
    C --> C5["SULPHUR S"]
    C --> C6["CHLORINE CL"]
    B2 --> D["FUEL PROPERTY INDICATOR"]
    B3 --> D
    C1 --> D
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    C4 --> E["FERTILIZER C/N RATIO"]
    C5 --> F["EMISSION CONTROL SOX HCL"]
    C6 --> F
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Instructions:** Each question carries 3 marks. Answer in 2–3 sentences with relevant formula/units.

### Question 1: Define Moisture Content of solid waste. State its significance in waste management.
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**
Moisture content is the **mass of water present in waste expressed as a percentage of its wet mass**, calculated as $MC = \dfrac{M_w - M_d}{M_w} \times 100$. It is significant because it **determines the suitability of waste for incineration** (low MC preferred) versus **biological treatment** (high MC is acceptable for composting). It also influences **leachate generation, weight for transport, and biogas yield** in landfills.

### Question 2: Differentiate between BOD and COD.
`[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**Model Answer:**
**BOD (Biochemical Oxygen Demand)** is the **oxygen consumed by microorganisms** to biodegrade organic matter over 5 days at 20 °C, and it measures only the **biodegradable fraction**. **COD (Chemical Oxygen Demand)** is the **oxygen consumed by strong chemical oxidants (K₂Cr₂O₇)** and measures **both biodegradable and non-biodegradable organics**. COD is always **greater than BOD**, and the BOD/COD ratio indicates the **biodegradability** of the waste.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part-B question has sub-parts (a) and (b), each carrying 7 marks. Internal choice is provided.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**A. (a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the physical characteristics of municipal solid waste. State the typical range of values for each parameter.
`[KTU University Exam - June 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

The physical characteristics of MSW describe the **mechanical and bulk properties** that affect handling, collection, and disposal. The key parameters are:

1. **Moisture Content (MC):**
   - Definition: water mass per unit wet mass of waste, expressed in %.
   - **Typical range: 20% to 70%** in Indian MSW.
   - Significance: governs choice between incineration (low MC) and biological treatment (high MC).

2. **Bulk Density (BD):**
   - Definition: mass per unit volume of waste in a container (kg/m³).
   - **Typical range:** 100–200 kg/m³ (loose); 300–500 kg/m³ (compacted in vehicle); 600–900 kg/m³ (landfill).
   - Significance: determines truck capacity and landfill airspace.

3. **Specific Gravity (SG):**
   - Definition: ratio of waste density to water density.
   - **Typical range: 0.4 to 0.9** (as-received); 0.85–1.25 (compacted).
   - Significance: used in landfill settlement and volume calculations.

4. **Particle Size and Distribution:**
   - Measured by sieve analysis.
   - Affects: composting aeration, sorting efficiency, compressibility.

5. **Volumetric Composition:**
   - The fraction of total volume occupied by each waste component (organic, recyclable, inert, hazardous).
   - Determines: the requirement for source segregation, MRF design, and recycling potential.

6. **Colour and Odour:**
   - Fresh waste is **light-coloured** with mild odour; older waste is **blackish** with strong **H₂S/NH₃/mercaptan** odour.
   - Significance: indicates decomposition stage and worker safety considerations.

7. **Field Capacity:**
   - The maximum moisture a waste can hold without releasing leachate.
   - **Typical range: 30–60%**.
   - Significance: determines when leachate collection is required in a landfill.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** Physical characterization is the first step in designing an SWM system. It dictates equipment sizing, transport economics, and landfill design.

---

**A. (b) [7 Marks]** A municipal area generates 250 tonnes of MSW per day. The moisture content of the waste is 60% (wet basis) and the bulk density in the landfill is 750 kg/m³. Estimate the annual volume of waste to be disposed of in the landfill, including 20% allowance for cover material.
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1: Daily waste mass.**
$M_{day} = 250 \text{ tonnes/day} = 250{,}000 \text{ kg/day}$

**Step 2: Annual waste mass.**

$$M_{annual} = 250{,}000 \times 365 = 9.125 \times 10^7 \text{ kg/yr}$$

**Step 3: Annual volume of waste in landfill (without cover).**

$$V_{waste} = \frac{M_{annual}}{BD_{landfill}} = \frac{9.125 \times 10^7}{750} = 1.2167 \times 10^5 \text{ m}^3/\text{yr}$$

$$V_{waste} = 121{,}667 \text{ m}^3/\text{yr}$$

**Step 4: Add 20% cover material allowance.**

$$V_{total} = V_{waste} \times 1.20 = 121{,}667 \times 1.20 = 146{,}000 \text{ m}^3/\text{yr}$$

**Step 5: Present the final result clearly.**

$$\boxed{V_{total} \approx 1.46 \times 10^5 \text{ m}^3/\text{yr} = 146{,}000 \text{ m}^3/\text{year}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on moisture content:** The 60% MC value is a *distractor* in this problem — it is not needed to compute landfill volume. Recognizing this is a sign of KTU 2024 readiness. However, MC would be relevant if the question asked for dry waste mass or leachate volume.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**B. (a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the chemical characteristics of MSW. Explain proximate and ultimate analysis in detail.
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

The chemical characteristics of MSW describe the **composition of the waste at the molecular level** and govern its **energy potential, biodegradability, and emissions**.

**1. Proximate Analysis:**
Proximate analysis quantifies four parameters on a dry basis:

| Parameter | Method | Typical MSW Range |
|---|---|---|
| Moisture | Oven drying at 103 °C | 20–60% (wet basis) |
| Volatile Matter (VM) | Ignition at 950 °C for 7 min | 40–60% (dry basis) |
| Fixed Carbon (FC) | By difference: $FC = 100 - VM - Ash$ | 5–10% (dry basis) |
| Ash | Combustion residue at 550 °C | 25–40% (dry basis) |

Significance: high VM and low ash indicate high-energy waste suitable for incineration.

**2. Ultimate Analysis (Elemental Analysis):**
Determines elemental composition:

| Element | Typical MSW Range | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Carbon (C) | 15–30% | Energy carrier |
| Hydrogen (H) | 2–5% | Contributes to CV; forms H₂O |
| Oxygen (O) | 10–25% | Reduces CV |
| Nitrogen (N) | 0.5–2% | Determines C/N ratio; forms NOₓ |
| Sulphur (S) | 0.1–0.5% | Forms SO₂ (acid rain) |
| Chlorine (Cl) | 0.2–1.0% | Forms HCl, dioxins in incineration |

Significance: ultimate analysis is essential for **combustion calculations, emission control, and compost quality assessment**.

**3. Other Chemical Parameters:**
- **Calorific Value (CV):** energy released on combustion; ranges 800–2200 kcal/kg.
- **pH:** 6–9 for stabilized waste.
- **BOD₅ and COD:** indicate biodegradable organic load; MSW leachate has BOD₅ = 5,000–20,000 mg/L.
- **Total Organic Carbon (TOC):** organic carbon in waste.
- **Total Kjeldahl Nitrogen (TKN):** nitrogen available for composting.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** Chemical characterization is the foundation for **biogas yield estimation, incineration design, leachate treatment, and compost quality control**.

---

**B. (b) [7 Marks]** A waste sample has a GCV of 2500 kcal/kg. The hydrogen content is 5% and the moisture content (wet basis) is 45%. Calculate the NCV. Comment on the suitability of this waste for incineration.
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1: Write the formula.**

$$NCV = GCV - 9 \times h \times MC \times 1000 \quad \text{(in kcal/kg, when MC and h are fractions)}$$

**Step 2: Substitute the given values.**
- $GCV = 2500 \text{ kcal/kg}$
- $h = 0.05$
- $MC = 0.45$

**Step 3: Compute the latent heat loss term.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Latent heat loss} &= 9 \times 0.05 \times 0.45 \times 1000 \\
&= 9 \times 0.0225 \times 1000 \\
&= 202.5 \text{ kcal/kg}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Compute NCV.**

$$NCV = 2500 - 202.5 = 2297.5 \text{ kcal/kg}$$

**Step 5: Comment on suitability.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Verdict:** With NCV = 2297.5 kcal/kg (above the 1500 kcal/kg threshold and well above the 1000 kcal/kg self-sustaining threshold), the waste is **suitable for incineration with energy recovery**. However, the 45% MC is **borderline high**; drying or co-combustion with low-MC waste (e.g., refuse-derived fuel) would further improve combustion efficiency.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Unit confusion:** Forgetting to convert tonnes → kg, or years → days. **Always show unit cancellations.**
> 2. **Wet vs. dry basis mix-up:** A common trap in KTU questions. Always state explicitly which basis is used.
> 3. **MC units:** If MC is given in % (e.g., 45%), divide by 100 before substituting into formulas.
> 4. **Missing cover material allowance:** Landfill volume problems ALWAYS require the 15–20% soil cover correction. Forgetting this loses 1 mark.
> 5. **Confusing GCV and NCV:** GCV > NCV. Use NCV for engineering design calculations.
> 6. **Lack of comments:** Numerical answers without engineering comments (e.g., "suitable for incineration") lose 1–2 marks in Part-B.
> 7. **Skipping formulas:** Always write the formula before substituting. Even if the final answer is correct, missing the formula loses a mark.
> 8. **Forgetting units in final answer:** KTU strictly requires the unit (e.g., kcal/kg, m³/yr) in the boxed answer.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 2:**

- ✅ **Moisture Content:** $MC_{wb} = \dfrac{M_w - M_d}{M_w} \times 100$; oven-dried at **103 °C for 24 h**; Indian MSW MC = **20–70%**.
- ✅ **Specific Gravity:** dimensionless; range **0.4 to 0.9** as-received; $\rho_{water} = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$.
- ✅ **Bulk Density:** 100–200 kg/m³ (loose); 300–500 kg/m³ (compacted vehicle); 600–900 kg/m³ (landfill).
- ✅ **Field Capacity:** typically **30–60%**; leachate forms when MC > field capacity.
- ✅ **Proximate Analysis:** four parameters — Moisture, Volatile Matter, Fixed Carbon, Ash; **VM by ignition at 950 °C for 7 min**; **Ash at 550 °C**.
- ✅ **Fixed Carbon formula:** $FC = 100 - VM - Ash$ (on dry basis).
- ✅ **Ultimate Analysis:** CHNSO + Cl; typical C = 15–30%, H = 2–5%, N = 0.5–2%.
- ✅ **Calorific Value:** GCV includes latent heat; NCV excludes it. **NCV = GCV - 9h·MC (kcal/kg)**.
- ✅ **Incineration threshold:** CV > **1000 kcal/kg** for self-sustaining combustion; > 1500 kcal/kg preferred.
- ✅ **BOD/COD:** BOD₅ measured at **20 °C for 5 days**; COD uses **K₂Cr₂O₇**; COD > BOD always.
- ✅ **C/N ratio for composting:** ideal **25:1 to 35:1**.
- ✅ **Landfill volume formula:** $V = \dfrac{M_{annual}}{BD} \times N_{years} \times 1.15$ (with cover material).
- ✅ **Distractors in numerical problems:** Identify which given data is needed; ignore irrelevant data (e.g., MC when computing landfill volume).
- ✅ **Standard practice:** Always present numerical answers in a **boxed format with explicit units**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Health and Environmental Effects (Public Health and Environmental)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET831 - WASTE MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Health and Environmental Effects (Public Health and Environmental)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **KTU 2024 Scheme – Waste Management (OECET831)**, the *Health and Environmental Effects* of solid and hazardous waste refer to the systematic, measurable, and often adverse impacts that improperly managed waste streams have on **human well-being (public health)** and on the **natural ecosystem (air, water, soil, and biota)**. The study of these effects is fundamentally rooted in the discipline of **Environmental Risk Assessment (ERA)**, which evaluates the probability and severity of harm caused by hazardous agents released from waste into the environment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** *Health and environmental effects* encompass both **direct (acute) impacts** — such as injuries, infections, and toxic exposures — and **indirect (chronic) impacts** — such as long-term bioaccumulation, ecosystem degradation, and climate forcing — arising from the lifecycle stages of waste (generation, collection, transport, treatment, and disposal).

The primary classification adopted by KTU follows the **WHO (World Health Organization)** and **CPCB (Central Pollution Control Board, India)** framework:

- **Public Health Effects:** Vector-borne diseases, respiratory illnesses, chemical toxicity, injuries, psychological stress.
- **Environmental Effects:** Air pollution, water contamination, soil degradation, biodiversity loss, and global warming.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine your home as a small, closed ecosystem. When you throw household waste (food scraps, plastic, e-waste) into an open bin and never empty it, what happens? **Flies, mosquitoes, and rats arrive.** The smell becomes unbearable, the leachate seeps into the kitchen floor (analogous to groundwater), and the rotting food releases gases (analogous to air pollution). Your family begins to cough (respiratory issues), and you may get food poisoning (chemical/microbial contamination).

> This is precisely what happens, on a city-scale, with **municipal solid waste (MSW)**: the open bin is a *landfill* or *dumping yard*, the flies and rats are *disease vectors*, the leachate is *groundwater contamination*, and the gases are *methane and VOCs (Volatile Organic Compounds)* causing *air pollution and global warming*.

## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics (Bolded for Quick Recall)

- **WHO PM2.5 Standard:** **15 µg/m³ (annual mean)**, **45 µg/m³ (24-hour mean)**
- **WHO PM10 Standard:** **45 µg/m³ (annual mean)**, **45 µg/m³ (24-hour mean — revised)**
- **NAAQS (India) PM2.5:** **40 µg/m³ (annual)**, **60 µg/m³ (24-hour)**
- **NAAQS (India) PM10:** **60 µg/m³ (annual)**, **100 µg/m³ (24-hour)**
- **Drinking Water (BIS 10500):** Lead **≤ 0.01 mg/L**, Arsenic **≤ 0.01 mg/L**, Mercury **≤ 0.001 mg/L**
- **BOD₅ for Surface Water (CPCB):** **≤ 3 mg/L** (Class A — Drinking water source)
- **LD₅₀ (Lethal Dose 50%):** Statistical measure (mg/kg body weight) causing 50% mortality in test population
- **NOAEL (No Observed Adverse Effect Level):** Highest dose at which no adverse effect is observed
- **LOAEL (Lowest Observed Adverse Effect Level):** Lowest dose at which an adverse effect is observed

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these metrics matter in KTU exams:** Examiners frequently test whether students can compare **WHO standards vs. NAAQS vs. CPCB norms** and explain why India follows different limits. The reason is **population density, baseline pollution levels, and technological feasibility** of achieving stricter standards.

## 1.4 Pathway of Waste-Induced Harm (Simplified)

The harm chain is universally described as:

$$\text{Waste Source} \longrightarrow \text{Pollutant Release} \longrightarrow \text{Environmental Medium} \longrightarrow \text{Exposure} \longrightarrow \text{Dose} \longrightarrow \text{Health/Environmental Effect}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** Whenever a KTU question asks *"How does waste affect human health?"*, structure your answer using this **5-step pathway**. It is the standard valuation key pattern and guarantees a full-marks response.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Public Health Effects — Detailed Breakdown

The **WHO (2016) classification** and the **CPCB Solid Waste Management Rules (2016)** jointly categorize public health effects as follows:

### A. Direct Effects (Acute / Short-Term)

- **Infections from Pathogens:** Bacteria (*E. coli*, *Salmonella*, *Vibrio cholerae*), viruses (Hepatitis A, Rotavirus), parasites (*Ascaris*, hookworm).
  - **Mechanism:** Open dumps allow faecal–oral transmission via flies, contaminated water, and direct contact.
  - **Common Diseases:** Cholera, typhoid, dysentery, hepatitis, gastroenteritis.

- **Vector-Borne Diseases:**
  - **Vectors:** Houseflies, mosquitoes, rodents, cockroaches.
  - **Diseases:** Malaria, dengue, plague, leptospirosis.

- **Physical Injuries:**
  - Sharp objects (glass, needles, metals) cause cuts, punctures, and tetanus.
  - **Waste pickers** are the most vulnerable group.

### B. Indirect / Chronic Effects

- **Chemical Toxicity:** Heavy metals (Pb, Hg, Cd, Cr⁶⁺) cause **neurotoxicity, nephrotoxicity, carcinogenicity**.
- **Endocrine Disruption:** Phthalates, Bisphenol A (BPA), dioxins affect hormonal balance.
- **Respiratory Illnesses:** PM2.5, PM10, dioxins, furans cause **asthma, bronchitis, COPD, lung cancer**.
- **Reproductive & Developmental Effects:** Lead crosses the placental barrier; mercury causes Minamata disease.
- **Carcinogenicity:** Benzene, PAHs, dioxins, PCBs are Group 1 IARC carcinogens.

### C. Vulnerable Groups (Highlight for KTU)

- **Waste pickers / rag-pickers** (highest exposure, lowest protection).
- **Children** (higher ingestion rate per kg body weight).
- **Pregnant women** (trans-placental transfer).
- **Communities near landfills** (within 1–2 km radius).

## 2.2 Environmental Effects — Detailed Breakdown

### A. Air Pollution

- **Landfill Gas (LFG):** ~**50% methane (CH₄)** and ~**50% CO₂** (anaerobic decomposition).
- **Global Warming Potential (GWP) of CH₄ = 28–34** (over 100 years, IPCC AR5).
- **VOCs:** Benzene, toluene, xylene → smog, tropospheric ozone.
- **Particulate Matter:** PM2.5, PM10 from open burning of waste — a major concern in Indian cities.

### B. Water Pollution

- **Leachate:** Liquid formed by water percolating through waste.
  - **Composition:** High BOD (often 10,000–60,000 mg/L), COD, ammonia, heavy metals, halogenated organics.
- **Groundwater Contamination:** Leachate percolates into aquifers.
- **Surface Water:** Runoff into rivers, lakes → eutrophication.

### C. Soil Pollution

- **Heavy Metal Accumulation:** Non-biodegradable; persists for decades.
- **Reduction in Soil Fertility:** Disrupts microbial communities.
- **Land Degradation:** Dumpsites destroy vegetation and alter topography.

### D. Other Environmental Effects

- **Biodiversity Loss:** Habitat destruction; animals ingesting plastic.
- **Ozone Depletion:** CFC emissions from e-waste.
- **Climate Change:** CH₄ and CO₂ are potent greenhouse gases.
- **Visual & Aesthetic Degradation:** Open dumps degrade urban landscapes.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Notation | Unit | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $R = H \times E$ | R = Risk, H = Hazard, E = Exposure | dimensionless | Risk Assessment |
| 2 | $\text{Dose} = \dfrac{C \times IR \times EF \times ED}{BW \times AT}$ | C=Conc., IR=Intake Rate, EF=Exposure Freq., ED=Exposure Duration, BW=Body Weight, AT=Averaging Time | mg/kg·day | EPA Exposure Equation |
| 3 | $\text{HQ} = \dfrac{\text{ADD}}{\text{RfD}}$ | HQ = Hazard Quotient, ADD = Avg. Daily Dose, RfD = Reference Dose | dimensionless | Non-cancer Risk |
| 4 | $\text{CR} = \text{LADD} \times \text{CSF}$ | CR = Cancer Risk, LADD = Lifetime ADD, CSF = Cancer Slope Factor | dimensionless | Cancer Risk |
| 5 | $\text{BOD}_5 = \dfrac{DO_i - DO_f}{P}$ | DO = Dissolved Oxygen, P = Dilution factor | mg/L | Water pollution |
| 6 | $\text{COD} = \dfrac{(B - S) \times N \times 8 \times 1000}{V}$ | B=Blank, S=Sample, N=Normality, V=Volume | mg/L | Leachate strength |
| 7 | $\text{GWP} = \dfrac{\text{RF}_i}{\text{RF}_{CO_2}} \times \tau_i$ | RF = Radiative Forcing, τ = Lifetime | times CO₂ | Climate impact |
| 8 | $\text{Bioaccumulation Factor} = \dfrac{C_{organism}}{C_{environment}}$ | Concentration ratio | L/kg or dimensionless | Heavy metals, POPs |
| 9 | $\eta_{collection} = \dfrac{W_c}{W_g} \times 100$ | Wc = Waste collected, Wg = Waste generated | % | MSW efficiency |
| 10 | $\text{LD}_{50} \text{ (probit model)}$: $P = \Phi(a + b \log D)$ | Φ = standard normal CDF, D = dose | mg/kg | Toxicity testing |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Pattern:** The equation $R = H \times E$ and the EPA exposure equation appear in **almost every KTU ESE (End Semester Exam)** under this module. Memorize them with units.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Design of Landfill Liners:** Engineers use leachate strength (COD, BOD) values to select **HDPE liner thickness (typically 1.5–2.5 mm)**.
- **Buffer Zone Design:** The 1–2 km buffer around landfills is set based on **LFG dispersion modeling (e.g., AERMOD, CALPUFF)**.
- **Worker Safety:** OSHA uses **PEL (Permissible Exposure Limit)** and **TLV-TWA (Threshold Limit Value – Time Weighted Average)** to set workplace standards.
- **Public Health Policy:** WHO guidelines inform **India's National Clean Air Programme (NCAP)**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — EPA Exposure Equation (Average Daily Dose)

The **Average Daily Dose (ADD)** is the cornerstone of quantitative risk assessment used by **US EPA, CPCB, and WHO**. It estimates the mass of contaminant ingested per kg of body weight per day.

### Given Quantities

- $C$ = Concentration of contaminant in medium (mg/L for water, mg/kg for soil, mg/m³ for air)
- $IR$ = Intake rate (L/day for water, kg/day for soil, m³/day for air)
- $EF$ = Exposure frequency (days/year)
- $ED$ = Exposure duration (years)
- $BW$ = Body weight (kg)
- $AT$ = Averaging time (days)

### Step-by-Step Derivation

The total mass of contaminant entering the body over the exposure period is:

$$
M_{total} = C \times IR \times EF \times ED
$$

To obtain the **average daily dose (mg/kg·day)**, we divide this total mass by the product of **body weight** and **total averaging time**:

$$
AT_{days} = ED \times 365 \ \dfrac{\text{days}}{\text{year}}
$$

Therefore, the ADD is given by:

$$
\text{ADD} = \dfrac{M_{total}}{BW \times AT} = \dfrac{C \times IR \times EF \times ED}{BW \times ED \times 365}
$$

For **non-carcinogens**, the averaging time is $ED \times 365$ days. For **carcinogens**, it is averaged over a **lifetime (typically 70 years = 25,550 days)**:

$$
\text{ADD} = \dfrac{C \times IR \times EF \times ED}{BW \times 365}
$$

$$
\text{LADD} = \dfrac{C \times IR \times EF \times ED}{BW \times 25{,}550}
$$

### Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A community uses groundwater contaminated with lead at $C = 0.05$ mg/L. Daily water intake $IR = 2$ L/day. Exposure frequency $EF = 350$ days/year. Exposure duration $ED = 30$ years. Body weight $BW = 70$ kg. Calculate ADD and compare with the **WHO RfD for Pb = 0.0035 mg/kg·day**.

**Step 1 — Plug values into ADD equation:**

$$
\text{ADD} = \dfrac{0.05 \times 2 \times 350 \times 30}{70 \times 30 \times 365}
$$

**Step 2 — Simplify numerator:**

$$
0.05 \times 2 \times 350 \times 30 = 1050
$$

**Step 3 — Simplify denominator:**

$$
70 \times 30 \times 365 = 7{,}66{,}500
$$

**Step 4 — Compute ADD:**

$$
\text{ADD} = \dfrac{1050}{7{,}66{,}500} = 1.37 \times 10^{-3} \ \text{mg/kg·day}
$$

**Step 5 — Compare with RfD:**

$$
\text{HQ} = \dfrac{\text{ADD}}{\text{RfD}} = \dfrac{1.37 \times 10^{-3}}{3.5 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.391
$$
