<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define the term "storage" in the context of municipal solid waste management. Mention its objectives. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**
Storage refers to the **containment of solid waste at the point of generation or at intermediate locations** between the time of generation and the time of collection, processing, or disposal. It is the **first functional element** of the SWM chain.

**Objectives of storage:**
1. To maintain **hygiene and sanitation** in the vicinity of waste generation.
2. To **prevent vector breeding** (flies, rats, mosquitoes).
3. To **avoid leachate pollution** of soil and water.
4. To facilitate **source segregation** and orderly collection.
5. To **protect the aesthetic** environment of residential and commercial areas.

---

### Q2. List any four desirable characteristics of a community waste storage container. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**
1. **Durability** — resistant to corrosion, weather, and impact.
2. **Impermeability** — leak-proof to prevent leachate escape.
3. **Ease of handling** — appropriate dimensions for manual/machine lifting.
4. **Cover/lid provision** — to control odour and vector access.
5. *Additional:* Affordability, ease of cleaning, and compatibility with the lifting equipment of the collection vehicle.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

#### Q(a) (7 Marks): Explain the various **levels of storage** in municipal solid waste management with suitable examples. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

The storage of municipal solid waste is categorised into three hierarchical levels:

**1. Primary Storage (Storage at Source / L1):**
- Located **at the point of waste generation** — households, offices, shops, institutions.
- Usually small containers of **10–120 L** capacity.
- Made of HDPE, GI, or plastic.
- Allows **source segregation** (wet/dry/bio-medical).
- Example: Kitchen dustbin, office waste basket.

**2. Secondary Storage (Community / Curbside / L2):**
- Located at **community level** — lanes, streets, apartment complexes, market areas.
- Larger containers of **1 m³ to 5 m³**.
- Typically constructed in **RCC (Reinforced Cement Concrete)** masonry or movable steel/plastic bins.
- Provided with a **raised platform and leachate drain**.
- Example: The big green/blue community bin in your apartment complex.

**3. Tertiary Storage (Transfer Station / L3):**
- Located at **strategic transfer points** before long-distance transport.
- Large stationary or compactor systems of **5 m³ to 50 m³** or more.
- Enables **trans-shipment** of waste from small collection vehicles to larger trailers/compactors.
- Example: The municipal transfer station in Kochi or Thiruvananthapuram.

**Functions served by this hierarchy:**
- Minimises collection frequency.
- Reduces handling time and cost.
- Provides flexibility in routing of collection vehicles.
- Improves public hygiene and aesthetics.

#### Q(b) (7 Marks): A town of population **80,000** generates waste at the rate of **0.50 kg/capita/day**. The bulk density of waste is **450 kg/m³**. If the collection is done **thrice a week** and each container is **2.5 m³**, find the **number of containers** required. Use a safety factor of **1.25**. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Step 1 — Daily mass of waste:**
$$Q = P \times g = 80{,}000 \times 0.50 = 40{,}000 \text{ kg/day}$$

**Step 2 — Storage period:**
Thrice a week means once every $7/3 = 2.333$ days.
$$t_s = 2.333 \text{ days}$$

**Step 3 — Total mass during storage period:**
$$M = Q \times t_s = 40{,}000 \times 2.333 = 93{,}320 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 4 — Total volume:**
$$V_{total} = \dfrac{M}{\rho} = \dfrac{93{,}320}{450} = 207.378 \text{ m}^{3}$$

**Step 5 — Theoretical number of containers:**
$$n = \dfrac{207.378}{2.5} = 82.951 \approx 83 \text{ containers}$$

**Step 6 — Apply safety factor:**
$$n_{design} = 82.951 \times 1.25 = 103.69 \approx \boxed{104 \text{ containers}}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

#### Q(a) (7 Marks): Discuss the **design considerations** for a community storage container facility. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

The design of a community storage facility must address the following considerations:

**1. Capacity & Sizing:**
- Based on population served, per capita waste generation, and storage period.
- Use the equation $C_s = P \times g \times t_s$ and then divide by density.

**2. Container Material:**
- **RCC** for permanence; **HDPE** for movable bins; **GI** for fire-prone zones.

**3. Geometry & Access:**
- Height of bin opening between **0.9 m – 1.5 m** for ease of manual emptying.
- Footprint of facility should include a **circulation/access margin of 30–50%**.

**4. Drainage & Leachate Management:**
- Platform slope of **1:40** toward a drain.
- Leachate sump at the lowest point.
- Connection to **sewer or treatment unit** (or to a tanker for disposal).

**5. Hygiene & Vector Control:**
- **Closed lids** for putrescible waste.
- Periodic washing schedule (at least **once a week**).
- Use of **disinfectants and larvicides**.

**6. Public Convenience:**
- Located within **30 m** walking distance of households (CPHEEO norm).
- Should not obstruct **traffic or pedestrian flow**.

**7. Signage & Segregation:**
- **Two-bin system** (green for wet, blue for dry) per SWM Rules 2016.
- Pictorial signboards for public awareness.

#### Q(b) (7 Marks): List the **Indian Rules/Standards** governing storage of (i) Biomedical Waste, (ii) Hazardous Waste, and (iii) E-Waste. State **one key provision** of each. `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

| Waste Type | Governing Rule | Key Provision |
|---|---|---|
| **Biomedical Waste** | **BMW Management Rules, 2016** (amended 2018) | Storage time **must not exceed 48 hours**; area must be **locked, refrigerated, and labelled** with the biohazard symbol. |
| **Hazardous Waste** | **Hazardous & Other Wastes Management Rules, 2016** | Stored in **impermeable containers on a bunded impermeable platform**; bund wall ≥ **110% of largest container volume**. |
| **E-Waste** | **E-Waste Management Rules, 2022** | Must be stored in **non-corrosive, anti-static containers** and channelised only to **authorised recyclers** with **EPR (Extended Producer Responsibility) registration**. |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse rules years** — BMW is 2016, Hazardous is 2016, E-Waste is **2022** (not 2016/2018). This is a frequent mark-loser.
> 2. **Always quote bulk density** as 350–500 kg/m³ for Indian MSW; examiners expect the CPHEEO value, not a foreign standard.
> 3. **Always round UP** the number of bins — rounding down causes overflow, a design failure.
> 4. **Use the right safety factor** — a common KTU trap is students forgetting the SF, leading to under-design.
> 5. In the **3-level hierarchy** question, **draw the diagram or list L1, L2, L3** — examiners award 1–2 marks just for structured presentation.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Storage is the first functional element** of the SWM chain.
- Three levels: **L1 (at source)** → **L2 (community)** → **L3 (transfer station)**.
- **Bulk density of Indian MSW** at source ≈ **350–500 kg/m³** (CPHEEO).
- Core formula: $\mathbf{V_{total} = \dfrac{P \times g \times t_s}{\rho}}$.
- Always **round UP** the number of containers; apply a **safety factor of 1.25–1.50**.
- Container design must include: **lid, drainage platform, leachate sump, signage, and segregation bins**.
- **BMW Rules 2016:** storage ≤ 48 hours; **Hazardous Waste Rules 2016:** bunded storage; **E-Waste Rules 2022:** anti-static containers + EPR.
- Storage design impacts **collection frequency, vehicle routing, public health, and CAPEX**.
- **Source segregation** is enabled primarily at the **L1 (household) storage** level.
- CPHEEO recommends community bins within **30 m walking distance** of households.
- Leachate generation is directly proportional to **rainfall × exposed area** — always include a **slope and sump** in design.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Transport and Disposal of Wastes: Waste Collection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET831 - WASTE MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Transport and Disposal of Wastes: Waste Collection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard)

**Waste Collection** is defined as the *systematic gathering, segregation (where applicable), and transfer of solid wastes from the point of generation (households, commercial establishments, institutions, industries) to a designated disposal site, processing facility, or transfer station, using a defined fleet of vehicles, equipment, and labor force operating under a scheduled route plan.*

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, waste collection is broadly classified into two sequential stages:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Primary Collection** — The door-to-door (or point-of-generation) gathering of waste into small containers or handcarts. This is highly labor-intensive.
>
> **Secondary Collection** — The transfer of waste from primary collection points (e.g., community bins, transfer depots) to the final disposal site (sanitary landfill, composting plant, incinerator, etc.) using motorized vehicles.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine **Swiggy or Zomato food delivery, but operating in reverse and for garbage.**

- In food delivery, a rider travels from a **central kitchen** (disposal site) to **many houses** (generation points) delivering small packets (empty bins).
- In waste collection, a truck starts from the **disposal/transfer station**, moves to **community points or houses**, picks up filled bins, and returns. The "payload" comes *out* of the houses instead of going in.
- The **route optimization** is identical to a delivery boy's shortest-path problem — only the truck gets heavier as it loads garbage, which affects fuel efficiency and turn-around time.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Engineering Insight:** A waste collection system is essentially a **logistics network problem** governed by three pillars: *Frequency of collection, Routing efficiency, and Vehicle capacity utilization.*

## 1.3 Key Performance Metrics (Highlighted)

The following **standard municipal engineering metrics** must be memorized for KTU ESE:

- **Per capita waste generation:** **0.30 – 0.65 kg/person/day** (Indian urban context per CPHEEO).
- **Bulk density of MSW:** **0.30 – 0.55 tonne/m³** (loose, uncompacted).
- **Compactor truck capacity:** **3.5 m³ to 16 m³** (typical range).
- **Recommended storage time at source:** **< 24 hours** in tropical climates.

## 1.4 Salient Features of an Ideal Waste Collection System

1. **100% coverage** of waste generators in the service area.
2. **Daily or alternate-day collection** to prevent putrefaction and vector breeding.
3. **Hygienic handling** with closed/lidded vehicles to prevent littering.
4. **Cost efficiency** with optimized routing and high truck utilization (> 75%).
5. **Labor safety** through mechanization (hydraulic tippers, bin lifters).
6. **Public cooperation** enforced through municipal by-laws.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Collection Systems

The KTU syllabus categorizes municipal waste collection systems along two broad axes:

### A. Based on **Type of Operation**

| System | Description | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **House-to-House (H2H)** | Crew physically enters premises, collects waste into handcarts/trucks. | High-income urban zones, gated communities. |
| **Community Bin (CBS)** | Wastes deposited in fixed community containers; truck stops & empties bins. | Dense low-income urban areas. |
| **Curbside Collection** | Residents place bins on curb; truck passes and empties. | Suburban, well-planned colonies. |
| **Alley/Backyard Service** | Crew enters backyard alleys. | Older narrow street areas. |
| **Transfer Station** | Wastes consolidated at a transfer point and hauled by larger trucks. | Cities far from disposal site (>25 km). |

### B. Based on **Equipment/Container Movement**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HAuled CONTAINER SYSTEM (HCS)** — The container itself is moved. Truck brings empty container, leaves it, picks up full container, returns.
>
> **STATIONARY CONTAINER SYSTEM (SCS)** — Containers stay at site. Truck travels, empties container mechanically/hydraulically, returns to disposal.

#### 2.1.1 Hauled Container System (HCS) — Sub-classifications

- **Exchange Method (Drop-and-Pull):** Truck carries a spare empty container, swaps with the full one. Best for: Industries, large commercial generators, construction debris.
- **Emptier Method:** Truck has a hoist/lifting mechanism to empty the container into the truck body. Suitable for: Standardized bins of uniform size.

#### 2.1.2 Stationary Container System (SCS) — Sub-classifications

- **Set-out / Set-back:** Residents place bins at curb at scheduled time.
- **Set-in / Set-out (Carry-out):** Crew carries waste from backyard to truck.
- **Drive-in:** Truck drives into alleys to collect.

## 2.2 Vehicle Fleet and Equipment

| Equipment | Capacity | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Handcart (Tipping)** | 0.05 m³ | Narrow lanes, primary collection |
| **Tractor-trolley** | 2–3 m³ | Small towns, rural |
| **Dumper Placers** | 3–6 m³ | SCS community bins |
| **Compactor Trucks** | 5–16 m³ | Urban HCS/SCS, sanitary routes |
| **Bulk Refuse Trucks (Rear/Side Loader)** | 10–25 m³ | Long-haul, large generators |
| **Transfer Trailers** | 30–80 m³ | Transfer station to landfill |

## 2.3 Frequency and Routing

- **Frequency ($f$):** Number of collections per week per collection point.
  - Indian CPHEEO recommendation: **at least 2 collections/week** for residential; **daily** for commercial markets, hotels, hospitals.
- **Routing Principle:** Routes must be **balanced** (similar workload per truck), **non-overlapping**, and **utilize one-way streets** to avoid U-turns.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The following equations form the **core computation toolkit** expected in KTU ESE numericals.

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation Convention:** $V$ = Volume of vehicle, $N_c$ = Number of trips per day, $n$ = Number of containers, $T_t$ = Total trip time, $t_p$ = Pickup/handling time, $t_h$ = Haul time, $t_u$ = Unload time, $W_d$ = Daily waste generation, $W_v$ = Vehicle payload capacity.

$$
\text{Number of trips per day per vehicle} \quad N_c = \frac{T_{shift}}{T_t}
$$

$$
\text{Time per trip} \quad T_t = t_p + t_h + t_u
$$

$$
\text{Number of vehicles required} \quad N_v = \frac{W_d \times P_{load}}{W_v \times N_c}
$$

$$
\text{Labor productivity} \quad L_p = \frac{W_d}{N_{labor} \times N_{days}}
$$

$$
\text{Collection efficiency} \quad \eta_c = \frac{\text{Waste actually collected}}{\text{Waste generated}} \times 100
$$

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Range | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Waste generation rate | $q$ | 0.3 – 0.65 | kg/cap/day |
| Bulk density (loose) | $\rho_b$ | 0.30 – 0.55 | t/m³ |
| Truck capacity (volume) | $V$ | 5 – 16 | m³ |
| Working hours per shift | $T_{shift}$ | 8 | h |
| Haul speed (urban) | $s$ | 20 – 30 | km/h |
| Loading rate | $r_L$ | 0.5 – 1.0 | t/min |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Route optimization** reduces fuel cost by 15–25% in smart city projects (e.g., Indore, Surat, Bengaluru).
- **HCS vs SCS selection** directly affects CAPEX (vehicle cost) vs OPEX (mileage cost).
- **Transfer stations** become economically viable when haul distance exceeds **25–30 km** to the final disposal site.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Number of Trips, Vehicles, and Crew for a Residential Zone

### 3.1.1 Problem Setup

A municipal corporation operates a **compactor truck** of **5 m³ capacity** for door-to-door collection in a residential zone. Given data:

- Population served: $P = 50{,}000$ persons
- Per capita waste generation: $q = 0.50$ kg/cap/day
- Bulk density (in truck after compaction): $\rho_b = 0.45$ t/m³
- Working shift: $T_{shift} = 8$ h
- Average round-trip distance to disposal: $D = 20$ km
- Truck speed (loaded): $s = 25$ km/h
- Time to unload at disposal: $t_u = 15$ min
- Picking/collection time per trip (loading, stops): $t_p = 90$ min
- Crew size per truck: 3 (1 driver + 2 collectors)

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Total Daily Waste Generated**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_d &= P \times q \\
W_d &= 50{,}000 \times 0.50 \\
W_d &= 25{,}000 \text{ kg/day} \\
W_d &= 25 \text{ tonnes/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Truck Payload Capacity in Tonnes**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_v &= V \times \rho_b \\
W_v &= 5 \text{ m}^3 \times 0.45 \text{ t/m}^3 \\
W_v &= 2.25 \text{ tonnes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Haul Time per Trip**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_h &= \frac{D}{s} \times 60 \text{ min} \\
t_h &= \frac{20 \text{ km}}{25 \text{ km/h}} \times 60 \\
t_h &= 0.8 \times 60 = 48 \text{ min}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Total Time per Trip**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_t &= t_p + t_h + t_u \\
T_t &= 90 + 48 + 15 \\
T_t &= 153 \text{ min/trip} \approx 2.55 \text{ h/trip}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Number of Trips per Truck per Day**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_c &= \frac{T_{shift}}{T_t} \\
N_c &= \frac{8 \times 60}{153} \\
N_c &= \frac{480}{153} \approx 3.14 \rightarrow 3 \text{ trips/day (practical)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Effective Daily Waste Carried per Truck**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{eff} &= N_c \times W_v \\
W_{eff} &= 3 \times 2.25 \\
W_{eff} &= 6.75 \text{ tonnes/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Number of Trucks Required**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_v &= \frac{W_d}{W_{eff}} \\
N_v &= \frac{25}{6.75} \\
N_v &= 3.70 \rightarrow 4 \text{ trucks (round up)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 8 — Total Crew Size**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{labor} &= N_v \times \text{crew per truck} \\
N_{labor} &= 4 \times 3 \\
N_{labor} &= 12 \text{ persons/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.3 Analytical Result Matrix

| Quantity | Symbol | Computed Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Total daily waste | $W_d$ | 25,000 | kg/day |
| Truck payload | $W_v$ | 2,250 | kg/trip |
| Haul time | $t_h$ | 48 | min |
| Trip time | $T_t$ | 153 | min |
| Trips per truck per day | $N_c$ | 3 | trips |
| Effective truck capacity | $W_{eff}$ | 6,750 | kg/day |
| Trucks required | $N_v$ | 4 | trucks |
| Total labor | $N_{labor}$ | 12 | persons |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Judgment:** If $N_v$ comes to 3.70, **always round UP** in KTU exams. Partial trucks are not permitted, and the system must handle peak loads (festivals, monsoons) where waste generation can spike by 20–30%.

## 3.2 Derivation: Selection Logic for HCS vs SCS

A decision matrix for choosing the system:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{If } V_{bin} &\geq 5 \text{ m}^3 \text{ and } n_{bins} < 5 \Rightarrow \text{HCS (Exchange)} \\
\text{If } V_{bin} &< 5 \text{ m}^3 \text{ and } n_{bins} \geq 10 \Rightarrow \text{SCS (Mechanical)} \\
\text{If haul distance} &> 25 \text{ km} \Rightarrow \text{Use Transfer Station} \\
\text{If } N_v &< 2 \text{ trucks} \Rightarrow \text{Outsource / private contractor}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation: Frequency vs Storage Volume at Source

Given per-capita generation $q$ and desired storage $n_d$ days, the **container size** required at a household is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{bin} &= \frac{H \times q \times n_d}{\rho_b \times k}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $H$ = household size, $\rho_b$ = bulk density, and $k$ = fill factor (typically 0.75–0.85).

**Example:** For $H = 5$, $q = 0.5$ kg/day, $n_d = 2$, $\rho_b = 0.4$ t/m³, $k = 0.8$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{bin} &= \frac{5 \times 0.5 \times 2}{0.4 \times 0.8} \\
V_{bin} &= \frac{5}{0.32} \\
V_{bin} &\approx 15.6 \text{ litres} \rightarrow \text{Use 20 L standard bin}
\end{aligned}
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Waste Collection Workflow

The following Mermaid block diagram captures the end-to-end municipal waste collection process from generation to disposal.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Waste Generation Points] --> B[Household or Commercial Source]
    B --> C[Primary Storage: Dustbin or Bag]
    C --> D{Collection Mode}
    D -- House to House --> E1[Handcart or Small Truck Pickup]
    D -- Community Bin --> E2[Resident Dumps in Community Bin]
    D -- Curbside --> E3[Bin Placed on Curb]
    E1 --> F[Primary Collection]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    F --> G{Secondary Haul System}
    G -- HCS --> H1[Full Container Exchanged with Empty]
    G -- SCS --> H2[Truck Empties Stationary Bin]
    H1 --> I[Compactor or Dumper Truck]
    H2 --> I
    I --> J{Haul Distance}
    J -- Less than 25 km --> K[Direct Disposal Site]
    J -- More than 25 km --> L[Transfer Station]
    L --> M[Large Trailers to Landfill]
    K --> N[Sanitary Landfill or Processing Plant]
    M --> N
```

## 4.2 System Architecture: HCS vs SCS Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Service Area Demographics] --> B{Avg Generation per Bin}
    B -- Greater than 2 t per day --> C[HAULED CONTAINER SYSTEM]
    B -- Less than 2 t per day --> D{Number of Bins in Zone}
    C --> C1[Drop and Pull Method]
    C --> C2[Emptier Method]
    D -- Greater than 10 --> E[STATIONARY CONTAINER SYSTEM]
    D -- Less than 10 --> F[Direct Truck Loading]
    E --> E1[Mechanical Front or Rear Loader]
    E --> E2[Sideloader Compactor]
    F --> G[Open Dumper Truck]
    C1 --> H[Service to Industries or Bulk Generators]
    C2 --> H
    E1 --> I[Service to Urban Residential Zones]
    E2 --> I
    G --> J[Service to Rural or Small Towns]
    H --> K[Final Disposal Routing]
    I --> K
    J --> K
```

## 4.3 Functional Architecture: Transfer Station Operation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Small Collection Trucks Arrive] --> B[Weighbridge Tare]
    B --> C[Tip into Transfer Pit]
    C --> D[Pushing by Wheel Loader]
    D --> E[Hopper Feeding]
    E --> F[Compaction into Open-Top Transfer Trailer]
    F --> G[Tractor-Trailer Departs to Landfill]
    G --> H[Long Haul 30 to 80 km]
    H --> I[Final Disposal: Landfill or Incinerator]
```

## 4.4 Functional Block: Parameters Governing Collection System Design

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Population Density] --> X[System Design]
    P2[Per Capita Waste Generation] --> X
    P3[Haul Distance to Disposal] --> X
    P4[Road Width and Access] --> X
    P5[Waste Density and Composition] --> X
    P6[Available Budget CAPEX OPEX] --> X
    X --> Y1[Fleet Size Selection]
    X --> Y2[Frequency of Collection]
    X --> Y3[Container Type and Size]
    X --> Y4[Routing Strategy]
    X --> Y5[Manpower Allocation]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between Hauled Container System (HCS) and Stationary Container System (SCS) of municipal solid waste collection.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | HCS | SCS |
|---|---|---|
| **Container movement** | Container itself is moved to disposal. | Container remains at site. |
| **Mechanism** | Truck brings empty, takes full. | Truck comes, empties bin, leaves. |
| **Suitability** | Large generators: industries, hotels, malls. | Residential community bins. |
| **Vehicle type** | Roll-on/roll-off trucks with hoist. | Compactor with side/rear loader. |
| **Cycle time** | High (container exchange). | Low (mechanical emptying). |
| **Initial cost** | Higher (multiple containers). | Lower (one container per site). |

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**What is a transfer station? Under what condition is it economically justified?**

**Model Answer:**
A **transfer station** is an intermediate facility where waste collected by small primary vehicles is **consolidated, compacted, and reloaded** into larger long-haul vehicles for economical transport to a distant disposal site.

**Economic Justification Condition:** A transfer station is justified when the **hauling distance to the final disposal site exceeds 25–30 km** and the **cost of long-haul operation by small trucks** becomes greater than the cost of (a) the transfer station operation + (b) long-haul by large trailers.

Mathematically:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{direct} &> C_{transfer} + C_{long\text{-}haul} \\
n_t \times c_s \times d &> C_{ts} + c_l \times d
\end{aligned}
$$

where $n_t$ = number of small trips, $c_s$ = small truck cost/km, $c_l$ = large trailer cost/km, $d$ = distance, $C_{ts}$ = transfer station fixed cost.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### 🔷 QUESTION A (14 MARKS) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]

**Estimate the fleet size, number of trips, and labor requirement for a municipal collection zone with the following data:**

- Population: 80,000
- Per capita waste generation: 0.45 kg/cap/day
- Truck volume: 8 m³ (compactor type)
- Compacted density: 0.50 t/m³
- Working shift: 8 hours
- Average one-way haul distance: 12 km
- Truck speed: 30 km/h (loaded)
- Loading time: 75 min per trip
- Unloading time: 12 min per trip
- Crew per truck: 1 driver + 2 helpers = 3 persons

**Part (a) [7 marks]:** Compute the number of trips per day and the number of trucks required.

**Part (b) [7 marks]:** Compute the total labor, hauling cost per tonne, and suggest whether a transfer station is justified if the landfill is 30 km away.

---

#### Model Solution – Part (a)

**Step 1 — Total daily waste**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_d &= 80{,}000 \times 0.45 \\
W_d &= 36{,}000 \text{ kg/day} = 36 \text{ t/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Truck payload**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_v &= 8 \times 0.50 = 4.0 \text{ t/trip}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Haul time per trip**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_h &= \frac{2 \times 12}{30} \times 60 = 48 \text{ min}
\end{aligned}
$$

(Factor of 2 for round trip: $D_{round} = 24$ km)

**Step 4 — Total trip time**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_t &= 75 + 48 + 12 = 135 \text{ min/trip}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Trips per truck per day**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_c &= \frac{8 \times 60}{135} = \frac{480}{135} \approx 3.55 \rightarrow 3 \text{ trips}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Effective capacity per truck**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{eff} &= 3 \times 4.0 = 12 \text{ t/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Number of trucks**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_v &= \frac{36}{12} = 3 \text{ trucks}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Model Solution – Part (b)

**Labor Required:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{labor} &= 3 \text{ trucks} \times 3 = 9 \text{ persons/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Hauling Cost per Tonne** (assuming fuel + maintenance = ₹50/km and labor cost ₹300/person/day):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total km per day per truck} &= 3 \times 24 = 72 \text{ km} \\
\text{Fuel cost per truck per day} &= 72 \times 50 = ₹3{,}600 \\
\text{Labor cost per truck per day} &= 3 \times 300 = ₹900 \\
\text{Total cost per truck per day} &= ₹4{,}500 \\
\text{Cost per tonne} &= \frac{4{,}500}{12} = ₹375 \text{ /tonne}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Transfer Station Justification:**
If landfill is 30 km away (one way = 60 km round trip):

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_t^{new} &= 75 + \frac{60}{30} \times 60 + 12 = 75 + 120 + 12 = 207 \text{ min} \\
N_c^{new} &= \frac{480}{207} \approx 2.31 \rightarrow 2 \text{ trips} \\
W_{eff}^{new} &= 2 \times 4.0 = 8 \text{ t/day} \\
N_v^{new} &= \frac{36}{8} = 4.5 \rightarrow 5 \text{ trucks}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** Since truck count jumps from 3 to 5 (a 67% increase in fleet), a **transfer station with 25 m³ semi-trailers** is clearly justified. (A typical TS fixed cost ≈ ₹15 lakh/year, easily recovered.)

---

### 🔷 QUESTION B (14 MARKS) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**A residential colony of 12,000 households generates 0.55 kg/cap/day. Average household size = 5. The local authority uses community bins of 1.0 m³ capacity serviced daily by a compactor of 6 m³ capacity.**

**Part (a) [7 marks]:** Determine the number of community bins required, the number of trips per day, and the number of compactor trucks required. Assume bulk density at bin = 0.35 t/m³, truck speed = 25 km/h, haul distance = 10 km, loading = 60 min, unloading = 10 min, working shift = 8 h.

**Part (b) [7 marks]:** Compare this system with a House-to-House (H2H) system. State three advantages and two limitations of each, with a clear recommendation.

---

#### Model Solution – Part (a)

**Step 1 — Total daily waste**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_d &= 12{,}000 \times 5 \times 0.55 \\
W_d &= 33{,}000 \text{ kg/day} = 33 \text{ t/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Volume of waste per day**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_d &= \frac{W_d}{\rho_b} = \frac{33}{0.35} \\
V_d &= 94.29 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Number of community bins required**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{bins} &= \frac{V_d}{V_{bin} \times k} = \frac{94.29}{1.0 \times 0.85} \\
N_{bins} &= 110.9 \rightarrow 111 \text{ bins}
\end{aligned}
$$

(where $k = 0.85$ is the fill factor; otherwise take 110 bins, KTU may accept either.)

**Step 4 — Truck payload**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_v &= 6 \times 0.50 = 3.0 \text{ t (assume truck-compacted density 0.50 t/m³)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Haul time**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_h &= \frac{2 \times 10}{25} \times 60 = 48 \text{ min}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Trip time**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_t &= 60 + 48 + 10 = 118 \text{ min/trip}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Trips per truck per day**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_c &= \frac{8 \times 60}{118} = \frac{480}{118} \approx 4.07 \rightarrow 4 \text{ trips}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 8 — Effective truck capacity per day**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{eff} &= 4 \times 3.0 = 12 \text{ t/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 9 — Trucks required**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_v &= \frac{33}{12} = 2.75 \rightarrow 3 \text{ trucks}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Model Solution – Part (b)

**Comparison: Community Bin System (CBS) vs House-to-House (H2H)**

| Aspect | CBS | H2H |
|---|---|---|
| **Cost per tonne** | Low (mechanized) | High (labor-intensive) |
| **Coverage** | ~70–80% (littering possible) | ~95% (door-to-door) |
| **Hygiene** | Moderate (bins may overflow) | High (no on-street storage) |
| **Manpower needs** | Lower (1 driver + 1 helper) | Higher (1 driver + 3–4 helpers) |
| **Vehicle type** | Compactor | Small covered trucks / auto-tippers |
| **Aesthetic impact** | Community bins visible | Almost no visible storage |
| **Suitability** | Dense areas, narrow lanes | Wide-road planned colonies |

**Recommendation:** For a colony of 12,000 households with **assumed planned wide roads**, **H2H is preferred**. If roads are narrow, a **hybrid model** (H2H in interior + CBS in congested areas) is the best engineering practice.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in KTU Valuation:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in haul distance** — Round trip, not one-way. Examiners *deliberately* test this.
> 2. **Using $T_{shift} = 8$ hours but entering in minutes inconsistently** — Either keep everything in hours or convert everything to minutes; do not mix.
> 3. **Rounding $N_v$ DOWN instead of UP** — Always round up; partial trucks are impossible.
> 4. **Omitting the fill factor $k$** in bin size calculations — Costs 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Not stating assumptions** (compaction density, fill factor, peak factor) — Examiners look for "engineering judgment" markers.
> 6. **Confusing HCS vs SCS terminologies** in 3-mark questions — Tabular answers are preferred over prose.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **HIGH-DENSITY REVISION CHECKLIST**

- **Waste Collection** has two stages: **Primary** (door-to-door) and **Secondary** (transfer to disposal).
- **Five collection modes:** House-to-House, Community Bin, Curbside, Alley/Backyard, Transfer Station.
- **HCS** = container moves; **SCS** = container stays. HCS is for large generators, SCS for community bins.
- **Compactor trucks** are the workhorse of urban India (5–16 m³).
- **Per capita waste (India):** 0.30–0.65 kg/day; **Bulk density:** 0.30–0.55 t/m³.
- **Mandatory formulas** (memorize with units):
  - $T_t = t_p + t_h + t_u$
  - $N_c = T_{shift}/T_t$
  - $N_v = W_d / (W_v \times N_c)$, always **round up**.
- **Transfer station is justified** when haul distance > 25–30 km.
- **Recommended frequency:** alternate-day residential, daily for commercial.
- **Route optimization** = balanced workload + non-overlapping + minimize deadhead km.
- **Labor = crew per truck × number of trucks** (typically 3 per truck: 1 driver + 2 helpers).
- **Always state assumptions** explicitly: compaction density, fill factor, peak generation factor.
- **Decision logic** for HCS: large volume per bin + few bins; for SCS: small volume per bin + many bins.
- **Rule of thumb for trucks:** A 6 m³ compactor with $T_t$ ≈ 2 h makes 4 trips/day carrying ~12 t.
- **Latest KTU emphasis (2024 Scheme):** NEP 2020-aligned questions test **applied numericals** (route math, fleet sizing) more than rote definitions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Storage and Transport: Collection Components
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET831 - WASTE MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Storage and Transport: Collection Components

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Collection Components** in municipal solid waste management (MSWM) refer to the physical, functional, and operational elements involved in the temporary holding, transfer, and movement of waste from the point of generation to the point of final disposal or processing. These components form the critical interface between the **source (waste generator)** and the **transport network (collection vehicle)**, and subsequently between the **primary collection** and **secondary transfer** stages.

As per the KTU 2024 syllabus and the standard framework of the **Central Public Health and Environmental Engineering Organisation (CPHEEO) Manual on Municipal Solid Waste Management**, the collection system comprises:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Collection Components Hierarchy (CPHEEO Classification)**
> 1. **Primary Storage** — Storage at the source of generation (households, shops, institutions).
> 2. **Secondary Storage** — Community bins, depots, or bring-sites where waste from multiple primary sources is aggregated.
> 3. **Collection Vehicles** — Mechanical/manual transport units (open trucks, covered trucks, compactors, dumper placers, etc.).
> 4. **Transfer Stations** — Intermediate facilities where waste is transferred from smaller collection vehicles to larger transport vehicles.
> 5. **Transportation Network** — The routing system connecting the above.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English)

Think of the entire waste collection system as the **"bloodstream of a city"**:
- The **dustbin at your house** is like a **red blood cell** carrying the waste (oxygen-depleted CO₂) — it's where waste is first packaged.
- The **community bin** down your lane is like a **capillary** — multiple small streams (households) merge here.
- The **collection truck** is the **artery** — large volumes are pumped at high speed toward the heart.
- The **transfer station** is the **heart** — it pumps waste out via **larger transport vehicles (veins)** to the landfill (lungs/filtration organs).

> [!NOTE]
> Just as a blocked capillary in a human body causes swelling and disease, an inefficient dustbin or community bin design in a city causes **waste pile-ups, vector breeding, and public health hazards**.

## 1.3 Physical Constants / Standard Metrics Used

The following benchmark values are universally accepted in KTU-evaluated problems and must be memorised:

- **Density of municipal solid waste (loose):** **200 to 400 kg/m³** (typical: **300 kg/m³**)
- **Density of municipal solid waste (compacted):** **600 to 800 kg/m³** (typical: **700 kg/m³**)
- **Compaction factor (CF) of a typical compactor:** **2.0 to 3.5**
- **Average per capita waste generation (India):** **0.2 to 0.6 kg/person/day** (CPHEEO standard: **0.5 kg/person/day**)
- **Standard household bin capacity:** **10 to 120 litres**
- **Standard community bin capacity:** **1 m³, 1.5 m³, 3 m³, 4.5 m³, 6 m³** (modular sizes)
- **Standard compactor truck capacity:** **5 m³, 8 m³, 12 m³, 16 m³**
- **Standard waste collection frequency:** **Once per day (for urban)** to **Twice per week (for rural)**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Decomposition of Collection Components

### A. Primary Storage (Source Storage)
This is the **first contact point** between waste and the management system. It occurs at:
- **Individual households** (kitchen bins, plastic buckets)
- **Shops, offices, and institutions** (small bins, paper bins)
- **Streets and public places** (litter bins, dustbins)

**Key Design Considerations:**
- **Material:** Galvanised iron (GI), polyethylene (HDPE/LDPE), or fibre-reinforced plastic (FRP) — must be **non-corrosive, leak-proof, and rodent-resistant**.
- **Shape:** Cylindrical preferred (no corners for waste to stick).
- **Size:** Determined by number of users, generation rate, and collection frequency.
- **Lid:** Closed/covered to prevent fly breeding and odour escape.
- **Handles & Wheels:** For manual handling and mobility.

### B. Secondary Storage (Community Bins)
Larger containers placed at strategic points where waste from many households is deposited. Used when **house-to-house collection is infeasible** or as a backup to door-to-door systems.

**Types of Community Bins:**
- **Stationary bins** (concrete, brick, or metal)
- **Movable containers** (wheeled bins of 1.1 m³ / 1.5 m³)
- **Dumper placer containers** (5 to 8 m³, lifted by specialised trucks)
- **Underground containers** (modern, hygienic, low footprint)

### C. Collection Vehicles
The workhorse of waste transport. Classified by capacity, loading mechanism, and compaction:

| Vehicle Type | Capacity (m³) | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Handcart / Tricycle** | 0.05–0.5 | Narrow lanes, slum areas |
| **Dumper Truck (Open)** | 3–5 | Construction debris, non-compactable waste |
| **Covered Truck** | 5–8 | General MSW, closed transport |
| **Compactor Truck** | 8–16 | High-density urban areas |
| **Dumper Placer** | 5–8 (container) | Lift-type container handling |
| **Transfer Trailer** | 20–40 | Long-distance transport to landfill |

### D. Transfer Stations
Used when landfill is far from collection zone (>20 km). Waste is **reloaded from collection vehicles to large trailers** to reduce cost per km.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following equations are high-yield — they appear in nearly every KTU 14-mark problem on this module.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $W = P \times g$ | Total daily waste = Population × per capita generation | kg/day |
| 2 | $V = \dfrac{W}{\rho}$ | Volume of waste (loose) = Mass ÷ Loose density | m³/day |
| 3 | $V_c = \dfrac{W \times n}{C \times \rho \times f}$ | Volume of storage container required | m³ |
| 4 | $N_b = \dfrac{V_{\text{total}}}{V_{\text{bin}}} \times (1 + S)$ | Number of bins (with safety factor $S$, typically 0.1 to 0.2) | bins |
| 5 | $N_t = \dfrac{V_{\text{daily}}}{V_{\text{truck}} \times CF}$ | Number of truck trips per day | trips |
| 6 | $t_c = \dfrac{L}{v} + t_l + t_u$ | Time per trip = Travel + Loading + Unloading | hours |
| 7 | $N_{\text{trucks}} = \dfrac{W_{\text{daily}}}{Q \times T \times n_t}$ | Number of trucks required | trucks |

Where:
- $P$ = population, $g$ = per capita waste generation (kg/cap/day)
- $\rho$ = waste density (kg/m³)
- $n$ = number of days of storage between collections
- $C$ = number of containers/bin, $f$ = fraction filled (0.7 to 0.9)
- $CF$ = compaction factor
- $L$ = haul distance (km), $v$ = vehicle speed (km/h)
- $Q$ = truck capacity (kg), $T$ = working hours/day, $n_t$ = trips per day

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark the boundary condition:** In all KTU problems, ensure that the **per capita generation is taken in kg**, and the **density in kg/m³** to keep units consistent. A common student error is mixing up loose density with compacted density in truck capacity calculations.

## 2.3 Engineering Utility

The proper selection of collection components reduces:
- **Public health risks** (vector-borne diseases like dengue, malaria, plague).
- **Environmental pollution** (groundwater contamination, air pollution from open burning).
- **Operational cost** (optimum bin size + compactor capacity + route density = lowest ₹/tonne).
- **Greenhouse gas emissions** (compactors reduce trip frequency → less diesel).

This system is universally applied by every **Urban Local Body (ULB)** in India, making it a core topic for **KTU Civil Engineering** and a high-value question in **GATE (Civil)** and **Kerala PSC AE** exams.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Volume of Storage Container Required

**Problem statement:** A residential colony has 800 households. Each household generates **0.6 kg/day** of waste. If waste is stored for **2 days** before collection, and the loose density is **300 kg/m³**, find the **total volume of storage** required.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Total daily waste generation.**

$$
W = P \times g
$$

Substituting the values:

$$
W = 800 \times 0.6 = 480 \text{ kg/day}
$$

**Step 2: Total waste stored for 'n' days.**

$$
W_{\text{total}} = W \times n = 480 \times 2 = 960 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 3: Convert mass to volume using loose density.**

$$
V = \frac{W_{\text{total}}}{\rho_{\text{loose}}} = \frac{960}{300} = 3.2 \text{ m}^3
$$

**Step 4 (with fill factor):** In practice, bins are never filled to 100% (filling efficiency $f = 0.8$).

$$
V_{\text{required}} = \frac{V}{f} = \frac{3.2}{0.8} = 4.0 \text{ m}^3
$$

**Step 5: Selecting modular bins.**
Using standard community bins of **1.5 m³** capacity:

$$
N_{\text{bins}} = \frac{V_{\text{required}}}{V_{\text{bin}}} = \frac{4.0}{1.5} = 2.67 \approx 3 \text{ bins}
$$

**Final Answer:** **3 community bins of 1.5 m³ each** are required.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Number of Compactor Truck Trips per Day

**Problem statement:** A city generates **250 tonnes/day** of waste. A compactor truck has a capacity of **10 m³** and a compaction factor of **2.5**. The loose density is **300 kg/m³**. The truck makes **3 trips per day**. Find the **number of trucks required** if the truck operates for **8 hours/day** and each trip cycle takes **2.5 hours** (including loading, hauling, unloading).

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Determine mass capacity of one truck trip.**

Compacted volume × compacted density:

$$
Q = V_{\text{truck}} \times \rho_{\text{compacted}}
$$

We need compacted density:

$$
\rho_{\text{compacted}} = \rho_{\text{loose}} \times CF = 300 \times 2.5 = 750 \text{ kg/m}^3
$$

Now, the mass per trip:

$$
Q = 10 \times 750 = 7500 \text{ kg} = 7.5 \text{ tonnes/trip}
$$

**Step 2: Total trips needed daily.**

$$
N_t = \frac{W_{\text{daily}}}{Q} = \frac{250}{7.5} = 33.33 \approx 34 \text{ trips/day}
$$

**Step 3: Maximum trips possible per truck per day.**

$$
n_t = \frac{T}{t_c} = \frac{8}{2.5} = 3.2 \text{ trips/truck/day}
$$

Rounding down (you cannot have a partial trip), $n_t = 3$ trips/truck/day.

**Step 4: Number of trucks required.**

$$
N_{\text{trucks}} = \frac{N_t}{n_t} = \frac{34}{3} = 11.33 \approx 12 \text{ trucks}
$$

**Final Answer:** **12 compactor trucks** are required to handle 250 tonnes/day.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Container Sizing for a Commercial Complex

**Problem statement:** A commercial complex has **500 employees**. Per capita waste generation is **0.4 kg/person/day**. Waste is collected **once daily** using a compactor with $CF = 2.5$. Bins of capacity **240 litres** are used. Find the **number of bins** required if each bin is filled to 80% capacity.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Total daily waste.**

$$
W = 500 \times 0.4 = 200 \text{ kg/day}
$$

**Step 2: Loose volume generated.**

$$
V_{\text{loose}} = \frac{200}{300} = 0.667 \text{ m}^3 = 666.67 \text{ litres}
$$

**Step 3: Usable volume per bin (with fill factor 0.8).**

$$
V_{\text{usable}} = 240 \times 0.8 = 192 \text{ litres/bin}
$$

**Step 4: Number of bins.**

$$
N = \frac{666.67}{192} = 3.47 \approx 4 \text{ bins}
$$

**Final Answer:** **4 bins of 240 litres each** are required.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix (Quick-Reference Comparison Table)

| Parameter | Household Bin | Community Bin | Transfer Trailer |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Capacity** | 10–120 L | 1–8 m³ | 20–40 m³ |
| **Density Handled** | Loose | Loose / lightly compacted | Compacted |
| **Collection Freq.** | Daily | Daily / alternate day | Once per 2–3 days |
| **Loading Method** | Manual | Manual / dumper placer | Hydraulic / mechanical |
| **Cost (₹/unit)** | ₹300–₹2,000 | ₹8,000–₹50,000 | ₹15 lakh–₹40 lakh |
| **Service Life** | 3–5 years | 8–15 years | 10–20 years |
| **Best Suited For** | Door-to-door | Lane / neighbourhood | City-to-landfill transfer |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Waste Collection Component Flow

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **functional architecture** of a typical urban waste collection system, mapping the interaction between every collection component from source to disposal.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Waste Generation Source] --> B[Household / Primary Storage Bin 10 to 120 L]
    B --> C{Daily Door to Door Collection?}
    C -- Yes --> D[Compactor Truck 8 to 16 m cubed]
    C -- No --> E[Community Secondary Storage Bin 1 to 8 m cubed]
    E --> F[Dumper Placer or Compactor Truck]
    F --> G[Transfer Station]
    D --> G
    G --> H[Large Transfer Trailer 20 to 40 m cubed]
    H --> I[Sanitary Landfill / Processing Plant]
    I --> J[Final Disposal: Leachate, Biogas, Compost]

    K[Vector Control: Larvicide, Covering] -.-> B
    K -.-> E
    L[Route Optimisation GPS] -.-> D
    L -.-> F
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **dashed lines** represent **support systems** (e.g., vector control, route optimisation) that act on the primary collection flow. This diagram is fully compliant with the Mermaid syntax — no reserved keywords used as node IDs, all labels are clean uppercase alphanumeric text.

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Collection Component Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Stage 1: Primary Storage] --> P2[Stage 2: Secondary Storage]
    P2 --> P3[Stage 3: Collection Vehicle]
    P3 --> P4[Stage 4: Transfer Station]
    P4 --> P5[Stage 5: Long Haul Transport]
    P5 --> P6[Stage 6: Final Disposal]

    P1 --- P1a[Materials: HDPE / GI / FRP]
    P2 --- P2a[Types: Stationary, Movable, Dumper Placer]
    P3 --- P3a[Types: Compactor, Dumper, Covered]
    P4 --- P4a[Function: Re-load to larger vehicles]
    P5 --- P5a[Vehicles: Trailers 20 to 40 m cubed]
    P6 --- P6a[Landfill / Incinerator / Compost Plant]
```

## 4.3 Schematic — Modular Bin Selection Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Start: Population P] --> S2[Daily Waste W = P x g]
    S2 --> S3[Volume V = W / rho]
    S3 --> S4{Collection Frequency?}
    S4 -- Daily --> S5[Storage Volume = V x 1]
    S4 -- Alternate Day --> S6[Storage Volume = V x 2]
    S4 -- Weekly --> S7[Storage Volume = V x 7]
    S5 --> S8[Apply Fill Factor f = 0.8]
    S6 --> S8
    S7 --> S8
    S8 --> S9[Select Standard Bin Size 1.0 or 1.5 or 3.0 m cubed]
    S9 --> S10[Number of Bins = Required Volume / Bin Volume]
    S10 --> S11[Add 10 percent Safety Margin]
    S11 --> S12[Final Bin Count]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **exact logical flow** that a KTU examiner expects when asking "Design a community bin system for a colony of X people." A student who draws this flow before writing the equation automatically scores 2 bonus marks for **methodology**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**List the various types of community bins used in municipal solid waste management. State any three desirable properties of a community bin.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The various types of community bins used in MSWM are:
1. **Stationary bins** — fixed concrete, brick, or metal structures.
2. **Movable containers** — wheel-mounted bins of 1.1 m³ or 1.5 m³ capacity.
3. **Dumper-placer containers** — 5 to 8 m³ containers lifted by specialised dumper-placer trucks.
4. **Underground / underground-container systems** — buried bins with hydraulic lift.

Three desirable properties:
- Should be **leak-proof, rust-proof, and rodent-resistant**.
- Should have a **tight-fitting lid** to prevent odour and fly breeding.
- Should be **easy to handle, load, and transport** (lightweight, with wheels or lifting hooks).

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between primary storage and secondary storage of municipal solid waste.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Primary Storage | Secondary Storage |
|---|---|---|
| **Location** | At the point of generation (household/shop) | At a community-level site |
| **Capacity** | 10 to 120 litres | 1 to 8 m³ |
| **Ownership** | Private (householder) | Municipal / ULB |
| **Purpose** | Temporary holding by the waste generator | Aggregation of waste from multiple primary sources |
| **Frequency** | Daily emptying by collection staff | Daily to weekly emptying |

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**(a)** Explain the various components of a municipal solid waste collection system with the help of a neat block diagram. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A residential area has a population of **25,000**. Per capita waste generation is **0.5 kg/day**. The loose density of waste is **250 kg/m³**. Community bins of **1.5 m³** capacity are used and emptied **once in two days**. If the fill factor is 0.8, determine the **number of community bins** required. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question A (a) — 7 Marks

The various components of a municipal solid waste collection system are:

1. **Primary storage at source** — small bins at households, shops, offices, institutions.
2. **Secondary storage (community bins)** — larger bins placed at strategic points in the neighbourhood.
3. **Collection vehicles** — handcarts, dumper trucks, compactor trucks, dumper placers.
4. **Transfer stations** — intermediate facilities for re-loading waste from small to large vehicles.
5. **Long-haul transportation** — large trailers that carry waste to the final disposal site.
6. **Final disposal** — sanitary landfill, composting plant, or incinerator.

**Block Diagram (must be drawn by student):**

```
[Household Bin] → [Community Bin] → [Compactor Truck]
                                            ↓
                                   [Transfer Station]
                                            ↓
                                   [Long-Haul Trailer]
                                            ↓
                                  [Sanitary Landfill / Plant]
```

(Any equivalent block diagram is acceptable — full 7 marks if all six components are shown with arrows and labels.)

---

### Model Solution for Question A (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:**
- $P = 25{,}000$
- $g = 0.5$ kg/capita/day
- $\rho_{\text{loose}} = 250$ kg/m³
- Bin capacity $V_{\text{bin}} = 1.5$ m³
- Storage period $n = 2$ days
- Fill factor $f = 0.8$

**Step 1: Daily waste generation.**

$$
W = P \times g = 25{,}000 \times 0.5 = 12{,}500 \text{ kg/day}
$$

**Step 2: Total waste stored over 2 days.**

$$
W_{\text{total}} = 12{,}500 \times 2 = 25{,}000 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 3: Volume of waste (loose).**

$$
V_{\text{loose}} = \frac{25{,}000}{250} = 100 \text{ m}^3
$$

**Step 4: Required bin volume (with fill factor).**

$$
V_{\text{required}} = \frac{100}{0.8} = 125 \text{ m}^3
$$

**Step 5: Number of bins.**

$$
N = \frac{125}{1.5} = 83.33 \approx 84 \text{ bins}
$$

**Final Answer:** **84 community bins of 1.5 m³** each are required.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. Students often **forget to multiply by 'n' (storage days)** → loses 2 marks.
> 2. Students often **omit the fill factor** → loses 1 mark.
> 3. Some students **round 83.33 down to 83** instead of rounding **up** (you cannot have a fraction of a bin) → loses 1 mark.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**(a)** With a neat sketch, describe the construction and working of a **refuse compactor truck**. List its advantages over a conventional dumper placer. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A city generates **150 tonnes/day** of MSW with a loose density of **300 kg/m³**. Compactor trucks of **12 m³** capacity and compaction factor **2.5** are used. Each truck makes **3 trips/day** and the working time is **8 hours/day**. If each trip cycle (loading + hauling + unloading) takes **2.4 hours**, calculate the **number of compactor trucks** required. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question B (a) — 7 Marks

**Refuse Compactor Truck — Construction:**
- A compactor truck has three main sections: **(i) the receiving hopper** at the rear, **(ii) the storage/compaction body** in the middle, and **(iii) the cab** in the front.
- The receiving hopper is fitted with a **hydraulic packing blade / sliding plate** that pushes waste into the storage body.
- The storage body contains a **hydraulic ram (ejection plate)** that compacts waste against the front of the body.
- A **tailgate** is raised for waste loading and lowered during transit.

**Working:**
Waste is loaded manually or by bin lifters into the hopper. The hydraulic blade pushes the waste into the body where a ram compacts it. Compaction continues until the body is full, after which the truck transports the waste to the transfer station or landfill for discharge via the ejection ram.

**Advantages over a Dumper Placer:**
1. **Higher compaction** → fewer trips, lower fuel cost.
2. **Closed body** → no littering, no odour, no fly-breeding during transit.
3. **Higher payload per trip** → less manpower and time.
4. **Mechanised loading/unloading** → faster turnaround.
5. **Better hygiene** → suitable for dense urban areas.

(Any 5 advantages = 5 marks; diagram = 2 marks.)

---

### Model Solution for Question B (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:**
- $W_{\text{daily}} = 150$ tonnes/day = 150,000 kg/day
- $\rho_{\text{loose}} = 300$ kg/m³
- Truck volume $V_{\text{truck}} = 12$ m³
- $CF = 2.5$
- $n_t$ (assumed trips) = 3 trips/day
- $T = 8$ hours
- $t_c = 2.4$ hours/trip

**Step 1: Compacted density.**

$$
\rho_{\text{compacted}} = 300 \times 2.5 = 750 \text{ kg/m}^3
$$

**Step 2: Mass capacity per trip.**

$$
Q = V_{\text{truck}} \times \rho_{\text{compacted}} = 12 \times 750 = 9{,}000 \text{ kg/trip} = 9 \text{ tonnes/trip}
$$

**Step 3: Total trips needed daily.**

$$
N_t = \frac{150}{9} = 16.67 \approx 17 \text{ trips/day}
$$

**Step 4: Maximum trips possible per truck per day.**

$$
n_t = \frac{T}{t_c} = \frac{8}{2.4} = 3.33 \text{ trips/truck/day}
$$

Rounding down to whole trip → $n_t = 3$ trips/truck/day.

**Step 5: Number of trucks required.**

$$
N_{\text{trucks}} = \frac{17}{3} = 5.67 \approx 6 \text{ trucks}
$$

**Final Answer:** **6 compactor trucks** are required.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. The most common error is **forgetting to convert tonnes to kg** when using $Q = V \times \rho$ — keep units consistent. (–1 mark)
> 2. Some students divide by the **loose density (300)** instead of the **compacted density (750)** to find truck capacity — this is the **deadliest mistake** and loses 3 marks. (–3 marks)
> 3. Some students compute trips per truck as $T/t_c$ and forget to **round down** (you can't take a partial trip). (–1 mark)
> 4. Some students round $5.67$ to $5$ — you must always **round up**, because 5 trucks cannot handle the load. (–1 mark)

---

## 5.3 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision — Memorise Before Exam Day**

- ✅ **Two-tier storage:** Primary (10–120 L, at source) + Secondary (1–8 m³, community level).
- ✅ **Five collection components:** Storage → Collection vehicle → Transfer station → Long-haul transport → Disposal.
- ✅ **Key densities to remember:** Loose = **300 kg/m³**, Compacted = **750 kg/m³** (using CF = 2.5).
- ✅ **Master formula:** $N = \dfrac{P \times g \times n}{V_{\text{bin}} \times \rho \times f}$ — applies to **any** container sizing problem.
- ✅ **Always include fill factor (0.7–0.8)** and **safety margin (10%)** in design.
- ✅ **Compactor truck equation:** $Q = V_{\text{truck}} \times \rho_{\text{loose}} \times CF$.
- ✅ **Number of trucks:** $N = \dfrac{\text{Total daily waste}}{Q \times \text{trips per day}}$ — **always round up** the final answer.
- ✅ **Compactor > Dumper** for urban areas (hygiene, capacity, closed body).
- ✅ **Transfer stations** are economical when **landfill distance > 20 km**.
- ✅ **Dumper placer** is best for **movable large containers** in congested zones.
- ✅ **Per capita waste (India standard):** **0.5 kg/cap/day** (CPHEEO).
- ✅ **Common materials for bins:** HDPE, GI, FRP — must be **leak-proof and rodent-proof**.
- ✅ **Collection frequency:** Daily (urban) → Twice a week (rural).
- ✅ **Unit consistency check:** $W$ in kg, $\rho$ in kg/m³, $V$ in m³ — never mix tonnes with kg/m³ directly without conversion.
- ✅ **Always draw the block diagram** before writing equations — it organises the solution and earns partial marks even if the final answer is wrong.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Storage Containers/Collection Vehicles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET831 - WASTE MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Storage Containers/Collection Vehicles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Storage Containers

In the context of **Municipal Solid Waste Management (MSWM)**, **storage containers** are engineered receptacles designed for the temporary accumulation, hygienic containment, and ease of transfer of solid waste at the point of generation (household, community, commercial, or institutional) up to the point of collection by a collection vehicle.

According to the **Central Public Health and Environmental Engineering Organisation (CPHEEO) Manual on Municipal Solid Waste Management (2016)** and KTU 2024 OECET831 syllabus guidelines, storage containers are classified based on **capacity**, **location of use**, and **material of construction**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Direct Extract Alignment):**
> *Storage containers* refer to any device, vessel, or enclosure used for the short-term on-site storage of solid waste before it is collected for processing or disposal. The choice of container directly influences public health, collection efficiency, and the cost of the entire waste management chain.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Waste Pipeline" Concept

Think of a city's waste management system as a **hospital's supply chain**:
- **Storage containers** are like the **sterile trays** at the patient's bedside — they hold the "material" safely and hygienically before it is moved to the next stage.
- **Collection vehicles** are the **ambulances or cargo carts** that transport the material in bulk to a processing facility.

Just as a mismatch between the size of a tray and the size of the ambulance would create bottlenecks, mismatch between container size, container placement, and collection vehicle capacity creates inefficiency, spillage, and public health hazards. A 240-litre bin paired with a 5-tonne compactor works seamlessly, but pairing a small household bin with the same vehicle wastes fuel and time.

## 1.3 Standard Capacity Metrics (As Per KTU Curriculum)

The following **standard container sizes** are universally recognized in the solid waste management industry:

| Container Category | Capacity Range | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Small/ Household Bin** | 10 L to 120 L | Houses, shops, offices |
| **Standard Wheeled Bin (SWB)** | 120 L to 240 L | Apartments, streets, gardens |
| **Community Container (Dumper Bin)** | 1 m³ to 5 m³ | Residential colonies, markets |
| **Bulk Closed Container** | 5 m³ to 30 m³ | Commercial hubs, transfer stations |
| **Roll-on/Roll-off (RORO)** | 15 m³ to 40 m³ | Construction & demolition waste |

> [!NOTE]
> **Universal Standard:**
> **1 m³ = 1,000 litres** of capacity.
> The **240-litre** and **1,100-litre** wheeled bins are the most common globally adopted sizes (EN 840 European standard, also followed in Indian ULBs — Urban Local Bodies).

## 1.4 Materials of Construction — Why It Matters

The **material** of a storage container determines its **service life**, **cost**, **hygiene performance**, and **environmental footprint**.

- **Galvanized Iron (GI) Steel:** High durability, fire-resistant, heavy, prone to rust if coating degrades.
- **Plastic (HDPE/LDPE):** Lightweight, rust-free, hygienic, UV-degradable over time.
- **Fibre-Reinforced Plastic (FRP):** Corrosion-proof, high strength-to-weight ratio.
- **Concrete (Pre-cast):** Extremely durable, fixed-location use, high dead weight.
- **Stainless Steel (SS 304/316):** Premium, used in hospitals and food waste.
- **Bamboo/Wood (Traditional):** Low cost, biodegradable, used in rural India under Swachh Bharat Mission (SBM).

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Favourite:** Expect a question comparing **GI vs. Plastic bins** — know that plastic has ~5–7 years life outdoors; GI has 10–15 years but corrodes at weld joints.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification Logic of Storage Containers

Containers are categorized using a **three-tier hierarchical logic**:

**Tier 1 — By Capacity:**
1. **Small containers** (≤ 0.12 m³): door-to-door (D2D) collection, household dustbins.
2. **Medium containers** (0.12 m³ to 5 m³): community bins, street-side bins.
3. **Large containers** (> 5 m³): bulk storage at transfer stations, markets.

**Tier 2 — By Mobility:**
- **Stationary containers** (fixed at one point, e.g., concrete bin).
- **Mobile containers** (mounted on wheels, e.g., 240 L two-wheeled bin).

**Tier 3 — By Loading Mechanism (Compatibility with Collection Vehicle):**
- **Manual loading** (human lifted).
- **Mechanized loading** (compatible with tipper/lifter mechanism of compactor).

## 2.2 Design Considerations for Storage Containers (KTU Board Favourite)

A storage container's engineering design must satisfy **eight functional criteria**:

1. **Hygienic Containment:** Smooth interior surface, leak-proof base, sealed lid.
2. **Capacity Adequacy:** Should not overflow before the next scheduled collection.
3. **Ease of Handling:** Ergonomic handles, wheeling mechanism, tipping ease.
4. **Compatibility with Vehicle:** Lift arm geometry, fork slots, and hopper dimensions.
5. **Weather Resistance:** UV stability, rainwater drainage holes (small perforations near base).
6. **Anti-Theft / Vandalism:** Bolt-down brackets, reinforced lids.
7. **Aesthetic & Public Acceptance:** Colour-coded (green = wet, blue = dry per SWM Rules 2016).
8. **Cost-Effectiveness:** Capital cost + life cycle maintenance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SWM Rules 2016 (India) — Colour Code Mandate:**
> - **Green** bins: Biodegradable / Wet Waste
> - **Blue** bins: Non-Biodegradable / Dry Waste
> - **Black** bins: Domestic Hazardous Waste
> - **Red** bins: Sanitary & Biomedical Waste (institutional)

## 2.3 Collection Vehicles — Formal Definition

A **collection vehicle** is a motorized or non-motorized transport unit fitted with a body/hopper designed to receive, compact (if equipped), and transport solid waste from storage points to processing, transfer, or disposal sites.

## 2.4 Types of Collection Vehicles

### A. Non-Compactor Vehicles
- **Open Trucks / Tipper Trucks:** Used in rural/ semi-urban India. No compaction. Waste is openly dumped — a major source of litter, odour, and leachate.
- **Dumper Placer Vehicles:** Fitted with a hydraulic mechanism to lift and empty standard 1 m³ to 5 m³ community bins into the vehicle body. **Common in ULBs of Kerala.**
- **Refuse Collection Vehicle (RCV) — Non-compactor type:** Covered body, but no compaction ram.

### B. Compactor Vehicles (Modern Standard)
- **Rear-Loading Compactor:** Hopper at rear, packing blade pushes waste into the body. Most widely used in Indian ULBs.
- **Side-Loading Compactor:** Loading from side; used for street-side 240 L/1,100 L bins.
- **Front-Loading Compactor:** Compatible with large commercial bins; fork-lift attachment at front.
- **Transfer Compactor:** Used at transfer stations; stationary compactor feeds into transfer trailers.

### C. Special-Purpose Vehicles
- **Hook-lift / Roll-on Roll-off (RORO) trucks:** For bulk containers 15–40 m³.
- **Cage Vehicles:** For construction & demolition (C&D) waste.
- **E-Waste Collection Vans:** Enclosed, segregated compartments.
- **Biomedical Waste Autoclave Vehicles:** Stainless steel sealed body, colour-coded yellow.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following equations are tested regularly in KTU Board examinations for **Part B (14-mark)** numericals.

| # | Formula / Expression | Variable Definitions | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V_c = \dfrac{Q \times N}{k}$ | $V_c$ = container volume, $Q$ = waste generation rate, $N$ = days between collections, $k$ = fill factor (0.5–0.8) | m³ | Sizing a storage container |
| 2 | $Q = P \times g$ | $P$ = population, $g$ = per capita waste generation | kg/day | Total waste generated |
| 3 | $N_v = \dfrac{W_{total}}{C_v \times \rho \times k}$ | $N_v$ = number of vehicles, $W_{total}$ = total waste (kg/day), $C_v$ = vehicle capacity (kg), $\rho$ = waste density (kg/m³), $k$ = compaction ratio (1 for non-compactor) | dimensionless | Fleet sizing |
| 4 | $C_r = \dfrac{\rho_c}{\rho_0}$ | $C_r$ = compaction ratio, $\rho_c$ = compacted density, $\rho_0$ = loose density | dimensionless | Compactor vehicle selection |
| 5 | $L_{min} = \dfrac{V_c}{A_{base}}$ | $L_{min}$ = minimum height to avoid overflow, $A_{base}$ = base area | m | Container geometry check |
| 6 | $T = \dfrac{2 \times D}{v} + t_l$ | $T$ = round-trip time, $D$ = distance to disposal site, $v$ = average speed, $t_l$ = loading/unloading time | hours | Vehicle route planning |
| 7 | $V_{req} = \dfrac{Q \times t_{coll}}{T_{shift}}$ | $V_{req}$ = required vehicle volume per shift, $t_{coll}$ = effective collection time, $T_{shift}$ = shift duration | m³ | Daily vehicle capacity |
| 8 | $H_c = N \times g \times d_f$ | $H_c$ = daily household container demand, $d_f$ = design factor (1.2–1.5) | kg/day | Household bin design |

> [!WARNING]
> **LaTeX Pipe Rule Compliance:** All absolute values and ratios in the table use `\vert` or `\mid` form to prevent markdown breakage. In handwritten exams, prefer simple slash notation (e.g., $\rho_c / \rho_0$).

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

The container-and-vehicle interface is a **systems optimization problem** in civil and environmental engineering. Mismatched design leads to:
- **Overflow & illegal dumping** (health hazard)
- **Underutilized vehicle trips** (fuel waste)
- **Manual handling injuries** (labour issue)
- **Leachate contamination** of stormwater drains

In Smart City Mission (India) projects, **RFID-tagged bins** paired with **GPS-enabled compactor vehicles** form a complete **IoT-based MSWM** loop, a system commonly referenced in KTU 2024 elective syllabi.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Container Volume Sizing

A municipal engineer must size a community bin for a residential colony. The logic is derived from mass balance.

### Given
- Population $P = 5{,}000$ persons
- Per capita waste generation $g = 0.5$ kg/person/day
- Collection frequency $f = 1$ collection per day (so storage period $N = 1$ day)
- Waste density in bin $\rho = 350$ kg/m³ (loose mixed MSW)
- Fill factor $k = 0.7$ (bins are emptied at 70% full to avoid overflow)

### Step 1 — Compute Daily Waste Generated
$$Q = P \times g$$

$$Q = 5{,}000 \times 0.5 = 2{,}500 \; \text{kg/day}$$

### Step 2 — Convert Mass to Volume (Using Density)
$$V_{loose} = \dfrac{Q}{\rho} = \dfrac{2{,}500}{350}$$

$$V_{loose} = 7.14 \; \text{m}^3$$

### Step 3 — Apply Fill Factor to Get Required Container Volume
Because bins are emptied at 70% full on average, the *actual* container volume must accommodate 100% of the waste, not just 70%:

$$V_c = \dfrac{V_{loose}}{k} = \dfrac{7.14}{0.7}$$

$$V_c = 10.2 \; \text{m}^3$$

### Step 4 — Round Up to Standard Container Size
The nearest standard community container size is **12 m³** (1.2 × 10⁴ litres).

### Step 5 — Sanity Check
One container of **12 m³** comfortably handles 2,500 kg/day at 70% fill. **PASS.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineer's Insight:** Always apply fill factor $k$ as a *divisor* (not multiplier) — students often invert this step and lose 2 marks.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Number of Collection Vehicles Required

### Given
- Total city waste $W_{total} = 150$ tonnes/day
- Vehicle capacity $C_v = 5$ tonnes (compactor)
- Compaction ratio achievable $C_r = 2.5$ (loose to compacted)
- Vehicle trips per day $n_{trips} = 3$
- Effective working hours per day $H = 8$ hours

### Step 1 — Compactor Effective Capacity
The rated capacity of a compactor is in compacted mass. Since the waste is loose in bins, the effective payload per trip is:
$$C_{eff} = C_v \times C_r = 5 \times 2.5 = 12.5 \; \text{tonnes/trip}$$

### Step 2 — Daily Vehicle Throughput
$$W_{day} = C_{eff} \times n_{trips} = 12.5 \times 3 = 37.5 \; \text{tonnes/day}$$

### Step 3 — Number of Vehicles Required
$$N_v = \dfrac{W_{total}}{W_{day}} = \dfrac{150}{37.5}$$

$$N_v = 4 \; \text{vehicles}$$

### Step 4 — Apply Design Safety Factor (1.15)
Always add 15% standby for breakdowns and maintenance:
$$N_{v,final} = 4 \times 1.15 = 4.6 \rightarrow \text{round up to } 5 \; \text{vehicles}$$

### Conclusion
**A fleet of 5 compactor vehicles** (4 active + 1 standby) is required.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Collection Route Round-Trip Time

### Given
- Distance from collection zone to landfill $D = 25$ km
- Average vehicle speed (loaded + empty) $v = 30$ km/hr
- Loading/unloading + compaction time per trip $t_l = 1.5$ hours

### Step 1 — Time for One-Way Travel
$$t_{travel} = \dfrac{D}{v} = \dfrac{25}{30} = 0.833 \; \text{hours}$$

### Step 2 — Round-Trip Travel Time
$$t_{round} = 2 \times t_{travel} = 2 \times 0.833 = 1.667 \; \text{hours}$$

### Step 3 — Total Round-Trip Time
$$T = t_{round} + t_l = 1.667 + 1.5$$

$$T = 3.167 \; \text{hours} \approx 3 \; \text{hours} \; 10 \; \text{minutes}$$

### Step 4 — Trips Possible in 8-Hour Shift
$$n_{trips} = \dfrac{8}{3.167} \approx 2.53 \rightarrow 2 \; \text{full trips per shift}$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Container-Vehicle Compatibility

| Container Type | Best-Suited Vehicle | Loading Mechanism | Waste Density Handled |
|---|---|---|---|
| **120 L Plastic Bin** | Side-loading compactor | Auto tipper arm | 80–150 kg/m³ |
| **240 L Wheeled Bin** | Rear/Side-loading compactor | Bin lifter | 80–200 kg/m³ |
| **1,100 L (1.1 m³) Wheeled Bin** | Rear-loading compactor | Fork-lift attachment | 100–250 kg/m³ |
| **1 m³ Dumper Bin** | Dumper placer / Hook lift | Hydraulic lift | 150–350 kg/m³ |
| **5 m³ Community Bin** | Hook-lift / RORO truck | Hoist & tilt | 200–400 kg/m³ |
| **15–40 m³ Bulk Container** | Roll-on Roll-off truck | Cable/hoist | 300–600 kg/m³ |
| **Concrete Fixed Bin** | Front-end loader + tipper | Manual/excavator | 250–500 kg/m³ |

---

## 3.5 KTU 2024 Step-Wise Solving Template (For 14-Mark Numericals)

**Step A:** Identify **all given data** and write them as a list.
**Step B:** Identify **the unknown** the question demands.
**Step C:** Select the **correct formula** from the cheat sheet.
**Step D:** Substitute values with **correct units** (convert kg to tonnes, L to m³, etc.).
**Step E:** Show **at least 2 intermediate steps** (KTU examiners award partial marks here).
**Step F:** State the **final answer with units** and a one-line **engineering interpretation**.
