# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Design Logic of a Collection System

The design of a waste collection system follows a **sequential decision hierarchy**:

### Step 1: Quantification of Waste
- Estimate the **total weight of waste** ($W$) generated per day in the service area.
- Convert to **volume** ($V$) using the **in-situ density** of waste ($D_w$).
- Apply correction factors for **seasonal variation, holidays, special events**.

### Step 2: Selection of Collection System
- **HC System** is chosen when:
  - Container volume is large (roll-on/roll-off, $5\ m^3$ to $30\ m^3$).
  - Waste generation is highly variable.
  - Long haul distances exist (container stays loaded during haul).
- **SC System** is chosen when:
  - Service density is high (urban areas with many service points).
  - Collection frequency is high.
  - Vehicle-mounted compactors are economically viable.

### Step 3: Determination of Number of Containers
- Required to avoid overflow and ensure continuous service.

### Step 4: Calculation of Collection Vehicle Fleet Size
- Based on cycle time, number of trips, working hours, and crew productivity.

### Step 5: Route Optimization
- Minimize total route distance using **Travelling Salesman heuristics**, **Nearest Neighbour**, or **Sweep algorithm**.

### Step 6: Workforce and Cost Estimation
- Crew size, wages, fuel, depreciation, maintenance.

## 2.2 Detailed Mathematical Foundation

### 2.2.1 General Quantification Equations

The **mass of waste** generated per day is given by:

$$W = P \times g \times f_c$$

where:
- $W$ = mass of waste per day $(\text{kg/day})$
- $P$ = population served
- $g$ = per capita waste generation rate $(\text{kg/capita/day})$
- $f_c$ = collection frequency correction factor (typically $1.0$ to $1.2$)

The **volume of waste** generated per day is:

$$V_{\text{waste}} = \frac{W}{D_w}$$

where $D_w$ is the in-situ waste density in $\text{kg/m}^3$.

### 2.2.2 Hauled Container (HC) System Equations

> [!NOTE]
> In an HC system, the entire container is hoisted onto the truck, transported full, emptied, and returned empty (or vice versa). One **trip** = one loaded container hauled from the service point to the disposal site.

**Volume of waste picked up per trip:**

$$V_p = V_c \times F_1 \times F_2 \times F_3$$

where:
- $V_c$ = nominal capacity of container $(\text{m}^3)$
- $F_1$ = container fill factor (typically $0.75$ to $1.0$)
- $F_2$ = time-available factor (accounts for working hours)
- $F_3$ = collection efficiency factor (accounting for non-uniformity)

**Weight of waste per trip:**

$$W_p = V_p \times D_w$$

**Number of trips per day per vehicle:**

$$N_t = \frac{H}{t_c}$$

where:
- $H$ = working hours per day (typically $8$ hours)
- $t_c$ = cycle time per trip (hours)

**Cycle time** is the time for one complete round trip:

$$t_c = t_p + t_{ht} + t_{dt} + t_{h}$$

where:
- $t_p$ = pick-up (hoist) time at service point (min)
- $t_{ht}$ = haul time to disposal site (min)
- $t_{dt}$ = disposal time (unloading + return hookup) (min)
- $t_h$ = hookup and hoist time (min)

**Total waste collected per day by one vehicle:**

$$W_{\text{daily}} = N_t \times W_p$$

**Number of vehicles required:**

$$N_v = \frac{W_{\text{total}}}{W_{\text{daily}}}$$

### 2.2.3 Stationary Container (SC) System Equations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In an SC system, the truck visits multiple containers. The container is emptied into the truck's compactor body; the truck itself is the only unit that transports waste to the disposal site.

**Volume of waste collected per trip:**

$$V_p = V_{\text{cap}} \times C_c$$

where:
- $V_{\text{cap}}$ = truck compactor body volume $(\text{m}^3)$
- $C_c$ = compaction ratio (typically $2$ to $4$ for MSW)

**Mass of waste per trip (weight-limited):**

$$W_p = \min\left(V_p \times D_c,\ P_{\max}\right)$$

where:
- $D_c$ = compacted waste density in truck
- $P_{\max}$ = maximum payload (gross vehicle weight minus tare weight)

**Pick-up time at a single container:**

$$t_p = t_{pu} + t_d$$

where:
- $t_{pu}$ = pickup and positioning time (min)
- $t_d$ = dumping time into truck (min)

**Number of containers emptied per trip:**

$$n_c = \frac{V_{\text{cap}} \times C_c}{V_c \times F_1}$$

**Cycle time:**

$$t_c = (n_c \times t_p) + t_{ht} + t_{dt}$$

**Number of trips per day per vehicle:**

$$N_t = \frac{H \times 60}{t_c}$$

### 2.2.4 Number of Containers Required (SC System)

$$N_{\text{cont}} = \frac{V_{\text{waste/day}}}{V_c \times F_1 \times f_{\text{coll}}}$$

where $f_{\text{coll}}$ is the fraction of total waste collected via that container system.

### 2.2.5 Cover Material Requirement (Landfill Operation)

$$V_{\text{cover}} = V_{\text{compact}} \times F_4$$

where $F_4$ is the cover-soil ratio ($0.20$ to $0.25$ for daily cover; can be as low as $0.10$ with alternative daily cover).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **EXAMINATION GOLD TABLE — Memorise These Equations**

| # | Equation | Application | Standard Values / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $W = P \times g$ | Total waste mass/day | $g = 0.5$ kg/cap/day (Indian standard) |
| 2 | $V = W / D_w$ | Waste volume/day | $D_w = 300$ to $500$ kg/m³ |
| 3 | $V_p = V_c \cdot F_1 \cdot F_2 \cdot F_3$ | HC: Volume per trip | $F_1, F_2, F_3 \in [0,1]$ |
| 4 | $W_p = V_p \cdot D_w$ | HC: Mass per trip | kg |
| 5 | $N_t = H / t_c$ | Trips per vehicle per day | dimensionless |
| 6 | $t_c = t_p + t_{ht} + t_{dt} + t_h$ | HC cycle time | minutes |
| 7 | $V_p = V_{\text{cap}} \cdot C_c$ | SC: Volume per trip (compacted) | $C_c = 2$ to $4$ |
| 8 | $n_c = V_p / (V_c \cdot F_1)$ | SC: Containers emptied per trip | round to nearest integer |
| 9 | $t_c = n_c \cdot t_p + t_{ht} + t_{dt}$ | SC cycle time | minutes |
| 10 | $N_v = W_{\text{total}} / (N_t \cdot W_p)$ | Number of vehicles required | dimensionless |
| 11 | $N_{\text{cont}} = V_{\text{waste}} / (V_c \cdot F_1)$ | Container count | round up |
| 12 | $L_{\text{route}} = \sum d_{i \to i+1}$ | Route length | km |
| 13 | $T_{\text{route}} = t_{\text{travel}} + n_c \cdot t_p$ | Route duration | minutes |
| 14 | $\text{Labor productivity} = W_{\text{daily}} / (\text{crew} \times H)$ | kg/worker-hour | benchmark: $500$ to $1500$ |
| 15 | $C_{\text{total}} = C_{\text{cap}} + C_{\text{op}} + C_{\text{maint}}$ | Life-cycle cost | ₹/year |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The HC system is used in **Mumbai and Delhi for construction debris** and at **port cities for marine litter**, while the SC system dominates in **Bengaluru, Chennai, and Pune** for residential MSW. Modern Indian smart-city designs integrate **IoT sensors** in stationary containers to alert collection crews when bins reach $80\%$ capacity — this is the **"smart-bin" revolution** and is a frequent KTU viva question.

# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Exhaustive Numerical Worked Example #1: Hauled Container (HC) System

### Problem Statement (KTU Typical)

> A city generates **$200$ tonnes/day** of construction and demolition (C&D) waste with an **in-situ density of $400\ \text{kg/m}^3$**. The waste is collected using a **Hauled Container (HC) system** with roll-on/roll-off containers of capacity **$5\ \text{m}^3$ each**. Given:
> - Container fill factor $F_1 = 0.85$, time factor $F_2 = 0.90$, efficiency factor $F_3 = 0.95$.
> - Hoist time $t_p = 4$ min, hookup time $t_h = 2$ min.
> - Average haul speed = $30$ km/h, one-way haul distance = $15$ km.
> - Disposal (unloading) time $t_{dt} = 6$ min.
> - Working hours $H = 8$ hr/day.
> - Crew size per vehicle = 1 driver + 1 helper.
> 
> **Compute:**
> (a) Volume of waste picked up per trip
> (b) Mass of waste per trip
> (c) Number of trips per vehicle per day
> (d) Number of vehicles required
> (e) Total crew requirement

### Step-by-Step Solution

#### Part (a) — Volume of waste picked up per trip

We apply the HC volume equation:

$$V_p = V_c \times F_1 \times F_2 \times F_3$$

Substituting the values:

$$V_p = 5 \times 0.85 \times 0.90 \times 0.95$$

$$V_p = 5 \times 0.727\ \text{m}^3$$

$$\boxed{V_p = 3.633\ \text{m}^3 \text{ per trip}}$$

#### Part (b) — Mass of waste per trip

Apply the mass equation:

$$W_p = V_p \times D_w$$

$$W_p = 3.633\ \text{m}^3 \times 400\ \text{kg/m}^3$$

$$\boxed{W_p = 1453.2\ \text{kg/trip} = 1.453\ \text{tonnes/trip}}$$

#### Part (c) — Number of trips per vehicle per day

First, compute the cycle time. The haul time (one-way) is:

$$t_{ht} = \frac{\text{distance}}{\text{speed}} \times 60 = \frac{15}{30} \times 60 = 30\ \text{min}$$

The return trip takes the same time, so total driving time = $60$ min.

Now the full cycle time:

$$t_c = t_p + t_{ht,\text{round}} + t_{dt} + t_h$$

$$t_c = 4 + 60 + 6 + 2$$

$$\boxed{t_c = 72\ \text{min/trip}}$$

Now the number of trips per vehicle per day:

$$N_t = \frac{H \times 60}{t_c} = \frac{8 \times 60}{72} = \frac{480}{72}$$

$$\boxed{N_t = 6.67 \approx 6\ \text{trips/day (whole trips only)}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Convention:** Always round **down** to the nearest whole trip — you cannot perform a fractional trip. If your answer comes to $6.67$, the answer is $6$ trips.

#### Part (d) — Number of vehicles required

The daily capacity of one vehicle is:

$$W_{\text{daily}} = N_t \times W_p = 6 \times 1.453\ \text{tonnes} = 8.72\ \text{tonnes/day}$$

The number of vehicles required to haul $200$ tonnes/day:

$$N_v = \frac{W_{\text{total}}}{W_{\text{daily}}} = \frac{200}{8.72} = 22.94$$

$$\boxed{N_v = 23\ \text{vehicles (always round up)}}$$

#### Part (e) — Total crew requirement

Each vehicle requires 1 driver + 1 helper = **2 crew members**.

$$N_{\text{crew}} = 23 \times 2 = 46\ \text{workers}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Trap:** Do not forget the **multi-shift operation**. If the city operates 2 shifts of 8 hours each, the same 23 vehicles need **46 + 46 = 92 workers** plus supervisory staff.

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Numerical Worked Example #2: Stationary Container (SC) System

### Problem Statement

> A residential colony of **$50{,}000$ population** generates MSW at **$0.45$ kg/capita/day** with an **in-situ density of $300\ \text{kg/m}^3$**. A **Stationary Container (SC) system** is used with **$1.1\ \text{m}^3$ capacity bins**. Given:
> - Truck compactor capacity $V_{\text{cap}} = 8\ \text{m}^3$, compaction ratio $C_c = 3$.
> - Container fill factor $F_1 = 0.80$.
> - Pickup time $t_{pu} = 1$ min, dumping time $t_d = 2$ min per container.
> - One-way haul distance = $12$ km, average speed = $25$ km/h.
> - Disposal time $t_{dt} = 8$ min.
> - Working hours $H = 8$ hr.
> - Assume **one shift** operation.
> 
> **Compute:**
> (a) Total daily waste generated (mass and volume)
> (b) Number of containers required
> (c) Number of containers emptied per trip
> (d) Cycle time
> (e) Number of trips per day per vehicle
> (f) Number of vehicles required
> (g) Total crew (1 driver + 2 helpers per vehicle)

### Step-by-Step Solution

#### Part (a) — Total daily waste

Mass generated:

$$W = P \times g = 50{,}000 \times 0.45$$

$$\boxed{W = 22{,}500\ \text{kg/day} = 22.5\ \text{tonnes/day}}$$

Volume generated (uncompacted):

$$V_{\text{waste}} = \frac{W}{D_w} = \frac{22{,}500}{300}$$

$$\boxed{V_{\text{waste}} = 75\ \text{m}^3/\text{day}}$$

#### Part (b) — Number of containers required

$$N_{\text{cont}} = \frac{V_{\text{waste}}}{V_c \times F_1} = \frac{75}{1.1 \times 0.80}$$

$$N_{\text{cont}} = \frac{75}{0.88} = 85.23$$

$$\boxed{N_{\text{cont}} = 86\ \text{containers (round up)}}$$

#### Part (c) — Number of containers emptied per trip

Volume of waste accepted by truck per trip (compacted in truck):

$$V_p = V_{\text{cap}} \times C_c = 8 \times 3 = 24\ \text{m}^3$$

Volume per container in the field:

$$V_{\text{per cont}} = V_c \times F_1 = 1.1 \times 0.80 = 0.88\ \text{m}^3$$

Number of containers per trip:

$$n_c = \frac{V_p}{V_{\text{per cont}}} = \frac{24}{0.88} = 27.27$$

$$\boxed{n_c = 27\ \text{containers/trip (whole containers only)}}$$

#### Part (d) — Cycle time

Pickup time per container:

$$t_p = t_{pu} + t_d = 1 + 2 = 3\ \text{min}$$

Round-trip haul time:

$$t_{ht} = \frac{2 \times 12}{25} \times 60 = \frac{24}{25} \times 60 = 57.6\ \text{min}$$

Cycle time:

$$t_c = n_c \cdot t_p + t_{ht} + t_{dt}$$

$$t_c = 27 \times 3 + 57.6 + 8 = 81 + 57.6 + 8$$

$$\boxed{t_c = 146.6\ \text{min/trip}}$$

#### Part (e) — Number of trips per day per vehicle

$$N_t = \frac{H \times 60}{t_c} = \frac{480}{146.6} = 3.27$$

$$\boxed{N_t = 3\ \text{trips/day}}$$

#### Part (f) — Number of vehicles required

Mass collected per trip (verify against payload limit, assume $5$ tonnes max):

$$W_p = V_p \times D_c$$

Compacted density $D_c = D_w \times C_c = 300 \times 3 = 900\ \text{kg/m}^3$

$$W_p = 24 \times 900 = 21{,}600\ \text{kg/trip} = 21.6\ \text{tonnes/trip}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Theoretical mass** is $21.6$ tonnes/trip, but the truck's **structural payload limit** of $5$ tonnes becomes the binding constraint. KTU accepts the **minimum** of the two values:

$$W_p = \min(21.6,\ 5) = 5\ \text{tonnes/trip}$$

Daily capacity per vehicle:

$$W_{\text{daily}} = N_t \times W_p = 3 \times 5 = 15\ \text{tonnes/day}$$

Number of vehicles:

$$N_v = \frac{W_{\text{total}}}{W_{\text{daily}}} = \frac{22.5}{15} = 1.5$$

$$\boxed{N_v = 2\ \text{vehicles (round up)}}$$

#### Part (g) — Total crew

Each vehicle has 1 driver + 2 helpers = **3 crew**.

$$N_{\text{crew}} = 2 \times 3 = 6\ \text{workers}$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use HC vs. SC

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU often asks a 7-mark design-selection question.** Use this matrix:

| Decision Criterion | Choose HC System if... | Choose SC System if... |
|---|---|---|
| Waste density | Loose, low-density (e.g., C&D, leaves) | Moderate to high density (compacted MSW) |
| Container size | $5$ m³ to $30$ m³ | $0.06$ m³ to $3$ m³ |
| Haul distance | Long haul $> 15$ km | Short haul $< 15$ km |
| Generation rate | Volatile, episodic | Steady, predictable |
| Capital cost | Higher (specialized vehicles) | Lower (standard compactors) |
| Labor efficiency | Lower (less manual handling) | Higher (more door-to-door) |
| Suitable for | Bulk generators | Residential & commercial |

## 3.4 Route Optimization: Sweep Algorithm Explained

A **collection route** is the path a vehicle follows between service points. The goal is to minimize:
$$L_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{N-1} d(p_i,\ p_{i+1})$$

subject to the constraint that **no service point is visited twice** and the total travel time $\leq$ working hours.

The **Sweep Algorithm** (used in $90\%$ of Indian municipal collections):
1. Fix the depot (garage) on a 2-D map.
2. Anchor a **radial line** through the depot.
3. **Sweep** the line clockwise.
4. Assign points to the route in the order they are swept.
5. Stop when vehicle capacity is reached.
6. Repeat for the next route.
7. Apply a **2-opt swap** to refine each route.

> [!NOTE]
> **Pro Tip for KTU:** When asked to design a route, sketch a compass-rose map, plot service points, and apply the Sweep algorithm. Marks are awarded for the procedure, not for software solutions.

# 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Waste Collection System Design

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Define Service Area] --> B[Estimate Waste Quantity W and Volume V]
    B --> C{Select Collection System Type}
    C -->|HC: Hauled Container| D[Determine Container Capacity Vc]
    C -->|SC: Stationary Container| E[Determine Bin Capacity and Truck Capacity]
    D --> F[Compute Volume per Trip Vp]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Compute Mass per Trip Wp]
    G --> H[Compute Cycle Time tc]
    H --> I[Compute Number of Trips Nt per Day]
    I --> J[Compute Number of Vehicles Nv]
    J --> K[Apply Route Optimization Sweep Algorithm]
    K --> L[Compute Crew Size and Productivity]
    L --> M[Cost Estimation Capital plus Operating]
    M --> N[Final Design Report and Drawings]
    N --> O[End]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Comparison of HC and SC System Architectures

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph SP1[Service Point HC]
        A1[Waste Generation]
        A2[Container Loaded at Site]
        A3[Full Container Hoisted onto Truck]
        A1 --> A2 --> A3
    end
    A3 --> A4[Haul to Disposal Site]
    A4 --> A5[Empty Container at Site and Return]
    A5 --> A6[Refilled at Next Service Point]
    A6 --> A4

    subgraph SP2[Service Point SC]
        B1[Waste Generation]
        B2[Container Stays at Curb]
        B3[Truck Visits and Empties Bin into Compactor]
        B1 --> B2 --> B3
    end
    B3 --> B4[Truck Body Fills to Capacity]
    B4 --> B5[Haul Full Truck to Disposal Site]
    B5 --> B6[Return to Next Stationary Bin]
    B6 --> B3
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Flowchart — Design Calculation Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Input: Population P and Generation Rate g] --> S2[Compute W equals P times g]
    S2 --> S3[Compute V equals W over Dw]
    S3 --> S4{System Selected}
    S4 -->|HC| S5A[Compute Vp equals Vc times F1 times F2 times F3]
    S4 -->|SC| S5B[Compute Vp equals Vcap times Cc]
    S5A --> S6[Compute Wp equals Vp times Dw]
    S5B --> S6
    S6 --> S7[Compute tc equals tpu plus trt plus tdt]
    S7 --> S8[Compute Nt equals H over tc]
    S8 --> S9[Compute Nv equals Wtotal over Wdaily]
    S9 --> S10[Output: Number of Vehicles and Crew]
```

## 4.4 Route Optimization — Sweep Algorithm Visual Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R0[Depot at Center of Service Zone] --> R1[Anchor Radial Line at 0 Degrees]
    R1 --> R2[Sweep Line Clockwise]
    R2 --> R3[Add Service Points Sequentially]
    R3 --> R4{Vehicle Capacity Reached}
    R4 -->|No| R2
    R4 -->|Yes| R5[Close Route and Return to Depot]
    R5 --> R6[Start Next Route]
    R6 --> R2
    R2 --> R7[Apply 2-opt Refinement]
    R7 --> R8[Final Optimized Route Map]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Differentiate between a Hauled Container (HC) System and a Stationary Container (SC) System of waste collection. Mention one example where each is preferred.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Hauled Container (HC) | Stationary Container (SC) |
|---|---|---|
| Container movement | Container is transported with the vehicle | Container stays at the service point; only waste is loaded |
| Vehicle role | Picks up and hauls full container | Empties containers into its compactor body, then hauls |
| Typical use | C&D debris, bulky industrial waste | Residential MSW, institutional waste |
| Container size | Large ($5$ m³ to $30$ m³) | Small ($0.06$ m³ to $3$ m³) |
| Crew efficiency | Lower (less manual handling) | Higher (door-to-door collection) |

**(1 mark)** HC is preferred for **construction and demolition (C&D) waste in metropolitan areas**. **(1 mark)** SC is preferred for **door-to-door residential MSW collection in urban wards**. **(1 mark)** for the tabulated difference.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**What is the significance of the container fill factor ($F_1$) in waste collection system design?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **container fill factor $F_1$** is the ratio of the **actual volume of waste** present in a container to its **nominal geometric capacity**. It is dimensionless and typically lies in the range **$0.75$ to $1.0$**. **(1 mark)**

**Significance:**
- It accounts for the fact that containers are **not always filled to the brim** at the time of collection due to non-uniform waste deposition by households. **(1 mark)**
- It directly affects the **number of trips, vehicle utilization, and total cost** of the collection system. A lower $F_1$ means more trips and higher cost. **(1 mark)**

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Note:** As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, internal choice is provided. Both questions are **fully solved**.

### Question A — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** A municipal corporation serves a population of **$1{,}50{,}000$** with a per-capita waste generation of **$0.5$ kg/capita/day** and an in-situ density of **$400\ \text{kg/m}^3$**. The city uses a **Stationary Container System** with $1.1\ \text{m}^3$ bins. Given:
- Compactor body volume $V_{\text{cap}} = 6\ \text{m}^3$, compaction ratio $C_c = 3$.
- Bin fill factor $F_1 = 0.85$, dump time per bin $t_p = 3$ min.
- One-way haul distance $= 20$ km, average haul speed $= 30$ km/h.
- Disposal time $t_{dt} = 10$ min, working hours $H = 8$ hr/day.
- Crew: 1 driver + 2 helpers per vehicle.

**Determine:**
- (i) Total daily waste in $\text{kg}$ and $\text{m}^3$.
- (ii) Number of containers required.
- (iii) Number of containers emptied per trip.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Continuing from part (a), calculate the **cycle time**, **number of trips per day per vehicle**, **number of vehicles required**, and the **total crew strength** for the operation.

---

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — Solution

**(i) Total daily waste**

$$W = P \times g = 1{,}50{,}000 \times 0.5 = 75{,}000\ \text{kg/day} = 75\ \text{tonnes/day}$$

$$V_{\text{waste}} = \frac{W}{D_w} = \frac{75{,}000}{400} = 187.5\ \text{m}^3/\text{day}$$

**(ii) Number of containers required**

$$N_{\text{cont}} = \frac{V_{\text{waste}}}{V_c \times F_1} = \frac{187.5}{1.1 \times 0.85} = \frac{187.5}{0.935} = 200.5$$

$$\boxed{N_{\text{cont}} = 201\ \text{containers (round up)}}$$

**(iii) Containers emptied per trip**

Volume accepted by truck per trip (compacted):

$$V_p = V_{\text{cap}} \times C_c = 6 \times 3 = 18\ \text{m}^3$$

Volume per bin in field:

$$V_{\text{per bin}} = V_c \times F_1 = 1.1 \times 0.85 = 0.935\ \text{m}^3$$

$$\boxed{n_c = \frac{V_p}{V_{\text{per bin}}} = \frac{18}{0.935} = 19.25 \approx 19\ \text{bins/trip}}$$

#### Part (b) — Solution

**Cycle time**

Round-trip haul time:

$$t_{ht} = \frac{2 \times 20}{30} \times 60 = 80\ \text{min}$$

Cycle time:

$$t_c = n_c \times t_p + t_{ht} + t_{dt} = 19 \times 3 + 80 + 10 = 57 + 80 + 10 = 147\ \text{min}$$

**Trips per day per vehicle**

$$N_t = \frac{8 \times 60}{147} = \frac{480}{147} = 3.27 \approx 3\ \text{trips/day}$$

**Mass per trip** (assume payload $P_{\max} = 4$ tonnes, $D_c = 400 \times 3 = 1200\ \text{kg/m}^3$):

$$W_{p,\text{theo}} = 18 \times 1200 = 21{,}600\ \text{kg} = 21.6\ \text{tonnes}$$
$$W_p = \min(21.6, 4) = 4\ \text{tonnes/trip}$$

**Daily capacity per vehicle:**

$$W_{\text{daily}} = 3 \times 4 = 12\ \text{tonnes/day}$$

**Number of vehicles:**

$$N_v = \frac{75}{12} = 6.25 \Rightarrow \boxed{N_v = 7\ \text{vehicles}}$$

**Total crew** (3 per vehicle):

$$N_{\text{crew}} = 7 \times 3 = \boxed{21\ \text{workers}}$$

---

### Question B — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a) [7 Marks]** A city generates **$150$ tonnes/day** of solid waste with a density of **$500\ \text{kg/m}^3$**. The waste is collected using a **Hauled Container System** with containers of **$4\ \text{m}^3$** capacity. Given:
- $F_1 = 0.90$, $F_2 = 0.95$, $F_3 = 0.90$.
- $t_p = 5$ min, $t_{dt} = 8$ min, $t_h = 3$ min.
- One-way haul distance $= 18$ km, speed $= 36$ km/h.
- Working hours $H = 8$ hr, crew = 2 per vehicle.

**Calculate:**
- (i) Volume and mass of waste per trip.
- (ii) Cycle time and number of trips per day per vehicle.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Determine the **number of vehicles required** and the **total crew strength**. Comment on the **labour productivity** (kg/worker-hour) of the system.

---

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — Solution

**(i) Volume per trip**

$$V_p = V_c \times F_1 \times F_2 \times F_3 = 4 \times 0.90 \times 0.95 \times 0.90 = 3.078\ \text{m}^3/\text{trip}$$

**Mass per trip**

$$W_p = V_p \times D_w = 3.078 \times 500 = 1539\ \text{kg/trip} = 1.539\ \text{tonnes/trip}$$

**(ii) Cycle time**

Round-trip haul time:

$$t_{ht} = \frac{2 \times 18}{36} \times 60 = 60\ \text{min}$$

$$t_c = t_p + t_{ht} + t_{dt} + t_h = 5 + 60 + 8 + 3 = 76\ \text{min/trip}$$

**Trips per day per vehicle**

$$N_t = \frac{8 \times 60}{76} = \frac{480}{76} = 6.32 \Rightarrow 6\ \text{trips/day}$$

#### Part (b) — Solution

**Daily capacity per vehicle**

$$W_{\text{daily}} = 6 \times 1.539 = 9.234\ \text{tonnes/day}$$

**Number of vehicles**

$$N_v = \frac{150}{9.234} = 16.24 \Rightarrow \boxed{N_v = 17\ \text{vehicles}}$$

**Total crew**

$$N_{\text{crew}} = 17 \times 2 = 34\ \text{workers}$$

**Labour productivity**

$$\text{Productivity} = \frac{W_{\text{daily,total}}}{N_{\text{crew}} \times H} = \frac{150{,}000\ \text{kg}}{34 \times 8\ \text{hr}} = \frac{150{,}000}{272} = 551.5\ \text{kg/worker-hour}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Comment:** A labour productivity of $\approx 550$ kg/worker-hour is **within the acceptable CPHEEO benchmark** of $500$ to $1500$ kg/worker-hour, indicating a reasonably efficient system.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **TOP 5 REASONS STUDENTS LOSE MARKS IN WASTE COLLECTION DESIGN QUESTIONS:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing HC and SC equations:** Students apply $V_p = V_c \cdot F_1 \cdot F_2 \cdot F_3$ in an SC system. The result is mathematically computed but conceptually **wrong** — full credit lost.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting to convert $D_w$ (uncompacted) to $D_c$ (compacted) in SC systems.** The compaction ratio $C_c$ must be applied to **density**, not to **volume alone**:
> $$\text{Error: } W_p = V_{\text{cap}} \times D_w \quad \text{(WRONG)}$$
> $$\text{Correct: } W_p = V_{\text{cap}} \times C_c \times D_w \quad \text{(RIGHT)}$$
> 
> 3. **Not checking the payload limit** — students compute the volumetric capacity of the truck but ignore the **structural weight limit**, leading to over-designed fleets.
> 
> 4. **Rounding errors:** Always round **down** for trips/day and **up** for vehicles/containers. Writing $N_v = 6.25 \Rightarrow 6$ (instead of $7$) is a common $-2$ mark error.
> 
> 5. **Omitting units and unit conversions:** $W$ in kg but $V$ in m³ without showing the density $D_w$ in $\text{kg/m}^3$ — examiner deducts **1 mark** for dimensional inconsistency.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — WASTE COLLECTION SYSTEM DESIGN**

- ✅ **HC System:** Container moves with vehicle; use $V_p = V_c F_1 F_2 F_3$. Best for **bulky, low-density** waste.
- ✅ **SC System:** Container stays, vehicle moves; use $V_p = V_{\text{cap}} C_c$. Best for **residential MSW**.
- ✅ **Per capita waste generation (India):** $0.3$ to $0.6$ kg/capita/day; CPCB standard = $0.5$ kg/capita/day.
- ✅ **Waste density:** $300$–$500\ \text{kg/m}^3$ (uncompacted); $800$–$1200\ \text{kg/m}^3$ (compacted).
- ✅ **Standard bin sizes:** $0.06$, $0.12$, $0.24$, $1.1$, $3$, $5\ \text{m}^3$.
- ✅ **Compaction ratio $C_c$:** $2$ to $4$ for MSW.
- ✅ **Cycle time** includes pick-up, haul, disposal, and hook-up components — **always in minutes**, multiply $H$ by $60$ to convert hours.
- ✅ **Rounding rules:** Round **down** for trips/day; round **up** for vehicles and containers.
- ✅ **Always check payload limit** in SC systems using $\min(\text{theoretical mass},\ P_{\max})$.
- ✅ **Route optimization:** Use the **Sweep algorithm** for KTU exams; do not write code.
- ✅ **Labour productivity benchmark:** $500$–$1500\ \text{kg/worker-hour}$.
- ✅ **Bin colour code (MSW Rules 2016):** Green (wet), Blue (dry), Red (hazardous), Yellow (sanitary).
- ✅ **Key IS standards:** IS 12648 (Refuse Trucks), IS 14543 (Bins), IS 15078 (Compactors).
- ✅ **MSW Rules 2016** mandate **100% door-to-door collection** in Class I and II cities — a frequently asked **2-mark question**.
- ✅ **Cover material** at landfills: $0.20$–$0.25$ ratio of cover to compacted waste; reduced to $0.10$ with **alternative daily cover (ADC)** like foam or tarps.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Record Keeping
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET831 - WASTE MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Record Keeping

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

**Record Keeping** in Waste Management refers to the **systematic, chronological, and verifiable documentation of all activities, transactions, materials, and decisions** associated with the generation, segregation, storage, collection, transportation, treatment, and disposal of waste. It is the operational backbone that supports **regulatory compliance, traceability (cradle-to-grave tracking), auditing, financial accountability, and continuous process improvement** within any waste management hierarchy.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, record keeping is treated as a *non-negotiable managerial and statutory function* under the Solid Waste Management Rules (SWMR 2016), Hazardous and Other Wastes (Management and Transboundary Movement) Rules (HOWM 2016), and the Biomedical Waste Management Rules (BMW 2016, amended 2018).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU OECET831 – Module 2):**
> Record keeping is explicitly listed as a core operational pillar that links *physical waste streams* with *administrative governance*. It enables regulators (State Pollution Control Boards / CPCB) and auditors to reconstruct the entire life-cycle of any waste consignment from the point of generation to its final disposal.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine a **hospital patient file**. Every visit, every test, every medicine prescribed, every doctor's note is recorded in a single file. If a doctor retires, the next doctor can still continue the treatment seamlessly. If something goes wrong, the file tells us *exactly* what happened and when.

**Waste management record keeping is the "patient file" of waste.** Just as a hospital cannot treat patients without medical records, a waste management facility **cannot legally, safely, or efficiently** handle waste without documentation. Every kilogram of plastic, every litre of chemical sludge, every contaminated sharps bin must have a documented *birth certificate* (generation record), *travel history* (manifest), *hospitalization* (storage/treatment record), and *death certificate* (disposal record).

### 1.3 Key Categories of Records in Waste Management

| S. No. | Record Category | Purpose | Statutory Reference |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Waste Generation Records** | Quantity, type, source, frequency of waste produced | SWMR 2016, Rule 4 |
| 2 | **Manifest Documents** | Cradle-to-grave tracking of waste consignment | HOWM 2016, Rule 6–9 |
| 3 | **Storage Records** | On-site storage duration, container compatibility, conditions | CPCB Guidelines |
| 4 | **Treatment & Disposal Records** | Method used, volume processed, residues generated | TSDF Authorization |
| 5 | **Compliance & Audit Records** | Permits, returns, inspection notes, non-conformities | Annual Returns Form |
| 6 | **Financial Records** | Cost of disposal, recycling revenue, EPR fees | GST / Tax Records |
| 7 | **Personnel Records** | Training, health check-ups, PPE issuance | Factory Act, OSHA |
| 8 | **Emergency / Incident Records** | Spills, fires, near-misses, corrective actions | EP Act 1986 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical / Quantitative Standard:**
> As per CPCB guidance, hazardous waste records must be retained for a minimum period of **3 years** (extendable to **5 years** for specific categories) and biomedical waste records for a minimum of **5 years** post-disposal.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Pillars of Effective Record Keeping

Record keeping in waste management rests on **three operational pillars**:

1. **Accuracy** – Records must reflect *what actually happened* in the field. False documentation is a punishable offence under Section 15 of the Environment (Protection) Act, 1986.
2. **Traceability** – Every record must allow reverse-tracking from disposal point back to the source (and forward-tracking from source to disposal).
3. **Accessibility** – Records must be retrievable on demand by regulators, auditors, and internal management within a stipulated time (typically **24–72 hours** for hazardous waste).

### 2.2 The Manifest System (Cradle-to-Grave Tracking)

The **manifest system** is the most legally significant record-keeping instrument in hazardous and biomedical waste management. It is a **multi-copy, sequentially numbered document** that accompanies every consignment of waste from the point of generation to its final treatment, storage, or disposal facility (TSDF).

**Standard Manifest Workflow (HOWM Rules, 2016):**

- **Copy 1 (White):** Retained by the **Generator** (waste producer).
- **Copy 2 (Yellow):** Travels with the **Transporter** and is handed to the TSDF.
- **Copy 3 (Pink):** Sent to the **State Pollution Control Board (SPCB)** by the generator.
- **Copy 4 (Green):** Returned by the TSDF to the **Generator** as proof of receipt.
- **Copy 5 (Blue):** Returned by the TSDF to the **SPCB** as final disposal acknowledgement.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A manifest is considered **"closed"** only when the SPCB receives confirmation from the TSDF that the waste has reached its final destination. An **"open manifest"** (i.e., waste in transit with no closure) for more than the stipulated transit time is a **reportable non-compliance**.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Metric / Formula | Expression | Units | Application |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Waste Generation Rate** | $W_{gr} = \dfrac{M_{gen}}{T_{period}}$ | $\text{kg/day}$ or $\text{MT/month}$ | Baseline estimation |
| **Storage Capacity Utilization** | $U_{s} = \dfrac{Q_{stored}}{Q_{max}} \times 100$ | $\%$ | Inventory control |
| **Recovery / Recycling Rate** | $R_{r} = \dfrac{M_{recycled}}{M_{generated}} \times 100$ | $\%$ | Circular economy KPI |
| **Disposal Compliance Ratio** | $C_{d} = \dfrac{N_{closed\,manifests}}{N_{total\,manifests}} \times 100$ | $\%$ | Regulatory KPI |
| **Specific Disposal Cost** | $C_{sp} = \dfrac{C_{total}}{M_{disposed}}$ | $\text{₹/kg}$ or $\text{₹/MT}$ | Financial auditing |
| **Record Completeness Index** | $RCI = \dfrac{N_{complete\,records}}{N_{total\,records}} \times 100$ | $\%$ | Audit readiness |
| **Hazardous Waste Diversion Rate** | $D_{r} = \dfrac{M_{treated\,and\,recovered}}{M_{total\,hazardous}} \times 100$ | $\%$ | Sustainability reporting |
| **Manifest Closure Time (Average)** | $t_{closure} = \dfrac{\sum (t_{final} - t_{generated})}{N}$ | $\text{days}$ | Efficiency metric |

> [!NOTE]
> **Important Note for Students:**
> In KTU answer scripts, always state the **unit of measurement** and the **legal compliance threshold** alongside any formula. For example, "Disposal Compliance Ratio is 100% in a fully compliant facility; any value below **95%** is a red flag for auditors."

### 2.4 Engineering Utility of Record Keeping

- **Operational Utility:** Enables *predictive maintenance* of incinerators, autoclaves, and composting units through downtime and throughput logs.
- **Legal Utility:** Forms the primary **evidence base** during SPCB inspections, NGT (National Green Tribunal) hearings, and environmental clearances.
- **Financial Utility:** Supports **Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR)** accounting, GST refunds on recycled materials, and corporate ESG (Environmental, Social, Governance) disclosures.
- **Scientific Utility:** Provides longitudinal data for **Life Cycle Assessment (LCA)** studies and **Material Flow Analysis (MFA)** at city / industry level.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Annual Record-Retention Volume (Worked Numerical)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a **high-frequency KTU problem** — students are often asked to compute the volume of records a facility must maintain over a statutory retention period.

**Problem Statement:**
A hospital generates an average of **120 kg/day of biomedical waste**. Each day, **8 physical record entries** (waste category, weight, segregation time, container ID, operator name, vehicle dispatch time, manifest number, and SPCB submission acknowledgment) are generated. Each record occupies approximately **2 pages** in the physical register. **How many total pages of records must the hospital retain at the end of 1 year**, given the statutory retention period of **5 years**?

#### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Compute daily record volume.**

$$
N_{daily} = 8 \text{ entries} \times 2 \text{ pages/entry} = 16 \text{ pages/day}
$$

This represents the *gross* documentation footprint generated per day of operation.

**Step 2 — Compute annual record volume.**

$$
N_{annual} = 16 \text{ pages/day} \times 365 \text{ days/year} = 5840 \text{ pages/year}
$$

We assume 365 operating days (continuous operation, as is typical for a hospital).

**Step 3 — Apply the statutory retention period of 5 years.**

$$
N_{retention} = 5840 \text{ pages/year} \times 5 \text{ years} = 29{,}200 \text{ pages}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert pages to standard physical storage units (reams).**

Since 1 ream = **500 pages**, the equivalent physical volume is:

$$
N_{reams} = \frac{29{,}200 \text{ pages}}{500 \text{ pages/ream}} = 58.4 \text{ reams}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute approximate storage footprint.**

If each ream occupies a volume of approximately **0.0025 m³**, total record storage volume is:

$$
V_{records} = 58.4 \times 0.0025 = 0.146 \text{ m}^{3}
$$

This fits comfortably within a single **standard office almirah (≈ 0.4 m³)**.

**Final Answer:**

$$
N_{retention} = 29{,}200 \text{ pages} \equiv 58.4 \text{ reams} \equiv 0.146 \text{ m}^{3} \text{ of physical archive}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic Recap:**
> Step 1 multiplies entries by pages per entry to obtain daily volume. Step 2 multiplies by 365 to annualize. Step 3 multiplies by 5 years (the statutory retention window). Step 4 normalizes pages to reams (the standard archival unit). Step 5 multiplies by the volumetric density of a ream to yield cubic-meter storage.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Record Type vs. Retention Period vs. Owner

| Record Type | Format | Retention Period | Custodian (Owner) | Audit Frequency |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Waste Manifest | Hard copy + digital | **5 years** post-disposal | Generator & TSDF | Quarterly |
| Daily Generation Log | Register / ERP | **3 years** | Waste Management Officer | Monthly |
| Annual Environmental Return (Form-IV) | Online (OCMMS portal) | **Permanent (server)** | SPCB / Occupier | Annually |
| Biomedical Waste Autoclave Log | Hard + digital | **5 years** | Hospital Infection Control | Monthly |
| Spill / Incident Report | Hard + digital | **Indefinite** (legal record) | EHS Officer | On occurrence |
| EPR Credit Certificates | Digital (CPCB portal) | **Permanent** | Producer / Recycler | Annually |
| Vehicle GPS / Trip Log | Digital (telematics) | **2 years** | Transport Contractor | Monthly |
| Employee Training Records | Hard + HRMS | **Duration of employment + 3 years** | HR / Training Cell | Annually |

### 3.3 Worked Example: Manifest Compliance Ratio

**Problem:** A common biomedical waste treatment facility (CBWTF) issued **1,200 manifests** in a calendar year. By the closing date, **1,134 manifests** were closed (i.e., confirmed disposal receipts were received from the facility). Calculate the **Disposal Compliance Ratio** and comment on the regulatory standing of the facility.

**Step 1 — Identify the relevant formula.**

$$
C_{d} = \frac{N_{closed\,manifests}}{N_{total\,manifests}} \times 100
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the values.**

$$
C_{d} = \frac{1134}{1200} \times 100
$$

**Step 3 — Perform the division.**

$$
\frac{1134}{1200} = 0.945
$$

**Step 4 — Multiply by 100 to express as a percentage.**

$$
C_{d} = 94.5\%
$$

**Step 5 — Interpret the result.**

Since the compliance ratio is **below 95%**, the facility is **non-compliant** with CPCB closure benchmarks. The **66 unclosed manifests** represent waste consignments whose final disposal cannot be legally proven, triggering a **show-cause notice** from the SPCB.

**Final Answer:**

$$
\boxed{C_{d} = 94.5\% \quad \text{(Non-compliant; immediate corrective action required)}}
$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Manifest Flow Diagram (Cradle-to-Grave Tracking)

The following Mermaid block diagrams the sequential flow of a hazardous / biomedical waste manifest from the point of generation to final SPCB closure.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Generator Site] --> B[Waste Segregation and Storage]
    B --> C[Manifest Preparation Copy 1 White]
    C --> D[Transporter Copy 2 Yellow]
    D --> E[TSDF Receipt and Acceptance]
    E --> F[TSDF Treatment Disposal]
    F --> G[Copy 3 Returned to Generator Pink]
    E --> H[Copy 4 Sent to SPCB Blue]
    F --> I[Copy 5 Sent to SPCB Green Final]
    I --> J[SPCB Manifest Closure]
    H --> J
    G --> J
    J --> K[Closed Manifest Archived 5 Years]
    K --> L[Annual Audit and EPR Reconciliation]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:**
> Node A is the waste origin (e.g., hospital, factory). Node D represents the transportation leg, during which the manifest physically accompanies the consignment. Nodes G, H, and I represent the three return paths (to generator, to SPCB pre-disposal, to SPCB post-disposal). Node J is the legal closure point. Node L represents downstream audit activity. **Until Node J is reached, the manifest is "open" and the waste is in regulatory limbo.**

### 4.2 Record-Keeping Functional Architecture Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Field Operations Layer] --> P2[Data Capture Layer]
    P2 --> P3[Storage and Database Layer]
    P3 --> P4[Analysis and Reporting Layer]
    P4 --> P5[Regulatory Submission Layer]
    P4 --> P6[Internal Audit Layer]
    P4 --> P7[Financial and EPR Layer]
    P5 --> P8[SPCB CPCB Portal]
    P6 --> P9[Internal EHS Review]
    P7 --> P10[Finance and ESG Disclosure]
    P8 --> Q[Compliance Status]
    P9 --> Q
    P10 --> Q
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Block-Level Explanation:**
> - **P1 (Field Operations):** Where the data originates — weighbridges, barcode scans, manual entry, IoT sensors.
> - **P2 (Data Capture):** Digitization layer — barcode scanners, mobile apps, GPS telematics, RFID tags.
> - **P3 (Storage):** Centralized ERP / cloud database, with **3-2-1 backup rule** (3 copies, 2 media, 1 offsite).
> - **P4 (Analysis):** Dashboards, KPIs, manifest closure rates, generation trends.
> - **P5/P6/P7:** Three downstream consumers — regulator, internal auditor, finance team.

### 4.3 Record-Keeping Compliance Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S{Is Record Mandatory under SWMR 2016 or HOWM 2016?}
    S -- Yes --> T[Document in Standardized Format]
    S -- No --> U[Voluntary Internal Record]
    T --> V{Is Waste Hazardous or Biomedical?}
    V -- Yes --> W[Apply 5 Year Retention]
    V -- No --> X[Apply 3 Year Retention]
    W --> Y[Submit Annual Return to SPCB]
    X --> Y
    Y --> Z{Is Manifest Closed within Transit Time?}
    Z -- Yes --> AA[Compliant]
    Z -- No --> AB[Trigger Non-Compliance Report]
    AB --> AC[Corrective Action and Re-Audit]
    AC --> Y
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define the term "Manifest" as used in hazardous waste management under the HOWM Rules, 2016. Mention any **three** key contents of a manifest document.*

**Model Answer:**

A **Manifest** is a standardized, sequentially numbered, multi-copy tracking document that accompanies every consignment of hazardous or other waste from the point of generation (occupier) to the TSDF (Treatment, Storage, and Disposal Facility), establishing *cradle-to-grave* accountability.

**Three key contents of a manifest:**
1. **Waste generator details** — name, address, authorization number, contact.
2. **Waste description** — category, quantity (kg/MT), physical state, UN hazard classification, H-code.
3. **Transporter and TSDF details** — vehicle number, transporter authorization, TSDF registration number.
4. (Bonus) **Date and time stamps** of pickup, transit, and receipt.

*(Full 3 marks awarded for: correct definition + 3 distinct contents + at least 1 statutory reference.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*State the **statutory record retention period** for (a) hazardous waste records, and (b) biomedical waste records, as per CPCB guidelines.*

**Model Answer:**

(a) **Hazardous waste records** (HOWM Rules, 2016): Minimum retention of **3 years**, extendable to **5 years** for certain categories and on specific SPCB directions.

(b) **Biomedical waste records** (BMW Rules, 2016, amended 2018): Minimum retention of **5 years** post-final disposal of the waste consignment.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> Students frequently confuse the retention period for *occupier annual returns* (which are **permanent** on the CPCB/SPCB portal) with the *physical record* retention period. Distinguish clearly between **physical/digital file retention** and **statutory server-side archival**.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU ESE 2024 Scheme regulations, students must answer **ONE full question of 14 marks** with internal choice. Below are **TWO alternative 14-mark questions**, each with sub-parts of 7 marks.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **manifest system** under the Hazardous and Other Wastes (Management and Transboundary Movement) Rules, 2016. Describe its **workflow**, the **five colour-coded copies**, and the role of each stakeholder. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A district hospital generates an average of **80 kg/day of biomedical waste**. The records indicate that during the year 2023, a total of **9,500 manifests** were issued by the hospital, of which **8,835 manifests** were closed within the stipulated time. Calculate the **Manifest Compliance Ratio** and comment on the hospital's regulatory standing. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Answer to Q.A(a):**

The **manifest system** under HOWM Rules, 2016 is a *cradle-to-grave* tracking mechanism designed to ensure that every kilogram of hazardous waste is legally accounted for from its point of origin to its final, environmentally sound disposal.

**Workflow of the Manifest System:**

1. **Generator (Occupier):** Identifies the waste, classifies it, packs it in compatible containers, and prepares the manifest in **six parts** (five physical copies + one for records).
2. **Transporter:** Verifies the manifest, signs it, and physically carries the consignment along with the **yellow copy** to the TSDF.
3. **TSDF:** Receives the waste, verifies quantities, signs the manifest, retains one copy, and returns confirmation copies to both the generator and the SPCB.
4. **SPCB:** Receives copies from both the generator (pre-transport declaration) and TSDF (post-disposal confirmation). It then **closes the manifest** in the official register.

**Five Colour-Coded Copies:**

| Copy | Colour | Custodian | Purpose |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | White | Generator (Occupier) | Proof of dispatch |
| 2 | Yellow | Transporter | In-transit accountability |
| 3 | Pink | Generator → SPCB | Pre-transport declaration |
| 4 | Green | TSDF → Generator | Receipt acknowledgement |
| 5 | Blue | TSDF → SPCB | Final disposal confirmation |

**Stakeholder Roles:**
- **Generator:** Correct classification, packaging, labelling, and manifest preparation.
- **Transporter:** Safe transit, manifest endorsement at pickup and delivery.
- **TSDF:** Acceptance, treatment, and final disposal with manifest closure.
- **SPCB:** Regulatory oversight, manifest closure, and enforcement.

*(Award 7 marks: 2 marks workflow, 2 marks five copies, 2 marks stakeholder roles, 1 mark statutory reference.)*

---

**Model Answer to Q.A(b):**

**Step 1 — Identify the formula.**

$$
C_{d} = \frac{N_{closed\,manifests}}{N_{total\,manifests}} \times 100
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the values.**

$$
C_{d} = \frac{8835}{9500} \times 100
$$

**Step 3 — Perform the division.**

$$
\frac{8835}{9500} = 0.93
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to percentage.**

$$
C_{d} = 93.0\%
$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation and Regulatory Standing.**

The Manifest Compliance Ratio of the hospital is **93.0%**, which falls **below the 95% benchmark** typically expected by the SPCB. The **665 unclosed manifests** represent waste consignments whose final disposal cannot be legally verified, exposing the hospital to:
- SPCB show-cause notice.
- Suspension / cancellation of authorization.
- Environmental compensation under the **Polluter Pays Principle**.
- Potential prosecution under Section 15 of the EP Act, 1986.

**Corrective Actions Recommended:**
- Real-time tracking of all manifests.
- Weekly reconciliation with the CBWTF.
- Digital manifest system with auto-alerts for unclosed consignments.
- Quarterly internal audits.

**Final Answer:**

$$
\boxed{C_{d} = 93.0\% \quad \text{(Non-compliant; corrective action and SPCB reporting mandated.)}}
$$

*(Award 7 marks: 1 mark formula, 2 marks substitution and computation, 2 marks interpretation, 2 marks corrective action discussion.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Discuss in detail the **types of records** that must be maintained by an occupier under the **Solid Waste Management Rules (SWMR), 2016** and the **Biomedical Waste Management Rules (BMW), 2016 (amended 2018)**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A municipal solid waste (MSW) processing plant receives an average of **450 MT/day** of mixed waste. The plant segregates **38%** as compostable, **22%** as recyclable, and the balance is sent to a sanitary landfill. From the recyclable fraction, the plant recovers **85%** as saleable material and rejects **15%** to landfill. Calculate: **(i)** the **Recovery Rate** of the plant, and **(ii)** the **daily mass of waste reaching the landfill** in MT. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Answer to Q.B(a):**

**Records under SWMR, 2016:**

1. **Daily waste generation register** — quantity segregated, by category.
2. **Collection and transportation records** — vehicle logs, route plans, GPS data.
3. **Processing facility records** — compost output, recyclables sold, rejects.
4. **Annual report to the local body / SPCB** (in **Form-I** for ULBs and **Form-II** for processing facilities).
5. **User fee / gate fee collection records.**
6. **Public complaint and grievance redressal register.**

**Records under BMW Rules, 2016 (amended 2018):**

1. **Biomedical waste generation and segregation log** (daily, category-wise).
2. **Pretreatment records** (for laboratory and chemical waste).
3. **Autoclave / incinerator log books** — temperature, pressure, cycle time, operator name.
4. **Manifest copies** (white for HCF, yellow for CBWTF, copies to SPCB).
5. **Annual biomedical waste return** (submitted in **Form-IV** by **31st January** of the following year).
6. **Training records** of all healthcare workers handling BMW.
7. **Accident reporting records** (needle-stick injuries, spills).

**Statutory Submission Deadlines:**
- SWMR Form-I / Form-II: **Annually, by 30th April**.
- BMW Form-IV: **Annually, by 31st January**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Difference for Examiners:**
> SWMR records emphasize *bulk urban waste streams*, while BMW records focus on *infection control, segregation colour-coding (yellow, red, white, blue), and pretreatment*. Confusing these is a common student error.

*(Award 7 marks: 3 marks for SWMR records, 3 marks for BMW records, 1 mark for statutory deadlines.)*

---

**Model Answer to Q.B(b):**

**Given Data:**
- Total MSW received: $M_{total} = 450 \text{ MT/day}$
- Compostable fraction: $f_c = 38\%$
- Recyclable fraction: $f_r = 22\%$
- Recovery efficiency from recyclables: $\eta_r = 85\%$
- Balance to landfill (residual + rejects): $f_{landfill} = 100\% - 38\% - 22\% = 40\%$

**Part (i): Recovery Rate**

The **Recovery Rate** is defined as the fraction of total waste that is *actually recovered* (composted + recycled as saleable material) relative to total waste.

**Step 1 — Mass of compostable fraction.**

$$
M_c = 450 \times 0.38 = 171 \text{ MT/day}
$$

**Step 2 — Mass of recyclable fraction.**

$$
M_r = 450 \times 0.22 = 99 \text{ MT/day}
$$

**Step 3 — Mass actually recovered from recyclables (saleable).**

$$
M_{r,recovered} = 99 \times 0.85 = 84.15 \text{ MT/day}
$$

**Step 4 — Total mass recovered (assuming 100% composting efficiency for compostable stream).**

$$
M_{recovered} = M_c + M_{r,recovered} = 171 + 84.15 = 255.15 \text{ MT/day}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute Recovery Rate.**

$$
R_r = \frac{M_{recovered}}{M_{total}} \times 100 = \frac{255.15}{450} \times 100
$$

**Step 6 — Final value.**

$$
\boxed{R_r = 56.7\%}
$$

**Part (ii): Daily Mass Reaching Landfill**

Landfill mass = (Residual non-segregated fraction) + (Rejected recyclable fraction).

**Step 1 — Residual non-segregated fraction (40% of total).**

$$
M_{residual} = 450 \times 0.40 = 180 \text{ MT/day}
$$

**Step 2 — Rejected recyclable fraction (15% of recyclable stream).**

$$
M_{reject} = 99 \times 0.15 = 14.85 \text{ MT/day}
$$

**Step 3 — Total landfill mass.**

$$
M_{landfill} = 180 + 14.85 = 194.85 \text{ MT/day}
$$

**Final Answer:**

$$
\boxed{M_{landfill} = 194.85 \text{ MT/day} \quad (\approx 43.3\% \text{ of total input})}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic Recap:**
> Part (i) Recovery Rate = [compostable (100% conversion assumed) + recyclable × recovery efficiency] / total × 100. Part (ii) Landfill = residual (unsegregated balance) + rejects (non-recoverable fraction of recyclables). The two parts together must sum to 100% of input (with a small check: 56.7% recovered + 43.3% landfilled = 100%).

*(Award 7 marks: 3 marks for Recovery Rate calculation with steps, 2 marks for landfill mass derivation, 2 marks for interpretation/percentage check.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the units** in the final answer (e.g., writing "94.5" instead of "94.5%") — costs **0.5 to 1 mark** per question.
> 2. **Confusing *manifest closure rate* with *manifest generation rate*** — these are two different KPIs.
> 3. **Omitting the statutory reference** (e.g., HOWM 2016, BMW 2016) in definition questions — examiners explicitly reward this in the model answer key.
> 4. **Not labelling the Mermaid-style workflow** in long answers (if asked for a flow diagram) — students often write the manifest sequence as text without proper structuring.
> 5. **Mixing up the colour codes of manifest copies** — the most common error is saying "TSDF retains the white copy" (it does not; the white copy is with the generator).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Exam:**

- **Definition:** Record keeping = systematic documentation of all waste management activities for compliance, traceability, and auditing.
- **Manifest System:** Cradle-to-grave tracking under HOWM 2016, with 5 colour-coded copies (White, Yellow, Pink, Green, Blue).
- **Statutory Retention Periods:** Hazardous waste = **3–5 years**; Biomedical waste = **5 years**; Annual returns = **Permanent (server-side)**.
- **Key Formulae to Memorize:** $W_{gr} = M_{gen}/T$, $R_r = M_{recycled}/M_{generated} \times 100$, $C_d = N_{closed}/N_{total} \times 100$, $C_{sp} = C_{total}/M_{disposed}$.
- **Key Compliance Benchmark:** Manifest Compliance Ratio $\geq$ **95%** is considered compliant.
- **Statutory Forms to Remember:** **Form-I / Form-II** (SWMR 2016, by 30th April); **Form-IV** (BMW 2016, by 31st January).
- **Record Custodians:** Generator, Transporter, TSDF, SPCB, EHS Officer, Finance / EPR Cell.
- **Three Pillars of Record Keeping:** **Accuracy**, **Traceability**, **Accessibility**.
- **Failure to Maintain Records** is punishable under **Section 15 of the EP Act, 1986** with imprisonment up to **5 years** and/or fine up to **₹1 lakh** (extendable to ₹5 lakh per day for continuing violations).
- **Best Practice:** Maintain records in **both** physical (hard copy) and digital (ERP / cloud) formats simultaneously — the **3-2-1 backup rule** (3 copies, 2 media, 1 offsite) is the gold standard.
- **EPR (Extended Producer Responsibility):** Records of producer-recycler transactions are now mandatory under the **EPR Framework for Plastic Packaging** (MoEFCC Notification, 2022, amended 2024).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET831 - WASTE MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

**Waste Control** refers to the systematic set of policies, engineering interventions, operational practices, and administrative strategies employed to *regulate*, *minimize*, *treat*, and *safely manage* the generation, storage, collection, processing, and disposal of solid, liquid, and gaseous waste streams. In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, waste control is positioned as a multi-barrier approach operating across the **waste management hierarchy**, with the primary objective of reducing environmental burden, public health risk, and resource depletion.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 — OECET831, Module 2):**
> Waste Control encompasses the integrated deployment of *source reduction*, *waste minimization*, *recycling and reuse*, *treatment technologies*, and *safe disposal mechanisms* to limit adverse environmental and human-health impacts of municipal, industrial, biomedical, and hazardous wastes.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a kitchen sink with a clogged drain. Water keeps pouring in, but nothing flows out properly. You have two options:
1. **Keep mopping the floor** (i.e., keep treating waste *after* it is generated — end-of-pipe control)
2. **Turn off the tap, use less water, and install a strainer to recover food bits** (i.e., control the *source*, reduce the *flow*, and *recover* useful material)

Modern waste control favours the **second** approach. It is not merely about "tidying up" waste after it is created — it is about *preventing* the mess in the first place, *reducing* its quantity, *reusing* what is recoverable, *recycling* raw materials, and *treating* the residue before it pollutes the environment. This integrated philosophy is the heart of **sustainable waste control**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition Recap:**
> - **Source Reduction:** Eliminating waste at the point of generation (most preferred).
> - **Waste Minimization:** Reducing the volume, toxicity, or environmental persistence of generated waste.
> - **Treatment:** Altering waste characteristics to make it less hazardous.
> - **Disposal:** Final, engineered confinement (least preferred — landfill, deep well injection).

### 1.3 Standard Metrics in Waste Control

The following quantitative indicators are universally applied in waste control engineering:

- **Waste Generation Rate (WGR):** Mass of waste produced per capita per day — **0.5 – 1.5 kg/person/day** (KTU/CPHEEO standard for Indian municipalities).
- **Per Capita Waste Generation:** Measured in **kg/capita/day**.
- **Collection Efficiency (η_c):** Ratio of waste collected to waste generated, expressed as a **percentage (%)**.
- **Recovery Rate (R_r):** Fraction of waste diverted from disposal through recycling/reuse, expressed as **%**.
- **Diversion Rate:** Percentage of waste redirected from landfills.
- **Treatment Efficiency (η_t):** Reduction in pollutant load achieved by a treatment process, expressed as **%** or in mass/volume units.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Hook:** "**P-R-R-R-T-D**" — *Prevent, Reduce, Reuse, Recycle, Treat, Dispose*. This is the canonical sequence of the **Waste Management Hierarchy**, read from most-preferred to least-preferred control option.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Parameter Sheet

### 2.1 The Waste Management Hierarchy (WMH)

The WMH is the **conceptual backbone** of all waste control frameworks, including those adopted by the **MoEFCC (India)**, **EPA (USA)**, and the **Basel Convention**. It is a tiered priority system that ranks control options from most environmentally desirable to least.

**Priority order (top = most preferred):**

1. **Prevention / Source Reduction** — *Avoid* waste creation.
2. **Minimization** — Reduce quantity/toxicity.
3. **Reuse** — Use the same item again for the same or different purpose.
4. **Recycle / Recovery** — Reprocess into new raw materials or energy.
5. **Treatment** — Detoxify, neutralize, decompose, or stabilize waste.
6. **Disposal** — Engineered landfilling, incineration with energy recovery (as a last resort).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Each successive lower tier has a **greater environmental footprint** and **higher economic cost**. KTU examiners frequently ask students to justify the WMH using the **3R / 4R / 5R principle**.

### 2.2 Detailed Control Strategies

#### A. Source Reduction (Tier 1 — Most Preferred)
Operational changes in production, packaging, and consumption patterns that prevent waste formation.

- **Material substitution** — replacing toxic/hazardous inputs with benign alternatives (e.g., lead-free solder, water-based paints).
- **Product redesign** — designing for durability, repairability, and disassembly.
- **Packaging reduction** — minimal, biodegradable, or reusable packaging.
- **Inventory control** — just-in-time (JIT) manufacturing to reduce obsolete stock.
- **Process modification** — closed-loop manufacturing, lean production.

#### B. Waste Minimization (Tier 2)
Achieved through:
- **Volume reduction** (compaction, shredding, dewatering).
- **Toxicity reduction** (substitution, dilution only as last option).
- **On-site segregation** at the source — the **first and most critical** step in Indian waste management (Solid Waste Management Rules 2016 mandate source segregation into **biodegradable, non-biodegradable, domestic hazardous, and sanitary waste**).

#### C. Reuse (Tier 3)
- Direct reuse without modification (refilling bottles, reusing containers).
- Repurposing (using glass bottles as storage jars).
- Reconditioning/refurbishing (refurbished electronics, rebuilt machinery).

#### D. Recycling / Recovery (Tier 4)
- **Material recycling** — converting waste into the same or different raw material (paper, plastic, metal, glass).
- **Composting** — aerobic biological stabilization of organic waste → humus.
- **Anaerobic Digestion (AD)** — controlled anaerobic decomposition → biogas (CH₄ + CO₂) + digestate.
- **Energy recovery / Refuse Derived Fuel (RDF)** — converting non-recyclable combustible fractions into fuel pellets for cement kilns/thermal plants.
- **Waste-to-Energy (WtE)** — incineration with energy capture.

#### E. Treatment (Tier 5)
- **Physical:** Screening, sedimentation, filtration, adsorption.
- **Chemical:** Neutralization, oxidation, reduction, precipitation, ion exchange.
- **Biological:** Activated sludge, trickling filters, biofilm reactors, bioremediation.
- **Thermal:** Incineration, pyrolysis, gasification, plasma arc.
- **Stabilization/Solidification:** Cement-based, pozzolanic, or polymer encapsulation of hazardous waste.

#### F. Disposal (Tier 6 — Last Resort)
- **Engineered Sanitary Landfill** with liner, leachate collection, and gas ventilation.
- **Secured Landfill** for hazardous waste (double-liner system, leachate treatment).
- **Deep well injection** (rare, used for specific industrial liquid waste).

### 2.3 Air Pollution Control from Waste Operations

Waste handling facilities (incinerators, landfills, composting yards) emit particulate matter (PM), oxides of nitrogen (NOₓ), sulphur dioxide (SO₂), carbon monoxide (CO), volatile organic compounds (VOCs), dioxins, furans, and methane (CH₄). Control devices include:

- **Cyclone separators** — for coarse PM removal.
- **Electrostatic precipitators (ESP)** — for fine PM (99%+ efficiency).
- **Baghouse filters** — fabric filtration for fine PM.
- **Wet scrubbers** — for acid gases (SO₂, HCl) and PM.
- **Selective Catalytic Reduction (SCR)** — for NOₓ reduction.
- **Activated carbon injection** — for dioxins/furans and mercury.

### 2.4 Water Pollution Control (Leachate Management)

Landfills generate **leachate** — a high-strength wastewater containing heavy metals, BOD, COD, ammonia, and chlorinated organics. Control systems include:

- **Low-permeability liners** (HDPE, compacted clay).
- **Leachate collection and removal systems (LCRS).**
- **Leachate treatment** — biological (sequencing batch reactor, moving bed biofilm reactor) + physical-chemical (reverse osmosis, adsorption, Fenton oxidation).

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Parameter / Formula Cheat Sheet

| Parameter | Symbol | Formula / Definition | Standard Unit / Value | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Per Capita Waste Generation | $W_p$ | $W_p = W_{total} / (P \cdot t)$ | **kg/capita/day** (India: 0.5 – 1.5) | Sizing bins, trucks, landfills |
| Collection Efficiency | $\eta_c$ | $\eta_c = (W_{collected} / W_{generated}) \times 100$ | **%** | Benchmarking municipal performance |
| Recovery Rate | $R_r$ | $R_r = (W_{recovered} / W_{generated}) \times 100$ | **%** | Quantifies recycling effectiveness |
| Treatment Efficiency (COD) | $\eta_{COD}$ | $\eta_{COD} = ((C_{in} - C_{out}) / C_{in}) \times 100$ | **%** | Performance of leachate/wastewater treatment |
| Particulate Removal Efficiency | $\eta_{PM}$ | $\eta_{PM} = ((C_{in} - C_{out}) / C_{in}) \times 100$ | **%** (ESP ≥ 99 %) | Air pollution control design |
| Moisture Content | $M$ | $M = (W_{water} / W_{wet}) \times 100$ | **%** (Indian MSW: 40 – 60 %) | Determines biodegradability, calorific value |
| Calorific Value | $CV$ | Measured by bomb calorimeter | **kCal/kg** (Indian MSW: 1000 – 1500) | Determines WtE / incineration feasibility |
| Leachate Generation Rate | $L_r$ | $L_r = (P \cdot A \cdot k) / (1 + k \cdot t)$ | **m³/ha/day** (Schmidt's formula) | Landfill design |
| Biogas Yield | $Y_{bg}$ | $Y_{bg} = Y_0 \cdot (1 - e^{-k \cdot t})$ | **m³ CH₄/kg VS** | AD plant sizing |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Cue:** When asked "list the components of waste control", never give a single answer. Always list **all six tiers of WMH** with at least **two examples each**. This is the most frequently tested conceptual layout.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Municipal level:** Used by ULBs (Urban Local Bodies) for Swachh Bharat Mission compliance.
- **Industrial level:** ISO 14001 Environmental Management Systems rely on waste control metrics.
- **National level:** CPCB/SPCB monitoring, EPR (Extended Producer Responsibility) compliance under PWM Rules 2016.
- **Global level:** UN Sustainable Development Goals (SDG 11, 12, 13, 14, 15) directly map onto waste control outcomes.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Logical Derivations

### 3.1 Analytical Matrix: Selection of Waste Control Method

The following matrix is a decision-support framework frequently referenced in KTU answers to "select the appropriate waste control method" type questions.

| Waste Type / Characteristic | Recommended Control Method | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| **Biodegradable organic kitchen waste** | Composting / Anaerobic Digestion | Nutrient recovery (N, P, K); biogas generation; low energy cost |
| **Dry recyclables (paper, plastic, metal, glass)** | Material Recycling (MRF route) | Closed-loop resource recovery; high market value |
| **Hazardous industrial waste** | Secure landfill + pre-treatment | Toxicity containment; engineered isolation |
| **Biomedical waste** | Autoclave / Incineration (as per BMWM Rules 2016) | Pathogen destruction; volume reduction |
| **E-waste** | Dismantling + Hydrometallurgical recovery | Recovery of Au, Ag, Cu, Pd; prevents toxic leaching |
| **Mixed MSW with high moisture** | Mechanical Drying → RDF / Biomethanation | Improves calorific value, enables WtE |
| **Construction & Demolition waste** | Crushing → aggregate recycling | Reduces sand mining pressure |
| **Plastic waste (multi-layer)** | Pyrolysis → fuel oil (where feasible) | Avoids landfill accumulation; energy recovery |

### 3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation: Collection Efficiency ($\eta_c$)

> **Question context:** A city generates **1,200 tonnes/day** of municipal solid waste. The collection network picks up **960 tonnes/day**. Calculate the **collection efficiency**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Identify given values.} \quad W_{generated} &= 1200 \text{ tonnes/day} \\
W_{collected} &= 960 \text{ tonnes/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 2: Recall the governing formula.} \quad \eta_c &= \frac{W_{collected}}{W_{generated}} \times 100
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 3: Substitute numerical values.} \quad \eta_c &= \frac{960}{1200} \times 100
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 4: Simplify the fraction.} \quad \eta_c &= 0.80 \times 100
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{\eta_c = 80\%}
$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** An 80 % collection efficiency is considered *moderately acceptable* by CPHEEO norms (target ≥ 95 % for Class I cities). The remaining 20 % (240 tonnes/day) represents litter, open burning, drain clogging, and disease-vector proliferation — the **residual uncontrolled waste** that the control system must reduce.

### 3.3 Step-by-Step Derivation: Treatment Efficiency of an ESP

> **Question context:** An electrostatic precipitator handles **1,000,000 m³/h** of flue gas from a municipal waste incinerator. Inlet particulate concentration = **2,500 mg/m³**, outlet = **25 mg/m³**. Calculate (a) **particulate removal efficiency**, and (b) **mass of PM captured per hour**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Define efficiency formula.} \quad \eta_{PM} &= \frac{C_{in} - C_{out}}{C_{in}} \times 100
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 2: Substitute values.} \quad \eta_{PM} &= \frac{2500 - 25}{2500} \times 100
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 3: Compute.} \quad \eta_{PM} &= \frac{2475}{2500} \times 100 = 99.0\% \quad \text{(Acceptable, meets CPCB norm of } \geq 99\% \text{)}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 4: Mass of PM captured per hour.} \quad m_{PM} &= Q \cdot (C_{in} - C_{out}) \\
m_{PM} &= 1{,}000{,}000 \text{ m}^3/\text{h} \times 2475 \text{ mg/m}^3 \\
m_{PM} &= 2{,}475{,}000{,}000 \text{ mg/h} \\
m_{PM} &= 2475 \text{ kg/h} = 2.475 \text{ tonnes/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{\eta_{PM} = 99\% \quad \text{and} \quad m_{PM} = 2.475 \text{ tonnes/hour}}
$$

### 3.4 Step-by-Step Derivation: Anaerobic Digestion Biogas Yield

> **Question context:** An anaerobic digester is fed with **10 tonnes/day** of kitchen waste with **Volatile Solids (VS)** content = **80 %**. Methane yield coefficient $Y_0 = 0.40 \text{ m}^3 \text{ CH}_4 / \text{kg VS}$, first-order rate constant $k = 0.25 \text{ day}^{-1}$, HRT $t = 20$ days. Calculate the **methane yield per day**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Mass of VS fed per day.} \quad M_{VS} &= 10{,}000 \text{ kg} \times 0.80 = 8000 \text{ kg VS/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 2: Methane yield fraction at HRT } t. \quad f &= 1 - e^{-k \cdot t} \\
f &= 1 - e^{-(0.25)(20)} \\
f &= 1 - e^{-5} \\
f &= 1 - 0.00674 \\
f &= 0.9933
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 3: Daily methane volume.} \quad V_{CH_4} &= M_{VS} \cdot Y_0 \cdot f \\
V_{CH_4} &= 8000 \times 0.40 \times 0.9933 \\
V_{CH_4} &= 3178.5 \text{ m}^3 \text{ CH}_4 / \text{day}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{V_{CH_4} \approx 3179 \text{ m}^3/\text{day} \;\; (\approx 14.2 \text{ GJ/day of energy at 35 MJ/m}^3 \text{ CH}_4)}
$$

### 3.5 Step-by-Step Derivation: Per-Capita Waste Projection

> **Question context:** A city has a current population of **5,00,000**, per capita waste generation $W_p = 0.5$ kg/day. Population growth rate = **2.5 %/year**. Estimate the **waste generated in 5 years** assuming $W_p$ remains constant.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Future population formula.} \quad P_5 &= P_0 \cdot (1 + r)^n \\
P_5 &= 5{,}00{,}000 \cdot (1.025)^5
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 2: Compute growth factor.} \quad (1.025)^5 &= 1.1314 \\
P_5 &= 5{,}65{,}700 \text{ persons}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 3: Daily waste generation.} \quad W_5 &= P_5 \cdot W_p \\
W_5 &= 5{,}65{,}700 \times 0.5 \\
W_5 &= 2{,}82{,}850 \text{ kg/day} = 282.85 \text{ tonnes/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{W_5 \approx 283 \text{ tonnes/day} \;\; (\text{increase of } \approx 13.14\% \text{ over 5 years})}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Engineering Insight:** This projection is the standard basis for designing landfills, transfer stations, and treatment plants with a **design horizon of 20–25 years**, typically applying **0.5–1 % per annum increase in per-capita waste generation** on top of population growth.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Waste Management Hierarchy (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[TIER 1: PREVENTION / SOURCE REDUCTION] --> B[TIER 2: WASTE MINIMIZATION]
    B --> C[TIER 3: REUSE]
    C --> D[TIER 4: RECYCLING / RECOVERY]
    D --> E[TIER 5: TREATMENT]
    E --> F[TIER 6: DISPOSAL]
    A -.->|Most Preferred| G[Goal: Zero Waste]
    F -.->|Last Resort| G
    A1[Material substitution, packaging reduction, product redesign] -.- A
    B1[Volume reduction, toxicity reduction, segregation] -.- B
    C1[Direct reuse, refurbishing, repurposing] -.- C
    D1[Material recycling, composting, AD, RDF, WtE] -.- D
    E1[Physical, Chemical, Biological, Thermal, Stabilization] -.- E
    F1[Sanitary landfill, secured landfill, deep well injection] -.- F
```

### 4.2 Control Strategy Selection Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Start: Identify Waste Type] --> Q1{Is waste biodegradable?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{Mixed with recyclables?}
    Q1 -->|No| Q3{Hazardous?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| R1[Source Segregation First]
    Q2 -->|No| R2[Composting or Anaerobic Digestion]
    Q3 -->|Yes| R3[Secure Landfill plus Pre-treatment]
    Q3 -->|No| R4[Material Recycling MRF Route]
    R1 --> R2
    R1 --> R4
    R2 --> END[Integrated Waste Management System]
    R3 --> END
    R4 --> END
```

### 4.3 Integrated Waste Management — Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph S1[INPUT STREAM]
        IN1[Municipal Solid Waste]
        IN2[Industrial Waste]
        IN3[Biomedical Waste]
        IN4[E Waste]
    end
    subgraph S2[PRIMARY CONTROL: SOURCE]
        SRC1[Source Segregation]
        SRC2[Source Reduction]
    end
    subgraph S3[SECONDARY CONTROL: COLLECTION and SEGREGATION]
        COL1[Door to Door Collection]
        COL2[Transfer Station]
        COL3[Material Recovery Facility MRF]
    end
    subgraph S4[TERTIARY CONTROL: TREATMENT]
        TR1[Composting]
        TR2[Anaerobic Digestion]
        TR3[Incineration with Energy Recovery]
        TR4[Autoclave for BMW]
        TR5[Secure Landfill]
    end
    subgraph S5[OUTPUT: RESOURCE and DISCHARGE]
        OUT1[Compost to Agriculture]
        OUT2[Biogas to Grid]
        OUT3[Electricity from WtE]
        OUT4[Recycled Materials to Industry]
        OUT5[Stabilized Residue to Engineered Landfill]
    end
    IN1 --> SRC1
    IN2 --> SRC1
    IN3 --> SRC1
    IN4 --> SRC1
    SRC1 --> SRC2
    SRC2 --> COL1
    COL1 --> COL2
    COL2 --> COL3
    COL3 --> TR1
    COL3 --> TR2
    COL3 --> TR3
    COL3 --> TR4
    COL3 --> TR5
    TR1 --> OUT1
    TR2 --> OUT2
    TR3 --> OUT3
    COL3 --> OUT4
    TR5 --> OUT5
```

### 4.4 Air Pollution Control Train for an MSW Incinerator

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    INC[MSW Incinerator] --> Q1[Quench: Rapid Cooling to 200C]
    Q1 --> DSR[Dry Sorbent Reactor: Lime Injection for Acid Gases]
    DSR --> BF[Baghouse Filter: PM Removal plus Dioxin Capture via Activated Carbon]
    BF --> SCR[Selective Catalytic Reduction: NOx Reduction using NH3]
    SCR --> STK[Stack Emission Monitoring: CEMS]
    Q1 -.->|PM10, PM2.5| DSR
    DSR -.->|HCl, SO2| BF
    BF -.->|PM, Dioxins, Hg| SCR
    SCR -.->|NOx| STK
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Style]** Define the *Waste Management Hierarchy* and list its tiers in the correct order of preference.

**Model Answer:**

The **Waste Management Hierarchy (WMH)** is a ranked sequence of waste control options, organized from the **most environmentally preferred** to the **least preferred**. The six tiers are:

1. **Prevention / Source Reduction** (most preferred — eliminates waste creation)
2. **Minimization** (reduces quantity and/or toxicity)
3. **Reuse** (redeploys the same item)
4. **Recycle / Recovery** (reprocesses material or energy)
5. **Treatment** (detoxifies, stabilizes, or decomposes)
6. **Disposal** (engineered landfilling, last resort)

Higher tiers have a **lower environmental footprint** and are aligned with circular-economy and sustainable-development principles.

---

#### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style]** Distinguish between *Source Reduction* and *Waste Minimization* with two examples each.

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Source Reduction | Waste Minimization |
|---|---|---|
| **Aim** | Eliminate or *avoid* waste generation | Reduce *quantity/toxicity* of waste already being generated |
| **Stage** | Pre-generation (design, procurement) | During / post-generation |
| **Examples** | 1. Replacing plastic bags with jute/cloth bags. 2. Lead-free solder in electronics manufacturing. | 1. Compaction of bulky waste at transfer stations. 2. Neutralization of acidic industrial effluent. |

Source reduction is a **subset** of waste minimization but ranks *higher* in the WMH.

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> **KTU Pattern:** Each 14-mark question typically has two sub-parts: **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Internal choice is offered between **Question A** and **Question B**.

---

#### ✅ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Style]**

**(a)** With a neat flowchart, explain the **Waste Management Hierarchy** and discuss the role of **source segregation** as the *first step* in municipal solid waste control under the Solid Waste Management Rules, 2016. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A city generates **1,500 tonnes/day** of MSW with the following composition: biodegradable 50 %, recyclable (paper, plastic, metal, glass) 30 %, inert 15 %, domestic hazardous 5 %. Calculate the **mass flow of each fraction per day**, and estimate the **potential compost yield** assuming a **25 % mass loss** during composting. Also compute the **collection efficiency** if only **1,275 tonnes/day** is collected. **[7 marks]**

---

##### Model Solution — Question A (a)

The **Waste Management Hierarchy (WMH)** ranks waste control options by environmental desirability. A neat hierarchical flowchart is as follows (also see Section 4.1):

**Tier 1 — Source Reduction (most preferred):**
- Eliminating waste at design and procurement stage.
- Examples: minimum-packaging products, lightweighting of PET bottles.

**Tier 2 — Waste Minimization:**
