topics.title: Planning of a Recycling Programme
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET831 - WASTE MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Planning of a Recycling Programme

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 4 — PLANNING OF A RECYCLING PROGRAMME

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Planning of a Recycling Programme** is the **systematic, multi-stage engineering and managerial process** of designing, organizing, financing, and operating a structured system to recover, process, and reintroduce waste materials into the production-consumption cycle, with the objectives of **resource conservation, landfill diversion, pollution reduction, and economic value recovery**, in alignment with the principles of **Integrated Solid Waste Management (ISWM)** and the **waste hierarchy (Reduce → Reuse → Recycle → Recovery → Disposal)**.

The planning stage constitutes the **conceptual and engineering foundation** of any municipal, industrial, or institutional recycling programme. It bridges the gap between **waste characterization** (Module 1–2 concepts) and **actual field implementation** (Module 5–6).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **🧠 Real-World Analogy — "Planning a School Recycling Drive"**
>
> Imagine your college wants to start a paper and plastic recycling initiative on campus. Before placing bins, the student council must:
> 1. **Survey** how much paper and plastic is generated daily.
> 2. **Decide** what the goal is (e.g., divert 50% from landfill).
> 3. **Identify** who will buy the segregated paper (scrap dealer market).
> 4. **Plan** bin placement, collection schedule, transport route.
> 5. **Calculate** cost of bins, transport vs. revenue from sale.
> 6. **Educate** students through posters and workshops.
> 7. **Monitor** monthly performance using weight data.
>
> This is exactly what a city or industry does at scale. **Planning** is the *blueprint*; without it, the recycling programme collapses under inefficiency, contamination, and financial loss.

### 1.3 Why Planning is the Most Critical Stage

| Aspect | Without Proper Planning | With Systematic Planning |
|---|---|---|
| **Cost** | Bleeding budget, unviable operations | Optimized cost per tonne |
| **Quality of Recyclables** | High contamination (>20%) | Low contamination (<5%) |
| **Public Participation** | Low, sporadic engagement | Sustained, behavioural change |
| **Market Linkage** | No buyers, stockpiling | Stable offtake agreements |
| **Environmental Outcome** | Negligible diversion | Measurable landfill diversion |
| **Regulatory Compliance** | Violations of SWM Rules 2016 | Full compliance with KMBR / PWM |

### 1.4 Key Standard Metrics Used

The following standard metrics (also in **bold** for KTU 2024 emphasis) are universally applied in recycling programme planning:

- **Recovery Rate (RR)**
- **Collection Efficiency (CE)**
- **Diversion Rate (DR)**
- **Contamination Rate (CR)**
- **Material Recovery Facility (MRF) Throughput**
- **Cost per Tonne (CPT)**
- **Participation Rate (PR)**

### 1.5 Regulatory Backdrop (KTU 2024 / India Context)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every recycling programme plan in India must be benchmarked against:
> - **Solid Waste Management Rules, 2016** (MoEFCC)
> - **Plastic Waste Management Rules, 2016 (Amended 2018, 2022)**
> - **E-Waste Management Rules, 2022**
> - **Construction & Demolition Waste Management Rules, 2016**
> - **Kerala Municipal Building Rules (KMBR)** — local enforcement
> - **Swachh Bharat Mission 2.0** — national targets
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The 10-Stage Framework of Recycling Programme Planning

A KTU-aligned recycling programme plan follows a **structured 10-stage engineering framework**, each stage delivering a specific decision input to the next:

### 🔹 Stage 1 — Baseline Assessment & Waste Characterization

- Conduct **waste generation surveys** (door-to-door, ward-level, institutional).
- Perform **seasonal composition analysis** (typically 4 seasons × 7 days).
- Quantify **per-capita waste generation** in kg/capita/day.
- Identify the **recyclable fraction** (paper, plastic, glass, metal, e-waste, textile, organic).

> The output of this stage is the **"Recyclable Material Inventory (RMI)"** — the foundation for all downstream planning.

### 🔹 Stage 2 — Goal Setting & Target Definition (SMART Objectives)

Goals must be **Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time-bound**:
- Example: *"Achieve 75% source-segregation compliance by Month 12."*
- Example: *"Divert 30% of total MSW from landfill within 24 months."*

### 🔹 Stage 3 — Market Analysis & Offtake Linkage

- Identify **end-market recyclers** (paper mills, plastic re-processors, aluminium smelters, e-waste dismantlers).
- Survey **commodity prices** (volatile — must use rolling 12-month average).
- Establish **buy-back agreements / MOUs** with at least 2 buyers per material to avoid monopsony risk.

### 🔹 Stage 4 — Collection System Design

- Decide on **collection modality**:
  - *Door-to-door segregated collection*
  - *Bring centres / drop-off points*
  - *Curbside collection with coloured bins (Blue – Dry, Green – Wet, Red – Domestic Hazard)*
- Design **collection routes** using **vehicle routing optimization** (CVRP – Capacitated Vehicle Routing Problem).

### 🔹 Stage 5 — Sorting & Processing Infrastructure (MRF Planning)

- Plan a **Material Recovery Facility (MRF)** — manual, semi-automatic, or fully automated.
- Decide on **technology level** based on waste volume:
  - < 50 TPD → Manual MRF
  - 50–500 TPD → Semi-automatic (trommel, magnetic separator, eddy current)
  - \> 500 TPD → Fully automated with optical sorters (NIR)

### 🔹 Stage 6 — Transportation Logistics

- Optimize **transfer station** locations (typically every 15–20 km).
- Calculate **vehicle capacity utilization** and **trip frequency**.
- Minimize **empty-running kilometres**.

### 🔹 Stage 7 — Financial & Economic Feasibility Analysis

- Prepare **Capital Expenditure (CAPEX)** and **Operational Expenditure (OPEX)**.
- Compute **Net Present Value (NPV), Internal Rate of Return (IRR), Payback Period**.
- Identify **revenue streams** (sale of recyclables, carbon credits, EPR income, RDF sales).

### 🔹 Stage 8 — Institutional & Governance Framework

- Assign **roles**: ULB / Panchayat, NGO, SHG, Private Operator, SPCBs.
- Define **PPP models**: *Service Contract, Management Contract, Concession, BOT*.
- Establish **KPI dashboards**.

### 🔹 Stage 9 — Public Awareness & Behavioural Change

- IEC (Information, Education, Communication) strategy.
- School programmes, street plays, social media campaigns.
- **Incentivization** (rewards for segregation compliance, penalties for violation).

### 🔹 Stage 10 — Monitoring, Evaluation & Adaptive Management

- Install **weighbridges** at MRF entry and exit.
- Monthly **material balance audits**.
- **Annual third-party impact assessment**.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master these 7 formulas — they cover ~80% of numerical questions in KTU ESE on this topic.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $RR = \dfrac{M_{recovered}}{M_{generated}} \times 100$ | Recovery Rate | % |
| 2 | $CE = \dfrac{M_{collected}}{M_{generated}} \times 100$ | Collection Efficiency | % |
| 3 | $DR = \dfrac{M_{diverted}}{M_{total\,waste}} \times 100$ | Diversion Rate from Landfill | % |
| 4 | $CR = \dfrac{M_{contaminant}}{M_{total\,collected}} \times 100$ | Contamination Rate | % |
| 5 | $CPT = \dfrac{Total\,Annual\,Cost}{M_{recovered}}$ | Cost per Tonne Recovered | ₹/tonne |
| 6 | $PR = \dfrac{H_{participating}}{H_{total}} \times 100$ | Household Participation Rate | % |
| 7 | $ROI = \dfrac{Benefit - Cost}{Cost} \times 100$ | Return on Investment | % |

### 2.3 Boundary Conditions & Assumptions

- **0% ≤ RR, CE, DR, PR ≤ 100%** (always).
- **CR should be < 5%** for commercially sellable recyclables; beyond this, reprocessors levy penalties.
- **CPT** must be benchmarked against **avoided landfill tipping fee** — if CPT > tipping fee, programme is uneconomical without subsidies.
- **Payback period** for a well-designed MRF: typically **3–5 years**; for collection systems: **1–2 years** (asset-light).

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These planning formulas are used by:
> - **KSPCB / CPCB** for assessing ULB compliance.
> - **Smart City Mission** project DPRs.
> - **World Bank / ADB** appraisal of MSW projects.
> - **EPR (Extended Producer Responsibility) audit** of producers (producers must report recycling % for plastic & e-waste).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Material Recovery Rate (RR) for a Mixed-Stream Recycling Programme

Consider a municipality with the following data (a typical KTU numerical):

- Total MSW generated: **$M_{total} = 500$ tonnes/day**
- Recyclable fraction: **$f_r = 0.30$** (30% of MSW is recyclable)
- Source-segregation compliance: **$S = 0.70$** (70% households segregate)
- MRF sorting efficiency: **$\eta_{MRF} = 0.85$**
- Market acceptance (offtake): **$O = 0.95$**

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 1 — Recyclable Material Available (Theoretical Maximum)**

$$
M_{recyclable} = M_{total} \times f_r
$$

Substituting:

$$
M_{recyclable} = 500 \times 0.30 = 150 \text{ tonnes/day}
$$

**Step 2 — Material Actually Collected as "Dry Waste" (after source segregation)**

$$
M_{collected} = M_{recyclable} \times S
$$

$$
M_{collected} = 150 \times 0.70 = 105 \text{ tonnes/day}
$$

**Step 3 — Material Recovered after MRF Sorting**

$$
M_{recovered} = M_{collected} \times \eta_{MRF}
$$

$$
M_{recovered} = 105 \times 0.85 = 89.25 \text{ tonnes/day}
$$

**Step 4 — Material Actually Sold / Re-introduced into Market**

$$
M_{sold} = M_{recovered} \times O
$$

$$
M_{sold} = 89.25 \times 0.95 = 84.79 \text{ tonnes/day}
$$

**Step 5 — Final Recovery Rate (with respect to total MSW)**

$$
RR = \frac{M_{sold}}{M_{total}} \times 100
$$

$$
RR = \frac{84.79}{500} \times 100 = 16.96\%
$$

**Step 6 — Effective Recovery Rate (with respect to recyclable fraction)**

$$
RR_{effective} = \frac{M_{sold}}{M_{recyclable}} \times 100
$$

$$
RR_{effective} = \frac{84.79}{150} \times 100 = 56.53\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Although the city recovers only **~17% of total MSW**, it has captured **~57% of all recyclables** available. This is the **planning insight**: always state RR in **both bases** to avoid misleading reporting.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Cost per Tonne (CPT) for a Recycling Programme

Given:
- Annual CAPEX (amortized): **₹50,00,000/year**
- Annual OPEX (collection + sorting + transport + admin): **₹1,20,00,000/year**
- Annual revenue from sale of recyclables: **₹35,00,000/year**
- Annual revenue from carbon credits / EPR: **₹8,00,000/year**
- Total material recovered per year: **$M_{recovered} = 30,000$ tonnes**

**Step 1 — Total Annual Programme Cost**

$$
C_{total} = CAPEX_{amortized} + OPEX
$$

$$
C_{total} = 50{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}20{,}00{,}000 = 1{,}70{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹/year}
$$

**Step 2 — Total Annual Revenue**

$$
R_{total} = R_{sale} + R_{carbon} + R_{EPR}
$$

$$
R_{total} = 35{,}00{,}000 + 8{,}00{,}000 = 43{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹/year}
$$

**Step 3 — Net Annual Cost (Subsidy Gap)**

$$
C_{net} = C_{total} - R_{total}
$$

$$
C_{net} = 1{,}70{,}00{,}000 - 43{,}00{,}000 = 1{,}27{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹/year}
$$

**Step 4 — Cost per Tonne (Gross)**

$$
CPT_{gross} = \frac{C_{total}}{M_{recovered}}
$$

$$
CPT_{gross} = \frac{1{,}70{,}00{,}000}{30{,}000} = 566.67 \text{ ₹/tonne}
$$

**Step 5 — Net Cost per Tonne (Subsidy required)**

$$
CPT_{net} = \frac{C_{net}}{M_{recovered}}
$$

$$
CPT_{net} = \frac{1{,}27{,}00{,}000}{30{,}000} = 423.33 \text{ ₹/tonne}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Planning Insight:** $CPT_{net}$ must be **≤ avoided landfill tipping fee** (typically ₹500–1500/tonne in India). If $CPT_{net}$ > tipping fee, the programme requires **cross-subsidization** from sanitation budget or EPR income.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — ROI for a Recycling Programme

Using the above data:

$$
ROI = \frac{R_{total} - C_{total}}{C_{total}} \times 100
$$

> Since revenue < cost, ROI is negative; calculate as **cost recovery %** instead:

$$
Cost\,Recovery\,\% = \frac{R_{total}}{C_{total}} \times 100
$$

$$
Cost\,Recovery\,\% = \frac{43{,}00{,}000}{1{,}70{,}00{,}000} \times 100 = 25.29\%
$$

> **Typical Indian ULBs recover 15–30% of recycling programme cost through material sales alone** — the rest must be funded from general taxation, SBM grants, or PPP annuity.

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix for Collection Strategy

| Criterion | Door-to-Door | Drop-off / Bring Centres | Curbside Bin System |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Source Segregation Quality** | Excellent | Moderate | Poor–Moderate |
| **CAPEX Intensity** | High (vehicle fleet) | Low | Medium |
| **Operational Flexibility** | Low (fixed routes) | High | Medium |
| **Public Convenience** | High | Low–Medium | Medium |
| **Best for** | Dense urban wards | Sparse / rural areas | Mixed zones |
| **KTU Exam Tip** | Most recommended for high-rise apartments | Cheapest entry point | Common in Tier-2 cities |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Planning Flow — Recycling Programme Design

```mermaid

    C --> F[Private designs builds operates 15 to 20 yr]
    D --> G[Private operates 5 to 7 yr ULB owns assets]
    E --> H[Private provides specific service 1 to 3 yr]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question]**

### Q1. Define "Recovery Rate" and "Diversion Rate" in a recycling programme. Why are both metrics necessary for programme evaluation? **[3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

- **Recovery Rate (RR)** = (Mass of material recovered and reintroduced into the economy / Mass of target material generated) × 100. It quantifies the **technical and operational efficiency** of the recycling chain (segregation × MRF efficiency × offtake).

- **Diversion Rate (DR)** = (Mass of waste diverted from landfill disposal / Total mass of waste generated) × 100. It quantifies the **environmental outcome** — how much waste avoided landfill.

- **Why both are needed:** A programme can have a high RR for one material (e.g., paper) but low DR overall if organic waste is not composted. RR is a **micro metric** (per material stream); DR is a **macro metric** (system-wide). KTU expects students to recognize the distinction — citing only one is incomplete. **[1 Mark each for definitions + 1 Mark for distinction]**

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]**

### Q2. List and briefly explain any THREE key factors that determine the success of a municipal recycling programme. **[3 Marks]**

**Model Answer (any three of the following, 1 mark each):**

1. **Source Segregation Compliance** — Without household-level segregation, downstream MRF efficiency collapses and contamination rises. The success of India's SWM Rules 2016 depends on this single factor.
2. **Stable End-Market Linkage** — Recyclables must have guaranteed offtakers with transparent pricing; otherwise, MRF output becomes valueless stockpile.
3. **Financial Viability** — Cost per tonne must be less than or equal to avoided landfill cost, with diversified revenue (sales + EPR + carbon credits).
4. **Public Awareness and IEC** — Sustained behavioural change requires continuous engagement, not one-time campaigns.
5. **Institutional Governance** — Clear roles, KPIs, and accountability among ULB, operator, and SPB.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 / Dec 2024 Model — Module 4 Internal Choice]**

### 📝 QUESTION A — 14 Marks

A municipality of **2,00,000 population** generates MSW at the rate of **0.5 kg/capita/day**. Waste characterization reveals that **40% is dry (recyclable) and 60% is wet (organic)**. The proposed recycling programme has the following design parameters:
- Household source segregation compliance: **60%**
- MRF sorting efficiency: **90%**
- Market offtake rate: **95%**
- Cost of setting up MRF: **₹3 Crore** (lifetime 10 years, straight-line depreciation)
- Annual OPEX: **₹45 Lakh**
- Annual revenue from sale of recyclables: **₹18 Lakh**
- Annual revenue from EPR / tipping fee savings: **₹12 Lakh**

**(a)** Calculate the **Recovery Rate (RR)** and **Diversion Rate (DR)** for the programme. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Compute the **Net Cost per Tonne** of recovered material and comment on the **financial viability** of the programme. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution

**Given:**
- Population $P = 2{,}00{,}000$
- Per capita waste $w = 0.5$ kg/day
- Total MSW $M_{total} = P \times w = 2{,}00{,}000 \times 0.5 = 1{,}00{,}000$ kg/day $= 100$ tonnes/day
- Dry fraction $f_r = 0.40$ → Recyclable potential $= 40$ tonnes/day
- Wet fraction $f_w = 0.60$ → Organic potential $= 60$ tonnes/day

**Source-segregation efficiency** $S = 0.60$, **MRF efficiency** $\eta_{MRF} = 0.90$, **Offtake** $O = 0.95$

---

**Part (a) — Recovery & Diversion Rate**

**Step 1 — Recyclable Material Collected**

$$
M_{collected} = 40 \times 0.60 = 24 \text{ tonnes/day}
$$

**Step 2 — Material Recovered at MRF**

$$
M_{recovered} = 24 \times 0.90 = 21.6 \text{ tonnes/day}
$$

**Step 3 — Material Sold to Market**

$$
M_{sold} = 21.6 \times 0.95 = 20.52 \text{ tonnes/day}
$$

**Step 4 — Recovery Rate (with respect to total MSW)**

$$
RR = \frac{20.52}{100} \times 100 = 20.52\%
$$

**Step 5 — Diversion Rate** (Assuming wet waste is composted, and dry recyclables are diverted)

$$
M_{diverted} = 20.52 \text{ (dry)} + 60 \text{ (wet composted, ideal)} = 80.52 \text{ tonnes/day}
$$

$$
DR = \frac{80.52}{100} \times 100 = 80.52\%
$$

> **Note:** In practice, diversion of wet waste is partial; assuming ideal diversion for theoretical max.

**Alternative interpretation** — if only dry waste diversion is considered:

$$
DR_{dry\,only} = \frac{20.52}{100} \times 100 = 20.52\%
$$

Students should explicitly state the assumption. **[7 Marks — full marks requires clear stepwise calculation + assumption statement]**

---

**Part (b) — Net Cost per Tonne & Financial Viability**

**Step 1 — Annual CAPEX (Amortized)**

$$
CAPEX_{annual} = \frac{3{,}00{,}00{,}000}{10} = 30{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹/year}
$$

**Step 2 — Total Annual Cost**

$$
C_{total} = 30{,}00{,}000 + 45{,}00{,}000 = 75{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹/year}
$$

**Step 3 — Total Annual Revenue**

$$
R_{total} = 18{,}00{,}000 + 12{,}00{,}000 = 30{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹/year}
$$

**Step 4 — Net Annual Cost**

$$
C_{net} = 75{,}00{,}000 - 30{,}00{,}000 = 45{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹/year}
$$

**Step 5 — Annual Material Recovered**

$$
M_{annual} = 20.52 \times 365 = 7{,}489.8 \text{ tonnes/year}
$$

**Step 6 — Gross Cost per Tonne**

$$
CPT_{gross} = \frac{75{,}00{,}000}{7{,}489.8} = 1001.4 \text{ ₹/tonne}
$$

**Step 7 — Net Cost per Tonne**

$$
CPT_{net} = \frac{45{,}00{,}000}{7{,}489.8} = 600.8 \text{ ₹/tonne}
$$

**Step 8 — Financial Viability Comment**

- $CPT_{net} = ₹600.8$/tonne is **comparable to typical Indian landfill tipping fees (₹500–1500/tonne)**. The programme is **marginally viable**.
- However, the **cost recovery ratio is only 40%** ($30L/75L$), meaning the ULB must subsidize 60% from sanitation budgets or SBM grants.
- **To improve viability**: (i) increase source segregation to 80%+ (doubles $M_{sold}$); (ii) capture EPR income fully; (iii) add carbon credit monetization. **[7 Marks]**

---

### 📝 QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

Discuss in detail the **10-stage framework for planning a municipal recycling programme**. For each stage, identify **at least one critical planning decision** and the **data or tool** required to make that decision. **[14 Marks]**

#### ✅ Model Solution Outline (Mark-Wise Distribution)

| Stage | Critical Planning Decision | Data / Tool Required | Marks |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1. Baseline Assessment** | Decide on survey sample size & stratification | Waste audit forms, GIS mapping | 1.5 |
| **2. Goal Setting** | Choose baseline year & KPI definitions | SMART template, benchmarking data | 1.0 |
| **3. Market Analysis** | Select primary vs secondary recyclers | Commodity price index, EPR registry | 1.5 |
| **4. Collection System Design** | Choose D2D vs curbside vs drop-off | Population density maps, traffic data | 1.5 |
| **5. MRF Planning** | Select technology level (manual/auto) | Waste volume projections, capital budget | 1.5 |
| **6. Transport Logistics** | Locate transfer stations | Origin-destination matrix, routing software | 1.0 |
| **7. Financial Feasibility** | Compute NPV, IRR, payback | Excel-based DCF model | 1.5 |
| **8. Institutional Framework** | Choose PPP model | Legal advisory, RFP templates | 1.0 |
| **9. Public Awareness** | Design IEC strategy & budget | Social surveys, focus group data | 1.0 |
| **10. Monitoring & Evaluation** | Define KPI dashboard | Weighbridge data, ERP/MIS software | 1.5 |

> A complete answer should also include a **concluding paragraph (≈ 80 words)** synthesizing how the 10 stages are interlinked and why skipping a stage leads to programme failure. (Implied 1 mark for synthesis.)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Always show units** in every step (kg/day, tonnes/year, ₹/tonne). Marks are deducted for unitless answers.
> 2. **State assumptions clearly** — e.g., "Assuming 100% diversion of wet waste to composting" — or risk being marked for ambiguity.
> 3. **For Part B numericals, write the formula first**, then substitute values. Examiners award **method marks** even if the final answer is wrong.
> 4. **Do not confuse "Diversion Rate" with "Recovery Rate"** — this is a **commonly tested trap** in KTU papers.
> 5. **For 14-mark theory questions**, structure the answer with **headings/bullets**, not paragraphs. Examiners scan for keywords; absence of keywords like "SMART", "MRF", "PPP", "NPV" can cost 2–3 marks.

---

## 5.3 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Read this 30 minutes before the exam.**

- ✅ Recycling programme planning is a **10-stage systematic process**, not ad-hoc activity.
- ✅ **Recovery Rate (RR)** = (recovered / generated) × 100 — measured per material stream.
- ✅ **Diversion Rate (DR)** = (diverted from landfill / total waste) × 100 — system-wide metric.
- ✅ **Collection Efficiency (CE)** and **Contamination Rate (CR)** are upstream drivers of RR.
- ✅ The **3 pillars of planning**: (1) Source Segregation, (2) MRF Technology, (3) End-Market Linkage.
- ✅ **Cost per Tonne (CPT)** must be benchmarked against **landfill tipping fee** for viability.
- ✅ **PPP Models**: Service Contract (low CAPEX) → Management Contract (medium) → Concession/BOT (high CAPEX).
- ✅ **EPR income** (Extended Producer Responsibility) is a critical revenue stream for plastic & e-waste recycling under Indian rules.
- ✅ **Behavourial change (IEC)** is as important as engineering design — a "soft" variable with "hard" outcomes.
- ✅ **Monitoring with weighbridges & material balance audits** is non-negotiable for SWM Rules 2016 compliance.
- ✅ **Cost recovery ratio** of 25–40% is typical for Indian ULBs; 100% recovery is unrealistic.
- ✅ Always present RR in **both** bases: % of total MSW **and** % of recyclable fraction.
- ✅ **NIR optical sorting, eddy current, magnetic separation, trommel** are the four key MRF unit processes.
- ✅ KTU loves asking **numericals** — practice at least 3 full 14-mark problems with all 5 formulas above.
- ✅ **Avoid the jargon trap**: in KTU, "Recycling" specifically means *material recovery*, NOT energy recovery (which is **waste-to-energy / RDF**).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Recycling Programme Elements
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET831 - WASTE MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Recycling Programme Elements

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Recycling Programme** is a systematically engineered, multi-stakeholder operational framework designed to intercept post-consumer or post-industrial solid waste streams, reprocess them into secondary raw materials or value-added products, and reintroduce them into the manufacturing cycle. As per KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned) terminology, a recycling programme is a closed-loop or open-loop intervention within the broader **Integrated Solid Waste Management (ISWM)** hierarchy, positioned between *waste prevention* and *energy recovery*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *Recycling Programme Elements* refer to the structural, operational, economic, institutional, and informational components that collectively constitute a functional municipal or industrial recycling system. These include collection logistics, sorting infrastructure, market linkages, policy instruments, public participation mechanisms, and performance-monitoring protocols.

### 1.2 Decomposition of the Term "Programme Elements"

The phrase can be decoupled into two operative units:

1. **Programme** — A planned, time-bound, financed, and outcome-measured intervention.
2. **Elements** — The discrete, interdependent functional components that must be designed and synchronized for the programme to achieve its stated **diversion targets**.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Hospital Recycling System"**
> Imagine a hospital. The hospital receives patients (raw waste), the reception desk collects them (collection), the triage unit separates critical from non-critical cases (sorting), the operation theatre treats them (processing), the pharmacy distributes medicine back to patients (market offtake), and the reception also sends awareness SMS to the public (public outreach). If even one of these units fails, the hospital collapses.
> 
> Similarly, a recycling programme is a *living system* where **waste = patients**, and each programme element = a hospital department. The success of recycling is determined by how well these elements are synchronized.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics Used in Recycling Programmes (KTU Frequently Tested)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Key benchmark metrics (must be memorized):
> - **Diversion Rate (%)** = `(Diverted Waste / Total Waste Generated) × 100`
> - **Recovery Rate (%)** = `(Material Recovered / Material Input) × 100`
> - **Participation Rate (%)** = `(Households Participating / Total Households) × 100`
> - **Contamination Rate (%)** = `(Contaminated Load / Total Collected Recyclables) × 100`
> - **Cost per Tonne (INR/MT or USD/MT)** = `Total Programme Cost / Total Tonnes Recycled`

### 1.5 The Five Pillars of a Recycling Programme (KTU Mnemonic: **C-S-M-P-E**)

| Pillar | Element | Primary Function |
|:------:|:-------:|:-----------------|
| **C** | Collection Infrastructure | Door-to-door, drop-off, buy-back |
| **S** | Sorting & Processing (MRF) | Separation, baling, shredding |
| **M** | Market Development | Offtake agreements, EPR linkage |
| **P** | Public Participation | IEC, segregation at source |
| **E** | Evaluation & Policy | KPIs, regulation, financing |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Eight Core Programme Elements (Detailed)

A robust KTU-mandated recycling programme is structured around the following eight functional elements. Each element addresses a specific stage in the material lifecycle:

#### Element 1: **Source Segregation Mandate**
- The foundational element — separation of waste at the point of generation.
- Operates under the **two-bin / three-bin system** (Wet / Dry / Hazardous).
- Aligned with the **Solid Waste Management Rules, 2016 (India)** — mandatory for ULBs above **1 lakh population**.
- Failure here cascades into contamination downstream, increasing MRF rejection rates by **15–30%**.

#### Element 2: **Collection Logistics Network**
- **Curbside Collection** — Scheduled door-to-door pickup (frequency: 2–7 times/week).
- **Drop-off Centres (Bring Systems)** — Strategically located community bins.
- **Buy-back Centres (Bottle Bills)** — Refundable deposits on PET, glass, aluminium.
- **Reverse Vending Machines (RVM)** — Automated incentive-based return.

#### Element 3: **Materials Recovery Facility (MRF)**
- The **central nervous system** of a recycling programme.
- Functions: Receiving → Manual Sorting → Magnetic Separation → Eddy Current → Optical Sorting → Baling → Dispatch.
- Typical capacity: **5 to 500 TPD (Tonnes Per Day)**.

#### Element 4: **Processing & Manufacturing Linkage**
- **Closed-loop recycling** → Same product (e.g., aluminium can → aluminium can).
- **Open-loop recycling** → Different product (e.g., PET bottle → polyester fabric).
- **Downcycling** → Loss of material quality per cycle.

#### Element 5: **Market Development & Offtake Agreements**
- Stable demand-side contracts with industries (paper mills, plastic converters, cement kilns).
- **Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR)** under PWM Rules, 2016 & 2022 amendments.

#### Element 6: **Public Education & Outreach (IEC)**
- Information, Education, Communication campaigns.
- School programmes, ward-level meetings, social media drives.
- **Target reduction in contamination rate: < 5%** (international best practice).

#### Element 7: **Policy, Regulation & Financing**
- SWM Rules 2016, PWM Rules 2022, PWM (Amendment) 2024, Plastic Waste EPR.
- Financing via **Swachh Bharat Mission (SBM 2.0)**, **Smart Cities Mission**, municipal budgets, PPP models.

#### Element 8: **Monitoring, Evaluation & Continuous Improvement**
- KPIs: diversion rate, recovery rate, cost/tonne, contamination rate, GHG avoidance.
- **Material Flow Analysis (MFA)** and **Life Cycle Assessment (LCA)** tools.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **board-exam verified** and align with KTU 2024 Scheme Part B (14-mark) question patterns.

$$
\text{Diversion Rate (\%)} = \frac{W_{\text{diverted}}}{W_{\text{generated}}} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{Recovery Rate (\%)} = \frac{M_{\text{recovered}}}{M_{\text{input to MRF}}} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{Participation Rate (\%)} = \frac{H_{\text{participating}}}{H_{\text{total}}} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{Contamination Rate (\%)} = \frac{W_{\text{contaminants}}}{W_{\text{recyclables collected}}} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{Cost per Tonne} = \frac{C_{\text{total programme}}}{T_{\text{recycled}}}
$$

$$
\text{GHG Avoided (tCO}_2\text{e)} = M_{\text{recycled}} \times EF_{\text{material}}
$$

$$
\text{Material Balance (MRF)}: \quad M_{\text{in}} = M_{\text{recovered}} + M_{\text{residual}} + M_{\text{loss}}
$$

$$
\text{Recycled Content (\%)} = \frac{M_{\text{recycled input}}}{M_{\text{total raw input}}} \times 100
$$

### 2.3 Quick-Reference Cheat Sheet (No Vertical Pipes Used)

| Symbol / Term | Definition | Typical Unit | Range or Benchmark |
|:-------------:|:-----------|:------------:|:------------------:|
| $W_{\text{diverted}}$ | Waste diverted from landfill | Tonnes/Year | $20\%-70\%$ in mature systems |
| $W_{\text{generated}}$ | Total municipal solid waste | Tonnes/Year | $0.4-0.6$ kg/capita/day (India) |
| $M_{\text{recovered}}$ | Material successfully recycled | Tonnes/Year | Varies by stream |
| $H_{\text{participating}}$ | Households actively segregating | Count | Target $\geq 80\%$ |
| $EF_{\text{material}}$ | Emission factor per material | $\text{tCO}_2\text{e/tonne}$ | Paper $\approx 0.9$, PET $\approx 1.5$, Al $\approx 9.0$ |
| $C_{\text{total}}$ | Programme operational + capital cost | INR/Year | $2000-8000$ INR/tonne |
| $T_{\text{recycled}}$ | Total tonnes recycled | Tonnes | Depends on ULB size |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Recycling programme elements form the **backbone of circular economy engineering**, **urban mine exploitation**, and **industrial ecology** networks. In Kerala's context, programmes such as **Haritha Keralam Mission** and the **Kerala Suchitwa Mission's Clean Kerala Programme** operationalize these elements at the **grama panchayat and municipality scales**, making this topic directly relevant to municipal engineers, environmental consultants, and EPR compliance officers.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Diversion Rate from First Principles

The **Diversion Rate** is the most-cited KTU metric. We derive it from the **mass balance principle** of solid waste management.

**Step 1 — Define the total waste stream entering the system:**

Let $W_{\text{gen}}$ be the total waste generated by a municipality in a year (in tonnes).

**Step 2 — Partition the waste stream:**

According to the ISWM hierarchy, the total waste is partitioned into three functional fates:

$$
W_{\text{gen}} = W_{\text{diversion}} + W_{\text{disposal}}
$$

Where:
- $W_{\text{diversion}}$ includes recycling, composting, anaerobic digestion, and reuse.
- $W_{\text{disposal}}$ includes landfill and incineration without energy recovery.

**Step 3 — Express the diversion rate as a ratio:**

$$
\text{Diversion Rate} = \frac{W_{\text{diversion}}}{W_{\text{gen}}} = \frac{W_{\text{diversion}}}{W_{\text{diversion}} + W_{\text{disposal}}}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to percentage form (KTU standard convention):**

$$
DR\,\big(\%\big) = \frac{W_{\text{diversion}}}{W_{\text{gen}}} \times 100
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute typical Indian urban values for verification:**

For a mid-sized Indian city: $W_{\text{gen}} = 500\,\text{TPD}$, $W_{\text{diversion}} = 175\,\text{TPD}$ (35%).

$$
DR = \frac{175}{500} \times 100 = 35\%
$$

This matches the KTU model answer benchmark of **30–40% diversion for Tier-2 Indian cities**.

### 3.2 Derivation of MRF Material Balance

A **Materials Recovery Facility (MRF)** operates on the **law of conservation of mass**. The sum of outputs must equal the sum of inputs (accounting for moisture loss and process loss).

**Step 1 — Define the input mass:**

Let $M_{\text{in}}$ be the total mass of mixed recyclables entering the MRF per day.

**Step 2 — Identify output streams:**

- $M_{\text{rec}}$ — Mass of recovered recyclables (paper, plastic, metal, glass).
- $M_{\text{res}}$ — Mass of residuals sent to landfill (non-recyclable fines).
- $M_{\text{loss}}$ — Process losses (moisture evaporation, dust, conveyor spillage).

**Step 3 — Apply the conservation equation:**

$$
M_{\text{in}} = M_{\text{rec}} + M_{\text{res}} + M_{\text{loss}}
$$

**Step 4 — Rearrange to compute recovery rate:**

$$
M_{\text{rec}} = M_{\text{in}} - M_{\text{res}} - M_{\text{loss}}
$$

$$
\text{Recovery Rate} = \frac{M_{\text{rec}}}{M_{\text{in}}} \times 100
$$

**Step 5 — Numerical example for KTU problem-solving:**

Given: $M_{\text{in}} = 100\,\text{tonnes/day}$, $M_{\text{res}} = 25\,\text{tonnes/day}$, $M_{\text{loss}} = 5\,\text{tonnes/day}$.

$$
M_{\text{rec}} = 100 - 25 - 5 = 70\,\text{tonnes/day}
$$

$$
\text{Recovery Rate} = \frac{70}{100} \times 100 = 70\%
$$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Programme Element Performance

| Programme Element | Key Performance Indicator (KPI) | Target (Best Practice) | Typical Indian ULB Status |
|:-----------------|:-------------------------------:|:----------------------:|:-------------------------:|
| Source Segregation | Compliance % | $\geq 90\%$ | $30\%-60\%$ |
| Collection Coverage | Households served | $100\%$ | $70\%-90\%$ |
| MRF Efficiency | Recovery Rate | $75\%-85\%$ | $50\%-65\%$ |
| Market Offtake | Tonnage sold / Tonnage processed | $\geq 95\%$ | $60\%-80\%$ |
| IEC Outreach | Awareness Index | $\geq 80\%$ | $40\%-70\%$ |
| Cost Efficiency | Cost per tonne | $<\$50$ USD | $\$60\text{-}120$ USD |
| GHG Mitigation | tCO$_2$e avoided/year | Maximize | Underreported |

### 3.4 Worked-Out Problem: Combined Diversion + Recovery + Cost Analysis

**Problem (KTU 2024 Pattern):** A municipal corporation generates **600 TPD** of solid waste. The recycling programme diverts **180 TPD** to material recovery. The MRF processes this **180 TPD** and recovers **126 TPD** of marketable recyclables. The total annual programme cost is **INR 12 crore**.

**Required:**
1. Calculate the Diversion Rate.
2. Calculate the Recovery Rate at the MRF.
3. Calculate the Cost per Tonne recycled.

**Solution:**

**Part 1 — Diversion Rate:**

$$
DR = \frac{180}{600} \times 100 = 30\%
$$

**Part 2 — Recovery Rate:**

$$
RR = \frac{126}{180} \times 100 = 70\%
$$

**Part 3 — Cost per Tonne Recycled:**

Annual tonnage recycled $= 126 \times 365 = 45{,}990$ tonnes/year.

$$
\text{Cost per Tonne} = \frac{12{,}00{,}00{,}000}{45{,}990} \approx \text{INR } 26{,}092 \text{ per tonne}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** A 30% diversion is *acceptable for a developing ULB*, the 70% MRF recovery indicates a *moderately efficient facility*, but the cost/tonne is **3–4× higher than global best practice**, signalling the need for process automation and economies of scale.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Recycling Programme

The following Mermaid block illustrates the **block-level functional flow** of all programme elements, from waste generation to final market reintegration.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Waste Generation at Source] --> B[Element 1: Source Segregation Mandate]
    B --> C[Element 2: Collection Logistics Network]
    C --> D[Element 3: Materials Recovery Facility MRF]
    D --> E[Element 4: Processing and Manufacturing Linkage]
    E --> F[Element 5: Market Development and Offtake]
    F --> G[Recycled Product to Consumer]
    G --> A

    H[Element 6: Public Education and Outreach] -.-> B
    H -.-> C

    I[Element 7: Policy Regulation and Financing] -.-> B
    I -.-> C
    I -.-> D
    I -.-> F

    J[Element 8: Monitoring and Evaluation] -.-> D
    J -.-> F
    J -.-> K[KPIs: Diversion Recovery Cost Contamination]
```

### 4.2 Detailed Sub-Flow: MRF Operations

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN[Mixed Recyclables Input] --> S1[Manual Sorting Platform]
    S1 --> S2[Magnetic Separator Ferrous]
    S1 --> S3[Eddy Current Non Ferrous]
    S1 --> S4[Optical Sorter Plastic by Resin]
    S2 --> B1[Baling Unit]
    S3 --> B1
    S4 --> B1
    B1 --> OUT[Dispatch to Market]
    S1 --> RES[Residual to Landfill]
    S1 --> LOSS[Process Loss Moisture Dust]
```

### 4.3 Stakeholder Interaction Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    PUB[Citizens and Households] --> ULB[Urban Local Body ULB]
    ULB --> MRFOP[MRF Operator Concessionaire]
    MRFOP --> IND[Industry Buyers]
    IND --> MRFOP
    ULB --> SPCB[State Pollution Control Board]
    ULB --> CPCB[Central Pollution Control Board]
    ULB --> EPR[Producer Responsibility Organisations PRO]
    EPR --> IND
    PUB --> NGONGO[NGOs and Self Help Groups SHGs]
    NGONGO --> ULB
```

### 4.4 The 5R Hierarchy within a Recycling Programme

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R1[R0 Refuse] --> R2[R1 Reduce]
    R2 --> R3[R2 Reuse]
    R3 --> R4[R3 Recycle]
    R4 --> R5[R4 Recover Energy]
    R5 --> R6[R5 Residual Disposal Landfill]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The Recycling Programme operates at the **R3 level** of the 5R hierarchy, but its *success is contingent on effective execution of R0–R2* upstream.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
*List any four essential elements of a municipal solid waste recycling programme.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The four essential elements of an MSW recycling programme are:
1. **Source Segregation** at household level (wet/dry/hazardous).
2. **Collection Network** (curbside, drop-off, buy-back centres).
3. **Materials Recovery Facility (MRF)** for sorting, baling, and processing.
4. **Market Linkages** with industries for offtake and EPR-driven demand.

*(1 mark for each correct point; 3 points required. KTU 2024 marking scheme.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*Define the term "Diversion Rate" in the context of a recycling programme.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The **Diversion Rate** is the percentage of total generated solid waste that is *diverted away from landfill disposal* through recycling, composting, reuse, or energy recovery. It is computed as:

$$
\text{Diversion Rate} = \frac{W_{\text{diverted}}}{W_{\text{generated}}} \times 100
$$

A higher diversion rate indicates better performance of the recycling programme and progress toward the **Swachh Bharat Mission (SBM 2.0)** and **circular economy** targets. *(1 mark definition + 1 mark formula + 1 mark contextual interpretation.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Scheme ESE Pattern: Each Part B question carries 14 marks, with sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Students must answer **one full question** (either full Q1 or full Q2). Module-level internal choice is standard.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam - July 2024]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **eight core elements of a recycling programme** with reference to the Indian policy framework. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** A city generates **900 TPD** of municipal solid waste. The recycling programme diverts **315 TPD** to material recovery. The MRF processes this **315 TPD** and recovers **220.5 TPD** of recyclables. The total annual programme cost is **INR 15 crore**. Calculate:
- (i) Diversion Rate
- (ii) Recovery Rate
- (iii) Cost per Tonne Recycled

*(7 Marks)*

---

#### **Model Answer — Question A**

**Part (a) — Eight Core Elements (7 Marks):**

A holistic recycling programme comprises the following eight elements:

1. **Source Segregation Mandate (1 Mark):** Under SWM Rules 2016, households must segregate waste into wet, dry, and hazardous categories. This is the *foundation* of clean recycling streams.

2. **Collection Logistics (1 Mark):** Includes curbside collection, drop-off centres, and buy-back centres. RVMs (Reverse Vending Machines) are modern additions for PET and aluminium.

3. **Materials Recovery Facility (MRF) (1 Mark):** Central hub for sorting (manual + magnetic + eddy current + optical), baling, and dispatch. Capacity range: 5–500 TPD.

4. **Processing Linkages (1 Mark):** Connects to paper mills, plastic recyclers, cement kilns (co-processing), and smelters. Determines whether recycling is *closed-loop* or *open-loop*.

5. **Market Development (1 Mark):** Stable offtake agreements and EPR-driven demand under PWM Rules 2022 ensure economic viability.

6. **Public Education & Outreach (1 Mark):** IEC campaigns, school programmes, SHG involvement; aim to drive participation rate above 80%.

7. **Policy & Financing (0.5 Mark):** SBM 2.0, Smart Cities Mission, PPP models, municipal budgets.

8. **Monitoring & Evaluation (0.5 Mark):** KPIs, Material Flow Analysis, LCA, and digital dashboards.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**Given:** $W_{\text{gen}} = 900$ TPD; $W_{\text{div}} = 315$ TPD; $M_{\text{in,MRF}} = 315$ TPD; $M_{\text{rec}} = 220.5$ TPD; $C_{\text{total}} = 15$ crore INR/year.

**(i) Diversion Rate (2 Marks):**

$$
DR = \frac{315}{900} \times 100 = 35\%
$$

**(ii) Recovery Rate (2 Marks):**

$$
RR = \frac{220.5}{315} \times 100 = 70\%
$$

**(iii) Cost per Tonne Recycled (3 Marks):**

Annual recycled tonnage $= 220.5 \times 365 = 80{,}482.5$ tonnes/year.

$$
\text{Cost per Tonne} = \frac{15{,}00{,}00{,}000}{80{,}482.5} \approx \text{INR } 18{,}638 \text{ per tonne}
$$

**Interpretation:** A 35% diversion is *reasonable*; the 70% MRF recovery is *moderate*; cost/tonne is *high*, suggesting need for automation.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Discuss the role of **Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR)** and **Material Recovery Facilities (MRFs)** as programme elements. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **functional flow of a recycling programme** from source segregation to market reintegration. Identify the key KPIs monitored at each stage. *(7 Marks)*

---

#### **Model Answer — Question B**

**Part (a) — EPR and MRF (7 Marks):**

**EPR (3.5 Marks):**
- EPR is a **policy principle** under the **Plastic Waste Management Rules, 2016 (amended 2022)** that mandates producers, brand owners, and importers to *finance and manage the end-of-life* of their products.
- **Producer Responsibility Organisations (PROs)** aggregate EPR obligations.
- EPR drives *market demand* for recyclables and funds collection infrastructure.
- It is a **demand-pull mechanism** that complements the *supply-push* from households.

**MRF (3.5 Marks):**
- The MRF is the **operational heart** of a recycling programme.
- Process sequence: Receiving → Manual Sorting → Magnetic Separation → Eddy Current → Optical Sorting → Baling → Storage → Dispatch.
- **Capacity design** depends on ULB size, waste characterization, and contamination profile.
- Efficiency measured by **Recovery Rate (target 75–85%)** and **Residue Rejection Rate**.

---

**Part (b) — Block Diagram and KPIs (7 Marks):**

**Diagram (3 Marks):**

$$
\text{Source} \rightarrow \text{Segregation} \rightarrow \text{Collection} \rightarrow \text{MRF} \rightarrow \text{Processing} \rightarrow \text{Market} \rightarrow \text{Consumer}
$$

(Students should draw a labelled flowchart; the Mermaid diagram in Section 4.1 is the reference template.)

**Stage-wise KPIs (4 Marks):**

| Stage | KPI | Benchmark |
|:------|:----|:----------|
| Segregation | Compliance % | $\geq 90\%$ |
| Collection | Coverage % | $100\%$ |
| MRF | Recovery Rate | $75\%-85\%$ |
| Market | Offtake Ratio | $\geq 95\%$ |
| Programme | Diversion Rate | $\geq 50\%$ |
| Programme | Cost/Tonne | $<\$50$ USD |
| Programme | Contamination | $< 5\%$ |
| Programme | GHG Avoided | Maximize |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse "Diversion Rate" with "Recovery Rate"** — these are *different* metrics. Diversion is at city level; Recovery is at MRF level. (Loses 2–3 marks.)
> 2. **Always include units** in numerical answers (TPD, INR/tonne, %). Examiners deduct 1 mark per missing unit.
> 3. **Memorize the eight elements verbatim** — KTU examiners award marks for keyword presence (segregation, MRF, EPR, IEC, etc.).
> 4. **Numerical answers must show the formula BEFORE substitution** — this fetches the "method" mark even if the arithmetic is wrong.
> 5. **Do NOT skip the policy reference** — always cite SWM Rules 2016, PWM Rules 2022, or SBM 2.0 to score the *context* mark.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — "The Recycling 10":**
> 
> 1. **Definition** — Recycling programme = structured multi-stakeholder intervention to reprocess waste into secondary materials.
> 2. **Position in ISWM** — Sits at **R3 (Recycle)** of the 5R hierarchy.
> 3. **Eight Elements** — Source Segregation, Collection, MRF, Processing, Market, IEC, Policy, M&E.
> 4. **Key Formulas** — Diversion Rate, Recovery Rate, Participation Rate, Contamination Rate, Cost/Tonne.
> 5. **MRF** — Manual + Magnetic + Eddy Current + Optical sorting sequence.
> 6. **EPR** — Demand-pull mechanism under PWM Rules 2016/2022.
> 7. **Policy Backbone** — SWM Rules 2016, PWM Rules 2022, SBM 2.0, EPR guidelines 2024.
> 8. **Material Balance** — Input = Recovered + Residual + Loss.
> 9. **Best Practice Targets** — Diversion $\geq 50\%$, Recovery $\geq 75\%$, Contamination $< 5\%$.
> 10. **Kerala Context** — Haritha Keralam Mission, Suchitwa Mission, Clean Kerala Programme operationalize these elements at ULB level.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Commonly Recycled Materials and Processes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET831 - WASTE MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Commonly Recycled Materials and Processes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Commonly Recycled Materials (CRM)** refer to the standard categories of post-consumer and post-industrial waste streams that are routinely diverted from landfills and reintroduced into secondary manufacturing cycles through established mechanical, chemical, or biological recovery processes. Under the **KTU 2024 Scheme** framework for OECET831 (Waste Management), the principal CRM categories include **paper/cardboard, glass, ferrous metals, non-ferrous metals (especially aluminum), plastics (PET, HDPE, PVC, LDPE, PP, PS), and e-waste (WEEE)**.

**Recycling Process Engineering** is defined as the sequence of unit operations—collection, sorting, pre-treatment, size reduction, separation, and re-processing—employed to convert discarded materials into secondary raw materials or new products without compromising functional integrity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):**
> *"Commonly recycled materials and processes – recycling of paper, glass, metals (ferrous and non-ferrous), plastics, and e-waste; mechanical recycling, chemical recycling, feedstock recycling, and primary/secondary/tertiary recycling hierarchies."*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Hospital for Materials"

Think of a recycling facility as a **hospital for spent materials**. Just as a hospital:
- **Triages** patients (Sorting & Segregation at MRF — Material Recovery Facility),
- **Diagnoses** the condition (Composition Analysis using NIR sensors, eddy currents, magnets),
- **Treats** them via surgery, medicine, or therapy (Mechanical, Chemical, or Biological recycling), and
- **Discharges** recovered citizens back into society (Secondary raw materials fed into manufacturing),

…the recycling industry performs identical functions on waste streams. The "patients" (paper, glass, metal, plastic) each require a **different treatment protocol** because their "physiology" (chemistry & structure) differs. Aluminium and steel are like athletes with strong bones (metallic bonds)—they can be melted and re-cast infinitely. Paper, however, is like a paper-mâché model with cellulose fibres—it can only be recycled **5 to 7 times** before the fibres become too short (a phenomenon called **hornification**).

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Performance Indicators

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Industrial Metrics (must-know for KTU exams):**
> - **Recycling Rate (R)** = (Material Recycled / Total Material Generated) × 100%
> - **Material Recovery Rate** ≈ **35–45%** globally; **>60%** in EU-27
> - **Energy Savings (Aluminum)** ≈ **95%** vs. primary production from bauxite
> - **Energy Savings (Steel)** ≈ **60–74%** via Electric Arc Furnace (EAF)
> - **Glass cullet ratio** optimally **60–70%** for bottle-to-bottle recycling
> - **Paper fibre length loss per cycle** ≈ **10–15%**

## 1.4 The Seven Standard Recycling Hierarchies (Per ISO 15270 & KTU Syllabus)

| Hierarchy Level | Method | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Primary Recycling** | Re-use of clean scrap in same process | In-house aluminium trimmings re-melted |
| **Secondary Recycling** | Conversion into new products via mechanical processing | PET bottles → polyester fibre |
| **Tertiary Recycling (Chemical)** | Depolymerization, pyrolysis, hydrolysis | PET → BHET monomer → virgin PET |
| **Quaternary Recycling (Energy)** | Combustion with energy recovery (incineration) | RDF (Refuse Derived Fuel) |
| **Quinary Recycling** | Biological / composting | Organic fraction → compost |
| **Disposal (Landfill)** | Last resort | Engineered sanitary landfill |
| **Avoidance/Reduction** | Source reduction (most preferred) | Light-weighting, reuse systems |

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Paper Recycling — Process & Engineering

### 2.1.1 Why Paper is Recycled
- Global paper production ≈ **400 million tonnes/year** (FAO 2023 estimates).
- Virgin pulp requires **17 trees, 26,500 L water, 4,000 kWh energy per tonne**.
- Recycled pulp requires **~60% less energy** and **~50% less water**.

### 2.1.2 The Pulping & De-inking Process
1. **Hydrapulper** — mixes paper with water (consistency 4–6%) to form slurry.
2. **Screening & Cleaning** — removes large contaminants (plastics, staples).
3. **Flotation De-inking** — air bubbles attach to ink particles; floated off.
4. **Washing/Thickening** — removes dispersed inks.
5. **Bleaching** — hydrogen peroxide (H₂O₂) or sodium hydroxide (NaOH) brightens fibres.
6. **Sheet Formation** — Fourdrinier machine forms recycled paper rolls.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hornification** is the irreversible stiffening and reduction of swelling capacity of cellulose fibres caused by repeated drying/re-wetting cycles. Each recycle cycle shortens fibres by **~10–15%**, limiting paper to **5–7 lifecycles**.

## 2.2 Glass Recycling

### 2.2.1 Glass Composition & Colours
- **Soda-lime glass**: ~72% SiO₂, ~14% Na₂O, ~10% CaO, ~4% other.
- Colours: **Flint (clear), Amber (brown), Emerald (green)** must be sorted separately to avoid colour contamination.

### 2.2.2 Process Steps
1. Collection → 2. Manual/Optical sorting → 3. Crusher (gyratory) → 4. Magnetic separation (removes ferrous caps) → 5. Eddy current separator (removes aluminium) → 6. Air classifier (removes lightweight labels/paper) → 7. Optical sorter (by colour) → 8. Cullet washing → 9. Storage silos → 10. Furnace feed.

### 2.2.3 Key Equations
$$T_{melt,cullet} = T_{melt,virgin} - \Delta T \approx 1000°C - 100°C = 900°C$$

> **Energy Savings Rule:** Every **10% cullet substitution** reduces furnace energy by **~2.5%** and CO₂ emissions proportionally.

## 2.3 Ferrous & Non-Ferrous Metal Recycling

### 2.3.1 Steel (Ferrous)
- **Basic Oxygen Furnace (BOF)** — 25–35% scrap input.
- **Electric Arc Furnace (EAF)** — 100% scrap input; produces **1.6 tonnes of CO₂ per tonne of steel** vs. **2.3 tonnes** in BOF.

### 2.3.2 Aluminum (Non-Ferrous)
- **Hall-Héroult process** (primary) consumes **~13–15 kWh/kg**.
- **Recycling remelting** consumes only **~0.7–1.0 kWh/kg** — a **95% energy saving**.
- Each tonne of recycled aluminium saves **~9 tonnes of CO₂ emissions**.

## 2.4 Plastic Recycling — The Six Resin Codes

| Code | Polymer | Density (g/cm³) | Major Application | Recycling Process |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **PET (1)** | Polyethylene Terephthalate | 1.38 | Bottles, fibres | Flake, pellet, fibre |
| **HDPE (2)** | High-Density Polyethylene | 0.95 | Containers, pipes | Blow-moulded re-granulate |
| **PVC (3)** | Polyvinyl Chloride | 1.40 | Pipes, profiles | Mechanical/Chemical |
| **LDPE (4)** | Low-Density Polyethylene | 0.92 | Films, bags | Extrusion pellets |
| **PP (5)** | Polypropylene | 0.90 | Caps, automotive | Injection moulding |
| **PS (6)** | Polystyrene | 1.05 | Foam, cups | Densification/granulation |

### 2.4.1 Mechanical Recycling of Plastics
- **Shredding** → **Wash tank** → **Sink-float separation (by density)** → **Friction washer** → **Hot-wash (NaOH)** → **Drying (centrifuge + thermal)** → **Extruder (with melt filter)** → **Pelletizer** → **Compounding**.

### 2.4.2 Chemical Recycling of PET (Glycolysis Example)
$$PET + EG \xrightarrow{Zn(OAc)_2, \Delta} BHET + EG$$
Where EG = Ethylene Glycol, BHET = Bis(2-Hydroxyethyl) Terephthalate (monomer).

## 2.5 E-Waste (WEEE) Recycling

### 2.5.1 Definition
**WEEE (Waste Electrical and Electronic Equipment)** as per **Directive 2012/19/EU**: any equipment dependent on electrical currents or electromagnetic fields for proper function.

### 2.5.2 Ten Categories of E-Waste
1. Large household appliances
2. Small household appliances
3. IT & Telecom equipment
4. Consumer electronics
5. Lighting equipment
6. Electrical tools (excluding large industrial)
7. Toys, leisure, sports
8. Medical devices
9. Monitoring & control instruments
10. Automatic dispensers

### 2.5.3 Pyrometallurgical vs. Hydrometallurgical Processing
- **Pyrometallurgy** — smelting in furnace; recovers Cu, Au, Ag, Pd; high energy.
- **Hydrometallurgy** — acid leaching (H₂SO₄, HNO₃, aqua regia); recovers rare earths and precious metals selectively.
- **Bio-hydrometallurgy** — uses bacteria (*Acidithiobacillus ferrooxidans*) for low-impact leaching.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbol / Meaning | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Recycling Rate R | R = M_recycled / M_total × 100 | % |
| 2 | Energy savings (Al) | E_save = 1 − (E_recycle / E_primary) ≈ **95%** | Dimensionless ratio |
| 3 | Cullet energy relation | ΔE ∝ −0.25 × C (per 10% cullet) | EAF / Furnace |
| 4 | Fibre length loss | L_n = L_0 × (0.85 to 0.90)^n | After *n* cycles |
| 5 | Material mass balance | M_in = M_product + M_loss + M_residue | Closed-loop check |
| 6 | CO₂ saved (Al) | ≈ **9 t CO₂ per t recycled Al** | LCA basis |
| 7 | CO₂ saved (Steel) | ≈ **0.7–1.5 t CO₂ per t recycled steel** | EAF route |
| 8 | Pyrolysis temperature | T ≈ 400–900°C (no oxygen) | Plastic-to-fuel |
| 9 | Glycolysis catalyst | Zn(OAc)₂ or Sb₂O₃ | PET depolymerization |
| 10 | E-waste recovery (Au) | ≈ **300 g Au per tonne of PCB** | WPCB mining |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Closed-Loop Mass Balance for a Recycling System

### 3.1.1 Problem Statement
For a Material Recovery Facility (MRF) processing **M_in = 1000 kg** of mixed municipal solid waste (MSW), the recovery fractions are: paper 60%, glass 70%, ferrous 80%, aluminium 65%, plastic 25%, with **3% process loss** to rejects. Compute the **net recovered mass** and **overall recovery efficiency**.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Identify the input mass stream.**
$$M_{in} = 1000 \text{ kg (mixed MSW input)}$$

**Step 2: Apply material composition assumption.**

Let us assume a typical Indian/Kerala MSW composition (CPCB data):
- Paper: 12%, Glass: 4%, Ferrous: 3%, Aluminium: 1%, Plastic: 6%, Other (organic/inerts): 74%

**Step 3: Compute mass of each stream entering MRF.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{paper} &= 0.12 \times 1000 = 120 \text{ kg} \\
M_{glass} &= 0.04 \times 1000 = 40 \text{ kg} \\
M_{ferrous} &= 0.03 \times 1000 = 30 \text{ kg} \\
M_{Al} &= 0.01 \times 1000 = 10 \text{ kg} \\
M_{plastic} &= 0.06 \times 1000 = 60 \text{ kg} \\
M_{other} &= 0.74 \times 1000 = 740 \text{ kg} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Apply individual recovery efficiencies (η_i).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{paper,recovered} &= 0.60 \times 120 = 72 \text{ kg} \\
M_{glass,recovered} &= 0.70 \times 40 = 28 \text{ kg} \\
M_{ferrous,recovered} &= 0.80 \times 30 = 24 \text{ kg} \\
M_{Al,recovered} &= 0.65 \times 10 = 6.5 \text{ kg} \\
M_{plastic,recovered} &= 0.25 \times 60 = 15 \text{ kg} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Sum the recovered mass.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{recovered} &= 72 + 28 + 24 + 6.5 + 15 \\
&= 145.5 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6: Calculate the overall MRF recovery efficiency.**

$$
\eta_{MRF} = \frac{M_{recovered}}{M_{in}} \times 100
$$

$$
\eta_{MRF} = \frac{145.5}{1000} \times 100 = 14.55\%
$$

**Step 7: Apply the 3% process loss adjustment.**

Process loss = 0.03 × 1000 = 30 kg (additional reject mass).
Therefore, net reusable mass = 145.5 kg (the 3% loss is already implicit in the η values used).

**Step 8: Final Interpretation.**

> The MRF achieves a **14.55% overall recovery efficiency**, typical of Indian MSW MRFs (which range 10–25%). The remaining 85% is sent to composting, landfill, or RDF preparation.

---

## 3.2 Derivation — Cullet Energy Savings in Glass Furnace

### 3.2.1 Empirical Linear Relation
The energy required (E) in kWh per tonne of glass is empirically given by:

$$E = E_0 - k \cdot c$$

Where:
- $E_0$ = baseline energy for 100% virgin batch ≈ **5800 kWh/tonne**
- $c$ = cullet fraction (0 to 1)
- $k$ = empirical constant ≈ **1450 kWh/tonne per unit cullet**

### 3.2.2 Numerical Verification

For $c = 0.6$ (60% cullet, a typical optimum):

$$
E = 5800 - 1450 \times 0.6 = 5800 - 870 = 4930 \text{ kWh/tonne}
$$

**Savings:**

$$
\Delta E = E_0 - E = 1450 \times 0.6 = 870 \text{ kWh/tonne}
$$

$$
\text{Saving \%} = \frac{870}{5800} \times 100 = 15.0\%
$$

> Hence, **60% cullet** yields ~**15% energy reduction** in glass furnace — directly testable in KTU problems.

---

## 3.3 Derivation — Multi-Cycle Paper Fibre Length Degradation

### 3.3.1 Governing Recurrence

The fibre length after *n* cycles is governed by:

$$L_n = L_0 \cdot (1 - \alpha)^n$$

Where:
- $L_0$ = initial fibre length ≈ **3.0 mm** (softwood kraft pulp)
- $\alpha$ = fractional loss per cycle ≈ **0.10 to 0.15**

### 3.3.2 Calculate fibre length after 5 cycles (α = 0.13)

$$
L_5 = 3.0 \times (1 - 0.13)^5
$$

$$
L_5 = 3.0 \times (0.87)^5
$$

$$
(0.87)^5 = 0.87 \times 0.87 \times 0.87 \times 0.87 \times 0.87 = 0.4984
$$

$$
L_5 = 3.0 \times 0.4984 = 1.495 \text{ mm}
$$

### 3.3.3 Practical Threshold

The minimum fibre length for paper-making is **~0.5 mm**. Therefore, maximum number of cycles:

$$
n_{max} = \frac{\ln(L_{min}/L_0)}{\ln(1-\alpha)} = \frac{\ln(0.5/3.0)}{\ln(0.87)} = \frac{-1.7918}{-0.1393} \approx 12.86
$$

> In practice, the limit is **5–7 cycles** because of hornification, fines accumulation, and ink residue, not just fibre length.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Recycling Technologies

| Parameter | Mechanical | Chemical (Glycolysis) | Pyrolysis | Energy Recovery |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Input form** | Clean sorted | Clean/soiled polymer | Mixed plastic | Mixed residual |
| **Output** | Pellets/flake | Monomer BHET | Fuel oil + char | Electricity/heat |
| **Temperature** | < 280°C | 180–220°C | 400–900°C | 850–1200°C |
| **Energy demand** | Low | Medium | High | High |
| **Emission profile** | Low (water/electricity) | Moderate (catalyst loss) | CO₂, dioxins risk | CO₂, fly ash |
| **Cost (₹/kg)** | 10–25 | 40–70 | 30–50 | 5–10 |
| **KTU Exam weight** | High | High | Moderate | Low |
