# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – June 2023, OECET831]**
*Define the term "Material Recovery Facility (MRF)." List any four unit operations typically carried out in an MRF.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
An MRF is a specialized waste-management plant where mixed municipal solid waste is sorted into recyclable streams (paper, glass, metals, plastics) and residual streams (RDF, rejects) through a series of mechanical and manual operations.

Four unit operations:
1. **Manual pre-sort** at the tipping floor for bulky/recyclable segregation.
2. **Magnetic separation** for ferrous metal recovery.
3. **Eddy current separation** for non-ferrous (aluminium) recovery.
4. **Optical-NIR sorting** for polymer type identification (PET/HDPE/PP).

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, OECET831]**
*What is hornification in paper recycling? State its practical significance.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Hornification is the **irreversible reduction in swelling capacity, flexibility, and bond strength of cellulose fibres** caused by repeated drying and rewetting during paper recycling cycles.

**Practical significance:**
- Limits paper to **5–7 lifecycles** before fibres become too short to form a coherent sheet.
- After 5 cycles, fibre length drops to ~1.5 mm (from 3.0 mm).
- Drives the need for **virgin pulp blending** in recycled paper mills.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks) with Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 4, OECET831]**

**(a) Describe the complete process of paper recycling with a neat flow diagram. Explain the role of flotation de-inking. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Collection & Baling (1 Mark):**
Used paper is collected from households, offices, and industries, sorted loosely by grade, and baled for transport.

**Step 2 — Hydrapulping (1 Mark):**
Bales are loaded into a hydrapulper with warm water (40–50°C) and chemicals (NaOH, Na₂SiO₃, H₂O₂) to form a slurry of **4–6% consistency**. Mechanical agitation defibres the paper.

**Step 3 — Screening & Cleaning (1 Mark):**
Coarse screens (perforated plates) remove large contaminants (staples, plastics); forward cleaners remove heavy grit by centrifugal force.

**Step 4 — Flotation De-inking (2 Marks):**
- Air bubbles injected into a flotation cell attach to hydrophobic **ink particles** (made hydrophobic by surfactants/fatty acids).
- Bubbles rise to the surface forming a **froth layer**, which is skimmed off.
- The cleaned pulp sinks and is collected from the bottom.
- This step removes **60–90% of ink**, critical for producing white paper grades.

**Step 5 — Bleaching & Stock Preparation (1 Mark):**
Hydrogen peroxide (H₂O₂) brightens the pulp; refining improves fibre bonding.

**Step 6 — Paper Machine (1 Mark):**
The Fourdrinier machine forms, presses, and dries the recycled paper into rolls.

> **Flow Diagram (1 Mark):** Same as Section 4.2 above.

---

**(b) Discuss mechanical recycling of plastics in detail. What are its limitations compared to chemical recycling? (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (1 Mark):**
Mechanical recycling is the physical reprocessing of plastics (shredding, washing, melting, pelletising) into secondary granules without altering polymer chemistry.

**Process Steps (4 Marks):**
1. **Collection & Sorting** — by resin code (1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6) using NIR optical sorters.
2. **Shredding** — to 10–50 mm flakes via rotary or shredder granulator.
3. **Wash Tank** — cold wash removes dirt, organics, labels.
4. **Sink-Float Separation** — exploits density differences (PET 1.38 sinks; HDPE 0.95 floats in water).
5. **Friction Washing** — removes adhered paper, glue.
6. **Hot Wash (NaOH 2–4%, 80°C)** — removes printing inks, surface contaminants.
7. **Drying** — centrifugal + thermal air dryer (moisture < 0.5%).
8. **Extrusion** — melt filter (80–200 µm screen) removes fine contaminants.
9. **Pelletising** — strand cutter or underwater pelletiser.

**Limitations vs. Chemical Recycling (2 Marks):**
| Mechanical Limitation | Chemical Alternative |
|---|---|
| Contamination > 5% reduces quality | Tolerates soiled input |
| Mixed plastics (PET+PE) cannot be processed | Glycolysis can selectively depolymerise PET |
| Quality degrades with each cycle | Monomer output equals virgin quality |
| Cannot handle composites/lacquered | Solvolysis can break laminates |

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4, OECET831]**

**(a) Explain the recycling of aluminium with emphasis on energy savings and environmental benefits. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Primary vs. Secondary Aluminium (2 Marks):**
- **Primary aluminium** is produced from bauxite ore via the **Bayer process** (Al₂O₃ extraction) followed by the **Hall-Héroult electrolytic reduction** (cryolite melt at ~960°C).
- **Secondary aluminium** is produced by remelting **aluminium scrap** (cans, foils, extrusions, turnings) in a **reverberatory or rotary furnace**.

**Energy Comparison (2 Marks):**
- Primary: **~13–15 kWh per kg** of aluminium.
- Secondary: **~0.7–1.0 kWh per kg** of aluminium.
- **Energy savings = 95%**, which is the highest of any recycled metal.

**Environmental Benefits (2 Marks):**
- CO₂ emissions: **0.5–1.0 t CO₂/t** (recycled) vs. **~12 t CO₂/t** (primary) — saving of **~9 t CO₂ per tonne of recycled Al**.
- **No bauxite mining** → reduced land degradation and red mud pollution.
- **No perfluorocarbon (CF₄, C₂F₆) emissions** from electrolysis cells (potent GHGs with GWP 6,500–12,000).

**Process Flow (1 Mark):**
Scrap → Sorting (magnetic separator removes steel) → Shredding → Decoating (rotary kiln, 500°C) → Melting (reverberatory furnace, 750°C) → Fluxing (Cl₂ or N₂ to remove H, Mg) → Degassing → Casting.

---

**(b) Describe the chemical recycling of PET by glycolysis. Write the chemical equation and state the products. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (1 Mark):**
Glycolysis is a **chemical depolymerisation process** in which PET waste reacts with excess **ethylene glycol (EG)** in the presence of a transesterification catalyst to yield the monomer **BHET (Bis(2-Hydroxyethyl) Terephthalate)**, which can be repolymerised to virgin-quality PET.

**Chemical Equation (3 Marks):**

$$PET_{(n)} + n \cdot HO-CH_2-CH_2-OH \xrightarrow{Zn(OAc)_2, \, 180-220°C} n \cdot BHET$$

Expanded:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&[-O-CO-C_6H_4-CO-O-CH_2-CH_2-]_n \; + \; n \cdot HOCH_2CH_2OH \\
&\xrightarrow{\Delta, \, \text{catalyst}} \; n \cdot HOCH_2CH_2-O-CO-C_6H_4-CO-O-CH_2CH_2OH
\end{aligned}
$$

**Reaction Conditions (1 Mark):**
- Temperature: **180–220°C**
- Catalyst: **Zinc acetate (Zn(OAc)₂)** or **antimony trioxide (Sb₂O₃)** at 0.5% by weight
- Pressure: atmospheric
- Time: 2–8 hours
- EG : PET ratio: 4 : 1 to 6 : 1 (excess EG for complete depolymerisation)

**Products (1 Mark):**
- **BHET monomer** (repolymerisable to PET)
- **Diethylene glycol (DEG)** — side product
- **Oligomers** — intermediate

**Significance (1 Mark):**
The BHET produced can be purified (recrystallisation) and repolymerised to give **virgin-grade PET**, achieving **closed-loop bottle-to-bottle recycling** — eliminating the downcycling problem of mechanical PET recycling.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Confusing glycolysis with hydrolysis** — glycolysis uses ethylene glycol + transesterification catalyst; hydrolysis uses water/acid/base. Examiners deduct up to **2 marks** for this confusion.
> 2. **Not stating the catalyst** (Zn(OAc)₂) in the equation → **1 mark lost**.
> 3. **Quoting hornification cycles as "infinite"** → wrong; limit is 5–7 cycles → **1 mark lost**.
> 4. **Mixing up cullet colour codes** (Flint/Amber/Emerald) — they must be sorted separately; mixing reduces quality → **1 mark lost**.
> 5. **Omitting the temperature in chemical equations** → KTU mandates process conditions → **1 mark lost**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid-Revision Checklist (Module 4):**

- **Paper recycling:** 5–7 cycles maximum; hornification limits; hydrapulper → flotation de-inking → H₂O₂ bleach.
- **Glass recycling:** 60–70% optimum cullet; 10% cullet = 2.5% energy saving; three colours (Flint, Amber, Emerald) sorted separately.
- **Aluminium recycling:** **95% energy saved**, 9 t CO₂ saved per tonne; reverberatory furnace; no PFCs emitted.
- **Steel recycling:** EAF route uses 100% scrap; saves 60–74% energy vs. BOF.
- **Plastic resin codes:** 1=PET, 2=HDPE, 3=PVC, 4=LDPE, 5=PP, 6=PS (other 7=other).
- **PET glycolysis:** PET + EG → BHET (catalyst: Zn(OAc)₂, 180–220°C).
- **Pyrolysis of plastics:** 400–900°C, no oxygen → fuel oil + char.
- **E-waste categories:** 10 (per EU Directive 2012/19/EU).
- **PCB urban mining:** ~300 g Au per tonne of waste PCB.
- **MRF overall efficiency (India):** 10–25% typically.
- **Recycling rate formula:** R = M_recycled / M_total × 100%.
- **Mass balance:** M_in = M_product + M_loss + M_residue.
- **Hierarchy (preference order):** Avoidance → Reduce → Reuse → Recycle → Energy Recovery → Disposal.
- **Fibre length after n cycles:** L_n = L₀ × (1 − α)ⁿ.
- **Cullet energy:** E = E₀ − k·c (linear relation).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: E-Waste Recycling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OECET831 - WASTE MANAGEMENT > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** E-Waste Recycling # 🟢 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **E-Waste (Waste Electrical and Electronic Equipment - WEEE)** refers to any electrical or electronic equipment that has been discarded by its owner as waste, without the intent of reuse, including all components, sub-assemblies, and consumables that are part of the product at the time of discarding. According to the **E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2022** notified by the **Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change (MoEFCC), Government of India**, e-waste is broadly classified into **two categories** for regulatory purposes: $$ \text{E-Waste} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (\text{Electrical Equipment}_i + \text{Electronic Equipment}_i) $$ Where the categorization is based on the **Indian Standard IS 17790:2021** covering **106 categories of EEE (Electrical and Electronic Equipment)** falling under **15 broad schedules**. **E-Waste Recycling** is the systematic process of **collection, segregation, dismantling, size reduction, separation, and recovery of valuable materials** (precious metals, base metals, plastics, glass) from discarded electronic products using mechanical, thermal, chemical, or biological processing routes, while ensuring environmentally sound disposal of hazardous fractions. ## 1.2 Intuitive Overview & Real-World Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Conceptual Analogy — "E-Waste as an Urban Mine":** > Think of a mobile phone like a small, concentrated goldmine. A single metric ton of **Printed Circuit Boards (PCBs)** from old computers can contain more **gold (Au)**, **copper (Cu)**, and **palladium (Pd)** than a ton of ore extracted from a conventional mine! For example, **1 ton of PCBs** typically yields approximately: > - **200–300 g of Gold (Au)** > - **800–1000 g of Silver (Ag)** > - **150–200 g of Palladium (Pd)** > - **150–200 kg of Copper (Cu)** Just as we mine the Earth for resources, **urban mining** is the process of "mining" discarded electronics. **Recycling** is essentially urban mining done responsibly — extracting useful materials without the environmental damage of primary mining. ## 1.3 Standard Classification Metrics | Parameter | Standard Value / Range | |---|---| | **Global E-Waste Generation (2022)** | **62 million tonnes** (UN Global E-waste Monitor) | | **Global E-Waste Growth Rate** | **3–4% per year** (5x faster than formal recycling) | | **Documented Recycling Rate** | **< 22.3%** globally | | **India's E-Waste Generation (2022)** | **1.6 million tonnes** (CPCB Estimate) | | **Projected India Generation (2030)** | **~3.4 million tonnes** | | **Hazardous Component % by Weight** | **3–5%** (lead, mercury, cadmium, brominated flame retardants) | | **Recyclable Component % by Weight** | **60–70%** (metals, plastics, glass) | | **Toxic Threshold Concentration (Lead in CRT glass)** | **> 2000 mg/kg** | ## 1.4 Sources and Composition of E-Waste > [!NOTE] > **Six Major Sources of E-Waste:** > 1. **IT & Telecom Equipment** (computers, laptops, mobile phones) — **47%** by weight > 2. **Consumer Electronics** (TVs, audio systems, cameras) — **15%** > 3. **Large Household Appliances** (refrigerators, washing machines) — **12%** > 4. **Small Household Appliances** (toasters, irons) — **8%** > 5. **Lighting Equipment** (CFL, LED lamps) — **6%** > 6. **Medical & Industrial Equipment** — **12%** --- # 🟡 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Framework of E-Waste Recycling The recycling process is not a single step but a **multi-stage value recovery chain** that maximizes material circularity. Each stage progressively concentrates the target material while rejecting hazardous fractions. ### Stage-by-Stage Logic > **Stage 1 — Collection & Logistics** > The weakest link globally. Less than **30%** of global e-waste enters formal collection systems. The remaining **70%** leaks to informal "backyard" recyclers in developing nations. > *Why it matters:* Without segregated feedstock, downstream recovery is contaminated and economically unviable. > **Stage 2 — Manual Sorting & Dismantling** > Skilled workers manually remove hazardous components (batteries, mercury lamps, PCB modules, capacitors) **before** any size reduction. > *How it works:* Segregation by **material type** (plastic, metal, glass) using visual and magnetic identification. > **Stage 3 — Size Reduction (Mechanical Processing)** > Shredders, hammer mills, and granulators reduce equipment to **5–50 mm** particles to liberate components from composite structures. > *Critical formula:* The liberation efficiency is governed by: $$ \eta_{liberation} = \frac{m_{liberated}}{m_{total}} \times 100\% $$ Where $m_{liberated}$ is the mass of target material physically freed from the matrix. > **Stage 4 — Separation Technologies** > Multiple physical properties are exploited to separate mixed shredded fractions: | Separation Technique | Property Exploited | Target Material | |---|---|---| | **Magnetic Separation** | Ferromagnetism | Ferrous metals (Fe, Ni) | | **Eddy Current Separation** | Electrical conductivity | Non-ferrous metals (Al, Cu) | | **Density (Sink-Float)** | Specific gravity | Plastics vs. metals | | **Electrostatic Separation** | Triboelectric charging | Mixed plastics | | **Optical Sorting** | Reflectance / color | Glass, plastic types | | **Froth Flotation** | Surface wettability | Fine metal/plastic particles | > **Stage 5 — Advanced Material Recovery** > Pyrometallurgical, hydrometallurgical, or biometallurgical processes are applied to PCB fractions to recover **precious metals (Au, Ag, Pd, Pt)** at purities exceeding **99.9%**. ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Equations for KTU 2024 Examination** | Concept | Formula | Description | |---|---|---| | **Material Recovery Efficiency** | $\eta_{recovery} = \frac{M_{recovered}}{M_{input}} \times 100\%$ | Mass balance across recycling line | | **Concentration Factor (CF)** | $CF = \frac{C_{concentrate}}{C_{feed}}$ | Ratio of target metal in product vs. feed | | **Hazardous Leaching Rate** | $L = \frac{C_{leachate} \times V_{leachate}}{M_{sample} \times t}$ | TCLP (Toxicity Characteristic Leaching Procedure) metric | | **E-Waste Generation Rate** | $E_{gen} = P \times M \times D$ | Where $P$ = population, $M$ = market penetration, $D$ = discard rate | | **Collection Rate** | $R_{collect} = \frac{W_{collected}}{W_{generated}} \times 100\%$ | EPR compliance metric | | **Pyrometallurgical Yield** | $Y_{pyro} = \frac{m_{metal\ out}}{m_{metal\ in}} \times 100\%$ | Smelting efficiency | | **Hydrometallurgical Extraction** | $E_{extract} = \frac{C_{aq,metal}}{C_{initial}} \times 100\%$ | Leaching efficiency in aqua regia or cyanide | | **Specific Energy Consumption** | $SEC = \frac{E_{total}}{M_{processed}}$ in kWh/tonne | Sustainability metric | | **Carbon Footprint Reduction** | $\Delta CO_2 = CO_{2,primary} - CO_{2,recycled}$ | LCA-based comparison | | **Cumulative Energy Demand (CED)** | $CED = E_{feedstock} + E_{process} + E_{transport}$ | Embodied energy accounting | | **Grade of Ore Equivalent** | $G_{e-waste} = \frac{m_{metal}}{m_{e-waste}} \times 10^6$ ppm | Comparison metric with mines | ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility E-waste recycling directly supports: - **Resource Security:** Reduces dependence on imported rare earth elements (REEs) critical for defense and clean energy technologies. - **Circular Economy Compliance:** Directly aligned with **UN SDG 12 (Responsible Consumption & Production)**. - **Carbon Mitigation:** Recycling 1 tonne of **copper** saves ~**85%** of the energy used in primary extraction. - **Toxic Pollution Control:** Prevents leaching of lead, mercury, and cadmium into groundwater. --- # 🔵 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Detailed Derivation: Material Recovery Efficiency from E-Waste Feedstock > **Problem Statement:** A formal e-waste recycling facility processes **10,000 kg** of mixed printed circuit boards (PCBs) in a single shift. After the complete sequence of mechanical, pyrometallurgical, and hydrometallurgical processing, the facility recovers: > - **Gold (Au):** 2.5 kg > - **Copper (Cu):** 180 kg > - **Silver (Ag):** 9.0 kg > - **Palladium (Pd):** 0.18 kg > > **Determine:** > 1. The total precious metals recovered (Au + Ag + Pd). > 2. The **overall mass recovery efficiency** considering the metallic fraction recovered. > 3. The **concentration factor** for gold, assuming the average gold grade in mobile phone PCBs is **280 g/tonne** (i.e., 280 ppm). > 4. The **energy savings** if primary gold mining requires 35,000 kWh/tonne and recycling requires only 4,500 kWh/tonne for the same yield. ### Step 1: Mass of Precious Metals (Au, Ag, Pd) Recovered We begin by summing the masses of the three precious metals recovered from the feedstock. This represents the *secondary production* of valuable elements from the urban mine. $$ m_{precious} = m_{Au} + m_{Ag} + m_{Pd} $$ Now substitute the given values into the equation: $$ m_{precious} = 2.5\ \text{kg} + 9.0\ \text{kg} + 0.18\ \text{kg} $$ Adding the terms term by term: $$ 2.5 + 9.0 = 11.5\ \text{kg} $$ $$ 11.5 + 0.18 = 11.68\ \text{kg} $$ Therefore, the total mass of precious metals recovered is: $$ \boxed{m_{precious} = 11.68\ \text{kg per 10,000 kg of PCBs}} $$ ### Step 2: Overall Mass Recovery Efficiency (Metallic Fraction) The total metallic fraction recovered includes all four metals (precious + base metal copper): $$ M_{recovered} = m_{Au} + m_{Ag} + m_{Pd} + m_{Cu} $$ Substituting the numerical values: $$ M_{recovered} = 2.5 + 9.0 + 0.18 + 180 $$ Performing the addition step by step: $$ 2.5 + 9.0 = 11.5 $$ $$ 11.5 + 0.18 = 11.68 $$ $$ 11.68 + 180 = 191.68\ \text{kg} $$ The mass recovery efficiency is then calculated using the standard efficiency formula: $$ \eta_{recovery} = \frac{M_{recovered}}{M_{input}} \times 100\% $$ $$ \eta_{recovery} = \frac{191.68\ \text{kg}}{10{,}000\ \text{kg}} \times 100\% $$ $$ \eta_{recovery} = 0.019168 \times 100\% $$ $$ \boxed{\eta_{recovery} \approx 1.92\%} $$ > **Interpretation:** Although the percentage is numerically small, this is the *mass yield of high-value metals* from e-waste. In monetary terms, 2.5 kg of gold alone is worth over ₹1.7 crore, demonstrating that e-waste recycling is a *value-intensive*, not volume-intensive, industry. ### Step 3: Concentration Factor for Gold The concentration factor compares the grade of the product stream with that of the original feedstock. For a concentrate of pure gold, the grade in the product is: $$ C_{concentrate} = 100\% = 1{,}000{,}000\ \text{ppm (by mass)} $$ The feed grade of gold in mobile phone PCBs is given as: $$ C_{feed} = 280\ \text{g/tonne} = 280\ \text{ppm} $$ Applying the definition of concentration factor: $$ CF = \frac{C_{concentrate}}{C_{feed}} $$ $$ CF = \frac{1{,}000{,}000\ \text{ppm}}{280\ \text{ppm}} $$ $$ \boxed{CF \approx 3571.4} $$ > **Interpretation:** The recycling process achieves a gold concentration enrichment factor of approximately **3,571×**, transforming a 280-ppm urban ore into a pure gold product. This enrichment is the cornerstone of urban mining economics. ### Step 4: Energy Savings Calculation The energy required by primary (mining-based) production for 2.5 kg of gold is: $$ E_{primary} = \left( \frac{2.5\ \text{kg}}{1000\ \text{kg/tonne}} \right) \times 35{,}000\ \text{kWh/tonne} $$ $$ E_{primary} = 0.0025 \times 35{,}000 = 87.5\ \text{kWh} $$ The energy required by the recycling (secondary) route for the same output is: $$ E_{recycled} = \left( \frac{2.5\ \text{kg}}{1000} \right) \times 4{,}500 = 11.25\ \text{kWh} $$ The energy saved is: $$ \Delta E = E_{primary} - E_{recycled} $$ $$ \Delta E = 87.5 - 11.25 $$ $$ \boxed{\Delta E = 76.25\ \text{kWh}} $$ The percentage energy savings is: $$ \%\ \text{Savings} = \frac{\Delta E}{E_{primary}} \times 100\% = \frac{76.25}{87.5} \times 100\% $$ $$ \boxed{\%\ \text{Savings} \approx 87.14\%} $$ > **Conclusion:** Recycling precious metals from e-waste can save up to **85–90%** of the energy required by primary mining, making it both an economically and environmentally superior route. --- ## 3.2 Worked Example: Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) Compliance > **Problem Statement:** A laptop manufacturer sold **5 million units** in 2021, with an average weight of **2.5 kg/laptop**. The EPR target under Indian E-Waste Rules 2022 requires collection of **70%** of the weight of products sold in previous years. By 2024, the company has collected **7,500 tonnes** of e-waste. > > **Determine:** Whether the company meets the EPR compliance target. ### Step 1: Calculate the Total Weight of Laptops Sold $$ W_{sold} = N_{units} \times m_{avg} $$ $$ W_{sold} = 5{,}000{,}000 \times 2.5\ \text{kg} $$ $$ W_{sold} = 12{,}500{,}000\ \text{kg} = 12{,}500\ \text{tonnes} $$ ### Step 2: Calculate the EPR Target Weight (70% of Sales) $$ W_{target} = 0.70 \times W_{sold} $$ $$ W_{target} = 0.70 \times 12{,}500 = 8{,}750\ \text{tonnes} $$ ### Step 3: Compare Collected vs. Target $$ W_{collected} = 7{,}500\ \text{tonnes} $$ $$ W_{target} = 8{,}750\ \text{tonnes} $$ $$ \text{Shortfall} = W_{target} - W_{collected} = 8{,}750 - 7{,}500 = 1{,}250\ \text{tonnes} $$ ### Step 4: Compute Compliance Ratio $$ \text{Compliance \%} = \frac{7{,}500}{8{,}750} \times 100\% \approx 85.7\% $$ $$ \boxed{\text{The company meets only 85.7\% of its EPR target and is in non-compliance.}} $$ The company would be required under the 2022 Rules to either collect the deficit, pay an **Environmental Compensation**, or purchase **EPR Credit Certificates** from compliant producers. --- # 🟣 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 E-Waste Recycling Process Flow — Functional Architecture The following block diagram illustrates the **integrated functional architecture** of a formal e-waste recycling facility, showing all major unit operations and material flow pathways. ```mermaid flowchart TD A1[Collection Centres
and Drop-off Points] --> B1[Pre-processing Unit
Weighing and Segregation] A2[Manufacturer Buy-back
Programmes] --> B1 A3[Informal Sector
Channel Integration] --> B1 B1 --> C1[Manual Dismantling
Line A] B1 --> C2[Manual Dismantling
Line B] C1 --> D1[Hazardous Component
Removal Battery CRT] C1 --> D2[Reusable Module
Extraction] C2 --> D1 C2 --> D2 D1 --> E1[Hazardous Waste
Treatment Stabilisation] D2 --> E2[Shredding and
Size Reduction 5 to 50 mm] E1 --> E2 E2 --> F1[Magnetic
Separation] E2 --> F2[Eddy Current
Separation] E2 --> F3[Density
Separation] E2 --> F4[Optical Sorting
Unit] F1 --> G1[Ferrous Metal
Fraction Fe and Ni] F2 --> G2[Non-ferrous Metal
Fraction Cu and Al] F3 --> G3[Plastic and Glass
Concentrate] F4 --> G3 G1 --> H1[Refining and
Smelting] G2 --> H1 G3 --> H2[Plastic Reprocessing
Pelletising] H1 --> I1[Hydrometallurgical
Leaching Aqua Regia] I1 --> I2[Precipitate Recovery
Au Ag Pd] I2 --> J1[Electrorefining
to 99.99 percent] J1 --> K1[Final Pure Metal
Products Au Ag Cu Pd] H2 --> K2[Recycled Plastic
Pellets] E1 --> K3[Hazardous Residue

Shredding] P2 --> P3[Copper Smelting
Pyrometallurgy] P3 --> P4[Anode Slime
By-product] P4 --> P5[Hydrometallurgical
Leaching] P5 --> P6[Aqua Regia
HCl plus HNO3] P6 --> P7[Selective
Precipitation] P7 --> P8[Gold Recovery
via Sodium Metabisulfite] P7 --> P9[Silver Recovery
via NaCl] P7 --> P10[Palladium Recovery
via DMG] P8 --> P11[Final 99.99 percent
Pure Au] P9 --> P12[Final AgCl Product] P10 --> P13[Final Pd Product] ``` ## 4.3 Stakeholder Mapping — Extended Producer Responsibility ```mermaid flowchart TD S1[Producer
Manufacturer or Importer] -->|Financial
Responsibility| S2[EPR Authorised
Recycler] S2 -->|Material
Return| S1 S3[Consumer
End User] -->|Return Used
EEE| S4[Collection
Centre] S4 -->|Transport| S2 S2 -->|Treatment and
Recovery| S5[Refining Industry
Metal Recovery] S5 -->|Secondary Raw
Material| S6[Manufacturing
Supply Chain] S1 -->|New Product
with Recycled Content| S3 S7[CPCB and SPCB
Regulatory Oversight] -.->|Monitoring| S1 S7 -.->|Monitoring| S2 S7 -.->|Monitoring| S4 S8[Producer Responsibility
Organisation PRO] -.->|Aggregates and
Coordinates| S1 S8 -.->|Coordinates| S2 ``` --- # 🔴 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Define E-waste. List any four major sources of e-waste generation in India.** **Model Answer:** **Definition:** E-waste or Waste Electrical and Electronic Equipment (WEEE) refers to discarded electrical and electronic equipment, components, sub-assemblies, and consumables that have reached the end of their useful life. Under the **E-Waste (Management) Rules 2022**, it includes all items listed in Schedules I and II of the notification, covering 106 categories of EEE. **Four Major Sources of E-Waste in India:** 1. **Information Technology (IT) and Telecommunication Equipment** — Computers, laptops, servers, mobile phones, tablets, routers. This category contributes the **largest share (~47%)** of total e-waste by weight in India. 2. **Consumer Electronics** — Televisions (CRT and LCD/LED), audio systems, digital cameras, gaming consoles, home theatres. 3. **Large Household Appliances (White Goods)** — Refrigerators, washing machines, air conditioners, microwave ovens, dishwashers. 4. **Medical and Industrial Equipment** — Hospital monitoring devices, laboratory instruments, control systems, factory automation equipment. --- ### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **What is Urban Mining? How does it differ from conventional mining?** **Model Answer:** **Definition:** Urban Mining is the process of recovering valuable raw materials and metals from waste products, buildings, and end-of-life equipment, including e-waste. It treats cities and waste streams as *anthropogenic ore deposits* that can be re-exploited. **Differences from Conventional Mining:** | Parameter | Urban Mining (E-Waste Recycling) | Conventional Mining | |---|---|---| | **Source Location** | Urban waste streams, landfills, WEEE | Natural ore bodies in Earth's crust | | **Typical Grade** | 200–300 g Au/tonne of PCBs | 1–5 g Au/tonne of gold ore | | **Energy Intensity** | ~4,500 kWh/tonne (Au recovery) | ~35,000 kWh/tonne (Au recovery) | | **Environmental Damage** | Low (contained processing) | High (deforestation, acid mine drainage) | | **CO₂ Emissions** | ~5 kg CO₂/kg Au | ~15,000 kg CO₂/kg Au | | **By-products** | Plastics, glass (recoverable) | Tailings, slag, mine waste | | **Renewability** | Continuous supply (growing) | Finite, depleting reserves | Urban mining therefore offers a **sustainable, circular alternative** to primary resource extraction. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** > - Students frequently lose marks in **Part (a) of Question A** by failing to draw a **labelled process flow diagram**. Marks are explicitly awarded for the schematic. > - For **recovery efficiencies** questions, **always show the formula before substitution** — substituting directly without the formula statement attracts zero marks in Part (b). > - **Indian context is mandatory** — referencing **E-Waste Management Rules 2022** and **CPCB guidelines** is expected. Generic global answers lose context marks. > - When asked to compare recycling technologies, students often write paragraphs without a **comparison table**. Always use a tabular format for technology comparisons. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks) — Choose One** #### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` **(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat flowchart, explain the **mechanical processing steps** involved in e-waste recycling. Discuss the role of magnetic separation, eddy current separation, and density separation in material recovery. **(b) [7 Marks]** Calculate the material recovery efficiency and concentration factor for a facility that processes 8,000 kg of mixed e-waste containing 0.5% copper, 0.08% gold, and 0.04% silver by weight, recovering 35 kg Cu, 6.2 kg Au, and 3.1 kg Ag. Compare these results with primary mining yields. --- #### **Model Answer — Part (a) [7 Marks]** **Mechanical Processing of E-Waste — Stepwise Process:** **Step 1 — Manual Sorting and Dismantling:** E-waste items (PCBs, cables, plastic housings) are manually sorted by trained workers. Hazardous components such as **batteries, mercury switches, capacitors, and CRTs** are removed for separate hazardous waste treatment. This step is critical for safety and downstream purity. **Step 2 — Size Reduction:** The dismantled material is fed into **primary shredders** (slow-speed, high-torque) that break it down to **50–100 mm** particles. Secondary **hammer mills** or **granulators** further reduce the size to **5–20 mm**, liberating metal components from plastic and ceramic matrices. **Step 3 — Magnetic Separation:** A **drum-type magnetic separator** (with magnetic field strength of 1,500–3,000 Gauss) attracts ferromagnetic materials (iron, steel, nickel) from the shredded stream. Recovery efficiency for ferrous fraction is **> 95%**. **Step 4 — Eddy Current Separation:** The non-ferrous stream passes over a rapidly rotating magnetic rotor (1,000–3,000 rpm). The induced eddy currents **repel** conductive non-ferrous metals (Al, Cu, brass, Zn) launching them over a splitter into a separate collection chute. Inert materials (plastics) fall straight through. This achieves **90–98% separation efficiency** for aluminum and copper. **Step 5 — Density (Sink-Float) Separation:** Using water or dense media (e.g., calcium chloride solution with specific gravity 1.5–2.5), lighter plastics float while heavier metals sink. This separates mixed plastics from metal concentrates. **Step 6 — Optical Sorting:** High-speed cameras and **near-infrared (NIR) sensors** identify plastic types by their spectral signature. Compressed air jets eject target materials. Final purities exceed **95%** for sorted plastic fractions. **Block Diagram of the Mechanical Processing Line:** ```mermaid flowchart LR Q1[Dismantled
E-Waste Feed] --> Q2[Shredder
50 mm Output] Q2 --> Q3[Magnetic
Separator] Q3 --> Q4[Eddy Current
Separator] Q4 --> Q5[Density
Separation] Q5 --> Q6[Optical
Sorter] Q3 --> R1[Ferrous Stream
Fe and Ni] Q4 --> R2[Non-ferrous Stream
Cu and Al] Q5 --> R3[Plastic Stream
Mixed Polymers] Q6 --> R4[Sorted Plastic
Types] ``` --- #### **Model Answer — Part (b) [7 Marks]** **Given Data:** - Total input mass: $M_{input} = 8{,}000\ \text{kg}$ - Feed composition: 0.5% Cu, 0.08% Au, 0.04% Ag - Recovered masses: 35 kg Cu, 6.2 kg Au, 3.1 kg Ag **Step 1: Calculate the Mass of Each Metal in the Feed** Mass of Cu in feed: $$ m_{Cu,feed} = 0.005 \times 8{,}000 = 40\ \text{kg} $$ Mass of Au in feed: $$ m_{Au,feed} = 0.0008 \times 8{,}000 = 6.4\ \text{kg} $$ Mass of Ag in feed: $$ m_{Ag,feed} = 0.0004 \times 8{,}000 = 3.2\ \text{kg} $$ **Step 2: Calculate the Mass Recovery Efficiency for Each Metal** For Copper: $$ \eta_{Cu} = \frac{35}{40} \times 100\% = 87.5\% $$ For Gold: $$ \eta_{Au} = \frac{6.2}{6.4} \times 100\% = 96.875\% \approx 96.9\% $$ For Silver: $$ \eta_{Ag} = \frac{3.1}{3.2} \times 100\% = 96.875\% \approx 96.9\% $$ **Step 3: Overall Recovery Efficiency (Metallic Fraction)** Total metal recovered: $$ M_{recovered} = 35 + 6.2 + 3.1 = 44.3\ \text{kg} $$ $$ \eta_{overall} = \frac{44.3}{8000} \times 100\% \approx 0.55\% $$ **Step 4: Concentration Factor for Gold** $$ CF_{Au} = \frac{C_{Au,concentrate}}{C_{Au,feed}} = \frac{100\%\ \text{(pure gold)}}{0.08\%} = 1{,}250 $$ **Step 5: Comparison with Primary Mining** | Parameter | E-Waste Recycling | Primary Gold Mining | |---|---|---| | **Ore Grade** | 0.08% (800 ppm) | 0.0001% (1 ppm) | | **Energy per kg Au** | 4,500 kWh | 35,000 kWh | | **CO₂ Emissions** | ~5 kg CO₂/kg Au | ~15,000 kg CO₂/kg Au | | **Concentration Factor** | 1,250× | 50,000× (from 1 ppm to 50%) | | **Recovery Efficiency** | ~97% (Au) | ~75% (Au) | **Conclusion:** Although the concentration factor for primary mining is higher (since the starting grade is far lower), the **net environmental and energy burden** of recycling is dramatically lower, making it the preferred route despite the modest concentration enrichment. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** #### **Question B1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]` **(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss in detail the **pyrometallurgical and hydrometallurgical routes** for the recovery of precious metals from e-waste. Compare their advantages and limitations. **(b) [7 Marks]** A **PC recycling facility** processes 5 tonnes of cathode ray tubes (CRTs) per day. The lead (Pb) content in the funnel glass is **22% by weight**. After leaching with 0.5 M nitric acid, 1,250 kg of lead is recovered per day. Calculate: 1. The mass of lead in the feed. 2. The leaching efficiency. 3. The daily revenue if lead sells for ₹190/kg. --- #### **Model Answer — Part (a) [7 Marks]** **Pyrometallurgical Route:** In pyrometallurgy, e-waste (especially PCBs) is **smelted in high-temperature furnaces** (1,200–1,500°C) with fluxes such as silica, limestone, and copper oxide. The process yields: - A **copper matte** that captures precious metals (Au, Ag, Pd). - A **lead bullion** that collects trace precious elements. - A **slag** of silicates that contains iron, zinc, and minor impurities. - **Off-gases** that must be treated for dioxins, furans, and heavy metals. **Advantages:** High recovery (95%+ for Au, Ag), established industrial scale, suitable for complex feedstocks. **Limitations:** High energy input, releases toxic gases (dioxins, furans from brominated flame retardants), generates large slag volumes, not selective for individual metals. **Hydrometallurgical Route:** In hydrometallurgy, metals are **dissolved selectively using aqueous solutions** (acids, cyanide, thiourea, aqua regia). For PCBs: - **Copper extraction:** Sulphuric acid + hydrogen peroxide at 60–80°C. - **Gold extraction:** Aqua regia (HCl:HNO₃ = 3:1) at 70°C. - **Silver precipitation:** Addition of NaCl to form AgCl. - **Palladium recovery:** Dimethylglyoxime (DMG) precipitation. - **Gold precipitation:** Sodium metabisulphite, ferrous sulphate, or oxalic acid. **Advantages:** High selectivity, lower energy, no toxic gas emissions, suitable for low-grade feedstocks. **Limitations:** Generates large volumes of acidic effluent, requires careful reagent management, slower than pyrometallurgical routes. **Comparative Table:** | Parameter | Pyrometallurgy | Hydrometallurgy | |---|---|---| | **Temperature** | 1,200–1,500°C | 25–80°C | | **Energy** | Very high | Low | | **Selectivity** | Low | High | | **Effluent** | Gaseous (dioxins) | Liquid (acidic) | | **Capital Cost** | High (furnaces) | Moderate (leach tanks) | | **Metal Purity** | 95–98% | 99.9%+ (with electrorefining) | | **Environmental Impact** | Air pollution | Water pollution | --- #### **Model Answer — Part (b) [7 Marks]** **Given Data:** - Daily feed = 5 tonnes = 5,000 kg of CRTs - Lead content = 22% by weight - Lead recovered = 1,250 kg/day - Lead price = ₹190/kg **Step 1: Mass of Lead in the Feed** $$ m_{Pb,feed} = 0.22 \times 5{,}000 = 1{,}100\ \text{kg} $$ **Step 2: Leaching Efficiency** $$ \eta_{leach} = \frac{m_{Pb,recovered}}{m_{Pb,feed}} \times 100\% $$ $$ \eta_{leach} = \frac{1{,}250}{1{,}100} \times 100\% \approx 113.6\% $$ > **Note:** Since recovery exceeds 100%, this implies that **the recovered lead may include lead from other feed streams** co-processed in the same day, or the stated lead content was an *underestimate* of true CRT composition. In actual practice, this would be flagged for mass balance reconciliation and would require a **re-baselining of the feed assay**. **Step 3: Daily Revenue** $$ R = m_{Pb,recovered} \times \text{Price per kg} $$ $$ R = 1{,}250 \times 190 = \text{₹}2{,}37{,}500 $$ Therefore: $$ \boxed{R = ₹2.375\ \text{lakh per day}} $$ Annualized (assuming 300 operating days per year): $$ R_{annual} = 2{,}37{,}500 \times 300 = \text{₹}7.125\ \text{crore per year} $$ --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 E-Waste Recycling Module** - [ ] **Definition:** E-waste = Waste Electrical and Electronic Equipment (WEEE) reaching end-of-life; classified under **E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2022** covering **106 categories** of EEE. - [ ] **Global & Indian Statistics:** Global generation = **62 million tonnes/year** (2022); India = **1.6 million tonnes/year**; document the **3–4% annual growth rate**. - [ ] **Composition Rule of Thumb:** E-waste is **60–70% recyclable** (metals, plastics, glass) and **3–5% hazardous** (Pb, Hg, Cd, BFRs). - [ ] **Recycling Stages (in order):** Collection → Manual Dismantling → Size Reduction → Separation → Advanced Recovery. - [ ] **Separation Technologies (must memorize):** Magnetic (ferrous), Eddy current (non-ferrous), Density (plastics vs. metals), Optical (plastic types), Electrostatic. - [ ] **Metal Recovery Technologies:** Pyrometallurgy (high T, smelting), Hydrometallurgy (aqueous leaching, aqua regia), Biometallurgy (microbial leaching — emerging). - [ ] **Critical Formulae (recite without reference):** - $\eta_{recovery} = \frac{M_{recovered}}{M_{input}} \times 100\%$ - $CF = \frac{C_{concentrate}}{C_{feed}}$ - $R_{collect} = \frac{W_{collected}}{W_{generated}} \times 100\%$ - [ ] **Energy Savings:** Recycling gold uses **~85% less energy** than primary mining. - [ ] **EPR (Extended Producer Responsibility):** Mandates producers to collect **70%** (target) of weight of products sold; enforced under **2022 Rules** with **Environmental Compensation** for non-compliance. - [ ] **Indian Regulatory Bodies:** **CPCB** (Central Pollution Control Board) and **SPCB** (State Pollution Control Boards) enforce e-waste rules. - [ ] **Hazardous Substances to Remember:** Lead (Pb) in CRT glass, Mercury (Hg) in switches, Cadmium (Cd) in batteries, Brominated Flame Retardants (BFR) in plastic housings. - [ ] **Urban Mining:** Recognize that **1 ton of PCBs** ≈ 200–300 g Au + 800–1000 g Ag + 150–200 kg Cu (much richer than primary ore). - [ ] **PRO (Producer Responsibility Organisation):** A collective platform that helps producers meet their EPR obligations. - [ ] **Common Pitfalls in Exams:** Confusing *ferrous* and *non-ferrous* separation; forgetting to convert tonnes to kg in numericals; writing **ppb** instead of **ppm**; missing the **process flow diagram** in long answers. > [!NOTE] > **End of KTU Module 4 — E-Waste Recycling Notes** > *Prepared as per KTU 2024 Scheme | OECET831 — Waste Management | Bloom's Taxonomy Levels: Remember → Understand → Apply → Analyse → Evaluate*

topics.title: A Case Study
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OECET831 - WASTE MANAGEMENT > - **Module:** Module Module 4 > - **Topic:** A Case Study # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is a "Case Study" in Waste Management Engineering? A **Case Study** in the context of waste management is a systematic, in-depth, and evidence-based investigation of a real-world waste management project, plant, or policy that has been actually implemented in a city, industry, or institution. It is used as a pedagogical tool in KTU 2024 Scheme courses to bridge the gap between theoretical engineering principles and their practical field-level application. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition:** A case study is a *detailed descriptive and analytical examination* of a specific waste management scenario — covering its **generation, characterization, collection, transportation, treatment, and disposal** — used to validate engineering design principles, evaluate system performance, and derive lessons for future projects. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Hospital for Trash" Think of a city or industrial zone as a **living organism** that constantly produces "waste" (the equivalent of metabolic by-products). A waste management case study is like a **doctor's clinical case file** of a patient: - The **patient** = the city/industry/zone under study - The **symptoms** = the quantity and type of waste generated - The **diagnosis** = waste characterization and analysis - The **treatment plan** = collection, treatment, and disposal methods - The **recovery** = resource recovery, recycling, and energy generation - The **post-treatment care** = monitoring, compliance, and policy review Just as a doctor studies a patient's history to recommend a cure, an environmental engineer studies a case to recommend sustainable solutions. ### 1.3 Objectives of Studying a Case Study > [!NOTE] > **Core Learning Objectives (Mapped to KTU COs):** > 1. **CO4 — Application:** Apply waste management principles to a real-life scenario. > 2. **CO4 — Analysis:** Analyze the strengths and weaknesses of an existing system. > 3. **CO5 — Design & Evaluation:** Evaluate treatment efficiencies and recommend improvements. > 4. **CO6 — Sustainability:** Assess environmental, economic, and social impacts. ### 1.4 Standard Metrics Used in a Case Study The following **standard performance indicators** must be reported: | **Metric** | **Standard Unit** | **Significance** | |---|---|---| | Waste Generation Rate | **kg/capita/day** (kcpd) | Per-person waste output | | Collection Efficiency | **%** | Fraction of waste actually collected | | Treatment Efficiency | **%** | Removal of pollutants/BOD/COD | | Recovery Rate | **%** | Material recycled or composted | | Cost per Tonne | **INR/tonne** | Economic feasibility | | Landfill Diversion | **%** | Waste diverted from landfill | | Leachate Strength | **mg/L** | Pollution potential of landfill liquid | --- # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Typical Structure of a Waste Management Case Study A KTU-standard case study follows this **7-pillar analytical structure**: ### Pillar 1 — Background & Geographic Setting - Name and type of location (Municipality / Corporation / Industry / Hospital / Campus) - Population served (for municipal) or production capacity (for industry) - Geographic, climatic, and demographic context - Regulatory framework applicable (MSW Rules 2016, Hazardous Waste Rules 2016, BMW Rules 2016, E-Waste Rules 2022) ### Pillar 2 — Waste Generation & Characterization - Total quantity generated per day (**Tonnes Per Day, TPD**) - **Per capita generation**: $$Q_{per\,capita} = \frac{Q_{total}}{P \times 1000} \quad \text{[kcpd]}$$ where $Q_{total}$ is in kg/day and $P$ is population. - **Density of waste**: $$\rho_{waste} = \frac{M_{waste}}{V_{container}} \quad \text{[kg/m}^3\text{]}$$ - **Composition** (typical Indian municipal waste): Organic 40–60%, Recyclables 15–30%, Inerts 15–25%, Hazardous/Household 1–5% ### Pillar 3 — Collection & Transportation System - Door-to-door collection coverage (%) - Number of collection vehicles and trips - **Collection efficiency**: $$\eta_{collection} = \frac{W_{collected}}{W_{generated}} \times 100 \quad [\%]$$ - **Vehicle capacity utilization**: $$\eta_{vehicle} = \frac{W_{load}}{C_{vehicle}} \times 100 \quad [\%]$$ ### Pillar 4 — Treatment Technologies Adopted For each waste stream, identify the treatment chain: - **Organic fraction** → Composting / Anaerobic Digestion / Vermicomposting - **Recyclables** → Material Recovery Facility (MRF) - **Inerts** → Sanitary Landfill / Construction use - **Hazardous** → Secured landfill / Incineration / Stabilization ### Pillar 5 — Design Equations for Treatment Units **Composting (aerobic) — Oxygen demand:** $$O_2 = 1.5 \times C_{organic} \quad \text{[kg O}_2\text{/kg waste]}$$ **Anaerobic Digestion — Biogas yield:** $$Y_{biogas} = V_{digester} \times G_{specific} \quad \text{[m}^3\text{/day]}$$ where $G_{specific}$ = 0.3 to 0.5 m³/kg VS (Volatile Solids) destroyed. **Incineration — Mass reduction:** $$\eta_{reduction} = \frac{M_{in} - M_{ash}}{M_{in}} \times 100 \quad [\%]$$ Typical value: 70–90% mass reduction, 90% volume reduction. **Landfill — Leachate generation (Water Balance Method):** $$L = P + W - E - S - \Delta S_{waste} \quad \text{[m}^3\text{/year]}$$ where $L$ = Leachate, $P$ = Precipitation, $W$ = Waste moisture, $E$ = Evaporation, $S$ = Surface runoff, $\Delta S_{waste}$ = Change in waste moisture storage. ### Pillar 6 — Environmental & Economic Analysis **Cost-benefit metric — Payback Period:** $$T_{payback} = \frac{C_{investment}}{S_{annual}} \quad \text{[years]}$$ **Carbon footprint avoided:** $$CO_{2e\,avoided} = W_{organic} \times EF_{compost} \quad \text{[kg CO}_2\text{e/year]}$$ where typical $EF_{compost} \approx 0.18$ kg CO₂e/kg waste. ### Pillar 7 — Lessons Learned & Recommendations - Identification of bottlenecks - Compliance gaps with statutory rules - Technology improvement suggestions - Community participation and awareness programs --- ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **High-Yield Formula Table for Case Study Numericals** | **S.No** | **Formula** | **Application** | |---|---|---| | 1 | $Q_{pc} = \dfrac{Q_{total}}{P \times 1000}$ | Per capita waste generation (kcpd) | | 2 | $\rho = \dfrac{M}{V}$ | Waste density in collection vehicle | | 3 | $\eta_c = \dfrac{W_c}{W_g} \times 100$ | Collection efficiency (%) | | 4 | $\eta_r = \dfrac{W_r}{W_g} \times 100$ | Recovery / recycling rate (%) | | 5 | $L = P + W - E - S$ | Leachate generation (Water Balance) | | 6 | $O_2 = 1.5 \times C_{org}$ | Oxygen required for composting | | 7 | $T_{payback} = \dfrac{C_i}{S_a}$ | Payback period of treatment plant | | 8 | $CO_{2a} = W \times EF$ | CO₂ avoided via composting | | 9 | $V_{biogas} = V_d \times G_s$ | Daily biogas production | | 10 | $\eta_{red} = \dfrac{M_{in} - M_{ash}}{M_{in}} \times 100$ | Incinerator mass reduction | > All distances and areas in the case study must be reported in **SI units** (m, m², m³, kg, tonnes) and the population in **lakhs**. --- # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked-Out Case Study Numerical — "Green City Municipal Solid Waste Management" ### Case Study Background (Hypothetical but realistic) **Green City** is a Class-II municipal town in Kerala with the following data: - **Population (P):** 5,00,000 (5 lakhs) - **Total MSW generated (Q_g):** 250 tonnes/day (TPD) - **Waste collected (W_c):** 200 TPD - **Waste recycled/recovered (W_r):** 60 TPD - **Waste composted (W_compost):** 80 TPD - **Waste sent to landfill (W_LF):** 60 TPD - **Composting plant capacity:** 100 TPD - **Investment cost (C_i):** ₹15 crores - **Annual revenue from compost + recyclables (S_a):** ₹2.5 crores/year - **Annual rainfall (P):** 3000 mm - **Landfill area (A):** 10 hectares (10 × 10⁴ m²) - **Evaporation + runoff losses (E + S):** 1800 mm/year ### Step 1: Calculate Per Capita Generation $$Q_{pc} = \frac{Q_{total}}{P \times 1000} = \frac{250 \times 1000}{5{,}00{,}000}$$ $$Q_{pc} = \frac{2{,}50{,}000}{5{,}00{,}000} = 0.50 \text{ kcpd}$$ **Interpretation:** The city generates **0.50 kg of waste per person per day** — consistent with the KTU reference range of **0.3–0.6 kcpd** for Indian Class-II towns. ### Step 2: Collection Efficiency $$\eta_c = \frac{W_c}{W_g} \times 100 = \frac{200}{250} \times 100 = 80\%$$ **Interpretation:** 80% efficiency — moderate; **target is 100%** as per MSW Rules 2016. ### Step 3: Recovery Rate (Recycling + Composting) $$W_{recovered} = W_r + W_{compost} = 60 + 80 = 140 \text{ TPD}$$ $$\eta_r = \frac{140}{250} \times 100 = 56\%$$ **Interpretation:** 56% of total waste is diverted from landfill — a reasonable performance for an Indian municipality. ### Step 4: Landfill Diversion $$\text{Diversion} = 100 - \frac{60}{250} \times 100 = 100 - 24 = 76\%$$ **Interpretation:** 76% diversion from landfill — **above the 75% benchmark** recommended by Swachh Bharat Mission 2.0. ### Step 5: Payback Period of Composting Plant $$T_{payback} = \frac{C_i}{S_a} = \frac{15}{2.5} = 6 \text{ years}$$ **Interpretation:** 6-year payback — **acceptable for municipal infrastructure** (typical range 5–10 years). ### Step 6: Annual Leachate Volume from Landfill Convert rainfall to equivalent volume over landfill area: $$P_{volume} = 3000 \text{ mm} \times 10 \times 10^4 \text{ m}^2 = 3 \text{ m} \times 10^5 \text{ m}^3 = 3 \times 10^5 \text{ m}^3/\text{year}$$ Losses: $$E_{volume} = 1800 \text{ mm} \times 10^5 \text{ m}^2 = 1.8 \times 10^5 \text{ m}^3/\text{year}$$ Net leachate (assuming waste moisture contribution is negligible for simplified case): $$L = P_{volume} - E_{volume} = 3 \times 10^5 - 1.8 \times 10^5 = 1.2 \times 10^5 \text{ m}^3/\text{year}$$ $$L = \frac{1.2 \times 10^5}{365} \approx 328.77 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}$$ **Interpretation:** Approximately **330 m³/day of leachate** must be treated — a critical design parameter for the leachate treatment plant (LTP). ### Step 7: Biogas Potential from Anaerobic Digestion (if AD is used) Assume 70% of organic fraction (say 50% of total) is sent to AD: $$W_{AD} = 250 \times 0.50 \times 0.70 = 87.5 \text{ TPD}$$ Using specific yield $G_s = 0.4$ m³/kg VS: $$V_{biogas} = 87.5 \times 1000 \times 0.4 = 35{,}000 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}$$ **Interpretation:** About **35,000 m³/day of biogas** — sufficient to power a **3–5 MW captive power plant**. ### Step 8: CO₂ Avoided via Composting $$CO_{2a} = W_{compost} \times EF_{compost} = 80 \times 1000 \times 0.18 = 14{,}400 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{e/day}$$ $$CO_{2a\,annual} = 14{,}400 \times 365 = 5{,}256{,}000 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{e/year} \approx 5256 \text{ tonnes CO}_2\text{e/year}$$ **Interpretation:** Composting offsets approximately **5,256 tonnes of CO₂ emissions annually** — a major climate dividend. --- ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Case Study Evaluation Framework | **Parameter** | **Calculated Value** | **Standard Benchmark** | **Status** | **Recommendation** | |---|---|---|---|---| | Per capita generation | 0.50 kcpd | 0.3–0.6 kcpd | ✅ Normal | Source segregation awareness | | Collection efficiency | 80% | 100% (MSW Rules) | ⚠️ Gap | Improve door-to-door coverage | | Recovery rate | 56% | 75% (SBM 2.0) | ⚠️ Below | Strengthen MRF operations | | Landfill diversion | 76% | 75% | ✅ Achieved | Continue segregation | | Payback period | 6 years | 5–10 years | ✅ Acceptable | Explore PPP model | | Leachate generation | 330 m³/day | Site-specific | ✅ Manageable | Adequate LTP capacity needed | | CO₂ avoided | 5,256 t/year | Positive value | ✅ Excellent | Replicate across other towns | --- # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Integrated Waste Management System — Block Flow Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TD A1[Source of Waste
Households, Markets, Industries, Institutions] --> B1[Segregation at Source
Wet, Dry, Hazardous] B1 --> C1[Door to Door Collection
Tipping Trucks, Auto Tipper] C1 --> D1[Secondary Collection Point
Community Bin, Transfer Station] D1 --> E1[Material Recovery Facility MRF
Manual and Mechanical Sorting] E1 --> F1[Recyclables
Plastic, Paper, Metal, Glass] E1 --> G1[Composting Plant
Aerobic Windrow or In Vessel] E1 --> H1[Anaerobic Digestion
Biogas Generation] G1 --> I1[Compost Output
Soil Conditioner] H1 --> J1[Biogas to Power Plant
3 to 5 MW Captive] E1 --> K1[Inert Fraction
Construction and Demolition Waste] K1 --> L1[Sanitary Landfill
Engineered Facility with Liner] L1 --> M1[Leachate Collection
Treatment Plant] L1 --> N1[Landfill Gas Capture
Flaring or Power] M1 --> O1[Treated Effluent Discharge
to Sewer or Surface Water] I1 --> P1[End Use Agriculture
Gardens, Horticulture] F1 --> P1 J1 --> P1 N1 --> P1 ``` ## 4.2 Case Study Analytical Workflow ```mermaid flowchart LR S1[Step 1: Background Study] --> S2[Step 2: Waste Audit
Quantity and Characterization] S2 --> S3[Step 3: System Mapping
Collection to Disposal] S3 --> S4[Step 4: Performance Metrics
Efficiency and Recovery] S4 --> S5[Step 5: Numerical Analysis
Formulas and Calculations] S5 --> S6[Step 6: Compliance Check
MSW Rules 2016] S6 --> S7[Step 7: Gap Analysis
Shortcomings Identified] S7 --> S8[Step 8: Recommendations
Engineering and Policy] ``` ## 4.3 Stakeholder Map for a Case Study ```mermaid flowchart TD ULB[Urban Local Body
Municipality or Corporation] --> Reg[Regulatory Body
KSPCB, CPCB, MoEFCC] ULB --> Oper[Operations
Concessionaire or NGO] ULB --> Cit[Citizens
Households and RWAs] Oper --> Tech[Technology Provider
Plant Manufacturer] Oper --> Lab[Testing Laboratory
NABL Accredited] Cit --> Seg[Source Segregation
Two Bin System] Reg --> Mon[Monitoring and Compliance
Annual Report] Lab --> Mon Mon --> ULB ``` --- # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **Q: Define "case study" in the context of waste management engineering. List any four key performance indicators used to evaluate a waste management system.** **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]** **Model Answer:** A *case study* in waste management is a systematic, in-depth investigation of a real-world waste management project or system to study its design, operation, performance, and socio-environmental impact. **Four Key Performance Indicators (KPIs):** 1. **Per Capita Waste Generation (kcpd)** — measures waste output per person per day. 2. **Collection Efficiency (%)** — fraction of generated waste that is actually collected. 3. **Recovery / Recycling Rate (%)** — fraction of waste diverted from landfill. 4. **Treatment Efficiency (%)** — pollutant removal efficiency of treatment plants. --- ### Question 2 **Q: What is the significance of the "Water Balance Method" in a landfill case study?** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **Model Answer:** The **Water Balance Method** is used to estimate the annual **leachate generation** from a sanitary landfill. $$L = P + W - E - S - \Delta S$$ where $L$ = Leachate volume, $P$ = Precipitation, $W$ = Waste moisture, $E$ = Evaporation, $S$ = Surface runoff, $\Delta S$ = Change in moisture storage. **Significance:** - Helps in **designing the capacity** of the Leachate Treatment Plant (LTP). - Essential for **sizing the leachate collection sump** and **pump flow rates**. - Required for **environmental impact assessment** and regulatory clearance. - A critical input for **long-term post-closure monitoring** of the landfill. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) ### QUESTION A (14 Marks) **Q: Discuss a case study of an integrated Municipal Solid Waste Management (MSWM) system in a Class-II town with population 4,00,000 and MSW generation of 200 TPD. The system comprises door-to-door collection, a Material Recovery Facility (MRF), an aerobic composting plant (capacity 80 TPD), and a sanitary landfill. Evaluate the system using the following data:** - Waste collected = 170 TPD - Waste recycled = 50 TPD - Waste composted = 70 TPD - Landfill waste = 50 TPD - Plant cost = ₹12 crores - Annual revenue = ₹2.0 crores **Calculate: (a) Per capita generation, collection efficiency, recovery rate, landfill diversion, and payback period. (b) Comment on the environmental and economic sustainability of the system and suggest three improvements.** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Module 4]** --- ### **Model Solution for Question A** #### **Part (a) — Numerical Analysis [7 Marks]** **Given Data:** - Population $P = 4{,}00{,}000$ - $Q_g = 200$ TPD, $W_c = 170$ TPD - $W_r = 50$ TPD, $W_{compost} = 70$ TPD, $W_{LF} = 50$ TPD - $C_i = ₹12$ crores, $S_a = ₹2.0$ crores/year **Step 1: Per Capita Generation** $$Q_{pc} = \frac{Q_g \times 1000}{P} = \frac{200 \times 1000}{4{,}00{,}000} = 0.50 \text{ kcpd}$$ **Step 2: Collection Efficiency** $$\eta_c = \frac{W_c}{Q_g} \times 100 = \frac{170}{200} \times 100 = 85\%$$ **Step 3: Recovery Rate (Recycling + Composting)** $$W_{recovered} = 50 + 70 = 120 \text{ TPD}$$ $$\eta_r = \frac{120}{200} \times 100 = 60\%$$ **Step 4: Landfill Diversion** $$\text{Diversion} = 100 - \frac{50}{200} \times 100 = 75\%$$ **Step 5: Payback Period** $$T_{payback} = \frac{C_i}{S_a} = \frac{12}{2.0} = 6 \text{ years}$$ --- #### **Part (b) — Sustainability Analysis & Recommendations [7 Marks]** **Environmental Sustainability:** - ✅ 75% landfill diversion reduces soil & groundwater contamination risk. - ✅ Composting 70 TPD organic waste avoids ~ **4,600 tonnes CO₂e/year** of methane emissions from open dumping. - ⚠️ Leachate from landfill (50 TPD) still requires treatment — must ensure LTP compliance with CPCB norms. **Economic Sustainability:** - ✅ 6-year payback period is **acceptable** for municipal infrastructure. - ⚠️ Revenue (₹2 crores/year) is modest; could be improved by selling **certified compost** at premium and **biogas-based power generation**. **Three Recommended Improvements:** 1. **Source Segregation Enforcement:** Mandate two-bin (wet/dry) segregation under MSW Rules 2016 to improve composting input quality. 2. **Integration of Anaerobic Digestion:** Replace 30% of composting with biogas + power generation to double revenue and energy recovery. 3. **Real-time Monitoring:** Install IoT-based sensors for vehicle tracking, bin-level monitoring, and emissions reporting for transparency. --- ### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **Q: A hospital complex in Kerala generates 500 kg/day of Biomedical Waste (BMW). The facility follows BMW Management Rules 2016, with segregation into Yellow, Red, White, and Blue containers. The autoclave handles 60% of the waste, incineration handles 20%, and the remaining 20% is sent to a Common BMW Treatment Facility (CBMWF).** **Calculate: (a) The mass of waste handled by each treatment method and the percentage compliance. (b) Discuss the strengths and gaps in this system, and suggest measures to improve the segregation efficiency.** **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 4]** --- ### **Model Solution for Question B** #### **Part (a) — Numerical Analysis [7 Marks]** **Given:** $Q_{total} = 500$ kg/day - **Autoclave (60%):** $$W_{auto} = 0.60 \times 500 = 300 \text{ kg/day}$$ - **Incineration (20%):** $$W_{inc} = 0.20 \times 500 = 100 \text{ kg/day}$$ - **CBMWF (20%):** $$W_{CB} = 0.20 \times 500 = 100 \text{ kg/day}$$ **Compliance check** — The system follows **color-coded segregation** (Yellow, Red, White, Blue) as mandated by **BMW Rules 2016**, with **100% treatment of all generated waste** (no open disposal). **Compliance percentage:** $$\eta_{compliance} = \frac{W_{auto} + W_{inc} + W_{CB}}{Q_{total}} \times 100 = \frac{500}{500} \times 100 = 100\%$$ --- #### **Part (b) — Strengths, Gaps & Improvements [7 Marks]** **Strengths:** - ✅ Full compliance with **BMW Rules 2016** — color-coded segregation. - ✅ Use of **autoclave** for infectious plastic waste is environmentally superior to incineration. - ✅ Partnership with **CBMWF** for sharps and chemical waste ensures safe disposal. **Gaps Identified:** - ⚠️ **20% incineration** may include plastics → potential **dioxin emissions**, a major public health concern. - ⚠️ Lack of **on-site effluent treatment** for autoclave condensate. - ⚠️ No mention of **autoclave validation** (biological indicator testing) — required by CPCB. **Improvement Measures:** 1. **Replace incineration with Plasma Pyrolysis or Autoclave + Shredder** for plastics to eliminate dioxin risk. 2. **Install a dedicated Effluent Treatment Plant (ETP)** for autoclave condensate discharge. 3. **Implement monthly third-party audits** and maintain a **BMW Management Register** with bar-coded bag tracking. --- ## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in Case Study Questions:** > 1. **Unit Errors:** Writing per capita generation in TPD instead of kg/capita/day (kcpd). **Always convert kg/day to kcpd by dividing by population × 1000.** > 2. **Misidentifying Recovery vs. Diversion:** Recovery = recycled + composted; Diversion = 100 − (Landfill/Generated). They are NOT the same. > 3. **Forgetting % to fraction conversion:** Multiplying TPD by percentages directly (e.g., 0.60 × 500 = 300) is correct only if % is converted. > 4. **No mention of statutory rules:** Any case study answer **must reference MSW Rules 2016 / BMW Rules 2016 / Hazardous Waste Rules 2016** depending on waste type. > 5. **Skipping sustainability analysis:** A KTU Part-B 14-mark case study question **always** expects 3–4 marks for environmental + economic + social sustainability discussion. > 6. **No diagrams:** Always include a **block flow diagram of the system** (worth 2–3 marks). --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — A Case Study (Module 4)** - ✅ A **case study** = real-world waste management system analysis with 7 pillars (background, generation, collection, treatment, design, economics, recommendations). - ✅ **Per capita generation** formula: $Q_{pc} = Q_{total} / (P \times 1000)$ — typical Indian range: **0.3–0.6 kcpd**. - ✅ **Collection efficiency** = $W_c / W_g \times 100$; target **100%** (MSW Rules 2016). - ✅ **Recovery rate** = (Recycled + Composted) / Generated × 100; benchmark ≥ **75%** (SBM 2.0). - ✅ **Payback period** = Capital investment / Annual revenue; municipal projects: **5–10 years**. - ✅ **Leachate estimation** uses Water Balance Method: $L = P + W - E - S$. - ✅ **Biogas yield** from AD: **0.3–0.5 m³/kg VS destroyed**. - ✅ **CO₂ avoided** via composting: $W \times 0.18$ kg CO₂e/kg. - ✅ Always mention the **applicable statutory rule** (MSW 2016 / BMW 2016 / Hazardous 2016 / E-Waste 2022). - ✅ Always include a **block flow diagram** and a **sustainability discussion** in your KTU answer. - ✅ Payback period, diversion, and recovery are the **three most-tested numericals** in KTU case study questions. ---
